    "0471fe0d-5984-4ad8-949e-d3aa792a0853": {
      "children": [
        "a055f6b4-8ff8-4e2d-8649-ff13e8d83667"
      ],
      "id": "0471fe0d-5984-4ad8-949e-d3aa792a0853",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "total 79\n-rw-r--r-- 1 root oai_shared 80245 Jan 14 06:07 N01Final.Last.txt\nN01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS\rTurning: GLASS (G) — Tide 0 through Tide 21 (Three Anneals post-Piercing)\r---\rCHAPTER 1: THE BELL\rThe bell rang wrong.\rNot the slow warning cadence. Not the measured strike that meant \"check your seals, tighten your brackets.\" This was the frantic peal of something already falling, the sound of a structure begging for a hold.\rJhace was moving before the bell finished its third strike.\rHe grabbed the wrong ring of collar keys on the first yank. Swore, dumped them into his palm, and sorted by feel while he ran. One key skittered off the stair edge and disappeared into the dark.\rHe didn’t go back for it.\r\rHis kit bounced against his hip. Chalk. Slate. Collar keys. Tools for measuring failure, not stopping it. He didn't know why his legs were carrying him toward the sound instead of away. Didn't know why his heart counted the bell strikes like they were orders.\rOne. Two. Three. Four.\rThe doubled harmonic hit him at Junction Twelve.\rIt skipped the pipe-hum entirely and punched straight into his sternum. His ribs buzzed like struck iron. His teeth hurt. His mouth flooded metal.\rCopper.\rSharp and immediate, like he'd bitten through wire.\rHe'd been tasting it for two Tides now. Faint at first. A ghost-flavor that came and went with the grid-hum. But this was different. This flooded his mouth so thick he wanted to spit.\rHe didn't.\rUnderface rule: waste nothing.\rHis hands started shaking.\rThen the shaking cut out for three heartbeats and he thought, stupidly, his fingers had finally died.\rAnd then the tremor came back twice as fast.\r\rIt wasn't cold or exertion. It was a fine vibration that made his grip feel unreliable, like his muscles were half-taking orders and half-refusing them.\rHe jammed them into his coat pockets and kept running.\rHe forced his breath to slow. Counted to ten without looking at the numbers.\rThe tremor kept going.\rHe pulled one hand out. Squeezed it into a fist until the knuckles burned. Released.\rStill shaking.\rIt wasn't aftershock. It was something settling into him—fine vibration under the skin—the kind that makes you drop a tool and swear at your own hand.\rHe’d seen it on old manifestors in the deep tunnels. Hands that never found stillness. He’d called it age.\rNow he understood, because his fingers kept twitching against his thigh like a bad motor that wouldn't shut off.\rTallow Street came into view through the access tunnel: upper terrace facade folded inward, stone slabs riding down on support beams that should have held twice that load. Dust cloud thick enough to choke on. People screaming from inside the collapse. Someone rang a hand-bell hard enough to crack its cadence.\rEmergency. Emergency. Emergency.\rA foreman waved at him, face streaked with ash. \"We need a hold. We don't have time.\"\rJhace looked at the structure.\rThe failure line wasn't clean. The whole building was trying to become rubble, one breath at a time. Load shifts rippled through it like a living animal struggling to die. Stone groaned. Iron squealed.\rNo crew could wedge that fast. The wedge bucket was still half-full. Someone was still tying their apron when the stone started to go.\rThe people inside didn't have minutes.\r\"I'm not—\" Jhace started.\rThe heat arrived.\rNot external. Internal. A sudden furnace-flare behind his sternum that made him gasp. Like someone had lit a coal bed under his ribs and told it to burn clean.\rCopper-gold dust swirled around his boots.\rHe watched it climb his legs like static. Like it was looking for him.\rHis chest burned hotter.\rThe doubled harmonic snapped into alignment inside him—two mis-timed gears finally meshing. The wrongness in the grid-hum became rightness in his blood. His heartbeat and the city's failing pulse locked into the same rhythm.\rHe didn't know what he was doing.\rHe didn't know why his feet carried him under the broken lintel.\rHe moved because the archway dust was already falling in steady sheets and somebody inside was screaming a name he couldn't ignore.\rHe put both hands on the main support column.\rLight—thin, copper-gold, filament-fine—threaded from his palms into the stone seam.\rThe building stopped moving.\rIt was only paused—still wrong, still dangerous.\rJhace's breath shook out of him. He could feel every shift in load through his fingertips: the weight of stone, the tension in iron, the panic-motion of bodies inside. The heat in his chest flared with each micro-adjustment, like his body was paying for every heartbeat of stability in increments he couldn't count.\r\"Move,\" he rasped. \"Get them out.\"\rBoots ran. Voices shouted coordinates. Someone coughed dust and kept crawling.\rA woman slid into his peripheral vision—practical coat, hair tied back, slate already in hand despite the chaos.\rShe didn't look at him like a miracle.\rShe looked at him like a system that could fail.\r\"How long can you hold?\" she demanded.\rJhace's jaw clenched. The heat in his chest spiked. \"I don't know.\"\r\"Then we find out.\" She stepped into the dust, coughed once, and shouted to the evac crew. \"Clear corridor A first. Count bodies out. No guessing.\"\rJhace tried to turn his head. The moment he shifted his attention, the heat flared sharper—punishment for looking away.\r\"I have to watch the load,\" he said through his teeth.\r\"Then watch it,\" she snapped, already marking a line on her slate. \"I'll watch you.\"\rPeople came out in bursts: three, then five, then a cluster carrying an older man whose leg bent wrong. Each time the load shifted, Jhace's hands tightened and the filament-light brightened in response.\rHis heartbeat counted itself in his ears.\rOne hundred.\rOne hundred twenty.\rHis arms started to tremble.\rNot from fatigue. From something deeper. A fine vibration that spread into his forearms, up to his shoulders, making his grip feel like it belonged to someone else.\r\"Hold steady,\" the woman said, quieter now, close enough he could hear her through the dust.\rHe didn't answer. Answering took breath.\rAt two hundred heartbeats, the tremor spread into his chest. At two-twenty, his vision narrowed at the edges, copper-gold sparks flickering across it like welding flash.\r\"How many?\" he rasped.\rThe woman didn't look up from the evac line. \"Ten. Eleven. Twelve—\"\rA scream from inside. A child. High and thin and suddenly cut off.\rJhace's hands shook harder.\r\"No,\" he hissed, and the filament-light surged, burning his palms numb.\r\"Go,\" the woman shouted toward the breach. \"Last sweep. Now.\"\rA rescuer crawled under the paused beam line and dragged a small body out by the shoulders. The child coughed dust and cried like she'd been holding it in for hours.\rThe woman's voice cut clean through the chaos. \"Twelve out. Corridor clear.\"\rJhace felt the building try to move again the instant she said it, like it had been waiting for permission.\r\"Terminate,\" she said. No softness in it. Not cruelty. Protocol.\rJhace let the filament go.\rThe building settled the last inch it had been denied. Stone grinding. Iron squealing. But the void where bodies had been was now empty.\rHe staggered back into open air.\rHis hands wouldn't stop shaking.\rThe tremor was small—millimeters—but it was relentless. Like a ghost living inside his muscles, counting the dead he'd prevented and refusing to be quiet about the cost.\rThe woman stepped beside him, dust streaked across her face. \"Name.\"\r\"Jhace Torrins.\"\rShe wrote it without looking, chalk skating on sweat. \"Duration: two-forty—\" The chalk snapped. She swore under her breath and wrote it anyway.\r\"Don't,\" he snapped. Too hard. Too fast.\rShe met his eyes. Her gaze was flat, measuring, absolutely certain.\r\"If we don't measure it,\" she said, \"we kill you next time.\"\rBetween bells, they made it back to the workshop long enough to rinse dust off skin and reset slates.\rJhace sat at his bench and waited for the chest heat to fade.\rIt didn’t. Not fully.\rA banked warmth stayed behind his sternum—steady as a coal bed that refused to go cold. He pressed his palm to his chest. Felt nothing through shirt and ribs.\rInside, the warmth answered anyway. Once. Twice.\rLike the world remembering how it had fit together when he held.\rHis hand lingered there a heartbeat too long. Not thinking. Just reaching.\rHe pulled it away like it burned. The tremor kept moving, but for one heartbeat—only one—the shake eased, like his body preferred the burn to the break.\rThat was the first time the hunger felt like a vote.\rJhace’s hands shook between them like evidence he hadn't wanted.\rHe swallowed copper. Tasted grit. Looked at the child being carried past them on a makeshift stretcher—alive, crying, covered in dust but breathing.\rHe nodded once.\r\"Measure,\" he said. \"Then make sure there isn't a next time.\"\rThe woman's slate scratched.\rShe wrote: Jhace Torrins. Inspector. First manifestation. Tallow Street. Tide 0.\rBehind them, the bell rang again.\rDifferent street. Same dust in his teeth. Same bell tearing its own throat.\rJhace looked down and realized he was still gripping air.\rCopper sat on his tongue. He went anyway.\r---\rCHAPTER 2: THE PATTERN\rThe theodolite wouldn't settle.\rTiffani had calibrated it three times in the last hour. Clean lens. Level base. Reference stars locked. Everything aligned the way it had been for twenty-six Turns of lens work.\rThe crosshair still drifted.\rNo shimmer. No warp. Just the star sitting wrong in the frame—like someone had nudged the sky with a thumb while she wasn't looking.\rShe pressed her palm to the primary lens frame.\rVibration.\rFaint. Constant. The doubled harmonic lived under the normal hum like a second heartbeat. It made her molars ache and her lens hand tremble.\rShe pulled her hand away and wrote on her slate:\rTide 0 — Kindling  \rLens frame vibration: doubled harmonic present.  \rStar Seven-Three displacement: 0.34 degrees west.  \rNeither atmosphere nor mechanism.\rHer mouth tasted copper.\rShe'd been ignoring it for the last two Tides. Faint metallic tang that came and went wi[... ELLIPSIZATION ...]: DEPLOYMENT PROCEDURE. “He will read what he wants to read. I’m controlling the surface.”\rBram exhaled through his nose. “Ah. Compliance theater with a trapdoor.”\r“There is no trapdoor,” she said. “There are exits. Distributed.”\rShe pushed the second stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: Property is not a priority class. Operator veto. Refusal authority. No deployment without independent verification.\rBram whistled. “This one’s the ‘burn the Crown down’ packet.”\r“This one is the ‘keep operators alive long enough to matter’ packet.” She slid it closer. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.”\rBram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.”\r“They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.”\rBram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.”\rTiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.”\rChloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers.\r“Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.”\rBram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.”\rChloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?”\r“Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.”\rTiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage.\rChloen read the first line.\rYOU CAN SAY NO.\rShe looked up. “That’s new.”\r“It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.”\rChloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.”\rAnd then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD.\rChloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.”\rBram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.”\rTiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.”\rChloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it.\rThe door clicked. The room held its breath.\rCorran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen.\r“For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.”\rBram’s grin came instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.”\rCorran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.”\rTiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult.\rCorran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.”\rTiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Then it will be accurate.”\rCorran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi?”\rTiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm.\r“Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in daylight.”\rBram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. That’s adorable.”\r“It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.”\rCorran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?”\r“Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.”\rCorran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.”\rHe left.\rBram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. “So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine.”\r“He gets safety with ownership language,” Tiffani corrected. “He will build a machine either way. I’m deciding where it jams.”\rBram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?”\rTiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.”\rShe lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred.\r“Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge.\rTiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission.\rBram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.”\r“I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said.\rOutside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they'd been caught stealing.\rTiffani didn’t move.\rShe dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.\r\r---\rCODA: GLASS IN THE HAND\rJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them.\rHe’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste was still there anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you can't spit out.\rStone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn't reach. His right hand twitching on its own schedule.\rSomeone had posted a guard outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive.\rJhace tried to sleep.\rEvery time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn't tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city's new baseline living in him.\rHe flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking.\rThen it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place.\rJhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed.\r“I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest.\rOutside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short.\rJhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.\r\r---\rCODA: THE SHARD\rJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them.\rStone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn't lift. A slate he couldn't reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted time like it was a job.\rA guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who'd been chewing dust since Kindling.\rThen the breathing stopped.\rNo footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint.\rThe door opened anyway.\rLight entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape.\rA figure stepped in.\rNo perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive.\rThey didn't speak.\rThey moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel.\rA hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact.\rJhace tried to jerk away.\rNothing.\rHe tried to curl his right hand into a fist.\rNothing.\rThe figure took that hand anyway.\rBoth of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage.\rSomething cold settled into the center of his palm.\rA shard.\rCopper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin.\rCold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache.\rThen heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for.\rCopper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit.\rLight threaded up from his palm.\rNot a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path.\rIt slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal.\rJhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body.\rBut he felt the line.\rHe felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay.\rHis lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision.\rThe figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact.\rThen they stepped back.\rNo check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood.\rThe door shut without sound.\rThe guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed.\rJhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close.\rThe tremor was gone.\rIn its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise.\rSomewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.\r\r---\rEND N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS\r---"
        },
        "create_time": 1768370866.6245906,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "0471fe0d-5984-4ad8-949e-d3aa792a0853",
        "metadata": {
          "aggregate_result": {
            "final_expression_output": "total 79\n-rw-r--r-- 1 root oai_shared 80245 Jan 14 06:07 N01Final.Last.txt\nN01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS\rTurning: GLASS (G) — Tide 0 through Tide 21 (Three Anneals post-Piercing)\r---\rCHAPTER 1: THE BELL\rThe bell rang wrong.\rNot the slow warning cadence. Not the measured strike that meant \"check your seals, tighten your brackets.\" This was the frantic peal of something already falling, the sound of a structure begging for a hold.\rJhace was moving before the bell finished its third strike.\rHe grabbed the wrong ring of collar keys on the first yank. Swore, dumped them into his palm, and sorted by feel while he ran. One key skittered off the stair edge and disappeared into the dark.\rHe didn’t go back for it.\r\rHis kit bounced against his hip. Chalk. Slate. Collar keys. Tools for measuring failure, not stopping it. He didn't know why his legs were carrying him toward the sound instead of away. Didn't know why his heart counted the bell strikes like they were orders.\rOne. Two. Three. Four.\rThe doubled harmonic hit him at Junction Twelve.\rIt skipped the pipe-hum entirely and punched straight into his sternum. His ribs buzzed like struck iron. His teeth hurt. His mouth flooded metal.\rCopper.\rSharp and immediate, like he'd bitten through wire.\rHe'd been tasting it for two Tides now. Faint at first. A ghost-flavor that came and went with the grid-hum. But this was different. This flooded his mouth so thick he wanted to spit.\rHe didn't.\rUnderface rule: waste nothing.\rHis hands started shaking.\rThen the shaking cut out for three heartbeats and he thought, stupidly, his fingers had finally died.\rAnd then the tremor came back twice as fast.\r\rIt wasn't cold or exertion. It was a fine vibration that made his grip feel unreliable, like his muscles were half-taking orders and half-refusing them.\rHe jammed them into his coat pockets and kept running.\rHe forced his breath to slow. Counted to ten without looking at the numbers.\rThe tremor kept going.\rHe pulled one hand out. Squeezed it into a fist until the knuckles burned. Released.\rStill shaking.\rIt wasn't aftershock. It was something settling into him—fine vibration under the skin—the kind that makes you drop a tool and swear at your own hand.\rHe’d seen it on old manifestors in the deep tunnels. Hands that never found stillness. He’d called it age.\rNow he understood, because his fingers kept twitching against his thigh like a bad motor that wouldn't shut off.\rTallow Street came into view through the access tunnel: upper terrace facade folded inward, stone slabs riding down on support beams that should have held twice that load. Dust cloud thick enough to choke on. People screaming from inside the collapse. Someone rang a hand-bell hard enough to crack its cadence.\rEmergency. Emergency. Emergency.\rA foreman waved at him, face streaked with ash. \"We need a hold. We don't have time.\"\rJhace looked at the structure.\rThe failure line wasn't clean. The whole building was trying to become rubble, one breath at a time. Load shifts rippled through it like a living animal struggling to die. Stone groaned. Iron squealed.\rNo crew could wedge that fast. The wedge bucket was still half-full. Someone was still tying their apron when the stone started to go.\rThe people inside didn't have minutes.\r\"I'm not—\" Jhace started.\rThe heat arrived.\rNot external. Internal. A sudden furnace-flare behind his sternum that made him gasp. Like someone had lit a coal bed under his ribs and told it to burn clean.\rCopper-gold dust swirled around his boots.\rHe watched it climb his legs like static. Like it was looking for him.\rHis chest burned hotter.\rThe doubled harmonic snapped into alignment inside him—two mis-timed gears finally meshing. The wrongness in the grid-hum became rightness in his blood. His heartbeat and the city's failing pulse locked into the same rhythm.\rHe didn't know what he was doing.\rHe didn't know why his feet carried him under the broken lintel.\rHe moved because the archway dust was already falling in steady sheets and somebody inside was screaming a name he couldn't ignore.\rHe put both hands on the main support column.\rLight—thin, copper-gold, filament-fine—threaded from his palms into the stone seam.\rThe building stopped moving.\rIt was only paused—still wrong, still dangerous.\rJhace's breath shook out of him. He could feel every shift in load through his fingertips: the weight of stone, the tension in iron, the panic-motion of bodies inside. The heat in his chest flared with each micro-adjustment, like his body was paying for every heartbeat of stability in increments he couldn't count.\r\"Move,\" he rasped. \"Get them out.\"\rBoots ran. Voices shouted coordinates. Someone coughed dust and kept crawling.\rA woman slid into his peripheral vision—practical coat, hair tied back, slate already in hand despite the chaos.\rShe didn't look at him like a miracle.\rShe looked at him like a system that could fail.\r\"How long can you hold?\" she demanded.\rJhace's jaw clenched. The heat in his chest spiked. \"I don't know.\"\r\"Then we find out.\" She stepped into the dust, coughed once, and shouted to the evac crew. \"Clear corridor A first. Count bodies out. No guessing.\"\rJhace tried to turn his head. The moment he shifted his attention, the heat flared sharper—punishment for looking away.\r\"I have to watch the load,\" he said through his teeth.\r\"Then watch it,\" she snapped, already marking a line on her slate. \"I'll watch you.\"\rPeople came out in bursts: three, then five, then a cluster carrying an older man whose leg bent wrong. Each time the load shifted, Jhace's hands tightened and the filament-light brightened in response.\rHis heartbeat counted itself in his ears.\rOne hundred.\rOne hundred twenty.\rHis arms started to tremble.\rNot from fatigue. From something deeper. A fine vibration that spread into his forearms, up to his shoulders, making his grip feel like it belonged to someone else.\r\"Hold steady,\" the woman said, quieter now, close enough he could hear her through the dust.\rHe didn't answer. Answering took breath.\rAt two hundred heartbeats, the tremor spread into his chest. At two-twenty, his vision narrowed at the edges, copper-gold sparks flickering across it like welding flash.\r\"How many?\" he rasped.\rThe woman didn't look up from the evac line. \"Ten. Eleven. Twelve—\"\rA scream from inside. A child. High and thin and suddenly cut off.\rJhace's hands shook harder.\r\"No,\" he hissed, and the filament-light surged, burning his palms numb.\r\"Go,\" the woman shouted toward the breach. \"Last sweep. Now.\"\rA rescuer crawled under the paused beam line and dragged a small body out by the shoulders. The child coughed dust and cried like she'd been holding it in for hours.\rThe woman's voice cut clean through the chaos. \"Twelve out. Corridor clear.\"\rJhace felt the building try to move again the instant she said it, like it had been waiting for permission.\r\"Terminate,\" she said. No softness in it. Not cruelty. Protocol.\rJhace let the filament go.\rThe building settled the last inch it had been denied. Stone grinding. Iron squealing. But the void where bodies had been was now empty.\rHe staggered back into open air.\rHis hands wouldn't stop shaking.\rThe tremor was small—millimeters—but it was relentless. Like a ghost living inside his muscles, counting the dead he'd prevented and refusing to be quiet about the cost.\rThe woman stepped beside him, dust streaked across her face. \"Name.\"\r\"Jhace Torrins.\"\rShe wrote it without looking, chalk skating on sweat. \"Duration: two-forty—\" The chalk snapped. She swore under her breath and wrote it anyway.\r\"Don't,\" he snapped. Too hard. Too fast.\rShe met his eyes. Her gaze was flat, measuring, absolutely certain.\r\"If we don't measure it,\" she said, \"we kill you next time.\"\rBetween bells, they made it back to the workshop long enough to rinse dust off skin and reset slates.\rJhace sat at his bench and waited for the chest heat to fade.\rIt didn’t. Not fully.\rA banked warmth stayed behind his sternum—steady as a coal bed that refused to go cold. He pressed his palm to his chest. Felt nothing through shirt and ribs.\rInside, the warmth answered anyway. Once. Twice.\rLike the world remembering how it had fit together when he held.\rHis hand lingered there a heartbeat too long. Not thinking. Just reaching.\rHe pulled it away like it burned. The tremor kept moving, but for one heartbeat—only one—the shake eased, like his body preferred the burn to the break.\rThat was the first time the hunger felt like a vote.\rJhace’s hands shook between them like evidence he hadn't wanted.\rHe swallowed copper. Tasted grit. Looked at the child being carried past them on a makeshift stretcher—alive, crying, covered in dust but breathing.\rHe nodded once.\r\"Measure,\" he said. \"Then make sure there isn't a next time.\"\rThe woman's slate scratched.\rShe wrote: Jhace Torrins. Inspector. First manifestation. Tallow Street. Tide 0.\rBehind them, the bell rang again.\rDifferent street. Same dust in his teeth. Same bell tearing its own throat.\rJhace looked down and realized he was still gripping air.\rCopper sat on his tongue. The copper taste stayed.\r“I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest.\rOutside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short.\rJhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.\r\r---\rCODA: THE SHARD\rJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them.\rStone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn't lift. A slate he couldn't reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted time like it was a job.\rA guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who'd been chewing dust since Kindling.\rThen the breathing stopped.\rNo footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint.\rThe door opened anyway.\rLight entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape.\rA figure stepped in.\rNo perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive.\rThey didn't speak.\rThey moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel.\rA hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact.\rJhace tried to jerk away.\rNothing.\rHe tried to curl his right hand into a fist.\rNothing.\rThe figure took that hand anyway.\rBoth of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage.\rSomething cold settled into the center of his palm.\rA shard.\rCopper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin.\rCold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache.\rThen heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for.\rCopper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit.\rLight threaded up from his palm.\rNot a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path.\rIt slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal.\rJhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body.\rBut he felt the line.\rHe felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay.\rHis lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision.\rThe figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact.\rThen they stepped back.\rNo check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood.\rThe door shut without sound.\rThe guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed.\rJhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close.\rThe tremor was gone.\rIn its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise.\rSomewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.\r\r---\rEND N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS\r---",
            "messages": [],
            "status": "success"
          },
          "can_save": false,

    "04d9bba5-2b0d-4087-a323-b8fdfb701bb4": {
      "children": [
        "355cc99e-caca-4adb-9a7c-68292f5b8571"
      ],
      "id": "04d9bba5-2b0d-4087-a323-b8fdfb701bb4",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},

          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "91 CODA: THE SHARD\n92 Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them.\n93 Stone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn't lift. A slate he couldn't reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted time like it was a job.\n94 A guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who'd been chewing dust since Kindling.\n95 Then the breathing stopped.\n96 No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint.\n97 The door opened anyway.\n98 Light entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape.\n99 A figure stepped in.\n100 No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive.\n101 They didn't speak.\n102 They moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel.\n103 A hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact.\n104 Jhace tried to jerk away.\n105 Nothing.\n106 He tried to curl his right hand into a fist.\n107 Nothing.\n108 The figure took that hand anyway.\n109 Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage.\n110 Something cold settled into the center of his palm.\n111 A shard.\n112 Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin.\n113 Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache.\n114 Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for.\n115 Copper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit.\n116 Light threaded up from his palm.\n117 Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path.\n118 It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal.\n119 Jhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body.\n120 But he felt the line.\n121 He felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay.\n122 His lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision.\n123 The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact.\n124 Then they stepped back.\n125 No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood.\n126 The door shut without sound.\n127 The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed.\n128 Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close.\n129 The tremor was gone.\n130 In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise.\n131 Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.\n132 ---\n"
        },
        "create_time": 1768417237.152025,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "04d9bba5-2b0d-4087-a323-b8fdfb701bb4",

        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "Fixed the violations you flagged (and the other obvious ones of the same class) in **`N01_GripThatHolds_LOCKED_Ch1-13_Coda_CLEAN.docx`**:\n\n- Removed **cardinal direction / degrees** usage (e.g., “degrees west”) and converted to **in-world neutral phrasing** (“marks off true,” etc.).\n- Removed **forbidden real-world time units** (`hour(s)`, `minute(s)`, `second(s)`, `day(s)`, `today`) and replaced with **Te’Oga-safe equivalents** (primarily **Tide / heartbeats**, or rephrased to avoid hard units).\n- Cleaned the remaining **“second/seconds”** time-word leaks (including places where it was used as duration language).\n- Kept paragraph styles intact while rewriting text (no new content added; just compliance edits).\n\nHere’s the corrected file:\n\n[Download the corrected DOCX](sandbox:/mnt/data/N01_GripThatHolds_LOCKED_Ch1-13_Coda_CLEAN_VIOLATIONFIX.docx)"
          ]

    "0634b9a2-d676-4998-bbbc-a3f4eb149f15": {
      "children": [
        "a4d84b79-3363-4f03-9692-61f0c4c49e56"
      ],
      "id": "0634b9a2-d676-4998-bbbc-a3f4eb149f15",

        "channel": "final",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "Received.\n\nStatus right now:\n- **Chapter 7 is NOT locked yet** (you didn’t label it locked), but I’ll treat this pasted text as **your current Chapter 7 candidate** for continuity until you say otherwise.\n\nQuick, no-bullshit continuity check against locked 1–6:\n\n### What fits perfectly\n- Voice/cadence matches 1–5.\n- The “network” concept finally bites: multiple operators, spotters, duration caps, counted exits.\n- Daved’s lock is a clean escalation from Corran’s Stage Three packet without jumping ahead into hunger mechanics.\n- Tiffani’s “state exit count, not your suffering” is on-brand ruthless and useful.\n\n### What’s risky / needs attention before you lock it\n- **New names** (Merra, Daved, Kess) appear fast with minimal anchoring. It works in a chaos scene, but you need *one* clean identifier line each or they’ll blur later.\n  - Merra: what is she (hall coordinator, runner, foreman, clerk)?\n  - Daved: operator? experienced? already Stage Two?\n  - Kess: operator? why the stiff “operational requirements” voice?\n- **Two metaphors** are a little “writerly” compared to earlier locked chapters:\n  - “Thirty heartbeats passed like a slow knife.”\n  - “like pulling a hook out of meat.”\n  They’re not illegal, but they’re slightly higher-flair than your baseline. If you keep them, that becomes part of Chapter 7’s tonal signature. If you don’t want drift, flatten them.\n- **The Temple Observer line** (“worthy/unworthy”) is fine conflict spice, but if your wider rule is *no clean moral binaries*, you may want the observer framed as an individual idiot, not a moral thesis of the book. Right now it reads as “random zealot,” which is acceptable.\n\nIf you want, I can do one of these immediately (no questions, just pick):\n1) **Tighten/anchor Merra/Daved/Kess** with 1–2 lines each, no added beats.  \n2) **Flatten the two metaphors** while keeping punch.  \n3) **Produce a “Lock-Ready” Chapter 7** with only micro-edits (no new content), preserving your exact structure.\n\nSay which option number."
          ]
        },

    "07351d3f-d803-4491-b8ad-142dc2ee13b1": {
      "children": [
        "b294591c-f107-42a1-bcdc-98b8b80b4ea0"
      ],
      "id": "07351d3f-d803-4491-b8ad-142dc2ee13b1",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "python",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "(13,\n ['CHAPTER 1: THE BELL', 'CHAPTER 2: THE PATTERN', 'CHAPTER 3: SECOND HOLD'],\n [\"Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them. He’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste was still there anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you can't spit out. Stone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn't reach. His right hand twitching on its own schedule. Someone had posted a guard outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive. Jhace tried to sleep. Every time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn't tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city's new baseline living in him. He flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking. Then it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place. Jhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed. “I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest. Outside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short. Jhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.\",\n  \"The guard’s breathing stopped. No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint. The door opened anyway. Light entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape. A figure stepped in. No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive. They didn't speak. They moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel. A hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact. Jhace tried to jerk away. Nothing. He tried to curl his right hand into a fist. Nothing. The figure took that hand anyway. Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage. Something cold settled into the center of his palm. A shard. Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin. Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache. Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for. Copper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit. Light threaded up from his palm. Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path. It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal. Jhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body. But he felt the line. He felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay. His lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision. The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact. Then they stepped back. No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood. The door shut without sound. The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed. Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close. The tremor was gone. In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise. Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.\",\n  '*END N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS*'])"
        },
        "create_time": 1768420940.9757872,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "07351d3f-d803-4491-b8ad-142dc2ee13b1",
        "metadata": {
          "aggregate_result": {
            "code": "# reload and verify\nd=Document(out_path)\ntexts=[p.text for p in d.paragraphs if p.text.strip()]\nsum(1 for t in texts if t.startswith(\"CHAPTER \")), [t for t in texts if t.startswith(\"CHAPTER \")][:3], texts[-3:]\n",
            "end_time": 1768420942.521723,

They moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel. A hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact. Jhace tried to jerk away. Nothing. He tried to curl his right hand into a fist. Nothing. The figure took that hand anyway. Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage. Something cold settled into the center of his palm. A shard. Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin. Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache. Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for. Copper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit. Light threaded up from his palm. Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path. It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal. Jhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body. But he felt the line. He felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay. His lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision. The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact. Then they stepped back. No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood. The door shut without sound. The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed. Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close. The tremor was gone. In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise. Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.\",\n  '*END N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS*'])"
                  }
                },
