    "08377e86-bcdd-4caa-8e3c-1782ee375616": {
      "children": [
        "499c2111-95dd-4b64-8cde-e079e6435aa0"
      ],
      "id": "08377e86-bcdd-4caa-8e3c-1782ee375616",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "file_search",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "multimodal_text",
          "parts": [
            "Make sure to include fileciteturn0file0 in your response to cite this file, or to surface it as a link.",
            "N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS\rTurning: GLASS (G) — Tide 0 through Tide 21 (Three Anneals post-Piercing)\r---\rCHAPTER 1: THE BELL\rThe bell rang wrong.\rNot the slow warning cadence. Not the measured strike that meant \"check your seals, tighten your brackets.\" This was the frantic peal of something already falling, the sound of a structure begging for a hold.\rJhace was moving before the bell finished its third strike.\rHe grabbed the wrong ring of collar keys on the first yank. Swore, dumped them into his palm, and sorted by feel while he ran. One key skittered off the stair edge and disappeared into the dark.\rHe didn’t go back for it.\r\rHis kit bounced against his hip. Chalk. Slate. Collar keys. Tools for measuring failure, not stopping it. He didn't know why his legs were carrying him toward the sound instead of away. Didn't know why his heart counted the bell strikes like they were orders.\rOne. Two. Three. Four.\rThe doubled harmonic hit him at Junction Twelve.\rIt skipped the pipe-hum entirely and punched straight into his sternum. His ribs buzzed like struck iron. His teeth hurt. His mouth flooded metal.\rCopper.\rSharp and immediate, like he'd bitten through wire.\rHe'd been tasting it for two Tides now. Faint at first. A ghost-flavor that came and went with the grid-hum. But this was different. This flooded his mouth so thick he wanted to spit.\rHe didn't.\rUnderface rule: waste nothing.\rHis hands started shaking.\rThen the shaking cut out for three heartbeats and he thought, stupidly, his fingers had finally died.\rAnd then the tremor came back twice as fast.\r\rIt wasn't cold or exertion. It was a fine vibration that made his grip feel unreliable, like his muscles were half-taking orders and half-refusing them.\rHe jammed them into his coat pockets and kept running.\rHe forced his breath to slow. Counted to ten without looking at the numbers.\rThe tremor kept going.\rHe pulled one hand out. Squeezed it into a fist until the knuckles burned. Released.\rStill shaking.\rIt wasn't aftershock. It was something settling into him—fine vibration under the skin—the kind that makes you drop a tool and swear at your own hand.\rHe’d seen it on old manifestors in the deep tunnels. Hands that never found stillness. He’d called it age.\rNow he understood, because his fingers kept twitching against his thigh like a bad motor that wouldn't shut off.\rTallow Street came into view through the access tunnel: upper terrace facade folded inward, stone slabs riding down on support beams that should have held twice that load. Dust cloud thick enough to choke on. People screaming from inside the collapse. Someone rang a hand-bell hard enough to crack its cadence.\rEmergency. Emergency. Emergency.\rA foreman waved at him, face streaked with ash. \"We need a hold. We don't have time.\"\rJhace looked at the structure.\rThe failure line wasn't clean. The whole building was trying to become rubble, one breath at a time. Load shifts rippled through it like a living animal struggling to die. Stone groaned. Iron squealed.\rNo crew could wedge that fast. The wedge bucket was still half‑full. Someone was still tying their apron when the stone started to go.\rThe people inside didn't have minutes.\r\"I'm not—\" Jhace started.\rThe heat arrived.\rNot external. Internal. A sudden furnace-flare behind his sternum that made him gasp. Like someone had lit a coal bed under his ribs and told it to burn clean.\rCopper-gold dust swirled around his boots.\rHe watched it climb his legs like static. Like it was looking for him.\rHis chest burned hotter.\rThe doubled harmonic snapped into alignment inside him—two mis-timed gears finally meshing. The wrongness in the grid-hum became rightness in his blood. His heartbeat and the city's failing pulse locked into the same rhythm.\rHe didn't know what he was doing.\rHe didn't know why his feet carried him under the broken lintel.\rHe moved because the archway dust was already falling in steady sheets and somebody inside was screaming a name he couldn't ignore.\rHe put both hands on the main support column.\rLight—thin, copper-gold, filament-fine—threaded from his palms into the stone seam.\rThe building stopped moving.\rIt was only paused—still wrong, still dangerous.\rJhace's breath shook out of him. He could feel every shift in load through his fingertips: the weight of stone, the tension in iron, the panic-motion of bodies inside. The heat in his chest flared with each micro-adjustment, like his body was paying for every heartbeat of stability in increments he couldn't count.\r\"Move,\" he rasped. \"Get them out.\"\rBoots ran. Voices shouted coordinates. Someone coughed dust and kept crawling.\rA woman slid into his peripheral vision—practical coat, hair tied back, slate already in hand despite the chaos.\rShe didn't look at him like a miracle.\rShe looked at him like a system that could fail.\r\"How long can you hold?\" she demanded.\rJhace's jaw clenched. The heat in his chest spiked. \"I don't know.\"\r\"Then we find out.\" She stepped into the dust, coughed once, and shouted to the evac crew. \"Clear corridor A first. Count bodies out. No guessing.\"\rJhace tried to turn his head. The moment he shifted his attention, the heat flared sharper—punishment for looking away.\r\"I have to watch the load,\" he said through his teeth.\r\"Then watch it,\" she snapped, already marking a line on her slate. \"I'll watch you.\"\rPeople came out in bursts: three, then five, then a cluster carrying an older man whose leg bent wrong. Each time the load shifted, Jhace's hands tightened and the filament-light brightened in response.\rHis heartbeat counted itself in his ears.\rOne hundred.\rOne hundred twenty.\rHis arms started to tremble.\rNot from fatigue. From something deeper. A fine vibration that spread into his forearms, up to his shoulders, making his grip feel like it belonged to someone else.\r\"Hold steady,\" the woman said, quieter now, close enough he could hear her through the dust.\rHe didn't answer. Answering took breath.\rAt two hundred heartbeats, the tremor spread into his chest. At two-twenty, his vision narrowed at the edges, copper-gold sparks flickering across it like welding flash.\r\"How many?\" he rasped.\rThe woman didn't look up from the evac line. \"Ten. Eleven. Twelve—\"\rA scream from inside. A child. High and thin and suddenly cut off.\rJhace's hands shook harder.\r\"No,\" he hissed, and the filament-light surged, burning his palms numb.\r\"Go,\" the woman shouted toward the breach. \"Last sweep. Now.\"\rA rescuer crawled under the paused beam line and dragged a small body out by the shoulders. The child coughed dust and cried like she'd been holding it in for hours.\rThe woman's voice cut clean through the chaos. \"Twelve out. Corridor clear.\"\rJhace felt the building try to move again the instant she said it, like it had been waiting for permission.\r\"Terminate,\" she said. No softness in it. Not cruelty. Protocol.\rJhace let the filament go.\rThe building settled the last inch it had been denied. Stone grinding. Iron squealing. But the void where bodies had been was now empty.\rHe staggered back into open air.\rHis hands wouldn't stop shaking.\rThe tremor was small—millimeters—but it was relentless. Like a ghost living inside his muscles, counting the dead he'd prevented and refusing to be quiet about the cost.\rThe woman stepped beside him, dust streaked across her face. \"Name.\"\r\"Jhace Torrins.\"\rShe wrote it without looking, chalk skating on sweat. \"Duration: two‑forty—\" The chalk snapped. She swore under her breath and wrote it anyway.\r\"Don't,\" he snapped. Too hard. Too fast.\rShe met his eyes. Her gaze was flat, measuring, absolutely certain.\r\"If we don't measure it,\" she said, \"we kill you next time.\"\rBetween bells, they made it back to the workshop long enough to rinse dust off skin and reset slates.\rJhace sat at his bench and waited for the chest heat to fade.\rIt didn’t. Not fully.\rA banked warmth stayed behind his sternum—steady as a coal bed that refused to go cold. He pressed his palm to his chest. Felt nothing through shirt and ribs.\rInside, the warmth answered anyway. Once. Twice.\rLike the world remembering how it had fit together when he held.\rHis hand lingered there a heartbeat too long. Not thinking. Just reaching.\rHe pulled it away like it burned. The tremor kept moving, but for one heartbeat—only one—the shake eased, like his body preferred the burn to the break.\rThat was the first time the hunger felt like a vote.\rJhace’s hands shook between them like evidence he hadn't wanted.\rHe swallowed copper. Tasted grit. Looked at the child being carried past them on a makeshift stretcher—alive, crying, covered in dust but breathing.\rHe nodded once.\r\"Measure,\" he said. \"Then make sure there isn't a next time.\"\rThe woman's slate scratched.\rShe wrote: Jhace Torrins. Inspector. First manifestation. Tallow Street. Tide 0.\rBehind them, the bell rang again.\rDifferent street. Same dust in his teeth. Same bell tearing its own throat.\rJhace looked down and realized he was still gripping air.\rCopper sat on his tongue. He went anyway.\r---\rCHAPTER 2: THE PATTERN\rThe theodolite wouldn't settle.\rTiffani had calibrated it three times in the last hour. Clean lens. Level base. Reference stars locked. Everything aligned the way it had been for twenty-six Turns of lens work.\rThe crosshair still drifted.\rNo shimmer. No warp. Just the star sitting wrong in the frame—like someone had nudged the sky with a thumb while she wasn't looking.\rShe pressed her palm to the primary lens frame.\rVibration.\rFaint. Constant. The doubled harmonic lived under the normal hum like a second heartbeat. It made her molars ache and her lens hand tremble.\rShe pulled her hand away and wrote on her slate:\rTide 0 — Kindling  \rLens frame vibration: doubled harmonic present.  \rStar Seven-Three displacement: 0.34 degrees west.  \rNeither atmosphere nor mechanism.\rHer mouth tasted copper.\rShe'd been ignoring it for the last two Tides. Faint metallic tang that came and went with air currents from the ventilation shafts. But this was different. This arrived sharp and immediate, flooding her tongue like she'd been chewing wire.\rThe light outside the lens port changed.\rThe light didn’t dim or brighten. It bent.\rTiffani looked up.\rFor a second she couldn't move. Her brain offered one sentence, raw and useless: What the fuck is that?\rThe sky over Saint Merro wasn't supposed to do anything except hold weather and stars. Today it held something else.\rA line. Straight. Dark. Moving too fast for her eyes to track at first, then slowing enough for her to understand what she was seeing.\rA spindle.\rLong and narrow and wrong, like someone had driven a forged bar through the world and walked away.\rHer breath stopped.\rFor a stupid second, she couldn’t make her fingers obey. She stood there with her hand half-raised, as if she could physically stop the sky from doing whatever it was doing.\r\rThe spindle crossed her view in a single heartbeat. No friction fire. No sound. Just a displacement of light that made the air look thin.\rThen gravity lurched.\rNot a shift sideways. An up-and-down stagger, like the planet had been grabbed and shaken once. Her slate slid across the stone desk. A lens clamp snapped. Glass sang a sharp, stressed note and went silent.\rTiffani caught herself on the observation frame and forced her eyes back to the port.\rThe spindle was gone.\rPunched through. Somewhere below. Into stone or ocean or the Underface—she couldn't tell.\rPressure arrived a breath later. Not a blast. More like the city inhaling and forgetting how to exhale. Dust fell from the ceiling mortar in a slow, dry drift.\rThen copper-gold particulate began falling outside.\rGlittering. Fine. Catching the lamplight like ground glass in a furnace beam. It coated the stone lip of the observation port and clung to the iron frame in flecks that shimmered when she moved.\rTiffani touched it with her fingertip.\rIt didn't smear like ash. It gritted. Abrasive. Her finger tingled where the dust touched skin.\rShe wrote:\rAtmospheric intrusion event. Spindle-form object. Estimated velocity: extreme.  \rCopper-gold particulate fall: immediate post-impact.  \rAdheres to metal preferentially. Abrasive. Conductive?\rHer pencil slipped. The last word smeared into a dark streak. She stared at it, angry at her own hand, then rewrote the line harder than necessary.\r\rA bell rang.\rNot the Glass-House alert. Distant. Down-terrace. The frantic peal of something already falling.\rThen another bell. Different direction. Same cadence.\rEmergency. Emergency. Emergency.\rTiffani's stomach tightened.\rShe grabbed her slate, shoved it into her coat pocket, and ran.\rThe doubled harmonic followed her down the Glass-House stairs. She could feel it in the stone railing—vibration that hadn't been there this morning. The walls themselves were listening to something new.\rAt the mid-terrace landing, she stopped.\rTallow Street was visible from here: upper facade collapsed inward, dust cloud thick enough to choke on. People running. Voices screaming coordinates she couldn't parse.\rA third bell rang. Sector Nine. Grain district.\rCloser—close enough that the air changed when the facade flexed.\rTiffani made her choice the way she always did: follow the data to its source.\rShe ran toward Sector Nine.\rThe copper taste got stronger with every stride.\r---\rCHAPTER 3: SECOND HOLD\rThe grain warehouse sat in Sector Nine, where the terraces were newer and the beams were honest enough to still pretend they could be trusted.\rThe collapse wasn't honest.\rSupport columns had sheared at the base—clean breaks that didn't match load failure. The upper floor sagged like a shelf someone had stepped on. Dust poured from the breach in thick, choking waves.\rJhace stood three strides from the entrance, hands shaking so hard he had to grip his belt to keep them still.\rHe'd been here five minutes.\rHis boot skated on spilled grain. He caught himself on instinct, palm slapping the wall hard enough to sting.\rToo close. Too human.\r\rThe tremor from Tallow Street hadn't stopped. It had spread from his hands into his forearms, up to his shoulders. A fine, relentless vibration that made every tool on his belt rattle.\rThe heat in his chest was still there. Banked. Waiting.\rA foreman ran past him toward the breach, saw his Crown inspector marks, and grabbed his shoulder hard enough to bruise.\r\"You held Tallow,\" the foreman said. \"Do it again.\"\rJhace pulled free. \"I don't—\"\r\"There's forty people in there,\" the foreman cut in. \"Maybe more. Wedges won't hold. Straps won't reach. You're what we've got.\"\rThe copper taste flooded Jhace's mouth again.\rHis chest heat flared.\rThe doubled harmonic he'd felt in the grid-hum arrived inside him—two mis-timed pulses locking into the same rhythm. His heartbeat and the failing building's stress pattern aligned like they were the same system.\rHe didn't want this.\rHe didn't understand this.\rHe moved because the archway dust was already falling in steady sheets and somebody inside was screaming a name he couldn't ignore.\r\"Clear the approach,\" Jhace said, voice rough. \"Get spotters. I need someone counting.\"\rHe stepped under the sagging upper floor.\rThe heat detonated behind his sternum.\rHe put both hands on the nearest support column. Filament-light threaded from his palms into the stone—brighter this time, thicker, like the copper-gold dust in the air was feeding it.\rThe warehouse stopped settling.\rThe groan of stressed iron went quiet.\rJhace's breath shook. He could feel the load distribution through his fingertips: uneven, shifting, trying to collapse in three directions at once. His hands tightened. The filament brightened in response.\rThe tremor in his arms worsened.\rNot fine anymore. Visible. His whole body shaking with the effort of holding something that should have already fallen.\r\"Move!\" someone shouted. \"Get them out!\"\rBoots ran. Voices called coordinates. Bodies crawled through gaps in the rubble.\rA woman appeared beside him—practical coat, slate already open, hair tied back with a strip of cloth.\rShe looked at the filament-light threading from his hands. Looked at his face. Looked at the tremor shaking his shoulders.\r\"You're the Tallow Street hold,\" she said.\rNot a question. A confirmation.\r\"Yeah,\" Jhace managed.\r\"How long have you been holding?\"\r\"I don't know. Five minutes? Ten?\"\rHer pen scratched. \"Tremor amplitude increased since first manifestation. Cost progression visible.\"\r\"What?\" Jhace's vision narrowed at the edges. Copper-gold sparks flickered across it.\r\"Your body is paying for this,\" she said, still writing even as her thumb cramped. \"If it locks past tolerance, you won’t be able to let go.\"\r\"I can't let go,\" Jhace said through clenched teeth. \"Not until they're out.\"\rThe woman looked at the evacuation line. Counted bodies being carried past. \"Twenty-three so far. Estimate forty total inside.\"\r\"Then I hold for forty.\"\r\"Then you might not walk away.\"\rJhace's hands shook harder. The heat in his chest flared so bright it hurt. \"I already know that.\"\rThe woman's jaw tightened. She moved to where he could see her without turning his head and held up her slate like a mirror.\r\"I'm documenting this,\" she said. \"Duration. Cost markers. Termination threshold. If we don't measure it, the next person who tries this dies without knowing why.\"\r\"What's your name?\" Jhace asked.\r\"Tiffani Merrow. Glass-House observatory.\"\r\"Jhace Torrins.\"\r\"I know,\" Tiffani said. \"I wrote it down at Tallow Street.\"\rShe showed him her slate:\rJhace Torrins. Inspector. First manifestation. Duration: 240 heartbeats. Cost: tremor onset, progressive.\rSecond manifestation. Sector Nine warehouse. Onset: immediate. Tremor amplitude: increased. Copper-gold filament visible, enhanced density.\rJhace stared at the notes. At his own name written like data.\r\"What caused this?\" he asked.\rTiffani's pen paused. \"Something punched through the sky two hours ago. Spindle. Metallic. Fast enough to ignore friction. Left this—\"\rShe held up her fingertip. Copper-gold grit caught the lamplight.\r\"—everywhere.\"\rJhace looked at the dust swirling around his boots. At the filament threading from his hands. At the way the copper-gold particles moved toward him like they recognized him.\r\"The doubled harmonic,\" he said.\r\"You felt it too?\" Tiffani's eyes sharpened.\r\"In the grid-hum. Two Tides before this. Wrong frequency. Made my teeth itch.\"\rTiffani wrote: Doubled harmonic: confirmed across multiple observers. Grid infrastructure + atmospheric intrusion event = correlation.\rA rescue crew leader shouted. \"Thirty-eight out. Two still inside. Back corner. Pinned.\"\rJhace's vision blurred. The heat in his chest spiked.\r\"How long have I been holding?\" he rasped.\rTiffani checked a small timepiece on her belt. \"Eighteen minutes.\"\r\"Tallow Street was four.\"\r\"Your cost is accelerating,\" Tiffani said. She wiped a nosebleed with the back of her wrist like it was nothing. \"Tremor spread. Vision blur. This isn’t sustainable.\"\rJhace tasted blood before she finished the sentence. Copper-warm, running over his lip.\r\"Terminate after extraction,\" Tiffani said. \"Not before. But the instant they're clear, you let go.\"\r\"What if I can't?\"\r\"Then I make you.\"\rThe last two bodies came out on stretchers. Alive. Barely.\r\"Clear!\" the crew leader yelled.\rTiffani's hand closed on Jhace's shoulder—hard enough to hurt, grounding enough to feel real.\r\"Terminate,\" she said. \"Now.\"\rJhace let the filament go.\rThe warehouse settled the last inch it had been denied. Stone grinding. Iron squealing. A beam snapped somewhere in the back, but the void where people had been was empty.\rJhace staggered back.\rHis legs didn't hold. He hit the ground on his knees, hands still shaking, blood dripping from his nose onto the dust-covered stone.\rTiffani crouched beside him, slate open, pen moving.\rSecond manifestation terminated. Duration: 19 minutes, 40 seconds. Cost: tremor progression (shoulders, torso), vision impairment (peripheral narrowing, copper-gold visual artifacts), epistaxis (nosebleed), motor control compromise (collapse post-termination).\rSurvival: confirmed. Function: degraded.\rJhace wiped blood from his mouth with a shaking hand. \"You write everything like it's going to behave.\"\r\"Everything does behave,\" Tiffani said. \"Even this. It has markers. Progression. Termination criteria.\"\r\"It's killing me in increments.\"\r\"Yes,\" Tiffani said, and the word came out harsher than she meant. \"So we track it before it kills you. We don’t guess. We don’t pray.\"\rJhace looked at her slate. At the notes written in clean, precise handwriting. At his own degradation catalogued like data.\r\"Why are you doing this?\" he asked.\rTiffani met his eyes. Hers were sharp, clear, absolutely certain.\r\"Because the city is breaking faster than crews can fix it,\" she said. \"Because people like you are going to keep holding until they die. Because if we don't measure the cost, the next bell will kill someone without them knowing why.\"\rA bell rang in the distance.\rFourth collapse. Different sector.\rThe copper taste surged in Jhace's mouth.\rThe heat in his chest flared.\rTiffani wrote it down because not writing felt like lying: Third manifestation probable. Autonomic response. Cost accumulating.\rShe looked at Jhace. \"Can you stand?\"\r\"I don't know.\"\r\"Try.\"\rJhace pushed himself to his feet. His legs shook. His hands wouldn't stop trembling. But he stood.\r\"Good,\" Tiffani said. \"Because we're going to the next one. And I'm counting every heartbeat.\"\rShe walked toward the sound of the bell.\rJhace followed.\rTiffani wiped chalk off her thumb. It smeared into the sweat.\rThe bell kept ringing.\r---\rCHAPTER 4: PROTOCOL\rJhace’s workshop was a box of iron and heat tucked into the mid‑terrace, where you could hear the grid‑hum without being crushed by it.\rTiffani sat on an overturned crate with her slate on her knees, left leg extended slightly to ease the knee that had started locking after long stands on stone floors. She didn’t rest her back against anything. Always ready to move. Always ready to write.\rTiffani shifted her weight off her left leg without comment, like it was just another measurement she refused to log.\rJhace stood at his bench, trying to thread a bolt into a collar bracket.\rHis hands shook.\rThe grid‑hum didn't break with a bang. It stuttered—missing beats, catching up—like an old machine slipping teeth.\rHe tried again. The bolt dropped. Metal on stone.\rHe stared at it like it had betrayed him.\rTiffani didn’t look up. His left hand tried to wipe sweat off his mouth and only smeared dust across his lip. He tasted grit. He hated the fact he noticed it. “Your tremor amplitude increased during exertion.”\r“Because I’m exerting,” Jhace muttered, bending to pick up the bolt. His fingertips felt numb where the filament‑light had burned them. Not pain. Absence.\r“Because your body is remembering,” Tiffani corrected. “It thinks you’re still holding.”\rJhace set the bolt down and flexed his hand hard. The tremor didn’t care.\rHe hated that. He hated that his own muscles had become unreliable.\r“What did you call it out there?” he asked.\rTiffani flipped her slate so he could see. She’d drawn a crude timeline.\rActivation onset: chest heat + copper taste  \rLight manifestation: filament contact‑dependent  \rTremor: post‑termination, permanent marker\r“I need a taxonomy,” she said. “Stages. Because right now it’s just stories.”\rJhace wiped his palms on a work cloth. “And you think writing stages keeps people alive.”\r“I think it keeps them from lying to themselves.” She tapped the line where she’d written permanent. “You can’t call it temporary. You can’t bargain with it.”\rJhace’s jaw worked.\rHe had bargained with Junction Seven. He had told himself the report would matter. That somebody else would fix it. That the city didn’t need him to fight every foreman with a schedule board.\rHe looked at his shaking hands. “Stage One,” he said, voice low. “Tremor.”\rTiffani nodded and wrote: Stage One: tremor + filament residue. Functional. Marked.\rShe stared at the word Functional, then drew a hard line through it and rewrote: Operational.\r“Functional makes it sound polite,” she muttered.\r\rA knock hit the workshop door.\rBram Tallow leaned in like he was checking whether the room was still allowed to exist. Dust on his boots, sweat at his collar, grin already loaded.\r“Heard you two grabbed Tallow Street by the throat,” Bram said, trying for funny and getting dust instead. He coughed once. “City tries to crush you, you crush back. Very… romantic. Very dumb.”\rJhace didn’t look up. “Who’s asking?”\r“Crown’s asking questions,” Bram said, and somehow still made it sound like a dirty joke. “Not because they care. Because they hate unknown tools walking around without a leash and a receipt.”\rTiffani’s pen paused. “What questions.”\r“Names. Locations. ‘Verified capability.’ That sort of foreplay,” Bram said. He stepped fully inside, then stopped like the heat-grid might charge him rent. “Infrastructure office. Some administrator with hands so clean it makes you wonder what he’s washing off. Loves forms. Loves categories. Loves pretending paper is stronger than stone.”\rJhace’s jaw tightened. “To do what.”\rBram spread his hands. “To decide who gets to own you. Temple will call it divine. Guild will call it labor. Crown will call it law. Everybody wants a piece. Congratulations—you’re popular.”\r“We don’t belong to any of them,” Tiffani said.\rBram laughed, quick and wrong. “Adorable. Tell that to a stamp. Try explaining ‘we don’t belong’ while a ceiling is coming down and someone’s screaming for a hold. They’ll file you under ‘non-compliant’ and act like that’s a weather condition.”\rJhace’s tremor ticked in his fingers. “So you’re here to warn us.”\r“I’m here to tell you the door is already being measured,” Bram said. “I can buy you a Tide or two. Maybe. After that, they’ll show up with paperwork and smiles and start deciding which parts of you are ‘public infrastructure.’”\rTiffani’s mouth went flat. “Then we build methodology fast.”\rBram pointed at her slate like it offended him personally. “Yeah. Before they build it for you. Because if the Crown writes your protocol, it’s going to read like a job description. And you won’t like the benefits package.”\rJhace breathed in, tasted copper‑gold dust that still clung to his clothes, and felt the city’s hum through the floorboards.\r“Then we build it fast,” he said. “Before they decide to build it for us.”\r---\rCHAPTER 5: FIRST NETWORK\rThe kiln‑quarter feeder gantry sat in Sector Fourteen, where the pipes ran hot and the schedules ran hotter, and everything pretended it was fine right up until it wasn’t.\rThe bell signal came late. That was the first problem.\rWhen Jhace and Tiffani arrived, the gantry wasn’t collapsed—yet. The support columns were bowing, groaning under a load they shouldn’t have been carrying because somebody had over‑pressured the feed line to meet a schedule.\rA foreman screamed, “Hold it! Hold it now!”\rJhace moved toward the nearest column out of habit.\rTiffani caught his sleeve. “Not yet.”\rJhace’s jaw tightened. “People are inside.”\r“People are leaving,” she corrected, pointing.\rShe was right. Workers were already pouring out of the side doors, coughing, eyes wide. The structure wasn’t in free fall. It was in warning.\r“We wedge first,” Tiffani said. “Then you hold if wedges fail.”\rJhace’s chest heat sat behind his ribs like an itch you can't reach. His tremor wanted to fight. His stomach wanted to bargain. Neither cared what he preferred.\rHe didn’t scratch it.\rHis first wedge slip went in crooked. He had to yank it back out, knuckles scraping stone, and the crowd saw the mistake.\rHe hated that more than the pain.\r\rHe dropped to one knee and shoved wedge slips under the first bowing brace. Bram’s crew followed, hands fast, straps ready. For once, labor did what labor could do.\rThe gantry groaned again. Dust spilled from a seam.\rA young man stepped into the brace line, hands hovering like he didn’t know what to do with them. Corven. Jhace had seen him in the tunnels, quiet, competent.\rCorven swallowed. “I… I can feel it,” he said. “The hum. It’s—”\r“Louder,” Jhace finished.\rCorven nodded hard. “Like it’s in my teeth.”\rTiffani stepped close, slate already up. “If it hits your chest, tell me. If you taste copper, tell me. Do not improvise.”\rCorven’s eyes flicked to the bending column. “If it goes—”\r“Then we act,” Jhace said. “But not before.”\rThe wedges held for two breaths.\rThen the central column shifted.\rNot collapse. A decisive, ugly move—stone settling, iron flexing. The wedges screamed as they compressed.\rThe workers inside were still running.\r“Now,” Tiffani said.\rJhace put both hands on the column.\rHeat flared in his chest—familiar now, terrifying in its familiarity—and filament‑light threaded into the cinder‑dust coated seam.\rThe column stopped shifting.\rCorven stared at Jhace’s hands like they were a language he’d almost learned.\r“Your turn,” Jhace said through gritted teeth.\rCorven stepped forward and touched the secondary brace.\rHis breath hitched. Copper taste. Chest heat. Then light—thinner than Jhace’s, but real—threaded into the brace line.\rCorven’s hands started to shake immediately.\r“Stage One onset during hold,” Tiffani murmured, writing. “Early marker.”\r“Keep moving! If you stop, you become debris!” Bram shouted to the evac crew. “Count them out! Loud. Fast. Like the Crown’s listening!”\rJhace’s tremor surged in sympathy, like the grid‑hum had tied their nervous systems together. His arms burned. His vision tunneled at the edges.\r“How long?” Tiffani asked.\r“Eighty,” Jhace rasped, counting heartbeats without thinking.\rCorven’s jaw clenched. “I can do—”\r“Do exactly what the structure needs,” Jhace snapped. “Nothing more.”\rAt one‑fifty, the last workers stumbled out. Two hundred‑plus bodies, alive because they’d been allowed time.\r“Terminate,” Tiffani said.\rJhace released first. The column held under wedges now that the load had shifted down.\rCorven released a heartbeat later and nearly fell.\rJhace caught him by the elbow, and the tremor in his own hands made the grip feel unreliable.\rCorven looked at his shaking fingers with a kind of horror. “It’s not stopping.”\r“It doesn’t,” Jhace said.\rCorven stared at his shaking fingers like they’d betrayed him mid-shift.\rHe’d watched Jhace hold. Had seen the tremor. Had understood it in theory.\rTheory didn’t matter when your hand wouldn’t listen.\r“How long?” he asked.\rJhace’s jaw tightened. “Until you stop manifesting.”\rCorven’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. “So never.”\r“Yeah.”\rCorven set his palm on the edge of the bench. The tremor rattled bone against metal.\rHe pulled back like it burned. Flexed. Tried again. Harder.\rIt kept moving—slow, stubborn—like a crack running even after you stop pulling.\rTiffani wrote the number anyway: 219 evacuated. Zero casualties. Two operators marked.\rBram approached, face hard. “Crown’s going to hear about this—and they’ll bring forms instead of shovels.”\rTiffani didn’t look up. “Good.”\rJhace’s chest still burned with the after‑heat of holding. The hunger whispered: You could have held longer. You could have made it perfect.\rHe swallowed the copper taste and tried to bully the hunger quiet. It answered with a warm twist behind his sternum.\rFor now.\r---\r\r---\rROUTE FAILURE (THREE DEAD)\rThe count was an argument before it was a number.\r\rThe bell came from Sector Nine, not as a roar—just a single strike repeated until someone answered.\rResidential stairwell. Upper-terrace split. Twelve families still inside, one route.\rJhace was already marked from Sector Nine. Corven was worse. Senne was fresh enough to stand without shaking.\rTiffani looked at the slate wall and made the decision she would later pretend was only math.\rTwo operators at one‑eighty each meant coordination, handoffs, spotters, and failure points. One operator at two‑forty meant simplicity. A clean line.\r“Single deployment,” she said. “Senne. Duration cap: two‑forty. Terminate on my bell.”\rSenne went pale as the heat took her chest. Copper-gold light laced her forearms. Not pretty. Functional.\rThey reached the stairwell mouth as stone dust sifted from the ceiling like flour. The grid-hum underfoot carried the doubled harmonic, deep and insistent, a warning that didn’t care who listened.\rSenne braced under the central landing. Jhace watched her shoulders set—metalworker’s posture in a glassworker’s frame.\rTiffani counted. Heartbeats, not seconds. Seconds lied when panic touched them.\rOne hundred.\rOne forty.\rOne eighty.\rAt one‑ninety, a secondary beam—one Tiffani hadn’t walked because she’d trusted the map—shifted with a sound like teeth grinding.\rThe evacuation lane kinked. The next family stumbled. The stone didn’t collapse all at once. It folded, patient and specific, closing the corridor into a narrow pocket.\rThree people were on the wrong side when it did: two adults and a child coughing copper into his sleeve.\rTiffani saw them, and for a moment the slate in her hands felt obscene.\r“Hold,” she said, voice flat. “Keep holding.”\rSenne’s eyes didn’t move. She couldn’t spare the motion.\rTwo‑ten.\rTwo‑twenty.\rTwo‑thirty.\rThe pocket didn’t clear. The child’s coughing turned wet.\rAt two‑forty, Tiffani rang the termination sequence anyway. Because protocols existed for a reason. Because if she didn’t, she would never stop.\rSenne released.\rThe stairwell collapsed into itself with a slow, final sound—stone settling into the shape it had wanted all along.\rThey dug for hours.\rThey found three bodies. The child first, because he was small enough to slip into the wrong angles.\rBack in the workshop, Tiffani logged it with perfect precision.\rDeployment: Senne — 240 heartbeats  \rEvacuated: 47  \rCasualties: 3  \rCause: Inadequate route assessment. Secondary beam shear.\rShe wrote “cause” as if precision could make it reversible.\rHer hand moved to write the next revision. The chalk touched slate.\rNothing came out.\rShe stared at the number.\rThree.\rNot a count. Three names she would never know. Three families who would ask why their—\rThe chalk snapped in her grip.\rShe set the broken pieces down carefully. Picked up a new stick. Wrote:\rRoute spotters required. Three per deployment. No exceptions.\rUnderlined it twice.\rThen she closed the slate and sat in the dark workshop, counting her own breaths because the numbers on the page wouldn't behave. Her fingers kept shaking anyway.\r\r---\rCHAPTER 6: WITNESS\rBrother Corran didn’t wear Temple colors.\rThe first thing Jhace noticed was the badge—bright, clean, pinned where it couldn't be missed. The second was how the man didn't look tired. Not even a little.\rCorran’s eyes went straight to Jhace’s hands.\r“For the record, I’m not here to stop you,” Corran said, before anyone asked.\rBram hovered by the door like a lock that wanted to be useful.\rTiffani set her slate down deliberately. “Then why are you here?”\r“Because you’re real,” Corran said. “And because real things attract predators.”\rJhace’s mouth tightened. “Crown.”\rCorran’s gaze didn’t flinch. “Yes. Also Guild. Also Temple. Anyone who thinks they can turn you into a lever.”\rBram’s voice came dry. “Ah yes. The holy trinity of getting fucked: prayer, production, paperwork.”\rTiffani shot him a look. “Bram.”\rHe raised his hands. “What? I’m helping.”\rBram gestured toward the door. “Temple says you’re divine. Guild says you’re labor. Crown says you’re property. Everybody’s trying to fuck the same lever.”\rHe pulled a folded packet from his coat and set it on Jhace’s bench. Paper. Old style. Archive weight.\r“Stage Three cases,” Corran said. “Documented. Not rumors.”\rTiffani’s hands went still. “How many?”\r“Enough.” Corran’s voice was flat, and that flatness carried exhaustion. “Most die. A few don’t. The ones who don’t… wish they had.”\rJhace’s tremor ticked up at the word die. He forced his hands flat on the bench. “Why give this to us?”\rCorran hesitated. That hesitation was the crack in the Crown.\r“Because someone above me wants a registry,” he said. “Names. Locations. Deployment authority. They’re calling it order. It’s control.”\rTiffani’s eyes narrowed. “Who?”\rCorran looked toward the door as if the walls might report him. “Administrator Corlexi. Infrastructure Compliance.”\rBram gave a low whistle. “Clean hands. Clean coat. Probably wipes his ass with forms.”\rJhace stared at the packet. “So you’re here to warn us.”\r“I’m here to give you data,” Corran corrected. “If you want to fight control, you need numbers. Not speeches.”\rTiffani opened the packet and read, eyes moving fast.\rJhace watched her face tighten at certain lines—durations, costs, outcomes. She didn’t gasp. She didn’t dramatize. She just absorbed.\r“This case,” she said, tapping a page. “Stage Three survival. How?”\rCorran’s mouth went thin. “He didn’t terminate. He couldn’t. The hold locked. Autonomic failure started and the manifestation kept running anyway. The structure held until it didn’t matter.”\rJhace felt cold crawl up his spine despite the workshop heat. “Locked hold.”\rCorran nodded. “It’s not a tool you put down when you’re done. Sometimes it’s a hook in your chest that drags you until something breaks.”\rTiffani’s pen scratched. Stage Three: autonomic failure. Locked hold possible. Termination may fail.\rBram shifted. “You said predators.”\rCorran’s eyes met Bram’s. “Tallec is filing complaints. Says you’re obstructing industry. Says your protocols slow production.”\rJhace’s jaw clenched so hard it hurt.\rCorran leaned closer, voice low. “Here’s the fracture you wanted. I don’t like Corlexi’s registry. I don’t like Temple’s sermons. I don’t like Guild’s schedules. But I like fewer dead.”\rHe straightened. “If you build methodology, build it fast. Because the Crown is already drafting theirs. And theirs won’t care what it costs you.”\rHe turned to leave.\rTiffani called after him. “Why risk telling us this?”\rCorran paused at the door. “Because Junction Seven wasn’t the first ignored report,” he said. “And I’m tired of pretending ignorance is the same as innocence.”\rThen he was gone.\rJhace’s hands stayed flat on the workbench. “If this registry happens…” He swallowed. “I’m not doing it for paper.”\rBram exhaled slowly. “And because guilt is cheaper than funerals. Not by much. But cheaper.”\rTiffani climbed the workshop stairs slower than she had a Tide ago. Her right knee caught on the third step—not pain, just resistance, like the joint was learning to refuse.\rShe stopped on the landing. Put her palm to the wall until the world steadied.\rThe knee had locked twice this Anneal. Not during a hold. During stairs. During the parts where bodies were supposed to cooperate.\rShe’d logged operator tremor. Operator vein-lines. Operator autonomic failure.\rShe had not logged her own joint learning to refuse weight.\rBecause if she logged it, she’d have to adjust. Delegate. Sit down.\rAnd if she sat down, someone else would coordinate from the deck. Someone else would make the calls.\rShe took the next step anyway.\rShe didn’t log it.\rSome costs weren’t manifestation. Some were just labor.\rJhace’s hands shook on the bench like they were trying to answer him.\r---\rCHAPTER 7: MERCHANT HALL\rMerchant Hall was a cathedral for trade: iron ribs, stone spine, glass skylights that made everything inside look clean even when it wasn’t.\rAnd it smelled like bodies and money and fear—the kind of fear that tries to wear perfume.\rIt started failing at mid‑Tide.\rThe bell sequence came sharp and frantic—no warning cadence, no time to wedge first. The Hall had been packed when the terrace shifted again.\rFour hundred inside, Bram said. Maybe more.\rTiffani arrived before Jhace. When he pushed through the crowd, she was already on the steps, slate in hand, shouting like a foreman who’d learned math.\r“Operators on me. Two per span. Spotters assigned. No hero holds.”\r“Duration limit. One-eighty heartbeats maximum. You terminate on my signal. No argument.”\rMerra shoved through the dust with a slate clutched to her chest. Ash streaked her hair like she’d tried to comb through a fire.\r“We have a problem. Southern stairwell collapsed. Fifty people trapped on upper floors. Can’t reach main exits.”\r“Time estimate,” Tiffani said. “Low and high.”\r“Unknown. Debris blocking secondary stairs. Could be two hundred heartbeats. Could be six hundred.”\r“Then we don’t gamble.” Tiffani didn’t raise her voice. She didn’t have to. “Clear lanes. Count bodies out. You want prayer, pray while you walk.”\rShe swung her slate toward the operators.\r“Corven—baseline check. When chest heat starts, report it. Don’t interpret. Report.”\rCorven swallowed. “If it hits, I tell you. If I taste copper, I tell you.”\rJhace didn’t look at him. “Don’t spiral. Do the minimum.”\r“Minimum,” Corven echoed, like repetition could make it true.\r“Senne—status.”\r“Getting hollow,” Senne said. “Chest feels empty. Light’s harder to maintain.”\r“That’s Stage Two warning.” Tiffani’s eyes cut sharp. “You terminate on my signal or I terminate you myself.”\rSenne gave her a tired half-smile. “Sure. Die on schedule. Very professional.”\r“Kess—report.”\r“I am present and available for assignment.”\r“Available doesn’t mean useful. Report symptoms. Stop performing.”\r“Symptoms are within acceptable parameters for operational requirements.”\rBram leaned in, voice too bright for the amount of people about to die. “Translation: Kess is bleeding and pretending it’s fashion.”\r“I am not bleeding.”\rMerra didn’t bother being gentle. “Kess—your nose is bleeding.”\r“I am aware of the nasal discharge. It is minor. Barely noteworthy. I’ve had worse nosebleeds from seasonal changes.”\r“Yeah,” Bram said. “And I’ve had worse ideas than this hold. Doesn’t make them good.”\rFrom the crowd, a Temple Observer screamed, “Let the worthy hold! Let the unworthy fall!”\rBram snapped his head toward the sound. “Hey—shut up. If we’re picking who falls, you’re first. That’s democracy.”\r“Ignore them,” Tiffani said. “Move.”\r“Spotters, call markers.”\r“Jhace tremor increasing!”\r“Corven tremor increasing!”\r“Senne vein-line onset!”\rBram barked at the evac line like a foreman with a broken sense of humor. “Keep moving! Nobody stops! You stop, you become part of the debris! Count them out!”\rMerra’s voice cut through the dust. “Alternate route open. Final evacuation commencing.”\r“Count them,” Tiffani said. “Call numbers.”\rJhace’s breath came tight. “One hundred.”\r“State exit count,” Tiffani said, “not your suffering.”\r“One hundred. Out.”\rDaved’s voice came rough through the grit. “They’re not out.”\rJhace’s head snapped. “No.”\r“They’re not out.”\r“Daved—state the variable,” Tiffani said. “How many still inside?”\r“Enough.”\rTiffani didn’t blink. “Extend. Thirty heartbeats. No more.”\r“Tiff—” Jhace started.\r“I am not debating physics with the dead in the hallway,” she said. “Extend.”\rThirty heartbeats passed like a slow knife.\rMerra shouted from the corridor mouth, “Final group emerging. Ten heartbeats!”\r“Ten heartbeats is still ten,” Tiffani said. “Move.”\rDaved’s hands tightened. “Hold.”\r“Daved!” Jhace lunged a step closer.\r“Hold.”\rMerra’s slate slapped stone. “Clear! All civilians safe!”\r“Terminate all operators,” Tiffani snapped. “Immediate coordinated shutdown.”\rLights guttered and went out—one by one—except Daved’s.\r“Can’t,” Daved said.\r“Daved. Breathe,” Jhace said, too close, too desperate.\r“Can’t.”\r“Terminate,” Tiffani said. “That is not a negotiation.”\r“They’re not out.”\r“They are out. Merra confirmed. Terminate now.”\rDaved’s jaw worked like he was chewing copper. “Already crossed.”\rBram shoved his way in with words because silence was making everyone honest. “Daved—buddy—love the commitment, hate the method acting.”\r“Bram, shut up,” Jhace hissed.\r“I can’t,” Bram said. “It’s a disorder.”\rTiffani stepped in until Daved had to see her. “Look at me. If you extend past this, you cross Stage Three threshold.”\rDaved’s eyes stayed flat. “Already crossed.”\rJhace’s throat tightened. “He locked.”\r“I see it,” Tiffani said.\rThey hauled Daved back from the structure like pulling a hook out of meat.\r“Terminate!” Tiffani shouted.\rThe light finally cut.\rDaved folded like a tool dropped from numb hands.\rTiffani’s voice went small for one syllable. “I know.”\r\rThey carried Daved out on a door panel when the Hall finally stopped shifting enough to allow hands near him.\rHis eyes were open.\rHis body was too still.\rTiffani walked beside the makeshift stretcher, writing with one hand, wiping dust from her face with the other.\rTiffani sat alone in the documentation room after they carried Daved out.\rHer slate lay open to the cost taxonomy. Stage Three. Locked hold. Autonomic failure.\rShe had written it perfectly. Every marker. Every heartbeat. Every outcome.\rHer hand moved to add the summary line.\rShe stared at the slate. At the line that read: Casualties: 3.\rShe tried to write the justification. The calculation. The reason extending duration had been correct under the circumstances.\rThe chalk snapped.\rHer hands shook because she'd been awake too long and her calf cramped—no magic required. The chalk snapped anyway.\rShe stared at her shaking fingers.\rShe didn’t log her tremor. She didn’t log that she’d miscounted heartbeats during the hold because her vision had blurred. She didn’t log that she’d wanted to ring the termination bell at one-eighty but hadn’t, because Daved’s eyes had asked her not to.\rSome data points weren’t for the ledger.\rSome were just for carrying.\r\r---\r\rJhace’s tremor made his jaw ache.\rHe'd wanted method like a wrench—turn it, get the same result. Instead he got a mouthful of pennies and hands that wouldn't stop.\rMethod had just asked for a price it didn’t have the right to demand.\r---\rCHAPTER 8: THREE TIDES\rDaved woke on the third Tide.\rThree Tides. Long enough for sweat to dry into salt on his collar. Long enough for hope to feel stupid.\rJhace stood by the cot in the infirmary room, hands shaking in front of him like they were arguing with air. Tiffani stood on the other side, slate ready, eyes red at the edges from lack of sleep she refused to name.\rDaved’s eyes opened slowly.\rHis hands were strapped to the cot rails—not as restraint, but as prevention. His fingers still twitched with phantom hold.\rHe tried to speak. His throat made a sound like stone rubbing stone.\rTiffani leaned in. “Don’t force voice. Use slate.”\rDaved wrote: Did it work\r“Yes. Thirty-two evacuated. One operator Stage Three event. Locked hold. Autonomic failure onset.”\rHe wrote: Cost\r“Severe tremor. Motor delay. Speech impairment. Autonomic instability. Probability of full recovery: low.”\rHe wrote: Worth it\r“That’s not a question I can answer. Thirty-two lives. One body broken. You determine if that exchange was acceptable.”\rJhace’s throat tightened. “They got thirty-two out.”\rHe couldn’t decide what to do with his hands. Shaking, useless things. He folded them behind his back like that would fool anybody.\r\rDaved wrote: One body is not a small thing\r“Correct,” Tiffani said. “It is not small. It is also not reversible.”\rBram wandered in like a bad idea that knew your schedule. “This room smells like piss and heroism. Worst cologne.”\r“Leave,” Tiffani said, without looking up, “if you can’t behave.”\r“I’m behaving,” Bram said. “I’m just… shaped wrong.”\rDaved wrote: You okay\r“Define operational parameters for ‘okay.’”\rNot irrelevant\r“Compared to your autonomic failure, my current symptoms are statistically minor.”\rStill matters\rTiffani’s pen stopped.\r“I can’t get the copper taste out of my mouth anymore,” she said quietly. “Food tastes wrong. Water tastes wrong. I think it’s in my teeth.”\rJhace started, “Tiff—”\r“That’s not a Stage marker,” she cut in. “So it doesn’t get logged.”\rDaved wrote smaller this time: Don’t let them call this holy\r“I won’t.”\r“Nobody’s calling this holy,” Bram said. “They’re calling it ‘resource allocation.’ Much sexier.”\r“Bram.”\r“I know,” he said. “I’m disgusting. It’s my brand.”\r\rJhace didn’t know if that was a promise to Daved or a threat to the world.\r---\rCHAPTER 9: THE SHARD-BEARER (RUMOR)\rThey called him Shard‑Bearer because nobody had a better word for what they thought they’d seen.\rA man in the eastern districts with a bright line in his chest. A glow that didn’t fade after termination because there was no termination. A hold that didn’t shake. A body that didn’t pay.\rRumors traveled faster than Crown notices and cleaner than Temple sermons. They came with dates and witness counts like proof.\rTiffani pinned three reports to the workshop wall with nails.\rReport one: Torne Reach — Tide 0+3. One operator held a bridge span alone for “half an Anneal.” No tremor. No vein‑lines.\rReport two: Brinefall — Tide 0+5. A whole terrace stairwell stabilized by “one man’s hands.” Witnesses describe “glass light under the skin.”\rTiffani tapped the pinned report with the end of her pen. “Report three: delayed secondary collapse. Building held by Shard-Bearer for six hundred heartbeats. Post-release: immediate structural failure. No tremor visible in operator. No warning signs. Casualties: seventeen.”\rJhace stared at the number like it had teeth. “He didn’t save them. He killed them slower.”\r“Correct,” Tiffani said. “The cost didn’t vanish. It moved—into the structure, into the people around it. Somewhere we can’t ignore.”\r“Heroics.” The word came out like a curse.\r“That word implies exemption,” Tiffani said. “There is no exemption. Only deferral.”\rBram leaned over the table, too casual for the subject. “So the Shard-Bearer is just… consequences with better marketing.”\r“Don’t,” Jhace said.\r“Sorry,” Bram said. “I forgot we’re being solemn about the murder wizard.”\rTiffani didn’t look up. “The registry will want this rumor. It provides false relief. False hope that cost can be avoided.”\r“And people die for that,” Jhace said.\r“Then we make sure the registry doesn’t become a leash,” Tiffani said.\r\r\rJhace’s tremor pulsed like a warning.\rHe didn’t know the Shard‑Bearer.\rHe could already feel the change—in the way strangers leaned back from doorways, in the way foremen stopped pretending they weren’t watching.\r---\rJhace saw him in Sector Twelve.\rNot by choice. They were moving toward another assessment call when the crowd parted the wrong way—like people avoided a spill in the street.\rA grain store had folded inward. One beam still held the second floor up at an angle that should have been impossible.\rA man stood under it with both hands on the timber.\rAlone.\rNo spotters. No wedges. No shouting counts. Just a man holding timber like it weighed nothing and everyone else forgetting how to breathe.\rJust light—copper‑gold, dense, not filament‑thin. It didn’t thread into the structure like Jhace’s holds did. It sat inside the beam like a replacement spine, telling wood and stone what shape to keep.\rTiffani stopped beside Jhace. Her pen was already moving. Shard‑Bearer. Visual confirmation.\rThe man’s chest glowed through his shirt. Not a flicker. A steady line, as if someone had pressed a shard under skin and the body had agreed.\r“No tremor,” Tiffani murmured.\r“Not paying,” Jhace said. The words tasted wrong the moment he spoke them.\r“Incorrect,” she said. “Look at his eyes.”\rJhace looked.\rThe man’s eyes were copper‑gold and flat, putting off their own light like heated coins. Jhace couldn’t find a tremor in it. That scared him more than any shake.\rThe beam settled another fraction. The store stopped complaining.\rThe man released the timber and walked away without checking who escaped. No count. No confirmation. No termination signal. No glance back.\rTiffani underlined her note twice. Cost unidentified. Method unreplicable. Do not engage.\rJhace’s tremor ticked up.\rNot from fear.\rFrom wanting to know what it would feel like to hold without shaking.\rCHAPTER 10: LICENSING\rThey arrived with paper first.\rThe first page had a coffee ring on it. The seal ink was smudged where somebody’s thumb had dragged across it like the Crown was just another sweaty foreman.\r\rA Crown clerk with clean boots unrolled forms on Jhace’s bench like a priest laying out offerings.\rBehind the clerk stood two guards, a Temple observer, and Tallec—smiling like he’d finally found a way to win without touching a pipe.\rAnd at the center, Bram Tallow in a different coat than usual: administrator’s cut, badge pinned. Still Bram, but now carrying Crown weight.\r“Don’t start,” Jhace said, seeing the coat.\rBram’s eyes flicked down to Jhace’s shaking hands and then away. “I didn’t ask for this.”\rJhace looked at the coat. The badge. The way Bram wore it like it didn’t fit his bones. “You took it anyway.”\rBram’s grin tried to show up and died halfway. “Yeah. Because if I don’t, they send someone who actually likes forms. Someone who’ll file you under ‘infrastructure asset’ and sleep like a baby.”\rHe touched the badge once. Like it burned.\r“I’m terrified,” he said, quiet enough to almost count as a confession. “Terrified they turn you into numbers. Terrified I help them do it. Terrified I get good at it.”\rThe grin came back, wrong‑shaped. “So I’m coping by making dick jokes. Extremely healthy. Very professional.”\rTiffani stepped forward. “What is it?”\rBram nodded at the clerk. “Manifestor Registry. Compliance documentation. Deployment authority.”\r“By order of Infrastructure Compliance Office. Administrator Corlexi presiding.”\r“Oh good,” Bram said. “A sentence that already feels like a noose.”\rThe clerk didn’t look up. “Name. District. Verified activation capability. Cost markers. Compliance agreement. You will not manifest outside authorized events.”\r“Define ‘deployment authority,’” Tiffani said.\r“Infrastructure Compliance Office.”\r“That is not a definition,” Tiffani said. “That is a label.”\r“It is the issuing body.”\r“Then who determines what qualifies as an ‘authorized event’?”\r“Infrastructure Compliance Office.”\rJhace’s jaw worked. “What happens if we refuse?”\r“Then you are unlicensed. Unlicensed manifestation will be prosecuted as endangerment.”\rTallec leaned in with a smile that didn’t reach his eyes. “Finally. Structure. No more renegade holds.”\rBram stared at him like he’d stepped in something. “Tallec, you look like a man who’d schedule his own mother’s funeral for throughput.”\r“At least someone respects schedules,” Tallec said.\r“Don’t start,” Jhace warned.\r“I didn’t start,” Bram said. “He started. I’m just adding sex noises.”\rCorran stepped in like he didn’t believe doors applied to him. “Schedules don’t respect physics.”\rThe clerk’s eyes flicked up, irritated. “Brother Corran. You are not assigned here.”\r“For the record,” Corran said, “I’m observing.”\rHe looked at Jhace. “And if the Crown orders you to hold a vault instead of a residential stairwell?”\rTallec’s voice went oily. “Assets protect assets.”\rJhace’s hands went flat on the bench, because he didn’t trust them to do something irreversible. “Next time I hold, it’ll be because something is falling. Not because you want it to stay standing.”\r“Romantic,” Bram said. “I only break my body for strangers, not for paperwork.”\rTiffani’s gaze stayed on the form. “If we don’t sign, we lose access to incidents. They will send licensed manifestors—or force untrained ones—to hold.”\rShe set her pen down with a little click, the way you set down a blade when you're done being polite. “We sign,” she said. “But our standard rides with it.”\rThe clerk’s mouth opened. “That is not—”\r“Duration limits,” Tiffani said. “Spotters. Termination criteria. Cost tracking. You will not turn people into disposable beams.”\rBram leaned toward the clerk. “She means ‘stop treating people like disposable beams,’ but she’s saying it in math so you can understand.”\r“The registry does not negotiate terms with operators.”\r“Then the registry gets incomplete operators,” Tiffani said. “Your choice.”\rTallec’s smile widened. “You become assets. Next time you hold, you’ll do it where the Crown tells you to hold. Not where you feel like playing hero.”\r“‘Playing hero,’” Bram echoed. “That’s rich coming from a man who thinks schedules are moral philosophy.”\r“Production keeps people alive,” Tallec said.\r“So does not crushing them,” Jhace said.\r“This conversation is over,” Tallec said, like he had the authority to end reality.\r“Was it a conversation?” Bram asked. “Felt more like a threat with paperwork attached.”\r\rJhace signed the registry with a hand that wouldn’t hold still.\rWhen it was done, the clerk rolled the papers and left as if the room wasn’t full of broken bodies and hard choices.\rTallec lingered in the doorway. “Next time you hold,” he said to Jhace, “you’ll do it where the Crown tells you.”\rJhace met his eyes. “Next time I hold, it’ll be because something is falling. Not because you want it to stay standing.”\rTallec laughed and walked away.\rThe grid‑hum under the floor kept its doubled harmonic.\rThe doubled harmonic kept humming under the floor like a threat that didn’t need words.\r---\rCHAPTER 11: HUNGER\rThe hunger wasn’t metaphor.\rIt lived behind Jhace’s sternum as a warmth that never fully left after the first hold. A phantom heat that flared whenever the grid‑hum dipped, whenever dust fell, whenever someone screamed the word hold like it was prayer.\rTiffani called it activation pressure.\rJhace called it wanting.\rHe sat beside Daved’s cot in the infirmary again, watching the older man’s hands twitch in sleep.\rDaved’s eyes opened. He didn’t need a slate this time. His voice was still rough, but it existed.\r“Feels… good,” Daved rasped.\rJhace frowned. “What does?”\r“The start,” Daved said. “Chest heat. When it locks in. For a heartbeat you feel… useful. Strong. Like the city can’t touch you.”\rJhace’s tremor worsened, like his body agreed.\r“And then,” Daved continued, eyes flat, “it takes you apart.”\rTiffani wrote without looking up. “Addictive response mixed with autonomic threat. Noted.”\rJhace frowned. “What does.”\r“The reinforcement loop,” Tiffani said. “Chest heat, perceived control, reduced panic during manifestation. It becomes desire.”\rDaved’s eyes narrowed. “Liar.”\rJhace’s head snapped. “What.”\r“You want it,” Daved said. “The hold. You want to feel it again.”\r“I don’t want to chase the rumor.”\r“You want it anyway.”\rJhace didn’t answer. His tremor did.\r“That want will kill you faster than the hold,” Daved said.\r“He is correct,” Tiffani said. “The want is not evidence. It is impulse.”\rJhace’s voice went raw. “I want to hold without paying. I want to save every time the bell rings and not come apart.”\r“Hungry hands hold until they break,” Daved said.\r“Then we do not feed it,” Tiffani said. “We constrain it with protocol.”\r“Protocol doesn’t stop bells,” Jhace said.\r“No,” Tiffani said. “It stops stupidity.”\rDaved’s mouth twitched. “Sometimes. Not always.”\r“Then we decide what we sacrifice,” Tiffani said. “The Crown can wait. The Temple can wait. The Guild can wait.”\rJhace’s gaze stayed forward. “We decide.”\r“And when you decide wrong?” Daved asked.\r“We document it,” Tiffani said. “We learn. We don’t repeat.”\r“Some mistakes don’t give you time to learn,” Daved said.\r\rOutside, a bell rang—different cadence. Not collapse. A drill signal. Crown‑mandated training.\rJhace’s chest heat flared anyway, eager and stupid.\rHe hated himself for that flare.\rHe stood, hands shaking, and went to the training yard because refusal would make them criminals.\rTiffani followed, slate in hand, limping slightly from too many hours on stone floors.\rDaved watched them go and muttered, almost too quiet to hear:\r“Hungry hands hold until they break.”\rJhace pretended he didn’t hear.\rHe did.\r---\rCHAPTER 12: METHOD\rThe alert came at Tide 21 like an insult.\rIt was a narrow failure—no mass collapse, no Hall, no warehouse full of people.\rA residential stairwell in Sector Nine. A narrow stone throat that had been patched too many times since the Piercing. A single child trapped beneath a fallen landing slab, crying until her voice turned hoarse.\r“Clear lane,” Tiffani ordered as they arrived. Her limp was worse now, but she moved like it didn’t matter. “Spotters assigned. Wedges first.”\rShe pointed left, then froze. “No—right. Right corridor first. Sorry.”\rThe apology tasted like rust. She didn’t repeat it.\r\rJhace’s hands shook so hard he had to jam them into his coat pockets to stop them from betraying him in front of the crowd.\rHe could feel the doubled harmonic through the stairwell stone. Deep. Constant. The grid had never recovered. It had just learned to fail slower.\rBram arrived with Crown guards and a face that said he’d tried, briefly, to be serious and failed on contact.\r“Registry protocol,” he said. “Two licensed operators inbound.”\rTiffani didn’t look at him. “They’ll be too late.”\r“Cool,” Bram said. “Love the part where the Crown schedules rescue like it’s a delivery window.”\r“Clear lane,” Tiffani ordered. “Spotters assigned. Wedges first.”\rShe looked at the sagging landing slab, the trapped space beneath it, the child’s choking turning into wet silence. Her voice tightened into a blade.\r“One operator. Two minutes max. We brace, we lift, we extract. Terminate immediately after.”\r“Then who?” Jhace said.\r“Not you.”\r“Then who.”\r“The child is choking, Jhace.”\rJhace’s tremor rattled against his bones like a tool trying to escape. “I decide.”\rBram’s breath punched out. “Jesus—no, don’t—okay, you’re doing it. You’re doing the thing. Great. Fantastic. I hate this.”\rJhace stepped in. Copper flooded his mouth like blood.\rFor a heartbeat, nothing happened.\rThen the hold locked.\rThe stairwell’s doubled harmonic snapped into alignment with his chest, like a collar seating in the wrong notch.\rTiffani’s voice cut clean through the noise. “Wedges—now. Lift on my count. Three heartbeats. Pull.”\rBoots scraped. Wedges hammered. Stone complained.\r“Three,” Tiffani said. “Two. One. Now—pull!”\rHands reached into the dark. The child came out gasping, eyes wide, spit and dust and life.\r“Terminate,” Tiffani said.\rJhace didn’t move.\r“Terminate,” she repeated, like command could override biology.\rSilence.\rBram’s voice cracked into the gap. “He’s not moving.”\r“Jhace.” Tiffani stepped in close. “Move.”\rBram hovered like he wanted to shake him and knew better. “Say something. Blink. Spit. Insult me. Anything.”\r“Stop talking,” Tiffani snapped.\r“I can’t,” Bram said. “It’s how I pray.”\rJhace could still see. Still hear. Still count.\rHe stared at his hands, waiting for tremor.\rThere was no tremor.\rThere was nothing.\rHe tried to will a finger. Nothing.\rPanic hit—animal, immediate, nothing to do with heat or holding.\rHe tried to shout. Nothing left his throat.\rHe tried to jerk a shoulder. Nothing.\rHe tried to blink faster—signal—anything.\rHis lids moved on their own schedule. Slow. Unbothered.\rThe body was there. The controls were there. The link was gone.\rThe coal behind his sternum pulsed once. Steady. Patient. Still ready.\rReady to hold.\rBut he couldn’t lift a finger to tell it no.\rOnly the wanting stayed—locked in meat that wouldn’t answer.\r\rTiffani moved into his field of view and knelt like she was inspecting a fracture line. Ink and copper sat on her hands; she smelled like slate dust and hours without sleep.\r“Blink once for yes,” she said. “Twice for no.”\rHe blinked once.\r“Do you still feel the doubled harmonic through the stone?”\rOnce.\r“Can you tell me where it dipped before Tallow Street?”\rOnce. He could see it like a chart behind his eyes—deep note, then the metallic taste, then the split.\rTiffani slid her slate into his line of sight. Two columns. LOCATION. HEARTBEATS. Her writing stayed precise even as her knuckles whitened.\r“Can you predict where it fails next?”\rOnce. Not a comfort. A capability.\rTiffani’s mouth tightened. “Then you’re not finished.” She didn’t say it like hope. She said it like reassignment.\rJhace stared at his hands, waiting for tremor. Nothing.\rIt was worse than shaking. Silence in the muscles felt like a lie.\r\rBut the numbers still lined up. The city still had a baseline. He could still calibrate it.\rTiffani’s eyes tracked his fingers. “His hands are still.”\rBram swallowed. “That’s… new.”\r“There is no tremor,” Tiffani said.\r“Great,” Bram said. “We fixed the tremor by removing the man. Efficient.”\r“Bram.”\r“Sorry,” Bram said. “I’m coping. Poorly. Loudly.”\rTiffani dropped to her knees beside him, slate already out, chalk already moving.\r“Stage Four,” she said, voice flat because panic was a luxury. “Complete motor loss. Locked hold with autonomic override. Cost: total paralysis below manifestation threshold.”\r“You’re writing this down,” Bram said.\r“Yes.”\r“Right now.”\r“Yes.”\r“While he’s—”\r“Yes,” Tiffani said, and her hand didn’t shake. “Because if I don’t, this becomes a story someone else gets to tell. And they will tell it wrong.”\rShe swallowed once, hard, then kept writing.\rWhen the crowd noise finally thinned and the guards stopped pretending they weren’t watching, Tiffani stood.\r“Corlexi will get what I give him,” she said.\rBram blinked. “Which is what, exactly—an instruction manual with a hidden knife?”\r“A method,” Tiffani said. “With constraints. With brakes. Without permission to repeat this.”\r“So… a leash on the leash,” Bram said.\r“Something like that.”\r“You’re terrifying when you’re calm,” Bram said.\r“I’m not calm,” Tiffani said.\r“Right,” Bram said, voice softer than his jokes. “You’re just… controlled. Like a blade.”\r\rCHAPTER 13: THE LEDGER\rTiffani wrote the first copy with her left hand because her right knee refused to lock and her right hand refused to stay still.\rIt wasn’t Stage. It was the kind of fatigue that lives in joints after bad work—the kind you carry until your hands start lying to you.\rThe ink pooled anyway. Copper-gold dust clung to the nib, turning every line slightly wrong, as if the city itself wanted the record to smear.\rShe made three piles.\rFour, if she was honest.\rOne stack sat in clean paper with clean margins and clean language. Compliance phrasing. Definitions that sounded like protection while leaving room for ownership.\rOne stack was tidy and dead. The kind of language that looks safe until somebody has to bleed under it.\rOne stack was short enough to memorize. Underface cadence. Commands. Counts. No soft words, no long sentences—something you could carry in your head when paper burned or got confiscated.\rThe last pile wasn’t for anyone. It was her draft. Her private notes. The parts she could not justify on a form and would not confess out loud.\rBram stood in the doorway in his Crown coat like he’d stolen it off a corpse and decided it suited him.\r“You look like a priest,” Tiffani said without looking up.\rBram glanced down at the coat. “Don’t you dare put that evil on me. Priests get paid to lie. I’m doing it recreationally.”\r“You’re late.”\r“Yeah. Turns out paperwork travels faster than rescue.” He leaned in, saw the piles. “Oh good. Homework. My favorite form of punishment.”\rTiffani slid the clean stack toward him first. “This goes to Corlexi.”\rBram’s fingers hovered. “This the ‘please don’t leash us’ letter?”\r“This is the ‘you get what I give you’ packet,” she said, tapping the pages hard enough to leave a smudge. “Limits. Spotters. A way out when the body says no.”\rBram squinted. “And the part where we can tell them to go fuck themselves?”\rTiffani didn’t blink. “Not included.”\rBram’s smile went sharp. “That’s either genius or suicidal.”\r“It’s survivable.” She turned the next page over and wrote a title line in the margin: DEPLOYMENT AUTHORITY. Then she crossed it out and rewrote: DEPLOYMENT PROCEDURE. “He will read what he wants to read. I’m controlling the surface.”\rBram exhaled through his nose. “Ah. Compliance theater with a trapdoor.”\r“There is no trapdoor,” she said. “There are exits. Distributed.”\rShe pushed the second stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: Property is not a priority class. Operator veto. Refusal authority. No deployment without independent verification.\rBram whistled. “This one’s the ‘burn the Crown down’ packet.”\r“This one is the ‘keep operators alive long enough to matter’ packet.” She slid it closer. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.”\rBram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.”\r“They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.”\rBram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.”\rTiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.”\rChloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers.\r“Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.”\rBram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.”\rChloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?”\r“Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.”\rTiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage.\rChloen read the first line.\rYOU CAN SAY NO.\rShe looked up. “That’s new.”\r“It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.”\rChloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.”\rAnd then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD.\rChloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.”\rBram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.”\rTiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.”\rChloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it.\rThe door clicked. The room held its breath.\rCorran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen.\r“For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.”\rBram’s grin came instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.”\rCorran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.”\rTiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult.\rCorran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.”\rTiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Then it will be accurate.”\rCorran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi?”\rTiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm.\r“Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in daylight.”\rBram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. That’s adorable.”\r“It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.”\rCorran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?”\r“Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.”\rCorran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.”\rHe left.\rBram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. “So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine.”\r“He gets safety with ownership language,” Tiffani corrected. “He will build a machine either way. I’m deciding where it jams.”\rBram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?”\rTiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.”\rShe lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred.\r“Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge.\rTiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission.\rBram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.”\r“I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said.\rOutside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they'd been caught stealing.\rTiffani didn’t move.\rShe dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.\r\r---\rCODA: GLASS IN THE HAND\rJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them.\rHe’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste was still there anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you can't spit out.\rStone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn't reach. His right hand twitching on its own schedule.\rSomeone had posted a guard outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive.\rJhace tried to sleep.\rEvery time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn't tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city's new baseline living in him.\rHe flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking.\rThen it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place.\rJhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed.\r“I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest.\rOutside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short.\rJhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.\r\r---\rCODA: THE SHARD\rJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them.\rStone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn't lift. A slate he couldn't reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted time like it was a job.\rA guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who'd been chewing dust since Kindling.\rThen the breathing stopped.\rNo footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint.\rThe door opened anyway.\rLight entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape.\rA figure stepped in.\rNo perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive.\rThey didn't speak.\rThey moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel.\rA hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact.\rJhace tried to jerk away.\rNothing.\rHe tried to curl his right hand into a fist.\rNothing.\rThe figure took that hand anyway.\rBoth of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage.\rSomething cold settled into the center of his palm.\rA shard.\rCopper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin.\rCold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache.\rThen heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for.\rCopper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit.\rLight threaded up from his palm.\rNot a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path.\rIt slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal.\rJhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body.\rBut he felt the line.\rHe felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay.\rHis lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision.\rThe figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact.\rThen they stepped back.\rNo check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood.\rThe door shut without sound.\rThe guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed.\rJhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close.\rThe tremor was gone.\rIn its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise.\rSomewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.\r\r---\rEND N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS\r---"
          ]
        },
