    "2e63a0d0-fbda-49b4-8528-76bb208b9445": {
      "children": [
        "fd0ca8bb-6507-401b-9270-56c60c745bce"
      ],
      "id": "2e63a0d0-fbda-49b4-8528-76bb208b9445",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "python",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "placement spine, telling wood and stone what shape to keep. Tiffani stopped beside Jhace. Her pen was already moving. Shard‑Bearer. Visual confirmation. The man’s chest glowed through his shirt. Not a flicker. A steady line, as if someone had pressed a shard under skin and the body had agreed. “No tremor,” Tiffani murmured. “Not paying,” Jhace said. The words tasted wrong the moment he spoke them. “Incorrect,” she said. “Look at his eyes.” Jhace looked. The man’s eyes were copper‑gold and flat, putting off their own light like heated coins. Jhace couldn’t find a tremor in it. That scared him more than any shake. The beam settled another fraction. The store stopped complaining. The man released the timber and walked away without checking who escaped. No count. No confirmation. No termination signal. No glance back. Tiffani underlined her note twice. Cost unidentified. Method unreplicable. Do not engage. Jhace’s tremor ticked up. Not from fear. From wanting to know what it would feel like to hold without shaking.\n\n--- paragraph 53 terms {'east'} ---\nA Crown clerk with clean boots unrolled forms on Jhace’s bench like a priest laying out offerings. Behind the clerk stood two guards, a Temple observer, and Tallec—smiling like he’d finally found a way to win without touching a pipe. And at the center, Bram Tallow in a different coat than usual: administrator’s cut, badge pinned. Still Bram, but now carrying Crown weight. “Don’t start,” Jhace said, seeing the coat. Bram’s eyes flicked down to Jhace’s shaking hands and then away. “I didn’t ask for this.” Jhace looked at the coat. The badge. The way Bram wore it like it didn’t fit his bones. “You took it anyway.” Bram’s grin tried to show up and died halfway. “Yeah. Because if I don’t, they send someone who actually likes forms. Someone who’ll file you under ‘infrastructure asset’ and sleep like a baby.” He touched the badge once. Like it burned. “I’m terrified,” he said, quiet enough to almost count as a confession. “Terrified they turn you into numbers. Terrified I help them do it. Terrified I get good at it.” The grin came back, wrong‑shaped. “So I’m coping by making dick jokes. Extremely healthy. Very professional.” Tiffani stepped forward. “What is it?” Bram nodded at the clerk. “Manifestor Registry. Compliance documentation. Deployment authority.” “By order of Infrastructure Compliance Office. Administrator Corlexi presiding.” “Oh good,” Bram said. “A sentence that already feels like a noose.” The clerk didn’t look up. “Name. District. Verified activation capability. Cost markers. Compliance agreement. You will not manifest outside authorized events.” “Define ‘deployment authority,’” Tiffani said. “Infrastructure Compliance Office.” “That is not a definition,” Tiffani said. “That is a label.” “It is the issuing body.” “Then who determines what qualifies as an ‘authorized event’?” “Infrastructure Compliance Office.” Jhace’s jaw worked. “What happens if we refuse?” “Then you are unlicensed. Unlicensed manifestation will be prosecuted as endangerment.” Tallec leaned in with a smile that didn’t reach his eyes. “Finally. Structure. No more renegade holds.” Bram stared at him like he’d stepped in something. “Tallec, you look like a man who’d schedule his own mother’s funeral for throughput.” “At least someone respects schedules,” Tallec said. “Don’t start,” Jhace warned. “I didn’t start,” Bram said. “He started. I’m just adding sex noises.” Corran stepped in like he didn’t believe doors applied to him. “Schedules don’t respect physics.” The clerk’s eyes flicked up, irritated. “Brother Corran. You are not assigned here.” “For the record,” Corran said, “I’m observing.” He looked at Jhace. “And if the Crown orders you to hold a vault instead of a residential stairwell?” Tallec’s voice went oily. “Assets protect assets.” Jhace’s hands went flat on the bench, because he didn’t trust them to do something irreversible. “Next time I hold, it’ll be because something is falling. Not because you want it to stay standing.” “Romantic,” Bram said. “I only break my body for strangers, not for paperwork.” Tiffani’s gaze stayed on the form. “If we don’t sign, we lose access to incidents. They will send licensed manifestors—or force untrained ones—to hold.” She set her pen down with a little click, the way you set down a blade when you're done being polite. “We sign,” she said. “But our standard rides with it.” The clerk’s mouth opened. “That is not—” “Duration limits,” Tiffani said. “Spotters. Termination criteria. Cost tracking. You will not turn people into disposable beams.” Bram leaned toward the clerk. “She means ‘stop treating people like disposable beams,’ but she’s saying it in math so you can understand.” “The registry does not negotiate terms with operators.” “Then the registry gets incomplete operators,” Tiffani said. “Your choice.” Tallec’s smile widened. “You become assets. Next time you hold, you’ll do it where the Crown tells you to hold. Not where you feel like playing hero.” “‘Playing hero,’” Bram echoed. “That’s rich coming from a man who thinks schedules are moral philosophy.” “Production keeps people alive,” Tallec said. “So does not crushing them,” Jhace said. “This conversation is over,” Tallec said, like he had the authority to end reality. “Was it a conversation?” Bram asked. “Felt more like a threat with paperwork attached.”\n\n--- paragraph 58 terms {'hour'} ---\nOutside, a bell rang—different cadence. Not collapse. A drill signal. Crown‑mandated training. Jhace’s chest heat flared anyway, eager and stupid. He hated himself for that flare. He stood, hands shaking, and went to the training yard because refusal would make them criminals. Tiffani followed, slate in hand, limping slightly from too many hours on stone floors. Daved watched them go and muttered, almost too quiet to hear: “Hungry hands hold until they break.” Jhace pretended he didn’t hear. He did. ---\n\n--- paragraph 114 terms {'hour'} ---\nTiffani moved into his field of view and knelt like she was inspecting a fracture line. Ink and copper sat on her hands; she smelled like slate dust and hours without sleep.\n\n--- paragraph 148 terms {'day', 'second'} ---\nTiffani wrote the first copy with her left hand because her right knee refused to lock and her right hand refused to stay still. It wasn’t Stage. It was the kind of fatigue that lives in joints after bad work—the kind you carry until your hands start lying to you. The ink pooled anyway. Copper-gold dust clung to the nib, turning every line slightly wrong, as if the city itself wanted the record to smear. She made three piles. Four, if she was honest. One stack sat in clean paper with clean margins and clean language. Compliance phrasing. Definitions that sounded like protection while leaving room for ownership. One stack was tidy and dead. The kind of language that looks safe until somebody has to bleed under it. One stack was short enough to memorize. Underface cadence. Commands. Counts. No soft words, no long sentences—something you could carry in your head when paper burned or got confiscated. The last pile wasn’t for anyone. It was her draft. Her private notes. The parts she could not justify on a form and would not confess out loud. Bram stood in the doorway in his Crown coat like he’d stolen it off a corpse and decided it suited him. “You look like a priest,” Tiffani said without looking up. Bram glanced down at the coat. “Don’t you dare put that evil on me. Priests get paid to lie. I’m doing it recreationally.” “You’re late.” “Yeah. Turns out paperwork travels faster than rescue.” He leaned in, saw the piles. “Oh good. Homework. My favorite form of punishment.” Tiffani slid the clean stack toward him first. “This goes to Corlexi.” Bram’s fingers hovered. “This the ‘please don’t leash us’ letter?” “This is the ‘you get what I give you’ packet,” she said, tapping the pages hard enough to leave a smudge. “Limits. Spotters. A way out when the body says no.” Bram squinted. “And the part where we can tell them to go fuck themselves?” Tiffani didn’t blink. “Not included.” Bram’s smile went sharp. “That’s either genius or suicidal.” “It’s survivable.” She turned the next page over and wrote a title line in the margin: DEPLOYMENT AUTHORITY. Then she crossed it out and rewrote: DEPLOYMENT PROCEDURE. “He will read what he wants to read. I’m controlling the surface.” Bram exhaled through his nose. “Ah. Compliance theater with a trapdoor.” “There is no trapdoor,” she said. “There are exits. Distributed.” She pushed the second stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: Property is not a priority class. Operator veto. Refusal authority. No deployment without independent verification. Bram whistled. “This one’s the ‘burn the Crown down’ packet.” “This one is the ‘keep operators alive long enough to matter’ packet.” She slid it closer. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.” Bram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.” “They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.” Bram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.” Tiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.” Chloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers. “Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.” Bram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.” Chloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?” “Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.” Tiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage. Chloen read the first line. YOU CAN SAY NO. She looked up. “That’s new.” “It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.” Chloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.” And then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD. Chloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.” Bram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.” Tiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.” Chloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it. The door clicked. The room held its breath. Corran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen. “For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.” Bram’s grin came instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.” Corran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.” Tiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult. Corran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.” Tiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Then it will be accurate.” Corran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi?” Tiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm. “Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in daylight.” Bram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. That’s adorable.” “It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.” Corran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?” “Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.” Corran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.” He left. Bram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. “So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine.” “He gets safety with ownership language,” Tiffani corrected. “He will build a machine either way. I’m deciding where it jams.” Bram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?” Tiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.” She lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred. “Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge. Tiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission. Bram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.” “I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said. Outside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they'd been caught stealing. Tiffani didn’t move. She dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.\n\n--- paragraph 150 terms {'day'} ---\n--- CODA: THE SHARD Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them. Stone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn't lift. A slate he couldn't reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted time like it was a job. A guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who'd been chewing dust since Kindling. Then the breathing stopped. No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint. The door opened anyway. Light entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape. A figure stepped in. No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive. They didn't speak. They moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel. A hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact. Jhace tried to jerk away. Nothing. He tried to curl his right hand into a fist. Nothing. The figure took that hand anyway. Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage. Something cold settled into the center of his palm. A shard. Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin. Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache. Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for. Copper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit. Light threaded up from his palm. Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path. It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal. Jhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body. But he felt the line. He felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay. His lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision. The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact. Then they stepped back. No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood. The door shut without sound. The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed. Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close. The tremor was gone. In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise. Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.\n\n--- paragraph 156 terms {'day'} ---\nThe guard’s breathing stopped. No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint. The door opened anyway. Light entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape. A figure stepped in. No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive. They didn't speak. They moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel. A hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact. Jhace tried to jerk away. Nothing. He tried to curl his right hand into a fist. Nothing. The figure took that hand anyway. Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage. Something cold settled into the center of his palm. A shard. Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin. Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache. Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for. Copper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit. Light threaded up from his palm. Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path. It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal. Jhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body. But he felt the line. He felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay. His lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision. The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact. Then they stepped back. No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood. The door shut without sound. The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed. Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close. The tremor was gone. In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise. Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.\n"
        },
        "create_time": 1768421251.0410266,

              {
                "message_type": "stream",

                "stream_name": "stdout",
                "text": "placement spine, telling wood and stone what shape to keep. Tiffani stopped beside Jhace. Her pen was already moving. Shard‑Bearer. Visual confirmation. The man’s chest glowed through his shirt. Not a flicker. A steady line, as if someone had pressed a shard under skin and the body had agreed. “No tremor,” Tiffani murmured. “Not paying,” Jhace said. The words tasted wrong the moment he spoke them. “Incorrect,” she said. “Look at his eyes.” Jhace looked. The man’s eyes were copper‑gold and flat, putting off their own light like heated coins. Jhace couldn’t find a tremor in it. That scared him more than any shake. The beam settled another fraction. The store stopped complaining. The man released the timber and walked away without checking who escaped. No count. No confirmation. No termination signal. No glance back. Tiffani underlined her note twice. Cost unidentified. Method unreplicable. Do not engage. Jhace’s tremor ticked up. Not from fear. From wanting to know what it would feel like to hold without shaking.\n\n--- paragraph 53 terms {'east'} ---\nA Crown clerk with clean boots unrolled forms on Jhace’s bench like a priest laying out offerings. Behind the clerk stood two guards, a Temple observer, and Tallec—smiling like he’d finally found a way to win without touching a pipe. And at the center, Bram Tallow in a different coat than usual: administrator’s cut, badge pinned. Still Bram, but now carrying Crown weight. “Don’t start,” Jhace said, seeing the coat. Bram’s eyes flicked down to Jhace’s shaking hands and then away. “I didn’t ask for this.” Jhace looked at the coat. The badge. The way Bram wore it like it didn’t fit his bones. “You took it anyway.” Bram’s grin tried to show up and died halfway. “Yeah. Because if I don’t, they send someone who actually likes forms. Someone who’ll file you under ‘infrastructure asset’ and sleep like a baby.” He touched the badge once. Like it burned. “I’m terrified,” he said, quiet enough to almost count as a confession. “Terrified they turn you into numbers. Terrified I help them do it. Terrified I get good at it.” The grin came back, wrong‑shaped. “So I’m coping by making dick jokes. Extremely healthy. Very professional.” Tiffani stepped forward. “What is it?” Bram nodded at the clerk. “Manifestor Registry. Compliance documentation. Deployment authority.” “By order of Infrastructure Compliance Office. Administrator Corlexi presiding.” “Oh good,” Bram said. “A sentence that already feels like a noose.” The clerk didn’t look up. “Name. District. Verified activation capability. Cost markers. Compliance agreement. You will not manifest outside authorized events.” “Define ‘deployment authority,’” Tiffani said. “Infrastructure Compliance Office.” “That is not a definition,” Tiffani said. “That is a label.” “It is the issuing body.” “Then who determines what qualifies as an ‘authorized event’?” “Infrastructure Compliance Office.” Jhace’s jaw worked. “What happens if we refuse?” “Then you are unlicensed. Unlicensed manifestation will be prosecuted as endangerment.” Tallec leaned in with a smile that didn’t reach his eyes. “Finally. Structure. No more renegade holds.” Bram stared at him like he’d stepped in something. “Tallec, you look like a man who’d schedule his own mother’s funeral for throughput.” “At least someone respects schedules,” Tallec said. “Don’t start,” Jhace warned. “I didn’t start,” Bram said. “He started. I’m just adding sex noises.” Corran stepped in like he didn’t believe doors applied to him. “Schedules don’t respect physics.” The clerk’s eyes flicked up, irritated. “Brother Corran. You are not assigned here.” “For the record,” Corran said, “I’m observing.” He looked at Jhace. “And if the Crown orders you to hold a vault instead of a residential stairwell?” Tallec’s voice went oily. “Assets protect assets.” Jhace’s hands went flat on the bench, because he didn’t trust them to do something irreversible. “Next time I hold, it’ll be because something is falling. Not because you want it to stay standing.” “Romantic,” Bram said. “I only break my body for strangers, not for paperwork.” Tiffani’s gaze stayed on the form. “If we don’t sign, we lose access to incidents. They will send licensed manifestors—or force untrained ones—to hold.” She set her pen down with a little click, the way you set down a blade when you're done being polite. “We sign,” she said. “But our standard rides with it.” The clerk’s mouth opened. “That is not—” “Duration limits,” Tiffani said. “Spotters. Termination criteria. Cost tracking. You will not turn people into disposable beams.” Bram leaned toward the clerk. “She means ‘stop treating people like disposable beams,’ but she’s saying it in math so you can understand.” “The registry does not negotiate terms with operators.” “Then the registry gets incomplete operators,” Tiffani said. “Your choice.” Tallec’s smile widened. “You become assets. Next time you hold, you’ll do it where the Crown tells you to hold. Not where you feel like playing hero.” “‘Playing hero,’” Bram echoed. “That’s rich coming from a man who thinks schedules are moral philosophy.” “Production keeps people alive,” Tallec said. “So does not crushing them,” Jhace said. “This conversation is over,” Tallec said, like he had the authority to end reality. “Was it a conversation?” Bram asked. “Felt more like a threat with paperwork attached.”\n\n--- paragraph 58 terms {'hour'} ---\nOutside, a bell rang—different cadence. Not collapse. A drill signal. Crown‑mandated training. Jhace’s chest heat flared anyway, eager and stupid. He hated himself for that flare. He stood, hands shaking, and went to the training yard because refusal would make them criminals. Tiffani followed, slate in hand, limping slightly from too many hours on stone floors. Daved watched them go and muttered, almost too quiet to hear: “Hungry hands hold until they break.” Jhace pretended he didn’t hear. He did. ---\n\n--- paragraph 114 terms {'hour'} ---\nTiffani moved into his field of view and knelt like she was inspecting a fracture line. Ink and copper sat on her hands; she smelled like slate dust and hours without sleep.\n\n--- paragraph 148 terms {'day', 'second'} ---\nTiffani wrote the first copy with her left hand because her right knee refused to lock and her right hand refused to stay still. It wasn’t Stage. It was the kind of fatigue that lives in joints after bad work—the kind you carry until your hands start lying to you. The ink pooled anyway. Copper-gold dust clung to the nib, turning every line slightly wrong, as if the city itself wanted the record to smear. She made three piles. Four, if she was honest. One stack sat in clean paper with clean margins and clean language. Compliance phrasing. Definitions that sounded like protection while leaving room for ownership. One stack was tidy and dead. The kind of language that looks safe until somebody has to bleed under it. One stack was short enough to memorize. Underface cadence. Commands. Counts. No soft words, no long sentences—something you could carry in your head when paper burned or got confiscated. The last pile wasn’t for anyone. It was her draft. Her private notes. The parts she could not justify on a form and would not confess out loud. Bram stood in the doorway in his Crown coat like he’d stolen it off a corpse and decided it suited him. “You look like a priest,” Tiffani said without looking up. Bram glanced down at the coat. “Don’t you dare put that evil on me. Priests get paid to lie. I’m doing it recreationally.” “You’re late.” “Yeah. Turns out paperwork travels faster than rescue.” He leaned in, saw the piles. “Oh good. Homework. My favorite form of punishment.” Tiffani slid the clean stack toward him first. “This goes to Corlexi.” Bram’s fingers hovered. “This the ‘please don’t leash us’ letter?” “This is the ‘you get what I give you’ packet,” she said, tapping the pages hard enough to leave a smudge. “Limits. Spotters. A way out when the body says no.” Bram squinted. “And the part where we can tell them to go fuck themselves?” Tiffani didn’t blink. “Not included.” Bram’s smile went sharp. “That’s either genius or suicidal.” “It’s survivable.” She turned the next page over and wrote a title line in the margin: DEPLOYMENT AUTHORITY. Then she crossed it out and rewrote: DEPLOYMENT PROCEDURE. “He will read what he wants to read. I’m controlling the surface.” Bram exhaled through his nose. “Ah. Compliance theater with a trapdoor.” “There is no trapdoor,” she said. “There are exits. Distributed.” She pushed the second stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: Property is not a priority class. Operator veto. Refusal authority. No deployment without independent verification. Bram whistled. “This one’s the ‘burn the Crown down’ packet.” “This one is the ‘keep operators alive long enough to matter’ packet.” She slid it closer. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.” Bram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.” “They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.” Bram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.” Tiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.” Chloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers. “Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.” Bram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.” Chloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?” “Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.” Tiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage. Chloen read the first line. YOU CAN SAY NO. She looked up. “That’s new.” “It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.” Chloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.” And then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD. Chloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.” Bram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.” Tiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.” Chloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it. The door clicked. The room held its breath. Corran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen. “For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.” Bram’s grin came instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.” Corran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.” Tiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult. Corran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.” Tiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Then it will be accurate.” Corran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi?” Tiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm. “Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in daylight.” Bram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. That’s adorable.” “It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.” Corran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?” “Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.” Corran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.” He left. Bram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. “So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine.” “He gets safety with ownership language,” Tiffani corrected. “He will build a machine either way. I’m deciding where it jams.” Bram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?” Tiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.” She lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred. “Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge. Tiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission. Bram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.” “I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said. Outside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they'd been caught stealing. Tiffani didn’t move. She dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.\n\n--- paragraph 150 terms {'day'} ---\n--- CODA: THE SHARD Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them. It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal. Jhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body. But he felt the line. He felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay. His lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision. The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact. Then they stepped back. No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood. The door shut without sound. The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed. Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close. The tremor was gone. In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise. Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.\n",
                "time": 1768421252.451559
              }
            ],
            "run_id": "d65a7cfb-fe62-4550-b134-407f2df60be0",

        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "er. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.”\nBram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.”\n“They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.”\nBram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.”\nTiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.”\nChloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers.\n“Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.”\nBram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.”\nChloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?”\n“Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.”\nTiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage.\nChloen read the first line.\nYOU CAN SAY NO.\nShe looked up. “That’s new.”\n“It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t\n"
        },
        "create_time": 1768417177.1526904,
        "end_turn": null,

    "2f0fea76-7ae1-46ad-9540-c7919d36c8af": {

      ],
      "id": "2f0fea76-7ae1-46ad-9540-c7919d36c8af",
      "message": {
        "author": {

          "name": null,

        },

        "content": {
          "content_type": "code",
          "language": "unknown",
          "response_format_name": null,
          "text": "for w in [\"degrees\",\"degree\",\"west\",\"east\",\"hour\",\"hours\",\"minute\",\"minutes\",\"today\",\"days\",\"day\",\"second\",\"seconds\"]:\n    res=find_paras_containing(w)\n    if res:\n        print(\"\\nWORD\",w)\n        for i,text in res:\n            print(i, text)\n"
        },

    "30201e11-eec0-41a1-bf21-003eec08c699": {
      "children": [
        "6db4ec21-3c14-430c-bf48-f873d06b9346"
      ],
      "id": "30201e11-eec0-41a1-bf21-003eec08c699",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "python",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "('/mnt/data/N01_GripThatHolds_LOCKED_Ch1-13_Coda_UPDATED.txt', 114380)"
        },
        "create_time": 1768420124.3985875,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "30201e11-eec0-41a1-bf21-003eec08c699",
        "metadata": {
          "aggregate_result": {
            "code": "from pathlib import Path\nimport re\n\nsrc = Path(\"/mnt/data/N01_GripThatHolds_LOCKED_Ch1-13_Coda.txt\")\ntext = src.read_text(encoding=\"utf-8\")\n\nchapter12_new = \"\"\"## CHAPTER 12: METHOD\n\nThe alert came late. Like an insult.\n\nIt was a narrow failure—no mass collapse, no Hall, no warehouse full of people.\n\nA residential stairwell in Sector Nine. A narrow stone throat that had been patched too many times since the Piercing. A single child trapped beneath a fallen landing slab, crying until her voice turned hoarse.\n\nTiffani arrived first. Her limp was worse now, but she moved like it didn’t matter. She scanned the structure in three heartbeats. Load paths. Weak seams. The slab’s weight distribution.\n\n“Clear lane,” she ordered as the others reached the site. “Spotters assigned. Wedges first.”\n\nShe pointed left, then froze. “No—right. Right corridor first. Sorry.”\n\nThe apology tasted like rust. She didn’t repeat it.\n\nJhace’s hands shook so hard he had to jam them into his coat pockets to stop them from betraying him in front of the crowd.\n\nHe could feel the doubled harmonic through the stairwell stone. Deep. Constant. The grid had never recovered. It had just learned to fail slower.\n\nBram arrived with Crown guards and a face that said he’d tried, briefly, to be serious and failed on contact.\n\n“Registry protocol,” he said. “Two licensed operators inbound.”\n\nTiffani didn’t look at him. “They’ll be too late.”\n\n“Cool,” Bram said. “Love the part where the Crown schedules rescue like it’s a delivery window.”\n\n“Clear lane,” Tiffani ordered. “Spotters assigned. Wedges first.”\n\nShe looked at the sagging landing slab, the trapped space beneath it, the child’s choking turning into wet silence. Her voice tightened into a blade.\n\n“One operator. One-twenty heartbeats max. We brace, we lift, we extract. Terminate immediately after.”\n\n“Then who?” Jhace said.\n\n“Not you.”\n\n“Then who.”\n\n“The child is choking, Jhace.”\n\nJhace’s tremor rattled against his bones like a tool trying to escape. “I decide.”\n\nBram’s breath punched out. “Fuck—no, don’t—okay, you’re doing it. You’re doing the thing. Great. Fantastic. I hate this.”\n\nJhace stepped in. Copper flooded his mouth like blood.\n\nFor a heartbeat, nothing happened.\n\nThen the hold locked.\n\nThe stairwell’s doubled harmonic snapped into alignment with his chest, like a collar seating in the wrong notch.\n\nTiffani’s voice cut clean through the noise. “Wedges—now. Lift on my count. Three heartbeats. Pull.”\n\nBoots scraped. Wedges hammered. Stone complained.\n\n“Three,” Tiffani said. “Two. One. Now—pull!”\n\nHands reached into the dark. The child came out gasping, eyes wide, spit and dust and life.\n\n“Terminate,” Tiffani said.\n\nJhace didn’t move.\n\n“Terminate,” she repeated, like command could override biology.\n\nSilence.\n\nBram’s voice cracked into the gap. “He’s not moving.”\n\n“Jhace.” Tiffani stepped in close. “Move.”\n\nBram hovered like he wanted to shake him and knew better. “Say something. Blink. Spit. Insult me. Anything.”\n\n“Stop talking,” Tiffani snapped.\n\n“I can’t,” Bram said. “It’s how I pray.”\n\nJhace could still see. Still hear. Still count.\n\nHe stared at his hands, waiting for tremor.\n\nThere was no tremor.\n\nThere was nothing.\n\nHe tried to will a finger. Nothing.\n\nPanic hit—animal, immediate, nothing to do with heat or holding.\n\nHe tried to shout. Nothing left his throat.\n\nHe tried to jerk a shoulder. Nothing.\n\nHe tried to blink faster—signal—anything.\n\nHis lids moved on their own schedule. Slow. Unbothered.\n\nThe body was there. The controls were there. The link was gone.\n\nThe coal behind his sternum pulsed once. Steady. Patient. Still ready.\n\nReady to hold.\n\nBut he couldn’t lift a finger to tell it no.\n\nOnly the wanting stayed—locked in meat that wouldn’t answer.\n\nTiffani moved into his field of view and knelt like she was inspecting a fracture line. Ink and copper sat on her hands; she smelled like slate dust and hours without sleep.\n\n“Blink once for yes,” she said. “Twice for no.”\n\nHe blinked once.\n\n“Do you still feel the doubled harmonic through the stone?”\n\nOnce.\n\n“Can you tell me where it dipped before Tallow Street?”\n\nOnce. He could see it like a chart behind his eyes—deep note, then the metallic taste, then the split.\n\nTiffani slid her slate into his line of sight. Two columns. LOCATION. HEARTBEATS. Her writing stayed precise even as her knuckles whitened.\n\n“Can you predict where it fails next?”\n\nOnce. Not a comfort. A capability.\n\nTiffani’s mouth tightened. “Then you’re not finished.” She didn’t say it like hope. She said it like reassignment.\n\nJhace stared at his hands, waiting for tremor. Nothing.\n\nIt was worse than shaking. Silence in the muscles felt like a lie.\n\nTiffani dropped to her knees beside him, slate already out, chalk already moving.\n\n“Stage Four,” she said, voice flat because panic was a luxury. “Complete motor loss. Locked hold with autonomic override. Cost: total paralysis below manifestation threshold.”\n\nBram swallowed. “You’re writing this down.”\n\n“Yes.”\n\n“Right now.”\n\n“Yes.”\n\n“While he’s—”\n\n“Yes,” Tiffani said, and her hand didn’t shake. “Because if I don’t, this becomes a story someone else gets to tell. And they will tell it wrong.”\n\nShe swallowed once, hard, then kept writing.\n\nWhen the crowd noise finally thinned and the guards stopped pretending they weren’t watching, Tiffani stood.\n\n“Corlexi will get what I give him,” she said.\n\nBram blinked. “Which is what, exactly—an instruction manual with a hidden knife?”\n\n“A method,” Tiffani said. “With constraints. With brakes. Without permission to repeat this.”\n\n“So… a leash on the leash,” Bram said.\n\n“Something like that.”\n\n“You’re terrifying when you’re calm,” Bram said.\n\n“I’m not calm,” Tiffani said.\n\n“Right,” Bram said, voice softer than his jokes. “You’re just… controlled. Like a blade.”\n\"\"\"\n\n# Replace Chapter 12 block\nm12 = re.search(r\"##\\s*CHAPTER\\s*12:.*?\\n\", text)\nm13 = re.search(r\"##\\s*CHAPTER\\s*13:.*?\\n\", text)\n\nif not (m12 and m13 and m12.start() < m13.start()):\n    raise RuntimeError(\"Could not locate Chapter 12/13 boundaries for replacement.\")\n\nupdated = text[:m12.start()] + chapter12_new + \"\\n\\n---\\n\\n\" + text[m13.start():]\n\nout = Path(\"/mnt/data/N01_GripThatHolds_LOCKED_Ch1-13_Coda_UPDATED.txt\")\nout.write_text(updated, encoding=\"utf-8\")\n\n(str(out), len(updated))\n",
            "end_time": 1768420126.4385498,
            "final_expression_output": "('/mnt/data/N01_GripThatHolds_LOCKED_Ch1-13_Coda_UPDATED.txt', 114380)",

    "316e69f6-439e-4b3e-93e3-1abbd8b4c403": {
      "children": [
        "32438e75-caf3-4899-ba69-6f54c850cb4c"
      ],
      "id": "316e69f6-439e-4b3e-93e3-1abbd8b4c403",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": "final",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "Chapter 4 is **reopened**.\n\n## CHAPTER 4: PROTOCOL\n\nJhace’s workshop was a box of iron and heat tucked into the mid-terrace, where you could hear the grid without being crushed by it.\n\nTiffani sat on an overturned crate. Slate on her knees. Left leg stretched out like a brace. The knee had started locking after long stands on stone floors.\n\nShe didn’t lean back.\n\nAlways ready to move. Always ready to write.\n\nJhace stood at his bench, trying to thread a bolt into a collar bracket.\n\nHis hands shook.\n\nThe grid didn’t fail with a bang. It stuttered. Missed beats. Caught up. Like an old machine slipping teeth.\n\nHe tried again.\n\nThe bolt dropped. Metal on stone.\n\nHe stared at it like it had betrayed him.\n\nTiffani didn’t look up. “Your tremor amplitude increased during exertion.”\n\n“Because I’m exerting,” Jhace muttered, bending to pick it up. His fingertips felt numb where the filament had burned them. Not pain.\n\nAbsence.\n\n“Because your body is remembering,” Tiffani said. “It thinks you’re still holding.”\n\nJhace set the bolt down. Flexed his hand hard.\n\nThe tremor didn’t care.\n\nHe hated that. Hated the way his own muscles had become unreliable.\n\n“What did you call it out there?” he asked.\n\nTiffani flipped her slate so he could see. A crude timeline. No poetry. Just sequence.\n\n**Activation onset:** chest heat + copper taste  \n**Light manifestation:** filament, contact-dependent  \n**Tremor:** post-termination, permanent marker\n\n“I need a taxonomy,” she said. “Stages. Right now it’s just stories.”\n\n“And you think writing stages keeps people alive.”\n\n“I think it keeps them from lying to themselves.” She tapped the word **permanent**. “You can’t call it temporary. You can’t bargain with it.”\n\nJhace’s jaw worked.\n\nHe had bargained with Junction Seven. Told himself the report would matter. That somebody else would fix it. That the city didn’t need him to fight every foreman with a schedule board.\n\nHe looked at his shaking hands.\n\n“Stage One,” he said. Low. “Tremor.”\n\nTiffani nodded and wrote:\n\n**Stage One:** tremor + filament residue. **Functional. Marked.**\n\nShe stared at **Functional** like it offended her.\n\nDrew a hard line through it.\n\nRewrote: **Operational.**\n\n“Functional makes it sound polite,” she muttered.\n\nA knock hit the workshop door.\n\nBram Kerris leaned in, dust on his boots, sweat at his collar, grin already loaded like a bad habit.\n\n“Heard you two grabbed Tallow Street by the throat,” Bram said, trying for funny and getting dust instead. He coughed once. “City tries to crush you, you crush back. Very… romantic. Very dumb.”\n\nJhace didn’t look up. “Who’s asking?”\n\n“Crown’s asking questions,” Bram said, and somehow still made it sound obscene. “Not because they care. Because they hate unknown tools walking around without a leash and a receipt.”\n\nTiffani’s pen paused. “What questions.”\n\n“Names. Locations. ‘Verified capability.’ That sort of foreplay.” Bram stepped fully inside, then stopped like the heat-grid might charge him rent. “Infrastructure office. Some administrator with hands so clean it makes you wonder what he’s washing off. Loves forms. Loves categories. Loves pretending paper is stronger than stone.”\n\nJhace’s jaw tightened. “To do what.”\n\nBram spread his hands. “To decide who gets to own you. Temple will call it divine. Guild will call it labor. Crown will call it law. Everybody wants a piece. Congratulations—you’re popular.”\n\n“We don’t belong to any of them,” Tiffani said.\n\nBram laughed, quick and wrong. “Adorable. Tell that to a stamp. Try explaining ‘we don’t belong’ while a ceiling is coming down and someone’s screaming for a hold. They’ll file you under ‘non-compliant’ and act like that’s a weather condition.”\n\nJhace’s tremor ticked in his fingers. “So you’re here to warn us.”\n\n“I’m here to tell you the door is already being measured,” Bram said. “I can buy you a Tide or two. Maybe. After that, they show up with paperwork and smiles and start deciding which parts of you are ‘public infrastructure.’”\n\nTiffani’s mouth went flat. “Then we build methodology fast.”\n\nBram pointed at her slate. “Yeah. Before they build it for you. Because if the Crown writes your protocol, it’s going to read like a job description. And you won’t like the benefits package.”\n\nJhace breathed in.\n\nTasted copper-gold dust that still clung to his clothes.\n\nFelt the city’s hum through the floorboards like a second pulse.\n\n“Then we build it fast,” he said. “Before they decide to build it for us.”\n\n---"
          ]
        },

        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "# N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS\r\n## CHAPTER 4: PROTOCOL — LOCKED\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\nJhace's workshop was a box of iron and heat tucked into the mid-terrace, where you could hear the grid without being crushed by it.\r\n\r\nTiffani sat on an overturned crate. Slate on her knees. Left leg extended to ease the knee that had started locking after long stands on stone floors.\r\n\r\nShe didn't lean back.\r\n\r\nAlways ready to move. Always ready to write.\r\n\r\nJhace stood at his bench, trying to thread a bolt into a collar bracket.\r\n\r\nHis hands shook.\r\n\r\nThe grid didn't fail with a bang. It stuttered. Missed beats. Caught up. Like an old machine slipping teeth.\r\n\r\nHe tried again.\r\n\r\nThe bolt dropped. Metal on stone.\r\n\r\nHe stared at it like it had betrayed him.\r\n\r\nTiffani didn't look up. \"Your tremor amplitude increased during exertion.\"\r\n\r\n\"Because I'm exerting,\" Jhace muttered, bending to pick it up. His fingertips felt numb where the filament had burned them. Not pain.\r\n\r\nAbsence.\r\n\r\n\"Because your body is remembering,\" Tiffani said. \"It thinks you're still holding.\"\r\n\r\nJhace set the bolt down. Flexed his hand hard.\r\n\r\nThe tremor didn't care.\r\n\r\nHe hated that. Hated the way his own muscles had become unreliable.\r\n\r\n\"What did you call it out there?\" he asked.\r\n\r\nTiffani flipped her slate so he could see. A crude timeline. No poetry. Just sequence.\r\n\r\nActivation onset: chest heat + copper taste\r\nLight manifestation: filament, contact-dependent\r\nTremor: post-termination, permanent marker\r\n\r\n\"I need a taxonomy,\" she said. \"Stages. Right now it's just stories.\"\r\n\r\n\"And you think writing stages keeps people alive.\"\r\n\r\n\"I think it keeps them from lying to themselves.\" She tapped the word permanent. \"You can't call it temporary. You can't bargain with it.\"\r\n\r\nJhace's jaw worked.\r\n\r\nHe had bargained with Junction Seven. Told himself the report would matter. That somebody else would fix it. That the city didn't need him to fight every foreman with a schedule board.\r\n\r\nHe looked at his shaking hands.\r\n\r\n\"Stage One,\" he said. Low. \"Tremor.\"\r\n\r\nTiffani nodded and wrote:\r\n\r\nStage One: tremor + filament residue. Functional. Marked.\r\n\r\nShe stared at Functional like it offended her.\r\n\r\nDrew a hard line through it.\r\n\r\nRewrote: Operational.\r\n\r\n\"Functional makes it sound polite,\" she muttered.\r\n\r\nA knock hit the workshop door.\r\n\r\nBram Kerris leaned in, dust on his boots, sweat at his collar, grin already loaded like a bad habit.\r\n\r\n\"Heard you two grabbed Tallow Street by the throat,\" Bram said, trying for funny and getting dust instead. He coughed once. \"City tries to crush you, you crush back. Very… romantic. Very dumb.\"\r\n\r\nJhace didn't look up. \"Who's asking?\"\r\n\r\n\"Crown's asking questions,\" Bram said, and somehow still made it sound obscene. \"Not because they care. Because they hate unknown tools walking around without a leash and a receipt.\"\r\n\r\nTiffani's pen paused. \"What questions.\"\r\n\r\n\"Names. Locations. 'Verified capability.' That sort of foreplay.\" Bram stepped fully inside, then stopped like the heat-grid might charge him rent. \"Infrastructure office. Some administrator with hands so clean it makes you wonder what he's washing off. Loves forms. Loves categories. Loves pretending paper is stronger than stone.\"\r\n\r\nJhace's jaw tightened. \"To do what.\"\r\n\r\nBram spread his hands. \"To decide who gets to own you. Temple will call it divine. Guild will call it labor. Crown will call it law. Everybody wants a piece. Congratulations—you're popular.\"\r\n\r\n\"We don't belong to any of them,\" Tiffani said.\r\n\r\nBram laughed, quick and wrong. \"Adorable. Tell that to a stamp. Try explaining 'we don't belong' while a ceiling is coming down and someone's screaming for a hold. They'll file you under 'non-compliant' and act like that's a weather condition.\"\r\n\r\nJhace's tremor ticked in his fingers. \"So you're here to warn us.\"\r\n\r\n\"I'm here to tell you the door is already being measured,\" Bram said. \"I can buy you a Tide or two. Maybe. After that, they show up with paperwork and smiles and start deciding which parts of you are 'public infrastructure.'\"\r\n\r\nTiffani's mouth went flat. \"Then we build methodology fast.\"\r\n\r\nBram pointed at her slate. \"Yeah. Before they build it for you. Because if the Crown writes your protocol, it's going to read like a job description. And you won't like the benefits package.\"\r\n\r\nJhace breathed in.\r\n\r\nTasted copper-gold dust that still clung to his clothes.\r\n\r\nFelt the city's hum through the floorboards.\r\n\r\n\"Then we build it fast,\" he said. \"Before they decide to build it for us.\"\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n**END CHAPTER 4**\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n*LOCKED — REVISION COMPLETE*"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1768372496.086,
        "end_turn": null,

        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "'CHAPTER 11: HUNGER\\nThe hunger wasn’t metaphor.\\nIt lived behind Jhace’s sternum as a warmth that never fully left after the first hold. A phantom heat that flared whenever the grid‑hum dipped, whenever dust fell, whenever someone screamed the word hold like it was prayer.\\nTiffani called it activation pressure.\\nJhace called it wanting.\\nHe sat beside Daved’s cot in the infirmary again, watching the older man’s hands twitch in sleep.\\nDaved’s eyes opened. He didn’t need a slate this time. His voice was still rough, but it existed.\\n“Feels… good,” Daved rasped.\\nJhace frowned. “What does?”\\n“The start,” Daved said. “Chest heat. When it locks in. For a heartbeat you feel… useful. Strong. Like the city can’t touch you.”\\nJhace’s tremor worsened, like his body agreed.\\n“And then,” Daved continued, eyes '\n"
        },

    "3524b54b-b3fc-4999-9685-5e004837fb72": {
      "children": [
        "bfcd0324-361e-4560-b56a-7cf31ba10e1f"

      "id": "3524b54b-b3fc-4999-9685-5e004837fb72",
      "message": {
        "author": {

          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "1:N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS\rTurning: GLASS (G) — Tide 0 through Tide 21 (Three Anneals post-Piercing)\r---\rCHAPTER 1: THE BELL\rThe bell rang wrong.\rNot the slow warning cadence. Not the measured strike that meant \"check your seals, tighten your brackets.\" This was the frantic peal of something already falling, the sound of a structure begging for a hold.\rJhace was moving before the bell finished its third strike.\rHe grabbed the wrong ring of collar keys on the first yank. Swore, dumped them into his palm, and sorted by feel while he ran. One key skittered off the stair edge and disappeared into the dark.\rHe didn’t go back for it.\r\rHis kit bounced against his hip. Chalk. Slate. Collar keys. Tools for measuring failure, not stopping it. He didn't know why his legs were carrying him toward the sound instead of away. Didn't know why his heart counted the bell strikes like they were orders.\rOne. Two. Three. Four.\rThe doubled harmonic hit him at Junction Twelve.\rIt skipped the pipe-hum entirely and punched straight into his sternum. His ribs buzzed like struck iron. His teeth hurt. His mouth flooded metal.\rCopper.\rSharp and immediate, like he'd bitten through wire.\rHe'd been tasting it for two Tides now. Faint at first. A ghost-flavor that came and went with the grid-hum. But this was different. This flooded his mouth so thick he wanted to spit.\rHe didn't.\rUnderface rule: waste nothing.\rHis hands started shaking.\rThen the shaking cut out for three heartbeats and he thought, stupidly, his fingers had finally died.\rAnd then the tremor came back twice as fast.\r\rIt wasn't cold or exertion. It was a fine vibration that made his grip feel unreliable, like his muscles were half-taking orders and half-refusing them.\rHe jammed them into his coat pockets and kept running.\rHe forced his breath to slow. Counted to ten without looking at the numbers.\rThe tremor kept going.\rHe pulled one hand out. Squeezed it into a fist until the knuckles burned. Released.\rStill shaking.\rIt wasn't aftershock. It was something settling into him—fine vibration under the skin—the kind that makes you drop a tool and swear at your own hand.\rHe’d seen it on old manifestors in the deep tunnels. Hands that never found stillness. He’d called it age.\rNow he understood, because his fingers kept twitching against his thigh like a bad motor that wouldn't shut off.\rTallow Street came into view through the access tunnel: upper terrace facade folded inward, stone slabs riding down on support beams that should have held twice that load. Dust cloud thick enough to choke on. People screaming from inside the collapse. Someone rang a hand-bell hard enough to crack its cadence.\rEmergency. Emergency. Emergency.\rA foreman waved at him, face streaked with ash. \"We need a hold. We don't have time.\"\rJhace looked at the structure.\rThe failure line wasn't clean. The whole building was trying to become rubble, one breath at a time. Load shifts rippled through it like a living animal struggling to die. Stone groaned. Iron squealed.\rNo crew could wedge that fast. The wedge bucket was still half-full. Someone was still tying their apron when the stone started to go.\rThe people inside didn't have minutes.\r\"I'm not—\" Jhace started.\rThe heat arrived.\rNot external. Internal. A sudden furnace-flare behind his sternum that made him gasp. Like someone had lit a coal bed under his ribs and told it to burn clean.\rCopper-gold dust swirled around his boots.\rHe watched it climb his legs like static. Like it was looking for him.\rHis chest burned hotter.\rThe doubled harmonic snapped into alignment inside him—two mis-timed gears finally meshing. The wrongness in the grid-hum became rightness in his blood. His heartbeat and the city's failing pulse locked into the same rhythm.\rHe didn't know what he was doing.\rHe didn't know why his feet carried him under the broken lintel.\rHe moved because the archway dust was already falling in steady sheets and somebody inside was screaming a name he couldn't ignore.\rHe put both hands on the main support column.\rLight—thin, copper-gold, filament-fine—threaded from his palms into the stone seam.\rThe building stopped moving.\rIt was only paused—still wrong, still dangerous.\rJhace's breath shook out of him. He could feel every shift in load through his fingertips: the weight of stone, the tension in iron, the panic-motion of bodies inside. The heat in his chest flared with each micro-adjustment, like his body was paying for every heartbeat of stability in increments he couldn't count.\r\"Move,\" he rasped. \"Get them out.\"\rBoots ran. Voices shouted coordinates. Someone coughed dust and kept crawling.\rA woman slid into his peripheral vision—practical coat, hair tied back, slate already in hand despite the chaos.\rShe didn't look at him like a miracle.\rShe looked at him like a system that could fail.\r\"How long can you hold?\" she demanded.\rJhace's jaw clenched. The heat in his chest spiked. \"I don't know.\"\r\"Then we find out.\" She stepped into the dust, coughed once, and shouted to the evac crew. \"Clear corridor A first. Count bodies out. No guessing.\"\rJhace tried to turn his head. The moment he shifted his attention, the heat flared sharper—punishment for looking away.\r\"I have to watch the load,\" he said through his teeth.\r\"Then watch it,\" she snapped, already marking a line on her slate. \"I'll watch you.\"\rPeople came out in bursts: three, then five, then a cluster carrying an older man whose leg bent wrong. Each time the load shifted, Jhace's hands tightened and the filament-light brightened in response.\rHis heartbeat counted itself in his ears.\rOne hundred.\rOne hundred twenty.\rHis arms started to tremble.\rNot from fatigue. From something deeper. A fine vibration that spread into his forearms, up to his shoulders, making his grip feel like it belonged to someone else.\r\"Hold steady,\" the woman said, quieter now, close enough he could hear her through the dust.\rHe didn't answer. Answering took breath.\rAt two hundred heartbeats, the tremor spread into his chest. At two-twenty, his vision narrowed at the edges, copper-gold sparks flickering across it like welding flash.\r\"How many?\" he rasped.\rThe woman didn't look up from the evac line. \"Ten. Eleven. Twelve—\"\rA scream from inside. A child. High and thin and suddenly cut off.\rJhace's hands shook harder.\r\"No,\" he hissed, and the filament-light surged, burning his palms numb.\r\"Go,\" the woman shouted toward the breach. \"Last sweep. Now.\"\rA rescuer crawled under the paused beam line and dragged a small body out by the shoulders. The child coughed dust and cried like she'd been holding it in for hours.\rThe woman's voice cut clean through the chaos. \"Twelve out. Corridor clear.\"\rJhace felt the building try to move again the instant she said it, like it had been waiting for permission.\r\"Terminate,\" she said. No softness in it. Not cruelty. Protocol.\rJhace let the filament go.\rThe building settled the last inch it had been denied. Stone grinding. Iron squealing. But the void where bodies had been was now empty.\rHe staggered back into open air.\rHis hands wouldn't stop shaking.\rThe tremor was small—millimeters—but it was relentless. Like a ghost living inside his muscles, counting the dead he'd prevented and refusing to be quiet about the cost.\rThe woman stepped beside him, dust streaked across her face. \"Name.\"\r\"Jhace Torrins.\"\rShe wrote it without looking, chalk skating on sweat. \"Duration: two-forty—\" The chalk snapped. She swore under her breath and wrote it anyway.\r\"Don't,\" he snapped. Too hard. Too fast.\rShe met his eyes. Her gaze was flat, measuring, absolutely certain.\r\"If we don't measure it,\" she said, \"we kill you next time.\"\rBetween bells, they made it back to the workshop long enough to rinse dust off skin and reset slates.\rJhace sat at his bench and waited for the chest heat to fade.\rIt didn’t. Not fully.\rA banked warmth stayed behind his sternum—steady as a coal bed that refused to go cold. He pressed his palm to his chest. Felt nothing through shirt and ribs.\rInside, the warmth answered anyway. Once. Twice.\rLike the world remembering how it had fit together when he held.\rHis hand lingered there a heartbeat too long. Not thinking. Just reaching.\rHe pulled it away like it burned. The tremor kept moving, but for one heartbeat—only one—the shake eased, like his body preferred the burn to the break.\rThat was the first time the hunger felt like a vote.\rJhace’s hands shook between them like evidence he hadn't wanted.\rHe swallowed copper. Tasted grit. Looked at the child being carried past them on a makeshift stretcher—alive, crying, covered in dust but breathing.\rHe nodded once.\r\"Measure,\" he said. \"Then make sure there isn't a next time.\"\rThe woman's slate scratched.\rShe wrote: Jhace Torrins. Inspector. First manifestation. Tallow Street. Tide 0.\rBehind them, the bell rang again.\rDifferent street. Same dust in his teeth. Same bell tearing its own throat.\rJhace looked down and realized he was still gripping air.\rCopper sat on his tongue. He went anyway.\r---\rCHAPTER 2: THE PATTERN\rThe theodolite wouldn't settle.\rTiffani had calibrated it three times in the last hour. Clean lens. Level base. Reference stars locked. Everything aligned the way it had been for twenty-six Turns of lens work.\rThe crosshair still drifted.\rNo shimmer. No warp. Just the star sitting wrong in the frame—like someone had nudged the sky with a thumb while she wasn't looking.\rShe pressed her palm to the primary lens frame.\rVibration.\rFaint. Constant. The doubled harmonic lived under the normal hum like a second heartbeat. It made her molars ache and her lens hand tremble.\rShe pulled her hand away and wrote on her slate:\rTide 0 — Kindling  \rLens frame vibration: doubled harmonic present.  \rStar Seven-Three displacement: 0.34 degrees west.  \rNeither atmosphere nor mechanism.\rHer mouth tasted copper.\rShe'd been ignoring it for the last two Tides. Faint metallic tang that came and went with air currents from the ventilation shafts. But this was different. This[... ELLIPSIZATION ...] DEPLOYMENT PROCEDURE. “He will read what he wants to read. I’m controlling the surface.”\rBram exhaled through his nose. “Ah. Compliance theater with a trapdoor.”\r“There is no trapdoor,” she said. “There are exits. Distributed.”\rShe pushed the second stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: Property is not a priority class. Operator veto. Refusal authority. No deployment without independent verification.\rBram whistled. “This one’s the ‘burn the Crown down’ packet.”\r“This one is the ‘keep operators alive long enough to matter’ packet.” She slid it closer. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.”\rBram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.”\r“They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.”\rBram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.”\rTiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.”\rChloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers.\r“Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.”\rBram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.”\rChloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?”\r“Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.”\rTiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage.\rChloen read the first line.\rYOU CAN SAY NO.\rShe looked up. “That’s new.”\r“It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.”\rChloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.”\rAnd then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD.\rChloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.”\rBram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.”\rTiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.”\rChloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it.\rThe door clicked. The room held its breath.\rCorran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen.\r“For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.”\rBram’s grin came instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.”\rCorran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.”\rTiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult.\rCorran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.”\rTiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Then it will be accurate.”\rCorran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi?”\rTiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm.\r“Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in daylight.”\rBram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. That’s adorable.”\r“It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.”\rCorran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?”\r“Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.”\rCorran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.”\rHe left.\rBram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. “So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine.”\r“He gets safety with ownership language,” Tiffani corrected. “He will build a machine either way. I’m deciding where it jams.”\rBram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?”\rTiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.”\rShe lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred.\r“Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge.\rTiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission.\rBram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.”\r“I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said.\rOutside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they'd been caught stealing.\rTiffani didn’t move.\rShe dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.\r\r---\rCODA: GLASS IN THE HAND\rJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them.\rHe’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste was still there anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you can't spit out.\rStone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn't reach. His right hand twitching on its own schedule.\rSomeone had posted a guard outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive.\rJhace tried to sleep.\rEvery time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn't tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city's new baseline living in him.\rHe flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking.\rThen it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place.\rJhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed.\r“I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest.\rOutside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short.\rJhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.\r\r---\rCODA: THE SHARD\rJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them.\rStone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn't lift. A slate he couldn't reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted time like it was a job.\rA guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who'd been chewing dust since Kindling.\rThen the breathing stopped.\rNo footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint.\rThe door opened anyway.\rLight entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape.\rA figure stepped in.\rNo perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive.\rThey didn't speak.\rThey moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel.\rA hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact.\rJhace tried to jerk away.\rNothing.\rHe tried to curl his right hand into a fist.\rNothing.\rThe figure took that hand anyway.\rBoth of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage.\rSomething cold settled into the center of his palm.\rA shard.\rCopper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin.\rCold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache.\rThen heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for.\rCopper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit.\rLight threaded up from his palm.\rNot a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path.\rIt slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal.\rJhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body.\rBut he felt the line.\rHe felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay.\rHis lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision.\rThe figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact.\rThen they stepped back.\rNo check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood.\rThe door shut without sound.\rThe guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed.\rJhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close.\rThe tremor was gone.\rIn its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise.\rSomewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.\r\r---\rEND N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS\r---\n"
        },
        "create_time": 1768370871.482062,
        "end_turn": null,
