Each time the load shifted, Jhace's hands tightened and the filament-light brightened in response.\rHis heartbeat counted itself in his ears.\rOne hundred.\rOne hundred twenty.\rHis arms started to tremble.\rNot from fatigue. From something deeper. A fine vibration that spread into his forearms, up to his shoulders, making his grip feel like it belonged to someone else.\r\"Hold steady,\" the woman said, quieter now, close enough he could hear her through the dust.\rHe didn't answer. Answering took breath.\rAt two hundred heartbeats, the tremor spread into his chest. At two-twenty, his vision narrowed at the edges, copper-gold sparks flickering across it like welding flash.\r\"How many?\" he rasped.\rThe woman didn't look up from the evac line. \"Ten. Eleven. Twelve—\"\rA scream from inside. A child. High and thin and suddenly cut off.\rJhace's hands shook harder.\r\"No,\" he hissed, and the filament-light surged, burning his palms numb.\r\"Go,\" the woman shouted toward the breach. \"Last sweep. Now.\"\rA rescuer crawled under the paused beam line and dragged a small body out by the shoulders. The child coughed dust and cried like she'd been holding it in for hours.\rThe woman's voice cut clean through the chaos. \"Twelve out. Corridor clear.\"\rJhace felt the building try to move again the instant she said it, like it had been waiting for permission.\r\"Terminate,\" she said. No softness in it. Not cruelty. Protocol.\rJhace let the filament go.\rThe building settled the last inch it had been denied. Stone grinding. Iron squealing. But the void where bodies had been was now empty.\rHe staggered back into open air.\rHis hands wouldn't stop shaking.\rThe tremor was small—millimeters—but it was relentless. Like a ghost living inside his muscles, counting the dead he'd prevented and refusing to be quiet about the cost.\rThe woman stepped beside him, dust streaked across her face. \"Name.\"\r\"Jhace Torrins.\"\rShe wrote it without looking, chalk skating on sweat. \"Duration: two-forty—\" The chalk snapped. She swore under her breath and wrote it anyway.\r\"Don't,\" he snapped. Too hard. Too fast.\rShe met his eyes. Her gaze was flat, measuring, absolutely certain.\r\"If we don't measure it,\" she said, \"we kill you next time.\"\rBetween bells, they made it back to the workshop long enough to rinse dust off skin and reset slates.\rJhace sat at his bench and waited for the chest heat to fade.\rIt didn’t. Not fully.\rA banked warmth stayed behind his sternum—steady as a coal bed that refused to go cold. He pressed his palm to his chest. Felt nothing through shirt and ribs.\rInside, the warmth answered anyway. Once. Twice.\rLike the world remembering how it had fit together when he held.\rHis hand lingered there a heartbeat too long. Not thinking. Just reaching.\rHe pulled it away like it burned. The tremor kept moving, but for one heartbeat—only one—the shake eased, like his body preferred the burn to the break.\rThat was the first time the hunger felt like a vote.\rJhace’s hands shook between them like evidence he hadn't wanted.\rHe swallowed copper. Tasted grit. Looked at the child being carried past them on a makeshift stretcher—alive, crying, covered in dust but breathing.\rHe nodded once.\r\"Measure,\" he said. \"Then make sure there isn't a next time.\"\rThe woman's slate scratched.\rShe wrote: Jhace Torrins. Inspector. First manifestation. Tallow Street. Tide 0.\rBehind them, the bell rang again.\rDifferent street. Same dust in his teeth. Same bell tearing its own throat.\rJhace looked down and realized he was still gripping air.\rCopper sat on his tongue. He went anyway.\r---\rCHAPTER 2: THE PATTERN\rThe theodolite wouldn't settle.\rTiffani had calibrated it three times in the last hour. Clean lens. Level base. Reference stars locked. Everything aligned the way it had been for twenty-six Turns of lens work.\rThe crosshair still drifted.\rNo shimmer. No warp. Just the star sitting wrong in the frame—like someone had nudged the sky with a thumb while she wasn't looking.\rShe pressed her palm to the primary lens frame.\rVibration.\rFaint. Constant. The doubled harmonic lived under the normal hum like a second heartbeat. It made her molars ache and her lens hand tremble.\rShe pulled her hand away and wrote on her slate:\rTide 0 — Kindling  \rLens frame vibration: doubled harmonic present.  \rStar Seven-Three displacement: 0.34 degrees west.  \rNeither atmosphere nor mechanism.\rHer mouth tasted copper.\rShe'd been ignoring it for the last two Tides. Faint metallic tang that came and went with air currents from the ventilation shafts. But this was different. This[... ELLIPSIZATION ...] DEPLOYMENT PROCEDURE. “He will read what he wants to read. I’m controlling the surface.”\rBram exhaled through his nose. “Ah. Compliance theater with a trapdoor.”\r“There is no trapdoor,” she said. “There are exits. Distributed.”\rShe pushed the second stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: Property is not a priority class. Operator veto. Refusal authority. No deployment without independent verification.\rBram whistled. “This one’s the ‘burn the Crown down’ packet.”\r“This one is the ‘keep operators alive long enough to matter’ packet.” She slid it closer. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.”\rBram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.”\r“They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.”\rBram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.”\rTiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.”\rChloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers.\r“Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.”\rBram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.”\rChloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?”\r“Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.”\rTiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage.\rChloen read the first line.\rYOU CAN SAY NO.\rShe looked up. “That’s new.”\r“It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.”\rChloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.”\rAnd then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD.\rChloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.”\rBram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.”\rTiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.”\rChloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it.\rThe door clicked. The room held its breath.\rCorran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen.\r“For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.”\rBram’s grin came instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.”\rCorran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.”\rTiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult.\rCorran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.”\rTiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Then it will be accurate.”\rCorran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi?”\rTiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm.\r“Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in daylight.”\rBram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. That’s adorable.”\r“It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.”\rCorran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?”\r“Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.”\rCorran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.”\rHe left.\rBram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. “So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine.”\r“He gets safety with ownership language,” Tiffani corrected. “He will build a machine either way. I’m deciding where it jams.”\rBram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?”\rTiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.”\rShe lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred.\r“Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge.\rTiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission.\rBram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.”\r“I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said.\rOutside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they'd been caught stealing.\rTiffani didn’t move.\rShe dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.\r\r---\rCODA: GLASS IN THE HAND\rJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them.\rHe’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste was still there anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you can't spit out.\rStone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn't reach. His right hand twitching on its own schedule.\rSomeone had posted a guard outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive.\rJhace tried to sleep.\rEvery time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn't tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city's new baseline living in him.\rHe flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking.\rThen it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place.\rJhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed.\r“I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest.\rOutside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short.\rJhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.\r\r---\rCODA: THE SHARD\rJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them.\rStone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn't lift. A slate he couldn't reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted time like it was a job.\rA guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who'd been chewing dust since Kindling.\rThen the breathing stopped.\rNo footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint.\rThe door opened anyway.\rLight entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape.\rA figure stepped in.\rNo perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive.\rThey didn't speak.\rThey moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel.\rA hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact.\rJhace tried to jerk away.\rNothing.\rHe tried to curl his right hand into a fist.\rNothing.\rThe figure took that hand anyway.\rBoth of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage.\rSomething cold settled into the center of his palm.\rA shard.\rCopper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin.\rCold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache.\rThen heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for.\rCopper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit.\rLight threaded up from his palm.\rNot a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path.\rIt slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal.\rJhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body.\rBut he felt the line.\rHe felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay.\rHis lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision.\rThe figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact.\rThen they stepped back.\rNo check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood.\rThe door shut without sound.\rThe guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed.\rJhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close.\rThe tremor was gone.\rIn its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise.\rSomewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.\r\r---\rEND N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS\r---\n",

      "message": {
        "author": {

          "language": "unknown",

          "text": "for phrase in [\"ROUTE FAILURE\",\"THREE DEAD\",\"Witness\",\"Brother Corran\",\"QUENCH PHASE\",\"FINAL ASSESSMENT\"]:\n    idx=ch5.lower().find(phrase.lower())\n    print(phrase, idx)\n"
        },

    "375a9ceb-0c64-4f40-873c-003d3e370029": {
      "children": [
        "0e39532c-dac5-4090-bf23-5e6a44e17567"
      ],
      "id": "375a9ceb-0c64-4f40-873c-003d3e370029",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": "final",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "## CHAPTER 13: THE LEDGER — REVISED (REPEATS TRIMMED)\n\nTiffani wrote the first copy with her left hand because her right knee refused to lock and her right hand refused to stay still.\n\nIt wasn't Stage. It was the kind of fatigue that lives in joints after bad work—the kind you carry until your hands start lying to you.\n\nThe ink pooled anyway. Copper-gold dust clung to the nib, turning every line slightly wrong, as if the city itself wanted the record to smear.\n\nShe made three piles.\n\nFour, if she was honest.\n\nOne stack sat in clean paper with clean margins and clean language. Compliance phrasing. Definitions that sounded like protection while leaving room for ownership.\n\nShe wrote \"deployment authority\" first. Crossed it out. Rewrote: \"deployment procedure.\"\n\nSurface control. Let Corlexi read what he wanted to see.\n\nOne stack was tidy and dead. The kind of language that looks safe until somebody has to bleed under it.\n\nOne stack was short enough to memorize. Underface cadence. Commands. Counts. No soft words, no long sentences—something you could carry in your head when paper burned or got confiscated.\n\nThe last pile wasn't for anyone. It was her draft. Her private notes. The parts she could not justify on a form and would not confess out loud.\n\nBram stood in the doorway in his Crown coat like he'd stolen it off a corpse and decided it suited him.\n\nHis badge caught lamplight. Clean metal. Official weight.\n\n\"You look like a priest,\" Tiffani said without looking up.\n\nBram glanced down at the coat. \"Don't you dare put that evil on me. Priests get paid to lie. I'm doing it recreationally.\"\n\n\"You're late.\"\n\n\"Yeah. Turns out paperwork travels faster than rescue.\" He leaned in, saw the piles. \"Oh good. Homework. My favorite form of punishment.\"\n\nTiffani slid the clean stack toward him first. \"This goes to Corlexi.\"\n\nBram's fingers hovered over the top page. \"This the 'please don't leash us' letter?\"\n\n\"This is the 'you get what I give you' packet,\" she said, tapping the pages hard enough to leave a smudge. Ink on her thumb now. Copper-tinted. \"Limits. Spotters. A way out when the body says no.\"\n\nBram squinted at the title line. \"And the part where we can tell them to go fuck themselves?\"\n\nTiffani didn't blink. \"Not included.\"\n\nBram's smile went sharp. \"That's either genius or suicidal.\"\n\n\"It's survivable,\" she said. \"He will read what he wants to read. I'm controlling the surface.\"\n\nBram exhaled through his nose. \"Compliance theater.\"\n\n\"There are exits,\" she said. \"Distributed.\"\n\nShe pushed the second stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: *Property is not a priority class.* *Operator veto.* *Refusal authority.* *No deployment without independent verification.*\n\nHer knee throbbed where it refused to straighten. She shifted weight without comment.\n\nBram whistled low. \"This one's the 'burn the Crown down' packet.\"\n\n\"This one is the 'keep operators alive long enough to matter' packet.\" She slid it closer. \"Guild custody. You're the custodian.\"\n\nBram stared at the words *operator refusal authority* like they were a weapon and a confession. \"They catch me with this, I'm a corpse with a promotion.\"\n\n\"They catch you without it, you're already dead,\" Tiffani said. \"Just slower. With a better title.\"\n\nBram laughed, too loud for the room. \"God, I hate when you're right. It's like being corrected by a scalpel.\"\n\nTiffani's pen scratched again. \"Underface next.\"\n\nHer right hand cramped. She switched the pen to her left without pausing.\n\nChloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. She didn't look at the bed. She didn't look at Jhace. She looked at the papers.\n\n\"Crown's sniffing,\" Chloen said. \"Foremen too. They heard there's a registry. Means there's a list. Means there's a leash.\"\n\nBram lifted a hand. \"Hi. I'm the leash's embarrassing middleman.\"\n\nChloen's mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn't trust. \"You always this loud?\"\n\n\"Only when I'm terrified,\" Bram said. \"So yes.\"\n\nTiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage.\n\nChloen read the first line.\n\n**YOU CAN SAY NO.**\n\nShe looked up. \"That's new.\"\n\n\"It's old,\" Tiffani said. \"It just wasn't written down.\"\n\nChloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. \"Wedges first.\" \"Hold only what's needed.\" \"Terminate on signal.\" \"If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.\"\n\nAnd then, again: **YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD.**\n\nChloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. \"Good. Because schedules don't give a damn about ribs.\"\n\nBram pointed at the underface strip. \"See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.\"\n\nTiffani didn't look at him. \"Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.\"\n\nChloen nodded once and left like she'd been given a weapon and didn't want to be seen holding it.\n\nThe door clicked. The room held its breath.\n\nCorran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he'd been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen.\n\n\"For the record,\" he said, voice formal out of habit, \"I'm not here.\"\n\nBram's grin came instantly. \"For the record, I'm a virgin.\"\n\nCorran ignored him and held out a thin binder. \"They've started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.\"\n\nTiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult.\n\nHer handwriting shifted mid-page. Legible. Uneven.\n\nCorran thumbed the top page. \"This will get you killed.\"\n\nTiffani's pen didn't stop. \"Then it will be accurate.\"\n\nCorran's mouth tightened. \"What did you withhold from Corlexi?\"\n\nTiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm.\n\n\"Consent,\" she said. \"The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don't give it, they have to steal it in daylight.\"\n\nBram snorted. \"She's making them commit crimes on paper. That's adorable.\"\n\n\"It's leverage,\" Tiffani said. \"The kind that stains.\"\n\nCorran tucked the packet into his binder. \"Where does this go?\"\n\n\"Somewhere it can't be conveniently lost,\" Tiffani said. \"Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.\"\n\nCorran nodded once. \"For the record, I didn't take that.\"\n\nHe left.\n\nBram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden.\n\n\"So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine,\" he said.\n\n\"He gets safety with ownership language,\" Tiffani corrected. \"He will build a machine either way. I'm deciding where it jams.\"\n\nBram looked at the clean packet again. \"You sure this doesn't just get people hurt?\"\n\nTiffani's jaw tightened. Her knee locked finally—sharp, sudden—then released. She didn't log the timing.\n\n\"People are already hurt,\" she said. \"Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.\"\n\nShe lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn't for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred.\n\nBram’s voice dropped. \"Not this one.\"\n\nTiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal.\n\nThe paper caught fast. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission.\n\nBram watched the burn. \"You just set fire to your own confession.\"\n\n\"I set fire to a target,\" Tiffani said.\n\nOutside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking.\n\nTiffani didn't move.\n\nShe dipped the pen again.\n\nHer right hand this time. The cramp had passed.\n\nShe wrote the next copy anyway."
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1768418276.027722,
        "end_turn": true,
        "id": "375a9ceb-0c64-4f40-873c-003d3e370029",

          "thinking_effort": "standard",
          "turn_exchange_id": "da854bef-6c2b-4de5-83ed-ce554dd9a5e4"

    "377b1e15-f88c-4e45-854a-010e65c5de50": {
      "children": [

        "metadata": {
          "aggregate_result": {
            "code": "# extract key events by searching within chapters\ndef find_in_chapter(chap_num, pattern):\n    chap = next(c for c in chap_data if c[0]==chap_num)[2]\n    return [(m.start(), chap[m.start()-60:m.start()+200]) for m in re.finditer(pattern, chap)]\nfor pat in [\"three dead\",\"Casualties\",\"registry\",\"Corlexi\",\"Tallec\",\"Senne\",\"Corven\",\"Daved\",\"hold locked\",\"Stage Three\",\"license\",\"Shard\"]:\n    hits=find_in_chapter(7, pat)\n    if hits:\n        print(\"Chapter7\",pat,len(hits))\n",

    "3ab3ee23-24ce-45c7-8579-48af098ebac9": {
      "children": [
        "e17619c6-b6f2-45aa-8c04-0dffce61f705"
      ],
      "id": "3ab3ee23-24ce-45c7-8579-48af098ebac9",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "user"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "CRITICAL CANON DISCOVERY\n\nBASE MANUSCRIPT STRUCTURE:\nCHAPTER 11: HUNGER\n\nInfirmary scene with Daved (dialogue about addiction)\nEnds with: \"He stood, hands shaking, and went to the training yard because refusal would make them criminals.\"\nTraining yard is MENTIONED but NOT SHOWN\nFinal line: \"Jhace pretended he didn't hear. He did.\"\n\nCHAPTER 12: METHOD\n\nNOT training yard at all!\nTide 21 residential stairwell emergency\nChild trapped under fallen slab\nJhace locks into Stage Four: Complete paralysis\nTiffani documents Stage Four\nEnds with \"A method... with constraints\"\n\n\nWHAT HAPPENED:\nGrok's expanded Chapter 11:\n\n✅ Included correct infirmary scene\n✗ ADDED training yard scene that doesn't exist in base manuscript\n\n\"Chapter 12: TRAINING YARD — VARIANT A\":\n\nThis appears to be invented content, not base manuscript\nBase manuscript Chapter 12 is the Stage Four paralysis scene, not training yard\n\n\nVERDICT\nThe training yard content is NOT CANON.\nBase manuscript mentions they go to training yard at end of Chapter 11, but never shows it. The story jumps directly to Chapter 12 (METHOD) - the Stage Four paralysis scene.\nRECOMMENDATION:\nLock original GROK Chapter 11 (infirmary scene only, ending with \"Jhace pretended he didn't hear. He did.\")\nDiscard training yard additions - they're not in base manuscript.\nChapter 12 should be METHOD (Stage Four paralysis scene), not training yard.\nCorrect?why did they both provide a training yard section?12:37 PMIdentified narrative gap both AIs independently filled without base manuscript support.The user is asking why both Grok and ChatGPT provided training yard sections when it's not in the base manuscript.\nLet me think about this:\n\nAt the end of base manuscript Chapter 11, it says: \"He stood, hands shaking, and went to the training yard because refusal would make them criminals. Tiffani followed, slate in hand, limping slightly from too many hours on stone floors.\"\nThis creates a narrative expectation - \"they went to the training yard\" - but then base manuscript DOESN'T show the training yard scene. It jumps to Chapter 12: METHOD which is the Tide 21 stairwell collapse/Stage Four paralysis.\nBoth AI models likely saw this as a narrative gap or unfulfilled promise and tried to \"complete\" the scene by showing what happens at the training yard.\nThis is a case where the AIs are \"filling in\" what they perceive as missing content, rather than respecting that the base manuscript deliberately doesn't show that scene.\nThe user needs to understand:\n\nThe mention of \"training yard\" at end of Ch 11 is a transition/time marker\nBase manuscript intentionally doesn't show that scene\nBoth AIs independently invented the same content to \"complete\" what they saw as incomplete\nThis is why checking base manuscript is critical\n\n\n\nThis is actually a really interesting case of AI behavior - they both made the same \"logical\" mistake of filling in what seemed like a narrative gap.EXCELLENT QUESTION - Here's Why:\n\nTHE NARRATIVE GAP TRAP\nEnd of Base Manuscript Chapter 11 says:\n\n\"He stood, hands shaking, and went to the training yard because refusal would make them criminals. Tiffani followed, slate in hand...\"\n\nBoth AI models saw this and thought:\n\n\"Oh, they're going to the training yard\"\n\"The chapter mentions it, so it must need to be shown\"\n\"This is a setup that needs payoff\"\n\nBut Base Manuscript does NOT show training yard. It jumps directly to Chapter 12: METHOD (the Tide 21 stairwell/Stage Four paralysis scene).\n\nWHY BOTH AIs MADE THE SAME \"MISTAKE\"\nThis is a Chekhov's Gun situation in reverse:\n\nNormal storytelling logic: If you mention they're going somewhere, you show what happens there\nBase manuscript breaks this expectation: Mentions training yard, then skips it entirely\nBoth AIs filled the \"gap\": They independently invented training yard content because it seemed like missing narrative material\n\nThis is exactly why we verify against base manuscript - both models made the same \"logical\" addition that violates actual canon.\n\nWHAT THIS MEANS\nThe base manuscript:\n\nUses \"went to the training yard\" as a time marker/transition\nDeliberately doesn't show that scene\nJumps to the next crisis (Chapter 12)\n\nBoth AIs \"helpfully\" filled what they perceived as incomplete, but the incompleteness was intentional.\n\nShould we proceed with locking original Grok Chapter 11 (infirmary only, no training yard)?"
          ]
        },

          "text": "idx 21272\nCHAPTER 4: PROTOCOLJhace’s workshop was a box of iron and heat tucked into the m\n---context---\n His legs shook. His hands wouldn't stop trembling. But he stood.\"Good,\" Tiffani said. \"Because we're going to the next one. And I'm counting every heartbeat.\"She walked toward the sound of the bell.Jhace followed.Tiffani wiped chalk off her thumb. It smeared into the sweat.The bell kept ringing.---CHAPTER 4: PROTOCOLJhace’s workshop was a box of iron and heat tucked into the mid-terrace, where you could hear the grid-hum without being crushed by it.Tiffani sat on an overturned crate with her slate on her knees, left leg extended slightly to ease the knee that had started locking after long stands on stone floors. She didn’t rest her back against anything. Always ready to move. Always ready to write.Tiffani shifted her weight off her left leg without comment, like it was just another measurement she refused to log.Jhace stood at his bench, trying to thread a bolt into a collar bracket.His hands shook.The grid-hum didn't break with a bang. It stuttered—missing beats, catching up—like an old machine slipping teeth.He tried again. The bolt dropped. Metal on stone.He stared at it like it had betrayed him.Tiffani didn’t look up. His left hand tried to wipe sweat off his mouth and only smeared dust across his lip. He tasted grit. He hated the fact he noticed it. “Your tremor amplitude increased during exertion.”“Because I’m exerting,” Jhace muttered, bending to pick up the bolt. His fingertips felt numb where the filament-light had burned them. Not pain. Absence.“Because your body \n"
        },

        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "'to do with his hands. Shaking, useless things. He folded them behind his back like that would fool anybody.\\n\\nDaved wrote: One body is not a small thing\\n“Correct,” Tiffani said. “It is not small. It is also not reversible.”\\nBram wandered in like a bad idea that knew your schedule. “This room smells like piss and heroism. Worst cologne.”\\n“Leave,” Tiffani said, without looking up, “if you can’t behave.”\\n“I’m behaving,” Bram said. “I’m just… shaped wrong.”\\nDaved wrote: You okay\\n“Define operational parameters for ‘okay.’”\\nNot irrelevant\\n“Compared to your autonomic failure, my current symptoms are statistically minor.”\\nStill matters\\nTiffani’s pen stopped.\\n“I can’t get the copper taste out of my mouth anymore,” she said quietly. “Food tastes wrong. Water tastes wrong. I think it’s in my teeth.”\\nJhace started, “Tiff—”\\n“That’s not a Stage marker,” she cut in. “So it doesn’t get logged.”\\nDaved wrote smaller this time: Don’t let them call this holy\\n“I won’t.”\\n“Nobody’s calling this holy,” Bram said. “They’re calling it ‘resource allocation.’ Much sexier.”\\n“Bram.”\\n“I know,” he said. “I’m disgusting. It’s my brand.”\\n\\nJhace didn’t know if that was a promise to Daved or a threat to the world.\\n---'"
        },

    "3ce3e7a3-70c7-42f2-a01d-66e6c1a4e8ad": {
      "children": [
        "8c2101ea-16fa-44c7-9335-b9b48db299d8"
      ],
      "id": "3ce3e7a3-70c7-42f2-a01d-66e6c1a4e8ad",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "python",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "20 Bram’s smile went sharp. “That’s either genius or suicidal.”\n21 “It’s survivable.” She turned the next page over and wrote a title line in the margin: DEPLOYMENT AUTHORITY. Then she crossed it out and rewrote: DEPLOYMENT PROCEDURE. “He will read what he wants to read. I’m controlling the surface.”\n22 Bram exhaled through his nose. “Ah. Compliance theater with a trapdoor.”\n23 “There is no trapdoor,” she said. “There are exits. Distributed.”\n24 She pushed the second stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: Property is not a priority class. Operator veto. Refusal authority. No deployment without independent verification.\n25 Bram whistled. “This one’s the ‘burn the Crown down’ packet.”\n26 “This one is the ‘keep operators alive long enough to matter’ packet.” She slid it closer. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.”\n27 Bram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.”\n28 “They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.”\n29 Bram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.”\n30 Tiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.”\n31 Chloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers.\n32 “Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.”\n33 Bram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.”\n34 Chloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?”\n35 “Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.”\n36 Tiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage.\n37 Chloen read the first line.\n38 YOU CAN SAY NO.\n39 She looked up. “That’s new.”\n40 “It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.”\n41 Chloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.”\n42 And then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD.\n43 Chloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.”\n44 Bram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.”\n45 Tiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.”\n46 Chloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it.\n47 The door clicked. The room held its breath.\n48 Corran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen.\n49 “For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.”\n50 Bram’s grin came instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.”\n51 Corran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.”\n52 Tiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult.\n53 Corran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.”\n54 Tiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Then it will be accurate.”\n55 Corran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi?”\n56 Tiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm.\n57 “Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in daylight.”\n58 Bram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. That’s adorable.”\n59 “It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.”\n"
        },
        "create_time": 1768417213.0477839,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "3ce3e7a3-70c7-42f2-a01d-66e6c1a4e8ad",

    "3d84439e-558b-4dc1-9ddc-ebb4f3e56d51": {
      "children": [
        "c3d56e34-1198-44d8-9506-ed4025e9595f"
      ],
      "id": "3d84439e-558b-4dc1-9ddc-ebb4f3e56d51",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": "final",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "# N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS  \n## CHAPTER 7: MERCHANT HALL — VARIANT A\n\n---\n\nMerchant Hall was a cathedral for trade: iron ribs, stone spine, glass skylights that made everything inside look clean even when it wasn’t.\n\nAnd it smelled like bodies and money and fear—the kind of fear that tries to wear perfume.\n\nIt started failing at mid-Tide.\n\nThe bell sequence came sharp and frantic—no warning cadence, no time to wedge first. The Hall had been packed when the terrace shifted again.\n\nFour hundred inside, Bram said. Maybe more.\n\nTiffani arrived before Jhace.\n\nWhen he pushed through the crowd, she was already on the steps, slate in hand, shouting like a foreman who’d learned math.\n\n“Operators on me. Two per span. Spotters assigned. No hero holds.”\n\n“Duration limit. One-eighty heartbeats maximum. You terminate on my signal. No argument.”\n\nMerra shoved through the dust with a slate clutched to her chest. Ash streaked her hair like she’d tried to comb through a fire.\n\n“We have a problem,” Merra said. “Southern stairwell collapsed. Fifty people trapped on upper floors. Can’t reach main exits.”\n\n“Time estimate,” Tiffani said. “Low and high.”\n\n“Unknown. Debris blocking secondary stairs. Could be two hundred heartbeats. Could be six hundred.”\n\n“Then we don’t gamble.” Tiffani didn’t raise her voice. She didn’t have to. “Clear lanes. Count bodies out. You want prayer, pray while you walk.”\n\nShe swung her slate toward the operators.\n\n“Corven—baseline check. When chest heat starts, report it. Don’t interpret. Report.”\n\nCorven swallowed. “If it hits, I tell you. If I taste copper, I tell you.”\n\nJhace didn’t look at him. “Don’t spiral. Do the minimum.”\n\n“Minimum,” Corven echoed, like repetition could make it true.\n\n“Senne—status.”\n\n“Getting hollow,” Senne said. “Chest feels empty. Light’s harder to maintain.”\n\n“That’s Stage Two warning.” Tiffani’s eyes cut sharp. “You terminate on my signal or I terminate you myself.”\n\nSenne gave her a tired half-smile. “Sure. Die on schedule. Very professional.”\n\n“Kess—report.”\n\n“I am present and available for assignment.”\n\n“Available doesn’t mean useful. Report symptoms. Stop performing.”\n\n“Symptoms are within acceptable parameters for operational requirements.”\n\nBram leaned in, voice too bright for the amount of people about to die. “Translation: Kess is bleeding and pretending it’s fashion.”\n\n“I am not bleeding.”\n\nMerra didn’t bother being gentle. “Kess—your nose is bleeding.”\n\n“I am aware of the nasal discharge. It is minor. Barely noteworthy. I’ve had worse nosebleeds from seasonal changes.”\n\n“Yeah,” Bram said. “And I’ve had worse ideas than this hold. Doesn’t make them good.”\n\nFrom the crowd, a Temple Observer screamed, “Let the worthy hold! Let the unworthy fall!”\n\nBram snapped his head toward the sound. “Hey—shut up. If we’re picking who falls, you’re first. That’s democracy.”\n\n“Ignore them,” Tiffani said. “Move.”\n\nThey moved.\n\nSpotters called markers like curses.\n\n“Jhace tremor increasing!”\n\n“Corven tremor increasing!”\n\n“Senne vein-line onset!”\n\nBram barked at the evac line like a foreman with a broken sense of humor. “Keep moving! Nobody stops! You stop, you become part of the debris! Count them out!”\n\nMerra’s voice cut through the dust. “Alternate route open. Final evacuation commencing.”\n\n“Count them,” Tiffani said. “Call numbers.”\n\nJhace’s breath came tight. “One hundred.”\n\n“State exit count,” Tiffani said, “not your suffering.”\n\n“One hundred,” Jhace said. “Out.”\n\nDaved’s voice came rough through the grit. “They’re not out.”\n\nJhace’s head snapped. “No.”\n\n“They’re not out.”\n\n“Daved—state the variable,” Tiffani said. “How many still inside?”\n\n“Enough.”\n\nTiffani didn’t blink. “Extend. Thirty heartbeats. No more.”\n\n“Tiff—” Jhace started.\n\n“I am not debating physics with the dead in the hallway,” she said. “Extend.”\n\nThirty heartbeats passed like a slow knife.\n\nMerra shouted from the corridor mouth, “Final group emerging. Ten heartbeats!”\n\n“Ten heartbeats is still ten,” Tiffani said. “Move.”\n\nDaved’s hands tightened. “Hold.”\n\n“Daved!” Jhace lunged a step closer.\n\n“Hold.”\n\nMerra’s slate slapped stone. “Clear! All civilians safe!”\n\n“Terminate all operators,” Tiffani snapped. “Immediate coordinated shutdown.”\n\nLights guttered and went out—one by one—\n\nExcept Daved’s.\n\n“Can’t,” Daved said.\n\n“Daved. Breathe,” Jhace said, too close, too desperate.\n\n“Can’t.”\n\n“Terminate,” Tiffani said. “That is not a negotiation.”\n\n“They’re not out.”\n\n“They are out. Merra confirmed. Terminate now.”\n\nDaved’s jaw worked like he was chewing copper. “Already crossed.”\n\nBram shoved his way in with words because silence was making everyone honest. “Daved—buddy—love the commitment, hate the method acting.”\n\n“Bram, shut up,” Jhace hissed.\n\n“I can’t,” Bram said. “It’s a disorder.”\n\nTiffani stepped in until Daved had to see her. “Look at me. If you extend past this, you cross Stage Three threshold.”\n\nDaved’s eyes stayed flat. “Already crossed.”\n\nJhace’s throat tightened. “He locked.”\n\n“I see it,” Tiffani said.\n\nThey hauled Daved back from the structure like pulling a hook out of meat.\n\n“Terminate!” Tiffani shouted.\n\nThe light finally cut.\n\nDaved folded like a tool dropped from numb hands.\n\nTiffani’s voice went small for one syllable. “I know.”\n\nThey carried Daved out on a door panel when the Hall finally stopped shifting enough to allow hands near him.\n\nHis eyes were open.\n\nHis body was too still.\n\nTiffani walked beside the makeshift stretcher, writing with one hand, wiping dust from her face with the other.\n\n---\n\nTiffani sat alone in the documentation room after they carried Daved out.\n\nHer slate lay open to the taxonomy. Stage Three. Locked hold. Autonomic failure.\n\nShe had written it perfectly. Every marker. Every heartbeat. Every outcome.\n\nHer hand moved to add the summary line.\n\nShe stared at the slate. At the line that read: **Casualties: 3.**\n\nShe tried to write the justification. The calculation. The reason extending duration had been correct under the circumstances.\n\nThe chalk snapped.\n\nHer hands shook because she’d been awake too long and her calf cramped—no magic required.\n\nThe chalk snapped anyway.\n\nShe stared at her shaking fingers.\n\nShe didn’t log her tremor. She didn’t log that she’d miscounted heartbeats during the hold because her vision had blurred. She didn’t log that she’d wanted to ring termination at one-eighty but hadn’t, because Daved’s eyes had asked her not to.\n\nSome data points weren’t for the ledger.\n\nSome were just for carrying.\n\n---\n\nJhace’s tremor made his jaw ache.\n\nHe’d wanted method like a wrench—turn it, get the same result.\n\nInstead he got a mouthful of pennies and hands that wouldn’t stop.\n\nMethod had just asked for a price it didn’t have the right to demand.\n\n---"
          ]
        },

    "3e5ee52c-26b7-46bb-9410-b501f04782bd": {
      "children": [
        "3ca49330-d9ae-4903-880e-331c39faf392"
      ],
      "id": "3e5ee52c-26b7-46bb-9410-b501f04782bd",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": "final",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "# N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS  \n## Chapters 1–13 + CODA — COMPILED MANUSCRIPT\n\n---\n\n## CHAPTER 1: THE BELL\n\nThe bell rang wrong.\n\nNot the slow warning cadence. Not the measured strike that meant check seals, tighten brackets. This was the frantic peal of something already falling.\n\nJhace moved before the bell finished its third strike.\n\nGrabbed the wrong ring of collar keys on the first yank. Swore. Dumped them into his palm and sorted by feel while he ran. One key skittered off the stair edge and disappeared into the dark.\n\nDidn't go back for it.\n\nHis kit bounced against his hip. Chalk. Slate. Collar keys. Tools for measuring failure, not stopping it.\n\nOne. Two. Three. Four.\n\nThe doubled hum hit him at Junction Twelve.\n\nSkipped the pipe entirely and punched straight into his sternum. His ribs buzzed like struck iron. His teeth hurt. His mouth flooded metal.\n\nCopper.\n\nSharp and immediate, like he'd bitten through wire.\n\nHe'd been tasting it for two Tides now. Faint at first. A ghost-flavor that came and went with the grid. But this was different. This flooded his mouth so thick he wanted to spit.\n\nDidn't.\n\nUnderface rule: waste nothing.\n\nHis hands started shaking.\n\nThen the shaking cut out for three heartbeats and he thought, stupidly, his fingers had finally died.\n\nAnd then the tremor came back twice as fast.\n\nIt wasn't cold or exertion. It was a fine vibration that made his grip feel unreliable, like his muscles were half-taking orders and half-refusing them.\n\nHe jammed them into his coat pockets and kept running.\n\nForced his breath to slow. Counted to ten without looking at the numbers.\n\nThe tremor kept going.\n\nPulled one hand out. Squeezed it into a fist until the knuckles burned. Released.\n\nStill shaking.\n\nIt wasn't aftershock. It was something settling into him—fine vibration under the skin—the kind that makes you drop a tool and swear at your own hand.\n\nHe'd seen it on old manifestors in the deep tunnels. Hands that never found stillness. He'd called it age.\n\nNow he understood, because his fingers kept twitching against his thigh like a bad motor that wouldn't shut off.\n\nTallow Street came into view through the access tunnel: upper terrace facade folded inward, stone slabs riding down on support beams that should have held twice that load. Dust cloud thick enough to choke on. People screaming from inside the collapse. Someone rang a hand-bell hard enough to crack its cadence.\n\nEmergency. Emergency. Emergency.\n\nA foreman waved at him, face streaked with ash. \"We need a hold. We don't have time.\"\n\nJhace looked at the structure.\n\nThe failure line wasn't clean. The whole building was trying to become rubble, one breath at a time. Load shifts rippled through it. Stone groaned. Iron squealed.\n\nNo crew could wedge that fast. The wedge bucket was still half-full. Someone was still tying their apron when the stone started to go.\n\nThe people inside didn't have Tides.\n\n\"I'm not—\" Jhace started.\n\nThe heat arrived.\n\nNot external. Internal. A sudden furnace-flare behind his sternum that made him gasp.\n\nCopper-gold dust swirled around his boots.\n\nHe watched it climb his legs. Static-cling. Looking for him.\n\nHis chest burned hotter.\n\nThe doubled hum snapped into alignment inside him—two mis-timed gears finally meshing. The wrongness in the grid became rightness in his blood. His heartbeat and the city's failing pulse locked into the same rhythm.\n\nHe didn't know what he was doing.\n\nHe didn't know why his feet carried him under the broken lintel.\n\nHe moved because the archway dust was already falling in steady sheets and somebody inside was screaming a name he couldn't ignore.\n\nPut both hands on the main support column.\n\nLight—thin, copper-gold, filament-fine—threaded from his palms into the stone seam.\n\nThe building stopped moving.\n\nIt was only paused—still wrong, still dangerous.\n\nJhace's breath shook out of him. He could feel every shift in load through his fingertips: the weight of stone, the tension in iron, the panic-motion of bodies inside. The heat in his chest flared with each micro-adjustment.\n\n\"Move,\" he rasped. \"Get them out.\"\n\nBoots ran. Voices shouted coordinates. Someone coughed dust and kept crawling.\n\nA woman slid into his peripheral vision—practical coat, hair tied back, slate already in hand despite the chaos.\n\nShe didn't look at him with awe.\n\nShe looked at him with assessment.\n\n\"How long can you hold?\" she demanded.\n\nJhace's jaw clenched. The heat in his chest spiked. \"I don't know.\"\n\n\"Then we find out.\" She stepped into the dust, coughed once, and shouted to the evac crew. \"Clear corridor A first. Count bodies out. No guessing.\"\n\nJhace tried to turn his head. The moment he shifted his attention, the heat flared sharper—punishment for looking away.\n\n\"I have to watch the load,\" he said through his teeth.\n\n\"Then watch it,\" she snapped, already marking a line on her slate. \"I'll watch you.\"\n\nPeople came out in bursts: three, then five, then a cluster carrying an older man whose leg bent wrong. Each time the load shifted, Jhace's hands tightened and the filament-light brightened in response.\n\nHis heartbeat counted itself in his ears.\n\nOne hundred.\n\nOne hundred twenty.\n\nHis arms started to tremble.\n\nNot from fatigue. From something deeper. A fine vibration that spread into his forearms, up to his shoulders, making his grip feel like it belonged to someone else.\n\n\"Hold steady,\" the woman said, quieter now, close enough he could hear her through the dust.\n\nHe didn't answer. Answering took breath.\n\nAt two hundred heartbeats, the tremor spread into his chest. At two-twenty, his vision narrowed at the edges, copper-gold sparks flickering across it.\n\n\"How many?\" he rasped.\n\nThe woman didn't look up from the evac line. \"Ten. Eleven. Twelve—\"\n\nA scream from inside. A child. High and thin and suddenly cut off.\n\nJhace's hands shook harder.\n\n\"No,\" he hissed, and the filament-light surged, burning his palms numb.\n\n\"Go,\" the woman shouted toward the breach. \"Last sweep. Now.\"\n\nA rescuer crawled under the paused beam line and dragged a small body out by the shoulders. The child coughed dust and cried.\n\nThe woman's voice cut clean through the chaos. \"Twelve out. Corridor clear.\"\n\nJhace felt the building try to move again the instant she said it.\n\n\"Terminate,\" she said. No softness in it. Not cruelty. Protocol.\n\nJhace let the filament go.\n\nThe building settled the last inch it had been denied. Stone grinding. Iron squealing. But the void where bodies had been was now empty.\n\nHe staggered back into open air.\n\nHis hands wouldn't stop shaking.\n\nThe tremor was small—millimeters—but it was relentless.\n\nThe woman stepped beside him, dust streaked across her face. \"Name.\"\n\n\"Jhace Torrins.\"\n\nShe wrote it without looking, chalk skating on sweat. \"Duration: two-forty—\" The chalk snapped. She swore under her breath and wrote it anyway.\n\n\"Don't,\" he snapped. Too hard. Too fast.\n\nShe met his eyes. Her gaze was flat, measuring, absolutely certain.\n\n\"If we don't measure it,\" she said, \"we kill you next time.\"\n\nBetween bells, they made it back to the workshop long enough to rinse dust off skin and reset slates.\n\nJhace sat at his bench and waited for the chest heat to fade.\n\nIt didn't. Not fully.\n\nA banked warmth stayed behind his sternum—steady as a coal bed that refused to go cold. He pressed his palm to his chest. Felt nothing through shirt and ribs.\n\nInside, the warmth answered anyway. Once. Twice.\n\nHis hand lingered there a heartbeat too long. Not thinking. Just reaching.\n\nHe pulled it away. The tremor kept moving, but for one heartbeat—only one—the shake eased.\n\nThat was the first time the hunger felt real.\n\nJhace's hands shook between them.\n\nHe swallowed copper. Tasted grit. Looked at the child being carried past them on a makeshift stretcher—alive, crying, covered in dust but breathing.\n\nHe nodded once.\n\n\"Measure,\" he said. \"Then make sure there isn't a next time.\"\n\nThe woman's slate scratched.\n\nShe wrote: **Jhace Torrins. Inspector. First manifestation. Tallow Street. Tide 0.**\n\nBehind them, the bell rang again.\n\nDifferent street. Same dust in his teeth. Same bell tearing its own throat.\n\nJhace looked down and realized he was still gripping air.\n\nCopper sat on his tongue. He went anyway.\n\n---\n\n## CHAPTER 2: THE PATTERN\n\nThe theodolite wouldn't settle.\n\nTiffani had calibrated it three times in the last hour. Clean lens. Level base. Reference stars locked. Everything aligned the way it had been for twenty-six Turns of lens work.\n\nThe crosshair still drifted.\n\nNo shimmer. No warp. Just the star sitting wrong in the frame.\n\nShe pressed her palm to the primary lens frame.\n\nVibration.\n\nFaint. Constant. The doubled hum lived under the normal grid pulse. It made her molars ache and her lens hand tremble.\n\nShe pulled her hand away and wrote on her slate:\n\n**Tide 0 — Kindling**  \n**Lens frame vibration: doubled hum present.**  \n**Star Seven-Three displacement: 0.34 degrees.**  \n**Neither atmosphere nor mechanism.**\n\nHer mouth tasted copper.\n\nShe'd been ignoring it for the last two Tides. Faint metallic tang that came and went with air currents from the ventilation shafts. But this was different. This arrived sharp and immediate, flooding her tongue.\n\nSwallowing felt like evidence.\n\nThe light outside the lens port changed.\n\nThe light didn't dim or brighten. It bent.\n\nTiffani looked up.\n\nFor a second she couldn't move. Her brain offered one sentence, raw and useless: *What the fuck is that?*\n\nThe sky over Saint Merro wasn't supposed to do anything except hold weather and stars. Today it held something else.\n\nA line. Straight. Dark. Moving too fast for her eyes to track at first, then slowing enough for her to understand what she was seeing.\n\nA spindle.\n\nLong and narrow and wrong.\n\nHer breath stopped.\n\nFor a stupid second, she couldn't make her fingers obey. She stood there with her hand half-raised.\n\nThe spindle crossed her view in a single heartbeat. No friction fire. No sound. Just a displacement of light that made the air look thin.\n\nThen gravity lurched.\n\nNot a shift sideways. An up-and-down stagger. Her slate slid across the stone desk. A lens clamp snapped. Glass sang a sharp, stressed note and went silent.\n\nTiffani caught herself on the observation frame and forced her eyes back to the port.\n\nThe spindle was gone.\n\nPunched through. Somewhere below. Into stone or ocean or the Underface—she couldn't tell.\n\nPressure arrived a breath later. Not a blast. More resistance than heat. Dust fell from the ceiling mortar in a slow, dry drift.\n\nThen copper-gold particulate began falling outside.\n\nGlittering. Fine. Catching the lamplight. It coated the stone lip of the observation port and clung to the iron frame in flecks that shimmered when she moved.\n\nTiffani touched it with her fingertip.\n\nIt didn't smear. It gritted. Abrasive. Her finger tingled where the dust touched skin.\n\nShe wrote:\n\n**Atmospheric intrusion event. Spindle-form object. Estimated velocity: extreme.**  \n**Copper-gold particulate fall: immediate post-impact.**  \n**Adheres to metal preferentially. Abrasive. Conductive?**\n\nHer pencil slipped. The last word smeared into a dark streak. She stared at it, angry at her own hand, then rewrote the line harder than necessary.\n\nA bell rang.\n\nNot the Glass-House alert. Distant. Down-terrace. The frantic peal of something already falling.\n\nThen another bell. Different direction. Same cadence.\n\nEmergency. Emergency. Emergency.\n\nTiffani's stomach tightened.\n\nShe grabbed her slate, shoved it into her coat pocket, and ran.\n\nThe doubled hum followed her down the Glass-House stairs. She could feel it in the stone railing—vibration that hadn't been there this morning. The walls themselves were listening to something new.\n\nAt the mid-terrace landing, she stopped.\n\nTallow Street was visible from here: upper facade collapsed inward, dust cloud thick enough to choke on. People running. Voices screaming coordinates she couldn't parse.\n\nA third bell rang. Sector Nine. Grain district.\n\nCloser—close enough that the air changed when the facade flexed.\n\nTiffani made her choice the way she always did: follow the data to its source.\n\nShe ran toward Sector Nine.\n\nThe copper taste got stronger with every stride.\n\n---\n\n## CHAPTER 3: SECOND HOLD\n\nThe grain warehouse sat in Sector Nine, where the terraces were newer and the beams were honest enough to still pretend they could be trusted.\n\nThe collapse wasn't honest.\n\nSupport columns had sheared at the base—clean breaks that didn't match load failure. The upper floor sagged. Dust poured from the breach in thick, choking waves.\n\nJhace stood three strides from the entrance, hands shaking so hard he had to grip his belt to keep them still.\n\nHe'd been here five breath-counts.\n\nHis boot skated on spilled grain. He caught himself on instinct, palm slapping the wall hard enough to sting.\n\nToo close. Too human.\n\nThe tremor from Tallow Street hadn't stopped. It had spread from his hands into his forearms, up to his shoulders. A fine, relentless vibration that made every tool on his belt rattle.\n\nThe heat in his chest was still there. Banked. Waiting.\n\nA foreman ran past him toward the breach, saw his Crown inspector marks, and grabbed his shoulder hard enough to bruise.\n\n\"You held Tallow,\" the foreman said. \"Do it again.\"\n\nJhace pulled free. \"I don't—\"\n\n\"There's forty people in there,\" the foreman cut in. \"Maybe more. Wedges won't hold. Straps won't reach. You're what we've got.\"\n\nThe copper taste flooded Jhace's mouth again.\n\nHis chest heat flared.\n\nThe doubled hum he'd felt in the grid arrived inside him—two mis-timed pulses locking into the same rhythm. His heartbeat and the failing building's stress pattern aligned.\n\nHe didn't want this.\n\nHe didn't understand this.\n\nHe moved because the archway dust was already falling in steady sheets and somebody inside was screaming a name he couldn't ignore.\n\n\"Clear the approach,\" Jhace said, voice rough. \"Get spotters. I need someone counting.\"\n\nHe stepped under the sagging upper floor.\n\nThe heat detonated behind his sternum.\n\nPut both hands on the nearest support column. Filament-light threaded from his palms into the stone—brighter this time, thicker.\n\nThe warehouse stopped settling.\n\nThe groan of stressed iron went quiet.\n\nJhace's breath shook. He could feel the load distribution through his fingertips: uneven, shifting, trying to collapse in three directions at once. His hands tightened. The filament brightened in response.\n\nThe tremor in his arms worsened.\n\nNot fine anymore. Visible. His whole body shaking with the effort of holding something that should have already fallen.\n\n\"Move!\" someone shouted. \"Get them out!\"\n\nBoots ran. Voices called coordinates. Bodies crawled through gaps in the rubble.\n\nA woman appeared beside him—practical coat, slate already open, hair tied back with a strip of cloth.\n\nShe looked at the filament-light threading from his hands. Looked at his face. Looked at the tremor shaking his shoulders.\n\n\"You're the Tallow Street hold,\" she said.\n\nNot a question. A confirmation.\n\n\"Yeah,\" Jhace managed.\n\n\"How long have you been holding?\"\n\n\"I don't know. Five breath-counts? Ten?\"\n\nHer pen scratched. \"Tremor amplitude increased since first manifestation. Cost progression visible.\"\n\n\"What?\" Jhace's vision narrowed at the edges. Copper-gold sparks flickered across it.\n\n\"Your body is paying for this,\" she said, still writing. \"If it locks past tolerance, you won't be able to let go.\"\n\n\"I can't let go,\" Jhace said through clenched teeth. \"Not until they're out.\"\n\nThe woman looked at the evacuation line. Counted bodies being carried past. \"Twenty-three so far. Estimate forty total inside.\"\n\n\"Then I hold for forty.\"\n\n\"Then you might not walk away.\"\n\nJhace's hands shook harder. The heat in his chest flared so bright it hurt. \"I already know that.\"\n\nThe woman's jaw tightened. She moved to where he could see her without turning his head and held up her slate.\n\n\"I'm documenting this,\" she said. \"Duration. Cost markers. Termination threshold. If we don't measure it, the next person who tries this dies without knowing why.\"\n\n\"What's your name?\" Jhace asked.\n\n\"Tiffani Merrow. Glass-House observatory.\"\n\n\"Jhace Torrins.\"\n\n\"I know,\" Tiffani said. \"I wrote it down at Tallow Street.\"\n\nShe showed him her slate:\n\n**Jhace Torrins. Inspector. First manifestation. Duration: 240 heartbeats. Cost: tremor onset, progressive.**\n\n**Second manifestation. Sector Nine warehouse. Onset: immediate. Tremor amplitude: increased. Copper-gold filament visible, enhanced density.**\n\nJhace stared at the notes. At his own name written as data.\n\n\"What caused this?\" he asked.\n\nTiffani's pen paused. \"Something punched through the sky two hours ago. Spindle. Metallic. Fast enough to ignore friction. Left this—\"\n\nShe held up her fingertip. Copper-gold grit caught the lamplight.\n\n\"—everywhere.\"\n\nJhace looked at the dust swirling around his boots. At the filament threading from his hands. At the way the copper-gold particles moved toward him.\n\n\"The doubled hum,\" he said.\n\n\"You felt it too?\" Tiffani's eyes sharpened.\n\n\"In the grid. Two Tides before this. Wrong frequency. Made my teeth itch.\"\n\nTiffani wrote: **Doubled hum: confirmed across multiple observers. Grid infrastructure + atmospheric intrusion event = correlation.**\n\nA rescue crew leader shouted. \"Thirty-eight out. Two still inside. Back corner. Pinned.\"\n\nJhace's vision blurred. The heat in his chest spiked.\n\n\"How long have I been holding?\" he rasped.\n\nTiffani checked a small timepiece on her belt. \"Eighteen breath-counts.\"\n\n\"Tallow Street was four.\"\n\n\"Your cost is accelerating,\" Tiffani said. She wiped a nosebleed with the back of her wrist. \"Tremor spread. Vision blur. This isn't sustainable.\"\n\nJhace tasted blood before she finished the sentence. Copper-warm, running over his lip.\n\n\"Terminate after extraction,\" Tiffani said. \"Not before. But the instant they're clear, you let go.\"\n\n\"What if I can't?\"\n\n\"Then I make you.\"\n\nThe last two bodies came out on stretchers. Alive. Barely.\n\n\"Clear!\" the crew leader yelled.\n\nTiffani's hand closed on Jhace's shoulder—hard enough to hurt, grounding enough to feel real.\n\n\"Terminate,\" she said. \"Now.\"\n\nJhace let the filament go.\n\nThe warehouse settled the last inch it had been denied. Stone grinding. Iron squealing. A beam snapped somewhere in the back, but the void where people had been was empty.\n\nJhace staggered back.\n\nHis legs didn't hold. He hit the ground on his knees, hands still shaking, blood dripping from his nose onto the dust-covered stone.\n\nTiffani crouched beside him, slate open, pen moving.\n\n**Second manifestation terminated. Duration: 19 breath-counts, 40 heartbeats. Cost: tremor progression (shoulders, torso), vision impairment (peripheral narrowing, copper-gold visual artifacts), epistaxis (nosebleed), motor control compromise (collapse post-termination).**\n\n**Survival: confirmed. Function: degraded.**\n\nJhace wiped blood from his mouth with a shaking hand. \"You write everything down.\"\n\n\"Everything behaves,\" Tiffani said. \"Even this. It has markers. Progression. Termination criteria.\"\n\n\"It's killing me in increments.\"\n\n\"Yes,\" Tiffani said, and the word came out harsher than she meant. \"So we track it before it kills you. We don't guess. We don't pray.\"\n\nJhace looked at her slate. At the notes written in clean, precise handwriting. At his own degradation catalogued as data.\n\n\"Why are you doing this?\" he asked.\n\nTiffani met his eyes. Hers were sharp, clear, absolutely certain.\n\n\"Because the city is breaking faster than crews can fix it,\" she said. \"Because people like you are going to keep holding until they die. Because if we don't measure the cost, the next bell will kill someone without them knowing why.\"\n\nA bell rang in the distance.\n\nFourth collapse. Different sector.\n\nThe copper taste surged in Jhace's mouth.\n\nThe heat in his chest flared.\n\nTiffani wrote it down because not writing felt wrong: **Third manifestation probable. Autonomic response. Cost accumulating.**\n\nShe looked at Jhace. \"Can you stand?\"\n\n\"I don't know.\"\n\n\"Try.\"\n\nJhace pushed himself to his feet. His legs shook. His hands wouldn't stop trembling. But he stood.\n\n\"Good,\" Tiffani said. \"Because we're going to the next one. And I'm counting every heartbeat.\"\n\nShe walked toward the sound of the bell.\n\nJhace followed.\n\nTiffani wiped chalk off her thumb. It smeared into the sweat.\n\nThe bell kept ringing.\n\n---\n\n## CHAPTER 4: PROTOCOL\n\nJhace's workshop was a box of iron and heat tucked into the mid-terrace, where you could hear the grid without being crushed by it.\n\nTiffani sat on an overturned crate. Slate on her knees. Left leg extended to ease the knee that had started locking after long stands on stone floors.\n\nShe didn't lean back.\n\nAlways ready to move. Always ready to write.\n\nJhace stood at his bench, trying to thread a bolt into a collar bracket.\n\nHis hands shook.\n\nThe grid didn't fail with a bang. It stuttered. Missed beats. Caught up. Like an old machine slipping teeth.\n\nHe tried again.\n\nThe bolt dropped. Metal on stone.\n\nHe stared at it like it had betrayed him.\n\nTiffani didn't look up. \"Your tremor amplitude increased during exertion.\"\n\n\"Because I'm exerting,\" Jhace muttered, bending to pick it up. His fingertips felt numb where the filament had burned them. Not pain.\n\nAbsence.\n\n\"Because your body is remembering,\" Tiffani said. \"It thinks you're still holding.\"\n\nJhace set the bolt down. Flexed his hand hard.\n\nThe tremor didn't care.\n\nHe hated that. Hated the way his own muscles had become unreliable.\n\n\"What did you call it out there?\" he asked.\n\nTiffani flipped her slate so he could see. A crude timeline. No poetry. Just sequence.\n\nActivation onset: chest heat + copper taste  \nLight manifestation: filament, contact-dependent  \nTremor: post-termination, permanent marker\n\n\"I need a taxonomy,\" she said. \"Stages. Right now it's just stories.\"\n\n\"And you think writing stages keeps people alive.\"\n\n\"I think it keeps them from lying to themselves.\" She tapped the word permanent. \"You can't call it temporary. You can't bargain with it.\"\n\nJhace's jaw worked.\n\nHe had bargained with Junction Seven. Told himself the report would matter. That somebody else would fix it. That the city didn't need him to fight every foreman with a schedule board.\n\nHe looked at his shaking hands.\n\n\"Stage One,\" he said. Low. \"Tremor.\"\n\nTiffani nodded and wrote:\n\nStage One: tremor + filament residue. Functional. Marked.\n\nShe stared at Functional like it offended her.\n\nDrew a hard line through it.\n\nRewrote: Operational.\n\n\"Functional makes it sound polite,\" she muttered.\n\nA knock hit the workshop door.\n\nBram Kerris leaned in, dust on his boots, sweat at his collar, grin already loaded like a bad habit.\n\n\"Heard you two grabbed Tallow Street by the throat,\" Bram said, trying for funny and getting dust instead. He coughed once. \"City tries to crush you, you crush back. Very… romantic. Very dumb.\"\n\nJhace didn't look up. \"Who's asking?\"\n\n\"Crown's asking questions,\" Bram said, and somehow still made it sound obscene. \"Not because they care. Because they hate unknown tools walking around without a leash and a receipt.\"\n\nTiffani's pen paused. \"What questions.\"\n\n\"Names. Locations. 'Verified capability.' That sort of foreplay.\" Bram stepped fully inside, then stopped like the heat-grid might charge him rent. \"Infrastructure office. Some administrator with hands so clean it makes you wonder what he's washing off. Loves forms. Loves categories. Loves pretending paper is stronger than stone.\"\n\nJhace's jaw tightened. \"To do what.\"\n\nBram spread his hands. \"To decide who gets to own you. Temple will call it divine. Guild will call it labor. Crown will call it law. Everybody wants a piece. Congratulations—you're popular.\"\n\n\"We don't belong to any of them,\" Tiffani said.\n\nBram laughed, quick and wrong. \"Adorable. Tell that to a stamp. Try explaining 'we don't belong' while a ceiling is coming down and someone's screaming for a hold. They'll file you under 'non-compliant' and act like that's a weather condition.\"\n\nJhace's tremor ticked in his fingers. \"So you're here to warn us.\"\n\n\"I'm here to tell you the door is already being measured,\" Bram said. \"I can buy you a Tide or two. Maybe. After that, they show up with paperwork and smiles and start deciding which parts of you are 'public infrastructure.'\"\n\nTiffani's mouth went flat. \"Then we build methodology fast.\"\n\nBram pointed at her slate. \"Yeah. Before they build it for you. Because if the Crown writes your protocol, it's going to read like a job description. And you won't like the benefits package.\"\n\nJhace breathed in.\n\nTasted copper-gold dust that still clung to his clothes.\n\nFelt the city's hum through the floorboards.\n\n\"Then we build it fast,\" he said. \"Before they decide to build it for us.\"\n\n---\n\n## CHAPTER 5: FIRST NETWORK\n\nThe kiln-quarter feeder gantry sat in Sector Fourteen, where the pipes ran hot and the schedules ran hotter.\n\nThe bell came late.\n\nThat was the first problem.\n\nWhen Jhace and Tiffani arrived, the gantry wasn't collapsed—yet. The support columns were bowing, groaning under a load they shouldn't have been carrying because somebody had over-pressured the feed line to meet quota and called it \"necessary.\"\n\nA foreman screamed, \"Hold it! Hold it now!\"\n\nJhace moved by habit.\n\nChest heat answered. Immediate.\n\nTiffani caught his sleeve.\n\n\"Not yet.\"\n\nJhace turned his head a fraction. The moment he shifted focus, the warmth behind his sternum flared—punishment for looking away from the load. The tremor in his hands ticked harder, like it wanted permission.\n\n\"People are inside,\" he rasped.\n\n\"People are leaving,\" she said, pointing.\n\nShe was right. Workers were already pouring out of the side doors, coughing, eyes wide. The structure wasn't in free fall. It was in warning.\n\n\"We wedge first,\" Tiffani said. \"Then you hold if wedges fail.\"\n\nJhace's jaw tightened.\n\nHe hated needing permission to do the only thing that had worked.\n\nHe dropped to one knee anyway.\n\nWedge slips. Strap loops. Collar brackets. The old tools. The honest ones.\n\nHis first wedge went in crooked.\n\nHe felt it before he saw it. The angle wrong. The bite uneven.\n\nHe yanked it back out. Knuckles scraped stone. Grainy dust cut under his nail.\n\nThe foreman saw.\n\nThe workers saw.\n\nThat was the second problem.\n\nHe wasn't a clean solution anymore. He was a man with shaking hands making visible mistakes.\n\nHe shoved the wedge in again. Straight this time. Hammered it home with the heel of his palm because the mallet handle wouldn't stay still.\n\nBram's crew swarmed the brace line behind him, hands fast, straps ready, mouths full of curses and grit.\n\nFor once, labor did what labor could do.\n\nThe gantry groaned again. Dust spilled from a seam.\n\nA young man stepped into the brace line, hands hovering like he couldn't decide whether touching the structure made him brave or stupid.\n\nCorven.\n\nJhace had seen him in the tunnels. Quiet. Competent. Always watching other men's hands.\n\nCorven swallowed. \"I… I can feel it,\" he said. \"The hum. It's—\"\n\n\"Louder,\" Jhace finished.\n\nCorven nodded hard. \"In my teeth.\"\n\nTiffani stepped close, slate already up. \"If it hits your chest, tell me. If you taste copper, tell me. Do not improvise.\"\n\nCorven's eyes flicked to the bending column. \"If it goes—\"\n\n\"Then we act,\" Jhace said. \"But not before.\"\n\nThe wedges held for two breath-counts.\n\nThen the central column shifted.\n\nNot collapse. A decisive, ugly move—stone settling, iron flexing. The wedges screamed as they compressed. The brace line bowed like it was thinking about surrender.\n\nWorkers were still inside.\n\n\"Tiff,\" Jhace said, voice rough.\n\nShe didn't hesitate.\n\n\"Now.\"\n\nJhace put both hands on the column.\n\nHeat flared behind his sternum—familiar now, terrifying in its familiarity—and copper-gold filament threaded into the seam like the stone had been waiting for him.\n\nThe column stopped shifting.\n\nThe groan of stressed iron went quiet.\n\nJhace's breath shook out of him. He could feel the load distribution through his fingertips: uneven, trying to walk itself sideways. His hands tightened. The filament brightened in response.\n\n\"Keep moving!\" Bram shouted, voice cutting through the noise like a saw. \"If you stop, you become debris! Count them out! Loud. Fast!\"\n\nBodies poured out. Coughing. Limping. Dragging each other by collars.\n\nTiffani didn't count in her head. She counted with her mouth. Audible. Aggressive. Like numbers could bully stone into patience.\n\n\"One. Two. Three—\"\n\nCorven stared at Jhace's hands.\n\nJhace kept his eyes forward. \"Your turn.\"\n\nCorven flinched.\n\n\"Secondary brace,\" Jhace said, through clenched teeth. \"Touch. Don't squeeze. Don't try to be a hero.\"\n\nCorven stepped in and laid both palms against the brace.\n\nHis breath hitched.\n\nJhace saw it in his face before he heard it: copper taste arriving like a slap.\n\nCorven swallowed hard. \"Copper.\"\n\nTiffani's pen scratched.\n\nCorven's eyes widened. \"Chest—\"\n\nHeat took him.\n\nNot metaphor. Not fear. Actual furnace bloom behind the ribs.\n\nCorven made a sound like he'd been punched.\n\nThen light—thinner than Jhace's, but real—threaded from Corven's hands into the brace line.\n\nThe brace stopped moving.\n\nCorven's hands started to shake immediately.\n\nNot after. Not later.\n\nNow.\n\n\"Stage One onset during hold,\" Tiffani murmured, writing. \"Early marker.\"\n\nCorven's jaw clenched. \"I can do—\"\n\n\"Do exactly what the structure needs,\" Jhace snapped. \"Nothing more.\"\n\nThe tremor in Jhace's arms surged in sympathy. His shoulders burned. Vision tunneled at the edges, copper-gold sparks skating across the periphery.\n\n\"How long?\" Tiffani asked.\n\nJhace didn't answer in words. He answered in counting. Heartbeats ticking behind his teeth.\n\nEighty.\n\nOne-twenty.\n\nOne-fifty.\n\nThe last workers stumbled out—two hundred-plus bodies alive because they'd been allowed time.\n\n\"Clear!\" someone shouted.\n\nTiffani's voice cut clean. \"Terminate.\"\n\nJhace released first.\n\nThe column held under wedges now that the load had shifted down and the pressure line had been throttled. Not safe. Not fixed. But no longer falling while people were inside.\n\nCorven released a heartbeat later and nearly fell.\n\nJhace caught him by the elbow. The tremor in his own hands made the grip feel unreliable, like even saving someone standing in front of him required negotiation with his nerves.\n\nCorven looked at his shaking fingers with horror. \"It's not stopping.\"\n\n\"It doesn't,\" Jhace said.\n\nCorven stared like he expected the sentence to end with a condition.\n\nIt didn't.\n\nTiffani wrote the numbers anyway.\n\n**Evacuated: 219.**  \n**Casualties: 0.**  \n**Operators marked: 2.**\n\nBram approached, face hard under the dust. \"Crown's going to hear about this. And they'll bring forms instead of shovels.\"\n\nTiffani didn't look up. \"Good.\"\n\nCorven swallowed again. Still tasting copper.\n\n\"How long does it last?\" he asked.\n\nJhace's jaw tightened. \"Until you stop manifesting.\"\n\nCorven's mouth twitched. Not a smile. Something worse. \"So never.\"\n\n\"Yeah.\"\n\nCorven set his palm on a railing. The tremor rattled bone against metal.\n\nHe pulled back. Flexed. Tried again. Harder.\n\nIt kept moving—slow, stubborn—running even after you stop pulling.\n\nTiffani's pen didn't pause. Because pausing didn't change what was true.\n\n---\n\n### ROUTE FAILURE (THREE DEAD)\n\nThe count was an argument before it was a number.\n\nThe bell came from Sector Nine, not as a roar—just a single strike repeated until someone answered.\n\nResidential stairwell. Upper-terrace split. Twelve families still inside, one route.\n\nJhace was already marked from the gantry. Corven was worse. Senne was fresh enough to stand without shaking.\n\nTiffani stared at the slate wall and made the decision she would later pretend was only math.\n\nTwo operators at one-eighty each meant coordination, handoffs, failure points.\n\nOne operator at two-forty meant simplicity.\n\nA clean line.\n\n\"Single deployment,\" she said. \"Senne. Duration cap: two-forty. Terminate on my bell.\"\n\nSenne went pale as the heat took her chest. Copper-gold light laced her forearms. Not pretty.\n\nFunctional.\n\nThey reached the stairwell mouth as stone dust sifted from the ceiling. The grid underfoot carried the doubled hum, deep and insistent, a warning that didn't care who listened.\n\nSenne braced under the central landing. Jhace watched her shoulders set—metalworker posture in a glassworker frame.\n\nTiffani counted. Heartbeats, not comfort. Numbers didn't soften just because people begged.\n\nOne hundred.\n\nOne-forty.\n\nOne-eighty.\n\nAt one-ninety, a secondary beam—one Tiffani hadn't walked because she'd trusted the map—shifted with a sound like teeth grinding.\n\nThe evacuation lane kinked.\n\nThe next family stumbled.\n\nThe stone didn't collapse all at once. It folded, patient and specific, closing the corridor into a narrow pocket.\n\nThree people were on the wrong side when it did.\n\nTwo adults and a child coughing copper into his sleeve.\n\nTiffani saw them and for a heartbeat the slate in her hands felt obscene.\n\n\"Hold,\" she said, voice flat. \"Keep holding.\"\n\nSenne's eyes didn't move. She couldn't spare the motion.\n\nTwo-ten.\n\nTwo-twenty.\n\nTwo-thirty.\n\nThe pocket didn't clear.\n\nThe child's coughing turned wet.\n\nAt two-forty, Tiffani rang the termination sequence anyway.\n\nBecause protocols existed for a reason.\n\nBecause if she didn't, she would never stop.\n\nSenne released.\n\nThe stairwell collapsed into itself with a slow, final sound—stone settling into the shape it had wanted all along.\n\nThey dug for hours.\n\nThey found three bodies. The child first, because he was small enough to slip into the wrong angles.\n\nBack in the workshop, Tiffani logged it with perfect precision.\n\n**Deployment:** Senne — 240 heartbeats  \n**Evacuated:** 47  \n**Casualties:** 3  \n**Cause:** Inadequate route assessment.
