    "446714ef-5daf-4451-85ab-ce4692930488": {
      "children": [
        "a2a66c5f-4a2b-4b44-b522-cd993192127e"
      ],
      "id": "446714ef-5daf-4451-85ab-ce4692930488",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "python",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "(77237,\n 'N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS\\nTurning: GLASS (G) — Tide 0 through Tide 21 (Three Anneals post-Piercing)\\n---\\nCHAPTER 1: THE BELL\\nThe bell rang wrong.\\nNot the slow warning cadence. Not the measured strike that meant \"check your seals, tighten your brackets.\" This was the frantic peal of something already falling, the sound of a structure begging for a hold.\\nJhace was moving before the bell finished its third strike.\\nHe grabbed the wrong ring of collar keys on the first yank. Swore, dumped them into his palm, and sorted by feel while he ran. One key skittered off the stair edge and disappeared into the dark.\\nHe didn’t go back for it.\\n\\nHis kit bounced against his hip. Chalk. Slate. Collar keys. Tools for measuring failure, not stopping it. He didn\\'t know why his legs were carrying him toward the sound instead of away. Didn\\'t know why his heart counted the bell strikes like they were orders.\\nOne. Two. Three. Four.\\nThe doubled harmonic hit him at Junction Twelve.\\nIt skipped the pipe-hum entirely and punched straight into his sternum. His ribs buzzed like struck iron. His teeth hurt. His mouth flooded metal.\\nCopper.\\nSharp and immediate, like he\\'d bitten through wire.\\nHe\\'d been tasting it for two Tides now. Faint at first. A ghost-flavor that came and went with the grid-hum. But this was different. This flooded his mouth so thick he wanted to spit.\\nHe didn\\'t.\\nUnderface rule: waste nothing.\\nHis hands started shaking.\\nThen the shaking cut out for three heartbeats and he thought, stupidly, his fingers had finally died.\\nAnd then the tremor came back twice as fast.\\n\\nIt wasn\\'t cold or exertion. It was a fine vibration that made his grip feel unreliable, like his muscles were half-taking orders and half-refusing them.\\nHe jammed them into his coat pockets and kept running.\\nHe forced his breath to slow. Counted to ten without looking at the numbers.\\nThe tremor kept going.\\nHe pulled one hand out. Squeezed it into a fist until the knuckles burned. Released.\\nStill shaking.\\nIt wasn\\'t aftersh')"
        },
        "create_time": 1768419533.090104,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "446714ef-5daf-4451-85ab-ce4692930488",

Not the measured strike that meant \"check your seals, tighten your brackets.\" This was the frantic peal of something already falling, the sound of a structure begging for a hold.\\nJhace was moving before the bell finished its third strike.\\nHe grabbed the wrong ring of collar keys on the first yank. His mouth flooded metal.\\nCopper.\\nSharp and immediate, like he\\'d bitten through wire.\\nHe\\'d been tasting it for two Tides now. Faint at first. A ghost-flavor that came and went with the grid-hum. But this was different. This flooded his mouth so thick he wanted to spit.\\nHe didn\\'t.\\nUnderface rule: waste nothing.\\nHis hands started shaking.\\nThen the shaking cut out for three heartbeats and he thought, stupidly, his fingers had finally died.\\nAnd then the tremor came back twice as fast.\\n\\nIt wasn\\'t cold or exertion. It was a fine vibration that made his grip feel unreliable, like his muscles were half-taking orders and half-refusing them.\\nHe jammed them into his coat pockets and kept running.\\nHe forced his breath to slow. Counted to ten without looking at the numbers.\\nThe tremor kept going.\\nHe pulled one hand out. Squeezed it into a fist until the knuckles burned. Released.\\nStill shaking.\\nIt wasn\\'t aftersh')"
                  }
                },

    "44686eaf-316e-4bba-8657-2a496bf2a12f": {
      "children": [
        "e8ba661b-78a7-4030-995d-1e2a16221a0f"
      ],
      "id": "44686eaf-316e-4bba-8657-2a496bf2a12f",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": "final",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "Locked.\n\n### CODA: GLASS IN THE HAND\n\nJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them.\n\nHe’d scrubbed his hands raw.\n\nThe copper taste was still there anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin he couldn’t spit out.\n\nStone walls.\n\nLinen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth.\n\nHis chest still rose. His heart still counted like it was a job.\n\nSomeone had posted a guard outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive.\n\nJhace tried to sleep.\n\nEvery time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—memory or afterimage or the city’s new baseline living in him.\n\nHe flexed his fingers.\n\nThe tremor stalled for three heartbeats.\n\nPerfect stillness.\n\nIt scared him worse than the shaking.\n\nThen it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place.\n\nJhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed.\n\n“I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest.\n\nOutside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short.\n\nJhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.\n\n---\n\n### CODA: THE SHARD\n\nJhace lay flat in the cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them.\n\nThe guard outside kept breathing.\n\nThen the breathing stopped.\n\nNo footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint.\n\nThe door opened anyway.\n\nLight entered that didn’t know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not corridor spill. It cut a clean rectangle across the floor and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape.\n\nA figure stepped in.\n\nMale.\n\nClean cloth. No soot. No sweat. No perfume.\n\nA faint copper-gold line sat under his shirt at the center of his chest—steady, embedded, like something had taken root behind his sternum and decided it belonged there.\n\nHis boots didn’t track dust.\n\nHis shadow didn’t arrive the way shadows should.\n\nHe didn’t speak.\n\nHe moved with the economy of someone who’d done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel.\n\nA hand touched Jhace’s eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb over pupil. Then two fingers on his wrist—pulse point—patient and exact.\n\nJhace tried to jerk away.\n\nNothing.\n\nHe tried to curl his right hand into a fist.\n\nNothing.\n\nThe man took that hand anyway.\n\nBoth of his, around Jhace’s, and he unfolded Jhace’s fingers like breaking a joint open, careful and firm, no apology. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage.\n\nSomething cold settled into the center of Jhace’s palm.\n\nA shard.\n\nCopper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been politely polished by somebody who didn’t give a damn about skin.\n\nCold first.\n\nDeep cold, the kind that made teeth ache.\n\nThen heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not filament strain.\n\nThis heat arrived already aligned.\n\nLike a wedge finding the notch it was cut for.\n\nCopper flooded Jhace’s mouth so fast he gagged and couldn’t move enough to spit.\n\nLight threaded up from his palm.\n\nNot a beam. Not a glow.\n\nA thin copper-gold line that behaved like it had instructions, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path.\n\nIt slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam.\n\nElbow to shoulder.\n\nShoulder to sternum.\n\nA map drawn in heat and refusal.\n\nJhace couldn’t feel his legs.\n\nCouldn’t feel the sheets.\n\nCouldn’t feel the weight of his own body.\n\nBut he felt the line.\n\nHe felt the doubled harmonic he’d been hearing in stone for Tides settle into him—deep, precise—like the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay.\n\nHis lungs pulled a breath that wasn’t his decision.\n\nThe man closed Jhace’s fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact.\n\nThen he stepped back.\n\nNo check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if Jhace understood.\n\nThe door shut without sound.\n\nThe guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed.\n\nJhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn’t chosen to close.\n\nThe tremor was gone.\n\nIn its place: stillness.\n\nTotal.\n\nHeavy.\n\nLike a tool locked in a vise.\n\nSomewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone coughed. Someone laughed at something ordinary.\n\nOrdinary noises.\n\nWhile Jhace’s palm burned around a piece of the sky."
          ]
        },

    "480b30b9-8cb5-4775-825b-a0977d48f416": {
      "children": [
        "763af741-4e5c-44f7-b6d7-74c2a025639a"
      ],
      "id": "480b30b9-8cb5-4775-825b-a0977d48f416",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "python",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "(\"## CODA\\n\\nJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them.\\nHe’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste was still there anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you can't spit out.\\nStone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn't reach. His right hand twitching on its own schedule.\\nSomeone had posted a guard outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive.\\nJhace tried to sleep.\\nEvery time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn't tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city's new baseline living in him.\\nHe flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for thr\",\n \"glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close.\\nThe tremor was gone.\\nIn its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise.\\nSomewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.\\n\\n---\\nEND N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS\\n---\\n\\n*END N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS*\",\n 1)"
        },
        "create_time": 1768419706.0698838,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "480b30b9-8cb5-4775-825b-a0977d48f416",
        "metadata": {
          "aggregate_result": {
            "code": "def clean_chapter_text(ch:str)->str:\n    # base has \"CHAPTER X: TITLE\" without markdown; convert to \"## CHAPTER X: TITLE\"\n    ch = re.sub(r'^CHAPTER\\s+(\\d+):\\s*(.+)$', r'## CHAPTER \\1: \\2', ch, flags=re.MULTILINE)\n    return ch.strip()\n\nchapters_md = [clean_chapter_text(chap_base[i]) for i in range(1,14)]\n\n# Build merged coda\n# Get first and second coda content without titles\n# segments: [before split empty, 'GLASS IN THE HAND\\n\\ncontent...', 'THE SHARD\\n\\ncontent...']\ncoda1 = segments[1].strip()\ncoda2 = segments[2].strip()\n# remove title line from each\ndef strip_title(seg):\n    # title is first line\n    lines=seg.splitlines()\n    # remove leading title line if uppercase words\n    return \"\\n\".join(lines[1:]).strip()\ncoda1_body = strip_title(coda1)\ncoda2_body = strip_title(coda2)\n# cut coda2 from \"Then the breathing stopped.\"\nstart_phrase=\"Then the breathing stopped.\"\nidx2=coda2_body.find(start_phrase)\ncoda2_tail = coda2_body[idx2:] if idx2!=-1 else coda2_body\ncoda2_tail = coda2_tail.replace(\"Then the breathing stopped.\", \"The guard’s breathing stopped.\", 1)\n# also adjust \"His heart still counted time\" to \"His heart still counted heartbeats\" maybe already in base? check\n# We'll keep as-is except change that one sentence if present\ncoda2_tail = coda2_tail.replace(\"His heart still counted time like it was a job.\", \"His heart still counted heartbeats like it was a job.\")\n# Build merged coda\nmerged_coda = \"## CODA\\n\\n\" + coda1_body.rstrip() + \"\\n\\n\" + coda2_tail.strip() + \"\\n\\n*END N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS*\"\n# remove duplicated END if already in tail\nmerged_coda = re.sub(r'\\n\\*END N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS\\*\\s*\\n\\*END N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS\\*', '\\n*END N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS*', merged_coda)\nmerged_coda[:800], merged_coda[-400:], merged_coda.count(\"*END N01\")\n",
            "end_time": 1768419707.32253,
            "final_expression_output": "(\"## CODA\\n\\nJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them.\\nHe’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste was still there anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you can't spit out.\\nStone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn't reach. His right hand twitching on its own schedule.\\nSomeone had posted a guard outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive.\\nJhace tried to sleep.\\nEvery time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn't tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city's new baseline living in him.\\nHe flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for thr\",\n \"glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close.\\nThe tremor was gone.\\nIn its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise.\\nSomewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.\\n\\n---\\nEND N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS\\n---\\n\\n*END N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS*\",\n 1)",
            "in_kernel_exception": null,
            "jupyter_messages": [
              {
                "content": {
                  "execution_state": "busy"
                },
                "msg_type": "status",
                "parent_header": {

              {
                "content": {
                  "data": {
                    "text/plain": "(\"## CODA\\n\\nJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them.\\nHe’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste was still there anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you can't spit out.\\nStone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn't reach. His right hand twitching on its own schedule.\\nSomeone had posted a guard outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive.\\nJhace tried to sleep.\\nEvery time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn't tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city's new baseline living in him.\\nHe flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for thr\",\n \"glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close.\\nThe tremor was gone.\\nIn its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.\\n\\n---\\nEND N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS\\n---\\n\\n*END N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS*\",\n 1)"

    "4ae24b85-7c87-448a-a549-35c33d9d665a": {

      "id": "4ae24b85-7c87-448a-a549-35c33d9d665a",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "code",
          "language": "unknown",
          "response_format_name": null,
          "text": "doc = \"# N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS\\n\\n---\\n\\n\" + \"\\n\\n---\\n\\n\".join(chapters_md) + \"\\n\\n---\\n\\n\" + merged_coda + \"\\n\"\nout_path=\"/mnt/data/N01_GripThatHolds_LOCKED_Ch1-13_Coda.txt\"\nwith open(out_path,'w',encoding='utf-8') as f:\n    f.write(doc)\nlen(doc), out_path\n"
        },

    "4ae67810-1dd4-47a8-93c6-b0e485f43aa8": {
      "children": [
        "aace54ee-170c-4f37-bbe7-7024f9ff8c2e"
      ],
      "id": "4ae67810-1dd4-47a8-93c6-b0e485f43aa8",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "code",
          "language": "unknown",
          "response_format_name": null,
          "text": "import re\npattern = re.compile(r'^(##\\s+(?:CHAPTER\\s+\\d+:[^\\n]*|CODA[^\\n]*))\\n', re.M)\nmatches=list(pattern.finditer(txt))\nlen(matches), matches[0].group(1), matches[1].group(1)\n"
        },
        "create_time": 1768420868.544747,
        "end_turn": false,
        "id": "4ae67810-1dd4-47a8-93c6-b0e485f43aa8",

    "4b84f5cb-07ab-40fd-827c-5d7c3104f502": {
      "children": [
        "dc595bd5-ed48-4b3b-b08a-124ba6d63453"
      ],
      "id": "4b84f5cb-07ab-40fd-827c-5d7c3104f502",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "start 507 end 611\nCHAPTER 7: MERCHANT HALL\nMerchant Hall was a cathedral for trade: iron ribs, stone spine, glass skylights that made everything inside look clean even when it wasn’t.\nAnd it smelled like bodies and money and fear—the kind of fear that tries to wear perfume.\nIt started failing at mid-Tide.\nThe bell sequence came sharp and frantic—no warning cadence, no time to wedge first. The Hall had been packed when the terrace shifted again.\nFour hundred inside, Bram said. Maybe more.\nTiffani arrived before Jhace. When he pushed through the crowd, she was already on the steps, slate in hand, shouting like a foreman who’d learned math.\n“Operators on me. Two per span. Spotters assigned. No hero holds.”\n“Duration limit. One-eighty heartbeats maximum. You terminate on my signal. No argument.”\nMerra shoved through the dust with a slate clutched to her chest. Ash streaked her hair like she’d tried to comb through a fire.\n“We have a problem. Southern stairwell collapsed. Fifty people trapped on upper floors. Can’t reach main exits.”\n“Time estimate,” Tiffani said. “Low and high.”\n“Unknown. Debris blocking secondary stairs. Could be two hundred heartbeats. Could be six hundred.”\n“Then we don’t gamble.” Tiffani didn’t raise her voice. She didn’t have to. “Clear lanes. Count bodies out. You want prayer, pray while you walk.”\nShe swung her slate toward the operators.\n“Corven—baseline check. When chest heat starts, report it. Don’t interpret. Report.”\nCorven swallowed. “If it hits, I tell you. If I taste copper, I tell you.”\nJhace didn’t look at him. “Don’t spiral. Do the minimum.”\n“Minimum,” Corven echoed, like repetition could make it true.\n“Senne—status.”\n“Getting hollow,” Senne said. “Chest feels empty. Light’s harder to maintain.”\n“That’s Stage Two warning.” Tiffani’s eyes cut sharp. “You terminate on my signal or I terminate you myself.”\nSenne gave her a tired half-smile. “Sure. Die on schedule. Very professional.”\n“Kess—report.”\n“I am present and available for assignment.”\n“Available doesn’t mean useful. Report symptoms. Stop performing.”\n“Symptoms are within acceptable parameters for operational requirements.”\nBram leaned in, voice too bright for the amount of people about to die. “Translation: Kess is bleeding and pretending it’s fashion.”\n“I am not bleeding.”\nMerra didn’t bother being gentle. “Kess—your nose is bleeding.”\n“I am aware of the nasal discharge. It is minor. Barely noteworthy. I’ve had worse nosebleeds from seasonal changes.”\n“Yeah,” Bram said. “And I’ve had worse ideas than this hold. Doesn’t make them good.”\nFrom the crowd, a Temple Observer screamed, “Let the worthy hold! Let the unworthy fall!”\nBram snapped his head toward the sound. “Hey—shut up. If we’re picking who falls, you’re first. That’s democracy.”\n“Ignore them,” Tiffani said. “Move.”\n“Spotters, call markers.”\n“Jhace tremor increasing!”\n“Corven tremor increasing!”\n“Senne vein-line onset!”\nBram barked at the evac line like a foreman with a broken sense of humor. “Keep moving! Nobody stops! You stop, you become part of the debris! Count them out!”\nMerra’s voice cut through the dust. “Alternate route open. Final evacuation commencing.”\n“Count them,” Tiffani said. “Call numbers.”\nJhace’s breath came tight. “One hundred.”\n“State exit count,” Tiffani said, “not your suffering.”\n“One hundred. Out.”\nDaved’s voice came rough through the grit. “They’re not out.”\nJhace’s head snapped. “No.”\n“They’re not out.”\n“Daved—state the variable,” Tiffani said. “How many still inside?”\n“Enough.”\nTiffani didn’t blink. “Extend. Thirty heartbeats. No more.”\n“Tiff—” Jhace started.\n“I am not debating physics with the dead in the hallway,” she said. “Extend.”\nThirty heartbeats passed like a slow knife.\nMerra shouted from the corridor mouth, “Final group emerging. Ten heartbeats!”\n“Ten heartbeats is still ten,” Tiffani said. “Move.”\nDaved’s hands tightened. “Hold.”\n“Daved!” Jhace lunged a step closer.\n“Hold.”\nMerra’s slate slapped stone. “Clear! All civilians safe!”\n“Terminate all operators,” Tiffani snapped. “Immediate coordinated shutdown.”\nLights guttered and went out—one by one—except Daved’s.\n“Can’t,” Daved said.\n“Daved. Breathe,” Jhace said, too close, too desperate.\n“Can’t.”\n“Terminate,” Tiffani said. “That is not a negotiation.”\n“They’re not out.”\n“They are out. Merra confirmed. Terminate now.”\nDaved’s jaw worked like he was chewing copper. “Already crossed.”\nBram shoved his way in with words because silence was making everyone honest. “Daved—buddy—love the commitment, hate the method acting.”\n“Bram, shut up,” Jhace hissed.\n“I can’t,” Bram said. “It’s a disorder.”\nTiffani stepped in until Daved had to see her. “Look at me. If you extend past this, you cross Stage Three threshold.”\nDaved’s eyes stayed flat. “Already crossed.”\nJhace’s throat tightened. “He locked.”\n“I see it,” Tiffani said.\nThey hauled Daved back from the structure like pulling a hook out of meat.\n“Terminate!” Tiffani shouted.\nThe light finally cut.\nDaved folded like a tool dropped from numb hands.\nTiffani’s voice went small for one syllable. “I know.”\n\nThey carried Daved out on a door panel when the Hall finally stopped shifting enough to allow hands near him.\nHis eyes were open.\nHis body was too still.\nTiffani walked beside the makeshift stretcher, writing with one hand, wiping dust from her face with the other.\nTiffani sat alone in the documentation room after they carried Daved out.\nHer slate lay open to the cost taxonomy. Stage Three. Locked hold. Autonomic failure.\nShe had written it perfectly. Every marker. Every heartbeat. Every outcome.\nHer hand moved to add the summary line.\nShe stared at the slate. At the line that read: Casualties: 3.\nShe tried to write the justification. The calculation. The reason extending duration had been correct under the circumstances.\nThe chalk snapped.\nHer hands shook because she'd been awake too long and her calf cramped—no magic required. The chalk snapped anyway.\nShe stared at her shaking fingers.\nShe didn’t log her tremor. She didn’t log that she’d miscounted heartbeats during the hold because her vision had blurred. She didn’t log that she’d wanted to ring the termination bell at one-eighty but hadn’t, because Daved’s eyes had asked her not to.\nSome data points weren’t for the ledger.\nSome were just for carrying.\n\n---\n\nJhace’s tremor made his jaw ache.\nHe'd wanted method like a wrench—turn it, get the same result. Instead he got a mouthful of pennies and hands that wouldn't stop.\nMethod had just asked for a price it didn’t have the right to demand.\n---\n"
        },
        "create_time": 1768402033.8782036,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "4b84f5cb-07ab-40fd-827c-5d7c3104f502",

          "aggregate_result": {

Ten heartbeats!”\n“Ten heartbeats is still ten,” Tiffani said. “Move.”\nDaved’s hands tightened. “Hold.”\n“Daved!” Jhace lunged a step closer.\n“Hold.”\nMerra’s slate slapped stone. “Clear! All civilians safe!”\n“Terminate all operators,” Tiffani snapped. “Immediate coordinated shutdown.”\nLights guttered and went out—one by one—except Daved’s.\n“Can’t,” Daved said.\n“Daved. Breathe,” Jhace said, too close, too desperate.\n“Can’t.”\n“Terminate,” Tiffani said. “That is not a negotiation.”\n“They’re not out.”\n“They are out. Merra confirmed. Terminate now.”\nDaved’s jaw worked like he was chewing copper. “Already crossed.”\nBram shoved his way in with words because silence was making everyone honest. “Daved—buddy—love the commitment, hate the method acting.”\n“Bram, shut up,” Jhace hissed.\n“I can’t,” Bram said. “It’s a disorder.”\nTiffani stepped in until Daved had to see her. “Look at me. If you extend past this, you cross Stage Three threshold.”\nDaved’s eyes stayed flat. “Already crossed.”\nJhace’s throat tightened. “He locked.”\n“I see it,” Tiffani said.\nThey hauled Daved back from the structure like pulling a hook out of meat.\n“Terminate!” Tiffani shouted.\nThe light finally cut.\nDaved folded like a tool dropped from numb hands.\nTiffani’s voice went small for one syllable. “I know.”\n\nThey carried Daved out on a door panel when the Hall finally stopped shifting enough to allow hands near him.\nHis eyes were open.\nHis body was too still.\nTiffani walked beside the makeshift stretcher, writing with one hand, wiping dust from her face with the other.\nTiffani sat alone in the documentation room after they carried Daved out.\nHer slate lay open to the cost taxonomy. Stage Three. Locked hold. Autonomic failure.\nShe had written it perfectly. Every marker. Every heartbeat. Every outcome.\nHer hand moved to add the summary line.\nShe stared at the slate. At the line that read: Casualties: 3.\nShe tried to write the justification. The calculation. The reason extending duration had been correct under the circumstances.\nThe chalk snapped.\nHer hands shook because she'd been awake too long and her calf cramped—no magic required. The chalk snapped anyway.\nShe stared at her shaking fingers.\nShe didn’t log her tremor. She didn’t log that she’d miscounted heartbeats during the hold because her vision had blurred. She didn’t log that she’d wanted to ring the termination bell at one-eighty but hadn’t, because Daved’s eyes had asked her not to.\nSome data points weren’t for the ledger.\nSome were just for carrying.\n\n---\n\nJhace’s tremor made his jaw ache.\nHe'd wanted method like a wrench—turn it, get the same result. Instead he got a mouthful of pennies and hands that wouldn't stop.\nMethod had just asked for a price it didn’t have the right to demand.\n---\n",
            "messages": [],
            "status": "success"
          },

    "4c644757-9115-4b97-b108-7d249cd7b383": {
      "children": [
        "87fe379a-22c2-42b8-ba61-f5f1e56ba0ac"
      ],
      "id": "4c644757-9115-4b97-b108-7d249cd7b383",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": "final",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "## CHAPTER 11: HUNGER — VARIANT A\n\nThe hunger wasn’t metaphor.\n\nIt lived behind Jhace’s sternum as a warmth that never fully left after the first hold. A phantom heat that flared whenever the grid-hum shifted, whenever a bell tried to ring, whenever someone screamed the word *hold* like it was prayer.\n\nTiffani called it activation pressure.\n\nJhace called it wanting.\n\nHe sat beside Daved’s cot in the infirmary again, watching the older man’s hands twitch in sleep. The room still smelled like antiseptic resin and copper you couldn’t wash off. The cot frame creaked with every small movement, like even rest was load.\n\nDaved’s eyes opened.\n\nHe didn’t need a slate this time. His voice was still rough, but it existed.\n\n“Feels… good,” Daved rasped.\n\nJhace frowned. “What does?”\n\n“The start,” Daved said. “Chest heat. When it locks in. For a heartbeat you feel… useful. Strong. Like the city can’t touch you.”\n\nJhace’s tremor worsened, like his body agreed without permission.\n\n“And then,” Daved continued, eyes flat, “it takes you apart.”\n\nTiffani wrote without looking up. “Addictive response mixed with autonomic threat. Noted.”\n\nJhace’s frown deepened. “What does.”\n\n“The reinforcement loop,” Tiffani said. “Chest heat. Perceived control. Reduced panic during manifestation. It becomes desire.”\n\nDaved’s eyes narrowed. “Liar.”\n\nJhace’s head snapped. “What.”\n\n“You want it,” Daved said. “The hold. You want to feel it again.”\n\nJhace’s jaw worked. His hands stayed jammed under his thighs like that could pin the tremor down. “I don’t want to chase the rumor.”\n\n“You want it anyway.”\n\nTiffani’s pen didn’t pause. “Denial expected. The body remembers the moment it stopped failing.”\n\nJhace swallowed. Copper sat on his tongue like a coin he couldn’t spit out.\n\n“Don’t make it holy,” Daved rasped.\n\n“No one’s making it holy,” Bram said from the doorway, because Bram couldn’t let silence stand there unchallenged. “They’re making it *useful.* Much worse.”\n\nTiffani didn’t look up. “We’re not discussing theology. We’re discussing compulsion.”\n\nJhace stared at Daved’s hands. The tremor there was smaller than it had been on Tide one, but it existed. Proof the body came back wrong, even when it came back.\n\nDaved watched Jhace watch.\n\n“You think method will save you,” Daved said.\n\n“It will keep us alive,” Tiffani said.\n\nDaved’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. “Alive enough to do it again.”\n\nJhace felt the warmth behind his sternum shift at the thought. Not flare. Just a quiet readiness.\n\nHe hated that readiness.\n\nTiffani finally lifted her eyes. “If you feel the pressure rising, you report it. Out loud. You don’t sit there and pretend you’re made of iron.”\n\nJhace’s throat tightened. “I’m not pretending.”\n\n“Yes you are,” Daved said. “You’ve been pretending since Junction Seven.”\n\nJhace went still for three heartbeats. The tremor didn’t.\n\nTiffani’s voice stayed flat. “We decide when we deploy. Not the heat. Not the crowd. Not the Crown.”\n\nJhace’s gaze stayed forward. “We decide.”\n\n“And when you decide wrong?” Daved asked.\n\n“We document it,” Tiffani said. “We learn. We don’t repeat.”\n\n“Some mistakes don’t give you time to learn,” Daved said.\n\nOutside, a bell rang—different cadence. Not collapse.\n\nA drill signal.\n\nCrown-mandated training.\n\nJhace’s chest heat flared anyway. Eager. Stupid.\n\nHe hated himself for that flare.\n\nHe stood, hands shaking, and went to the training yard because refusal would make them criminals.\n\nTiffani followed, slate in hand, limping slightly from too many stands on stone floors.\n\nDaved watched them go and muttered, almost too quiet to hear:\n\n“Hungry hands hold until they break.”\n\nJhace pretended he didn’t hear.\n\nHe did.\n\n---"
          ]
        },

    "4dfe4f21-a58e-4edc-b590-f26b8545bd3b": {
      "children": [
        "01f2abd9-175c-4a2b-843f-aea6fa3e1a9a"
      ],
      "id": "4dfe4f21-a58e-4edc-b590-f26b8545bd3b",
      "message": {

        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "451: CHAPTER 6: WITNESS\n452: Brother Corran didn’t wear Temple colors.\n453: The first thing Jhace noticed was the badge—bright, clean, pinned where it couldn't be missed. The second was how the man didn't look tired. Not even a little.\n454: Corran’s eyes went straight to Jhace’s hands.\n455: “For the record, I’m not here to stop you,” Corran said, before anyone asked.\n456: Bram hovered by the door like a lock that wanted to be useful.\n457: Tiffani set her slate down deliberately. “Then why are you here?”\n458: “Because you’re real,” Corran said. “And because real things attract predators.”\n459: Jhace’s mouth tightened. “Crown.”\n460: Corran’s gaze didn’t flinch. “Yes. Also Guild. Also Temple. Anyone who thinks they can turn you into a lever.”\n461: Bram’s voice came dry. “Ah yes. The holy trinity of getting fucked: prayer, production, paperwork.”\n462: Tiffani shot him a look. “Bram.”\n463: He raised his hands. “What? I’m helping.”\n464: Bram gestured toward the door. “Temple says you’re divine. Guild says you’re labor. Crown says you’re property. Everybody’s trying to fuck the same lever.”\n465: He pulled a folded packet from his coat and set it on Jhace’s bench. Paper. Old style. Archive weight.\n466: “Stage Three cases,” Corran said. “Documented. Not rumors.”\n467: Tiffani’s hands went still. “How many?”\n468: “Enough.” Corran’s voice was flat, and that flatness carried exhaustion. “Most die. A few don’t. The ones who don’t… wish they had.”\n469: Jhace’s tremor ticked up at the word die. He forced his hands flat on the bench. “Why give this to us?”\n470: Corran hesitated. That hesitation was the crack in the Crown.\n471: “Because someone above me wants a registry,” he said. “Names. Locations. Deployment authority. They’re calling it order. It’s control.”\n472: Tiffani’s eyes narrowed. “Who?”\n473: Corran looked toward the door as if the walls might report him. “Administrator Corlexi. Infrastructure Compliance.”\n474: Bram gave a low whistle. “Clean hands. Clean coat. Probably wipes his ass with forms.”\n475: Jhace stared at the packet. “So you’re here to warn us.”\n476: “I’m here to give you data,” Corran corrected. “If you want to fight control, you need numbers. Not speeches.”\n477: Tiffani opened the packet and read, eyes moving fast.\n478: Jhace watched her face tighten at certain lines—durations, costs, outcomes. She didn’t gasp. She didn’t dramatize. She just absorbed.\n479: “This case,” she said, tapping a page. “Stage Three survival. How?”\n480: Corran’s mouth went thin. “He didn’t terminate. He couldn’t. The hold locked. Autonomic failure started and the manifestation kept running anyway. The structure held until it didn’t matter.”\n481: Jhace felt cold crawl up his spine despite the workshop heat. “Locked hold.”\n482: Corran nodded. “It’s not a tool you put down when you’re done. Sometimes it’s a hook in your chest that drags you until something breaks.”\n483: Tiffani’s pen scratched. Stage Three: autonomic failure. Locked hold possible. Termination may fail.\n484: Bram shifted. “You said predators.”\n485: Corran’s eyes met Bram’s. “Tallec is filing complaints. Says you’re obstructing industry. Says your protocols slow production.”\n486: Jhace’s jaw clenched so hard it hurt.\n487: Corran leaned closer, voice low. “Here’s the fracture you wanted. I don’t like Corlexi’s registry. I don’t like Temple’s sermons. I don’t like Guild’s schedules. But I like fewer dead.”\n488: He straightened. “If you build methodology, build it fast. Because the Crown is already drafting theirs. And theirs won’t care what it costs you.”\n489: He turned to leave.\n490: Tiffani called after him. “Why risk telling us this?”\n491: Corran paused at the door. “Because Junction Seven wasn’t the first ignored report,” he said. “And I’m tired of pretending ignorance is the same as innocence.”\n492: Then he was gone.\n493: Jhace’s hands stayed flat on the workbench. “If this registry happens…” He swallowed. “I’m not doing it for paper.”\n494: Bram exhaled slowly. “And because guilt is cheaper than funerals. Not by much. But cheaper.”\n495: Tiffani climbed the workshop stairs slower than she had a Tide ago. Her right knee caught on the third step—not pain, just resistance, like the joint was learning to refuse.\n496: She stopped on the landing. Put her palm to the wall until the world steadied.\n497: The knee had locked twice this Anneal. Not during a hold. During stairs. During the parts where bodies were supposed to cooperate.\n498: She’d logged operator tremor. Operator vein-lines. Operator autonomic failure.\n499: She had not logged her own joint learning to refuse weight.\n500: Because if she logged it, she’d have to adjust. Delegate. Sit down.\n501: And if she sat down, someone else would coordinate from the deck. Someone else would make the calls.\n502: She took the next step anyway.\n503: She didn’t log it.\n504: Some costs weren’t manifestation. Some were just labor.\n505: Jhace’s hands shook on the bench like they were trying to answer him.\n506: ---\n"
        },
        "create_time": 1768401541.2262583,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "4dfe4f21-a58e-4edc-b590-f26b8545bd3b",
        "metadata": {
          "aggregate_result": {
            "code": "for idx in range(chap6_start, chap7_start):\n    print(f\"{idx}: {lines[idx-1]}\")\n",
            "end_time": 1768401543.4287834,
            "final_expression_output": null,
            "in_kernel_exception": null,
            "jupyter_messages": [
              {
                "content": {
                  "execution_state": "busy"
                },
                "msg_type": "status",
                "parent_header": {
                  "msg_id": "4b095b1e-4173e9198116e6ec70ba131c_167_9",
                  "version": "5.3"
                }
              },
              {
                "msg_type": "execute_input",
                "parent_header": {
                  "msg_id": "4b095b1e-4173e9198116e6ec70ba131c_167_9",
                  "version": "5.3"
                }
              },
              {
                "content": {
                  "name": "stdout",
                  "text": "451: CHAPTER 6: WITNESS\n452: Brother Corran didn’t wear Temple colors.\n453: The first thing Jhace noticed was the badge—bright, clean, pinned where it couldn't be missed. The second was how the man didn't look tired. Not even a little.\n454: Corran’s eyes went straight to Jhace’s hands.\n455: “For the record, I’m not here to stop you,” Corran said, before anyone asked.\n456: Bram hovered by the door like a lock that wanted to be useful.\n457: Tiffani set her slate down deliberately. “Then why are you here?”\n458: “Because you’re real,” Corran said. “And because real things attract predators.”\n459: Jhace’s mouth tightened. “Crown.”\n460: Corran’s gaze didn’t flinch. “Yes. Also Guild. Also Temple. Anyone who thinks they can turn you into a lever.”\n461: Bram’s voice came dry. “Ah yes. The holy trinity of getting fucked: prayer, production, paperwork.”\n462: Tiffani shot him a look. “Bram.”\n463: He raised his hands. “What? I’m helping.”\n464: Bram gestured toward the door. “Temple says you’re divine. Guild says you’re labor. Crown says you’re property. Everybody’s trying to fuck the same lever.”\n465: He pulled a folded packet from his coat and set it on Jhace’s bench. Paper. Old style. Archive weight.\n466: “Stage Three cases,” Corran said. “Documented. Not rumors.”\n467: Tiffani’s hands went still. “How many?”\n468: “Enough.” Corran’s voice was flat, and that flatness carried exhaustion. “Most die. A few don’t. The ones who don’t… wish they had.”\n469: Jhace’s tremor ticked up at the word die. He forced his hands flat on the bench. “Why give this to us?”\n470: Corran hesitated. That hesitation was the crack in the Crown.\n471: “Because someone above me wants a registry,” he said. “Names. Locations. Deployment authority. They’re calling it order. It’s control.”\n472: Tiffani’s eyes narrowed. “Who?”\n473: Corran looked toward the door as if the walls might report him. “Administrator Corlexi. Infrastructure Compliance.”\n474: Bram gave a low whistle. “Clean hands. Clean coat. Probably wipes his ass with forms.”\n475: Jhace stared at the packet. “So you’re here to warn us.”\n476: “I’m here to give you data,” Corran corrected. “If you want to fight control, you need numbers. Not speeches.”\n477: Tiffani opened the packet and read, eyes moving fast.\n478: Jhace watched her face tighten at certain lines—durations, costs, outcomes. She didn’t gasp. She didn’t dramatize. She just absorbed.\n479: “This case,” she said, tapping a page. “Stage Three survival. How?”\n480: Corran’s mouth went thin. “He didn’t terminate. He couldn’t. The hold locked. Autonomic failure started and the manifestation kept running anyway. The structure held until it didn’t matter.”\n481: Jhace felt cold crawl up his spine despite the workshop heat. “Locked hold.”\n482: Corran nodded. “It’s not a tool you put down when you’re done. Sometimes it’s a hook in your chest that drags you until something breaks.”\n483: Tiffani’s pen scratched. Stage Three: autonomic failure. Locked hold possible. Termination may fail.\n484: Bram shifted. “You said predators.”\n485: Corran’s eyes met Bram’s. “Tallec is filing complaints. Says you’re obstructing industry. Says your protocols slow production.”\n486: Jhace’s jaw clenched so hard it hurt.\n487: Corran leaned closer, voice low. “Here’s the fracture you wanted. I don’t like Corlexi’s registry. I don’t like Temple’s sermons. I don’t like Guild’s schedules. But I like fewer dead.”\n488: He straightened. “If you build methodology, build it fast. Because the Crown is already drafting theirs. And theirs won’t care what it costs you.”\n489: He turned to leave.\n490: Tiffani called after him. “Why risk telling us this?”\n491: Corran paused at the door. “Because Junction Seven wasn’t the first ignored report,” he said. “And I’m tired of pretending ignorance is the same as innocence.”\n492: Then he was gone.\n493: Jhace’s hands stayed flat on the workbench. “If this registry happens…” He swallowed. “I’m not doing it for paper.”\n494: Bram exhaled slowly. “And because guilt is cheaper than funerals. Not by much. But cheaper.”\n495: Tiffani climbed the workshop stairs slower than she had a Tide ago. Her right knee caught on the third step—not pain, just resistance, like the joint was learning to refuse.\n496: She stopped on the landing. Put her palm to the wall until the world steadied.\n497: The knee had locked twice this Anneal. Not during a hold. During stairs. During the parts where bodies were supposed to cooperate.\n498: She’d logged operator tremor. Operator vein-lines. Operator autonomic failure.\n499: She had not logged her own joint learning to refuse weight.\n500: Because if she logged it, she’d have to adjust. Delegate. Sit down.\n501: And if she sat down, someone else would coordinate from the deck. Someone else would make the calls.\n502: She took the next step anyway.\n503: She didn’t log it.\n504: Some costs weren’t manifestation. Some were just labor.\n505: Jhace’s hands shook on the bench like they were trying to answer him.\n506: ---\n"
                },
                "msg_type": "stream",
                "parent_header": {
                  "msg_id": "4b095b1e-4173e9198116e6ec70ba131c_167_9",
                  "version": "5.3"
                }

    "4f8fc9ad-d543-45da-bd02-54ee4a2381f1": {
      "children": [
        "a5c0c607-9425-42ee-96d5-e94cfa136f17"
      ],
      "id": "4f8fc9ad-d543-45da-bd02-54ee4a2381f1",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": "final",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "Got it. This fills the one gap from **our compiled 1–13**: Chapter 12 is now **from your pasted text**, not from base.\n\nTwo issues before you lock it:\n\n1) **Time-unit violation**\n- You wrote: **“Two minutes max.”**\n- That breaks your hard rule (no real-world time units).\n- Clean swap: **“One-twenty heartbeats max.”** (keeps the intent, stays in-system.)\n\n2) **Real-world reference**\n- You wrote: **“Jesus—”**\n- That’s a real-world religious proper noun. If you’re enforcing “no Earth bleed,” it should go.\n- Clean swap: **“Fuck—”** (or any in-world oath you prefer).\n\nIf you want the lock-ready Chapter 12 with **only those two fixes and no other changes**, here it is:\n\n## CHAPTER 12: METHOD\n\nThe alert came at Tide 21 like an insult.\n\nIt was a narrow failure—no mass collapse, no Hall, no warehouse full of people.\n\nA residential stairwell in Sector Nine. A narrow stone throat that had been patched too many times since the Piercing. A single child trapped beneath a fallen landing slab, crying until her voice turned hoarse.\n\nTiffani arrived first. Her limp was worse now, but she moved like it didn’t matter. She scanned the structure in three heartbeats. Load paths. Weak seams. The slab’s weight distribution.\n\n“Clear lane,” she ordered as the others reached the site. “Spotters assigned. Wedges first.”\n\nShe pointed left, then froze. “No—right. Right corridor first. Sorry.”\n\nThe apology tasted like rust. She didn’t repeat it.\n\nJhace’s hands shook so hard he had to jam them into his coat pockets to stop them from betraying him in front of the crowd.\n\nHe could feel the doubled harmonic through the stairwell stone. Deep. Constant. The grid had never recovered. It had just learned to fail slower.\n\nBram arrived with Crown guards and a face that said he’d tried, briefly, to be serious and failed on contact.\n\n“Registry protocol,” he said. “Two licensed operators inbound.”\n\nTiffani didn’t look at him. “They’ll be too late.”\n\n“Cool,” Bram said. “Love the part where the Crown schedules rescue like it’s a delivery window.”\n\n“Clear lane,” Tiffani ordered. “Spotters assigned. Wedges first.”\n\nShe looked at the sagging landing slab, the trapped space beneath it, the child’s choking turning into wet silence. Her voice tightened into a blade.\n\n“One operator. One-twenty heartbeats max. We brace, we lift, we extract. Terminate immediately after.”\n\n“Then who?” Jhace said.\n\n“Not you.”\n\n“Then who.”\n\n“The child is choking, Jhace.”\n\nJhace’s tremor rattled against his bones like a tool trying to escape. “I decide.”\n\nBram’s breath punched out. “Fuck—no, don’t—okay, you’re doing it. You’re doing the thing. Great. Fantastic. I hate this.”\n\nJhace stepped in. Copper flooded his mouth like blood.\n\nFor a heartbeat, nothing happened.\n\nThen the hold locked.\n\nThe stairwell’s doubled harmonic snapped into alignment with his chest, like a collar seating in the wrong notch.\n\nTiffani’s voice cut clean through the noise. “Wedges—now. Lift on my count. Three heartbeats. Pull.”\n\nBoots scraped. Wedges hammered. Stone complained.\n\n“Three,” Tiffani said. “Two. One. Now—pull!”\n\nHands reached into the dark. The child came out gasping, eyes wide, spit and dust and life.\n\n“Terminate,” Tiffani said.\n\nJhace didn’t move.\n\n“Terminate,” she repeated, like command could override biology.\n\nSilence.\n\nBram’s voice cracked into the gap. “He’s not moving.”\n\n“Jhace.” Tiffani stepped in close. “Move.”\n\nBram hovered like he wanted to shake him and knew better. “Say something. Blink. Spit. Insult me. Anything.”\n\n“Stop talking,” Tiffani snapped.\n\n“I can’t,” Bram said. “It’s how I pray.”\n\nJhace could still see. Still hear. Still count.\n\nHe stared at his hands, waiting for tremor.\n\nThere was no tremor.\n\nThere was nothing.\n\nHe tried to will a finger. Nothing.\n\nPanic hit—animal, immediate, nothing to do with heat or holding.\n\nHe tried to shout. Nothing left his throat.\n\nHe tried to jerk a shoulder. Nothing.\n\nHe tried to blink faster—signal—anything.\n\nHis lids moved on their own schedule. Slow. Unbothered.\n\nThe body was there. The controls were there. The link was gone.\n\nThe coal behind his sternum pulsed once. Steady. Patient. Still ready.\n\nReady to hold.\n\nBut he couldn’t lift a finger to tell it no.\n\nOnly the wanting stayed—locked in meat that wouldn’t answer.\n\nTiffani moved into his field of view and knelt like she was inspecting a fracture line. Ink and copper sat on her hands; she smelled like slate dust and hours without sleep.\n\n“Blink once for yes,” she said. “Twice for no.”\n\nHe blinked once.\n\n“Do you still feel the doubled harmonic through the stone?”\n\nOnce.\n\n“Can you tell me where it dipped before Tallow Street?”\n\nOnce. He could see it like a chart behind his eyes—deep note, then the metallic taste, then the split.\n\nTiffani slid her slate into his line of sight. Two columns. LOCATION. HEARTBEATS. Her writing stayed precise even as her knuckles whitened.\n\n“Can you predict where it fails next?”\n\nOnce. Not a comfort. A capability.\n\nTiffani’s mouth tightened. “Then you’re not finished.” She didn’t say it like hope. She said it like reassignment.\n\nJhace stared at his hands, waiting for tremor. Nothing.\n\nIt was worse than shaking. Silence in the muscles felt like a lie.\n\nTiffani dropped to her knees beside him, slate already out, chalk already moving.\n\n“Stage Four,” she said, voice flat because panic was a luxury. “Complete motor loss. Locked hold with autonomic override. Cost: total paralysis below manifestation threshold.”\n\nBram swallowed. “You’re writing this down.”\n\n“Yes.”\n\n“Right now.”\n\n“Yes.”\n\n“While he’s—”\n\n“Yes,” Tiffani said, and her hand didn’t shake. “Because if I don’t, this becomes a story someone else gets to tell. And they will tell it wrong.”\n\nShe swallowed once, hard, then kept writing.\n\nWhen the crowd noise finally thinned and the guards stopped pretending they weren’t watching, Tiffani stood.\n\n“Corlexi will get what I give him,” she said.\n\nBram blinked. “Which is what, exactly—an instruction manual with a hidden knife?”\n\n“A method,” Tiffani said. “With constraints. With brakes. Without permission to repeat this.”\n\n“So… a leash on the leash,” Bram said.\n\n“Something like that.”\n\n“You’re terrifying when you’re calm,” Bram said.\n\n“I’m not calm,” Tiffani said.\n\n“Right,” Bram said, voice softer than his jokes. “You’re just… controlled. Like a blade.”"
          ]
        },

    "4fd417bb-0ca4-4b7b-b214-ba6309f6e33b": {
      "children": [
        "e17a9823-d482-40bf-8812-8eabf8e36302"
      ],
      "id": "4fd417bb-0ca4-4b7b-b214-ba6309f6e33b",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": "final",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "# N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS  \n## CHAPTER 6: WITNESS — VARIANT A\n\n---\n\nBrother Corran didn’t wear Temple colors.\n\nThat was the first thing Jhace noticed.\n\nNot the lack of robe—plenty of men worked without ceremony. It was the badge. Bright. Clean. Pinned high where it couldn’t be missed. A symbol meant to make doors open and mouths close.\n\nThe second thing was how Corran didn’t look tired.\n\nNo grit in his hairline. No dust ground into the seams of his coat. No red rim around the eyes from too many bells and not enough breath.\n\nLike he’d been watching collapse from behind glass.\n\nJhace’s workshop smelled like quench water and iron filings and blood that had dried wrong.\n\nTiffani sat at the bench with her slate open to the route failure.\n\nThree.\n\nShe’d underlined the spotter rule twice. The chalk grooves were deep enough to catch a fingernail.\n\nJhace stood with his palms flat on the wood, trying to pin his hands to something that wouldn’t shake.\n\nIt didn’t work.\n\nCorran’s eyes went straight to Jhace’s tremor.\n\n“For the record,” Corran said, before anyone asked anything, “I’m not here to stop you.”\n\nBram Kerris hovered by the door, half inside, half ready to vanish. He’d stopped smiling two bells ago.\n\nTiffani didn’t stand. She didn’t offer a crate. She didn’t perform courtesy for badges.\n\n“Then why are you here?” she asked.\n\n“Because you’re real,” Corran said. “And because real things attract predators.”\n\nBram let out a dry little breath. “There it is.”\n\nJhace’s jaw tightened. “Crown.”\n\nCorran didn’t flinch. “Yes. Also Guild. Also Temple. Anyone who thinks they can turn you into a lever.”\n\nBram lifted his hands in a slow, mocking blessing. “The holy trinity. Prayer. Production. Paperwork.”\n\nTiffani shot him a look without looking away from Corran. “Names.”\n\nCorran stepped farther into the workshop like he had the right to breathe the heat. He didn’t touch anything. Didn’t smear dust into their work. Clean hands stayed clean.\n\n“Administrator Corlexi,” he said. “Infrastructure Compliance.”\n\nJhace felt the tremor tick higher at the name. Not fear.\n\nAnger.\n\n“He was already here,” Jhace said. “He already offered.”\n\nCorran nodded once. “Voluntary. For now.”\n\nTiffani’s pen moved. She didn’t ask permission. The scratch of it was the only sound that behaved.\n\nCorran’s eyes flicked to her slate. To the line that said **Casualties: 3**. To the break in chalk that still lay on the bench like a snapped tooth.\n\n“Three,” he said.\n\nTiffani didn’t correct him. Didn’t justify it.\n\n“Secondary beam shear,” she said. Flat. “Map was wrong.”\n\nCorran’s mouth tightened. “Maps are always wrong. People forget that.”\n\nJhace tasted copper at the back of his tongue.\n\nNot flood. Not activation.\n\nJust the low, stubborn tang that had been living there since the sky split.\n\nCorran reached into his coat and pulled out a folded packet.\n\nPaper. Old weight. Archive stock.\n\nNot a slate page. Not a workshop note. Something that had been handled by offices and sealed by hands that never touched rubble.\n\nHe set it on the bench between them.\n\nTiffani didn’t touch it immediately.\n\n“What is that?” she asked.\n\n“Stage Three cases,” Corran said. “Documented. Not rumors.”\n\nJhace’s chest heat stirred behind his sternum like a coal shifting in ash.\n\nTiffani’s eyes sharpened. “How many?”\n\n“Enough.” Corran’s voice stayed level. “Most die. A few don’t. The ones who don’t… wish they had.”\n\nBram muttered, “That’s comforting.”\n\nTiffani opened the packet.\n\nHer eyes moved fast. Too fast. She read like she was trying to outrun what she already suspected.\n\nJhace watched her face change in small, controlled fractures: jaw tightening. A blink held one heartbeat too long. A breath she didn’t fully release.\n\nNumbers did that to her. Not because she loved them.\n\nBecause they didn’t let her lie.\n\n“This one,” she said, tapping a page. “Survival past lock. How?”\n\nCorran didn’t look proud of having an answer.\n\n“He didn’t terminate,” he said. “He couldn’t. The hold locked. Autonomic failure started and the manifestation kept running anyway.”\n\nJhace felt cold crawl up his spine despite the heat of the forge.\n\nLocked hold.\n\nThe phrase sat in his skull like a tool you couldn’t put down.\n\n“What happens to the body?” Jhace asked.\n\nCorran didn’t hesitate. “Seizure. Blackout. Respiratory failure. Heart irregularity. Sometimes the light keeps going while the person is already dead.”\n\nBram swallowed. The sound was loud in the quiet.\n\nTiffani’s pen scratched:\n\n**Stage Three marker:** termination failure possible.  \n**Locked hold:** manifestation continues under autonomic collapse.  \n**Outcome:** survival rare. Post-hold function catastrophic.\n\nJhace stared at his hands.\n\nThey shook, yes. But they still listened when he told them to clench.\n\nHe imagined a Tide where they didn’t.\n\nA Tide where his body became a platform for the hold to continue without him.\n\nHis mouth went dry and copper at the same time.\n\nTiffani looked up from the packet. “These cases—were they all contact holds?”\n\nCorran nodded. “Hands. Forearms. Sometimes full-body brace when they couldn’t find a seam.”\n\n“And the hunger?” Tiffani asked, like saying the word didn’t make it true, but not saying it didn’t make it go away.\n\nCorran’s gaze flicked to Jhace’s sternum. Like he could see the coal.\n\n“Yes,” he said. “They describe it differently. But it’s the same thing. The body starts wanting the hold.”\n\nJhace felt the warmth behind his ribs tighten.\n\nA warm twist. A gentle pull.\n\nNot pain.\n\nInvitation.\n\nHis tremor eased for a single heartbeat.\n\nJhace froze.\n\nHe hadn’t moved. Hadn’t braced. Hadn’t held anything.\n\nThe tremor had just… softened.\n\nHe put a hand to his chest on instinct.\n\nThe warmth answered, pleased.\n\nThen the tremor came back, angry, like it didn’t like being noticed.\n\nTiffani saw the motion.\n\nHer eyes narrowed. “Did it—”\n\nJhace didn’t lie. “For one heartbeat.”\n\nTiffani wrote without looking down:\n\n**Hunger interaction:** tremor relief noted on chest contact.  \n**Potential reinforcement loop.**\n\nBram exhaled slowly. “That’s not a tool. That’s a trap.”\n\nCorran watched them like he’d been watching this happen in other rooms for too many Tides.\n\n“I’m not here to scare you,” Corran said. “You’re already scared. I’m here because Corlexi is building language. Registry language. Authorization language. Ownership language.”\n\nTiffani’s voice went hard. “So are we.”\n\n“Yes,” Corran said. “But your language keeps people alive. His language keeps people manageable.”\n\nBram snorted. “Same difference if you’re the one getting managed.”\n\nCorran didn’t rise to it. “Tallec is filing complaints,” he said.\n\nJhace’s jaw clenched so hard it hurt. “About what.”\n\n“Obstruction,” Corran said. “Interference with production schedules. Deliberate slowdowns. She says your protocols are costing the Crown output.”\n\nTiffani’s mouth went flat. “We saved two hundred nineteen.”\n\n“Numbers don’t matter when they want control,” Corran said. “Only leverage matters. Tallec gives them leverage. Corlexi gives them structure.”\n\nBram leaned off the doorframe, shoulders rolling like he was getting ready to fight paperwork with teeth. “So what’s the play, Brother.”\n\nCorran’s eyes met Bram’s. Not friendly. Not hostile.\n\nTired.\n\n“I shouldn’t be here,” Corran said. “That’s the truth. If Corlexi knew I brought you this packet, I’d be reassigned to somewhere quiet and full of locks.”\n\n“Why do it, then?” Tiffani asked.\n\nCorran hesitated.\n\nThat hesitation was the crack in the badge.\n\n“Because Junction Seven wasn’t the first ignored report,” he said. “And I’m tired of watching people pretend ignorance is the same as innocence.”\n\nBram let out a short laugh. It wasn’t humor. It was recognition. “Yeah. That’s the city’s favorite lie.”\n\nCorran straightened. “Build your methodology fast,” he said. “Because the Crown is already drafting theirs. And theirs won’t care what it costs you.”\n\nJhace stared at the packet. At the documented deaths. At the survival cases that weren’t really survival.\n\nHe thought of Senne holding until the bell told her to stop.\n\nHe thought of Tiffani ringing termination anyway, even when she could see the pocket.\n\nHe thought of his own hand going to his sternum and feeling the hunger purr.\n\nTiffani closed the packet carefully. Not gently.\n\nCarefully, like it was volatile.\n\n“What does Corlexi want?” she asked.\n\nCorran’s expression didn’t change. “Compliance. Names. Locations. Capabilities. Deployment authority through channels.”\n\n“And if we refuse.”\n\nCorran didn’t bother with comfort. “Then unregistered manifestation becomes a category. And categories become crimes.”\n\nBram’s smile tried to form and failed. “Cool. Saving children is now illegal without a permit.”\n\nTiffani looked at Jhace. “We finish the ledger.”\n\nJhace’s hands shook on the bench.\n\nHe could taste copper.\n\nHe could feel the grid through the floorboards. Doubled hum. New baseline. Predictable enough to plan around.\n\nPredictable enough to weaponize.\n\n“If this registry happens,” Jhace said, voice low, “I’m not holding for paper.”\n\n“I’m not asking you to,” Tiffani said.\n\nCorran moved toward the door.\n\nTiffani called after him. “Why risk telling us this?”\n\nCorran paused with his hand on the latch.\n\n“Because I like fewer dead,” he said. “Not because I’m good. Because I’m tired.”\n\nThen he was gone.\n\nThe workshop stayed hot.\n\nThe tremor stayed.\n\nTiffani opened her slate to a clean page and wrote a new heading in hard chalk:\n\n**Stage Three: Lock.**\n\nUnder it she wrote one line:\n\n**If termination fails, who cuts the hold.**\n\nJhace watched the words settle into place like a wedge driven home.\n\nHis chest heat answered, warm and patient.\n\nHungry.\n\n---"
          ]
        },

    "5068f602-3b03-40cf-bbb3-2eca57062998": {
      "children": [
        "9f1b1150-924d-466d-bdf2-4b9b68a7a821"
      ],
      "id": "5068f602-3b03-40cf-bbb3-2eca57062998",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "user"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "## CHAPTER 13: THE LEDGER\r\n\r\nTiffani wrote the first copy with her left hand because her right knee refused to lock and her right hand refused to stay still.\r\n\r\nIt wasn't Stage. It was the kind of fatigue that lives in joints after bad work—the kind you carry until your hands start lying to you.\r\n\r\nThe ink pooled anyway. Copper-gold dust clung to the nib, turning every line slightly wrong, as if the city itself wanted the record to smear.\r\n\r\nShe made three piles.\r\n\r\nFour, if she was honest.\r\n\r\nOne stack sat in clean paper with clean margins and clean language. Compliance phrasing. Definitions that sounded like protection while leaving room for ownership.\r\n\r\nShe wrote \"deployment authority\" first. Crossed it out. Rewrote: \"deployment procedure.\"\r\n\r\nSurface control. Let Corlexi read what he wanted to see.\r\n\r\nOne stack was tidy and dead. The kind of language that looks safe until somebody has to bleed under it.\r\n\r\nOne stack was short enough to memorize. Underface cadence. Commands. Counts. No soft words, no long sentences—something you could carry in your head when paper burned or got confiscated.\r\n\r\nThe last pile wasn't for anyone. It was her draft. Her private notes. The parts she could not justify on a form and would not confess out loud.\r\n\r\nBram stood in the doorway in his Crown coat like he'd stolen it off a corpse and decided it suited him.\r\n\r\nHis badge caught lamplight. Clean metal. Official weight.\r\n\r\nHe touched it once like testing if it would burn.\r\n\r\n\"You look like a priest,\" Tiffani said without looking up.\r\n\r\nBram glanced down at the coat. \"Don't you dare put that evil on me. Priests get paid to lie. I'm doing it recreationally.\"\r\n\r\n\"You're late.\"\r\n\r\n\"Yeah. Turns out paperwork travels faster than rescue.\" He leaned in, saw the piles. \"Oh good. Homework. My favorite form of punishment.\"\r\n\r\nTiffani slid the clean stack toward him first. \"This goes to Corlexi.\"\r\n\r\nBram's fingers hovered over the top page. \"This the 'please don't leash us' letter?\"\r\n\r\n\"This is the 'you get what I give you' packet,\" she said, tapping the pages hard enough to leave a smudge. Ink on her thumb now. Copper-tinted. \"Limits. Spotters. A way out when the body says no.\"\r\n\r\nBram squinted at the title line. \"And the part where we can tell them to go fuck themselves?\"\r\n\r\nTiffani didn't blink. \"Not included.\"\r\n\r\nBram's smile went sharp. \"That's either genius or suicidal.\"\r\n\r\n\"It's survivable.\" She turned the next page over and wrote a title line in the margin: DEPLOYMENT AUTHORITY. Then she crossed it out and rewrote: DEPLOYMENT PROCEDURE. \"He will read what he wants to read. I'm controlling the surface.\"\r\n\r\nBram exhaled through his nose. \"Ah. Compliance theater with a trapdoor.\"\r\n\r\n\"There is no trapdoor,\" she said. \"There are exits. Distributed.\"\r\n\r\nShe pushed the second stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: *Property is not a priority class.* *Operator veto.* *Refusal authority.* *No deployment without independent verification.*\r\n\r\nHer knee throbbed where it refused to straighten. She shifted weight without comment.\r\n\r\nSome costs weren't Stage markers. Some were just carrying the work.\r\n\r\nBram whistled low. \"This one's the 'burn the Crown down' packet.\"\r\n\r\n\"This one is the 'keep operators alive long enough to matter' packet.\" She slid it closer. \"Guild custody. You're the custodian.\"\r\n\r\nBram stared at the words *operator refusal authority* like they were a weapon and a confession. \"They catch me with this, I'm a corpse with a promotion.\"\r\n\r\n\"They catch you without it, you're already dead,\" Tiffani said. \"Just slower. With a better title.\"\r\n\r\nBram laughed, too loud for the room. \"God, I hate when you're right. It's like being corrected by a scalpel.\"\r\n\r\nTiffani's pen scratched again. \"Underface next.\"\r\n\r\nHer right hand cramped. She switched the pen to her left without pausing.\r\n\r\nAmbidextrous by fatigue. A skill she hadn't asked for.\r\n\r\nChloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek—purple-green, three Tides old at least. She didn't look at the bed. She didn't look at Jhace. She looked at the papers.\r\n\r\n\"Crown's sniffing,\" Chloen said. \"Foremen too. They heard there's a registry. Means there's a list. Means there's a leash.\"\r\n\r\nBram lifted a hand. \"Hi. I'm the leash's embarrassing middleman.\"\r\n\r\nChloen's mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn't trust. \"You always this loud?\"\r\n\r\n\"Only when I'm terrified,\" Bram said. \"So yes.\"\r\n\r\nTiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage.\r\n\r\nChloen read the first line.\r\n\r\n**YOU CAN SAY NO.**\r\n\r\nShe looked up. \"That's new.\"\r\n\r\n\"It's old,\" Tiffani said. \"It just wasn't written down.\"\r\n\r\nChloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. \"Wedges first.\" \"Hold only what's needed.\" \"Terminate on signal.\" \"If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.\"\r\n\r\nAnd then, again: **YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD.**\r\n\r\nChloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. \"Good. Because schedules don't give a damn about ribs.\"\r\n\r\nBram pointed at the underface strip. \"See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.\"\r\n\r\nTiffani didn't look at him. \"Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.\"\r\n\r\nChloen nodded once and left like she'd been given a weapon and didn't want to be seen holding it.\r\n\r\nThe door clicked. The room held its breath.\r\n\r\nCorran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he'd been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen.\r\n\r\n\"For the record,\" he said, voice formal out of habit, \"I'm not here.\"\r\n\r\nBram's grin came instantly. \"For the record, I'm a virgin.\"\r\n\r\nCorran ignored him and held out a thin binder. \"They've started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.\"\r\n\r\nTiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult.\r\n\r\nShe'd written it with both hands when the cramp got too bad. Her handwriting shifted mid-page. Legible. Uneven.\r\n\r\nEvidence of labor. Not Stage.\r\n\r\nCorran thumbed the top page. \"This will get you killed.\"\r\n\r\nTiffani's pen didn't stop. \"Then it will be accurate.\"\r\n\r\nCorran's mouth tightened. \"What did you withhold from Corlexi?\"\r\n\r\nTiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm.\r\n\r\n\"Consent,\" she said. \"The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don't give it, they have to steal it in daylight.\"\r\n\r\nBram snorted. \"She's making them commit crimes on paper. That's adorable.\"\r\n\r\n\"It's leverage,\" Tiffani said. \"The kind that stains.\"\r\n\r\nCorran tucked the packet into his binder. \"Where does this go?\"\r\n\r\n\"Somewhere it can't be conveniently lost,\" Tiffani said. \"Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.\"\r\n\r\nCorran nodded once. \"For the record, I didn't take that.\"\r\n\r\nHe left.\r\n\r\nBram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. The badge caught light again. He touched it like checking if it had gotten heavier.\r\n\r\n\"So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine,\" he said.\r\n\r\n\"He gets safety with ownership language,\" Tiffani corrected. \"He will build a machine either way. I'm deciding where it jams.\"\r\n\r\nBram looked at the clean packet again. \"You sure this doesn't just get people hurt?\"\r\n\r\nTiffani's jaw tightened. Her knee locked finally—sharp, sudden—then released. She didn't log the timing. She didn't measure the interval.\r\n\r\nSome failures weren't for the ledger.\r\n\r\n\"People are already hurt,\" she said. \"Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.\"\r\n\r\nShe lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn't for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred.\r\n\r\nCost markers she couldn't justify. Observations she wouldn't confess. The parts where method looked like murder and she'd chosen anyway.\r\n\r\n\"Not this one,\" Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge.\r\n\r\nTiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal.\r\n\r\nThe paper caught fast. Hungry.\r\n\r\nThe pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission.\r\n\r\nSmoke rose thin and copper-tinted. She watched it go.\r\n\r\nConfession as carbon. Untraceable.\r\n\r\nBram watched the burn. \"You just set fire to your own confession.\"\r\n\r\n\"I set fire to a target,\" Tiffani said.\r\n\r\nOutside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they'd been caught stealing.\r\n\r\nTiffani didn't move.\r\n\r\nShe dipped the pen again.\r\n\r\nHer right hand this time. The cramp had passed.\r\n\r\nShe wrote the next copy anyway."
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1768417980.096,
        "end_turn": null,

          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "start 21272 end 26040\nCHAPTER 4: PROTOCOLJhace’s workshop was a box of iron and heat tucked into the mid-terrace, where you could hear the grid-hum without being crushed by it.Tiffani sat on an overturned crate with her slate on her knees, left leg extended slightly to ease the knee that had started locking after long stands on stone floors. She didn’t rest her back against anything. Always ready to move. Always ready to write.Tiffani shifted her weight off her left leg without comment, like it was just another measurement she refused to log.Jhace stood at his bench, trying to thread a bolt into a collar bracket.His hands shook.The grid-hum didn't break with a bang. It stuttered—missing beats, catching up—like an old machine slipping teeth.He tried again. The bolt dropped. Metal on stone.He stared at it like it had betrayed him.Tiffani didn’t look up. His left hand tried to wipe sweat off his mouth and only smeared dust across his lip. He tasted grit. He hated the fact he noticed it. “Your tremor amplitude increased during exertion.”“Because I’m exerting,” Jhace muttered, bending to pick up the bolt. His fingertips felt numb where the filament-light had burned them. Not pain. Absence.“Because your body is remembering,” Tiffani corrected. “It thinks you’re still holding.”Jhace set the bolt down and flexed his hand hard. The tremor didn’t care.He hated that. He hated that his own muscles had become unreliable.“What did you call it out there?” he asked.Tiffani flipped her slate so he could see. She’d drawn a crude timeline.Activation onset: chest heat + copper taste  Light manifestation: filament contact-dependent  Tremor: post-termination, permanent marker“I need a taxonomy,” she said. “Stages. Because right now it’s just stories.”Jhace wiped his palms on a work cloth. “And you think writing stages keeps people alive.”“I think it keeps them from lying to themselves.” She tapped the line where she’d written permanent. “You can’t call it temporary. You can’t bargain with it.”Jhace’s jaw work\n\n...tail...\n\nhace breathed in, tasted copper-gold dust that still clung to his clothes, and felt the city’s hum through the floorboards.“Then we build it fast,” he said. “Before they decide to build it for us.”---\n"
        },
        "create_time": 1768370896.2596176,
        "end_turn": null,

        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "'CHAPTER 9: THE SHARD-BEARER (RUMOR)\\nThey called him Shard‑Bearer because nobody had a better word for what they thought they’d seen.\\nA man in the eastern districts with a bright line in his chest. A glow that didn’t fade after termination because there was no termination. A hold that didn’t shake. A body that didn’t pay.\\nRumors traveled faster than Crown notices and cleaner than Temple sermons. They came with dates and witness counts like proof.\\nTiffani pinned three reports to the workshop wall with nails.\\nReport one: Torne Reach — Tide 0+3. One operator held a bridge span alone for “half an Anneal.” No tremor. No vein‑lines.\\nReport two: Brinefall — Tide 0+5. A whole terrace stairwell stabilized by “one man’s hands.” Witnesses describe “glass light under the skin.”\\nTiffani tapped the pinned report with the end of her pen. “Report three: delayed secondary collapse. Building held by Shard-Bearer for six hundred heartbeats. Post-release: immediate structural failure. No tremor visible in operator. No warning signs. Casualties: seventeen.”\\nJhace stared at the number like it had teeth. “He didn’t save them. He killed them slower.”\\n“Correct,” Tiffani said. “The cost didn’t vanish. It moved—into the structure, into the people around it. Somewhere we can’t ignore.”\\n“Heroics.” The word came out like a curse.\\n“That word implies exemption,” Tiffani said. “There is no exemption. Only defer'"
        },
        "create_time": 1768370438.3539336,

            "end_time": 1768370439.30587,
            "final_expression_output": "'CHAPTER 9: THE SHARD-BEARER (RUMOR)\\nThey called him Shard‑Bearer because nobody had a better word for what they thought they’d seen.\\nA man in the eastern districts with a bright line in his chest. It moved—into the structure, into the people around it. Somewhere we can’t ignore.”\\n“Heroics.” The word came out like a curse.\\n“That word implies exemption,” Tiffani said. “There is no exemption.                 "msg_type": "execute_input",
                "parent_header": {
                  "msg_id": "825a2629-e2fa71a337c653cfbf511609_239_24",
                  "version": "5.3"
                }
              },
              {
                "content": {
                  "data": {
                    "text/plain": "'CHAPTER 9: THE SHARD-BEARER (RUMOR)\\nThey called him Shard‑Bearer because nobody had a better word for what they thought they’d seen.\\nA man in the eastern districts with a bright line in his chest. A glow that didn’t fade after termination because there was no termination. A hold that didn’t shake. A body that didn’t pay.\\nRumors traveled faster than Crown notices and cleaner than Temple sermons. They came with dates and witness counts like proof.\\nTiffani pinned three reports to the workshop wall with nails.\\nReport one: Torne Reach — Tide 0+3. One operator held a bridge span alone for “half an Anneal.” No tremor. No vein‑lines.\\nReport two: Brinefall — Tide 0+5. A whole terrace stairwell stabilized by “one man’s hands.” Witnesses describe “glass light under the skin.”\\nTiffani tapped the pinned report with the end of her pen. “Report three: delayed secondary collapse. Building held by Shard-Bearer for six hundred heartbeats. Post-release: immediate structural failure. No tremor visible in operator. No warning signs. Casualties: seventeen.”\\nJhace stared at the number like it had teeth. “He didn’t save them. He killed them slower.”\\n“Correct,” Tiffani said. “The cost didn’t vanish. It moved—into the structure, into the people around it. Somewhere we can’t ignore.”\\n“Heroics.” The word came out like a curse.\\n“That word implies exemption,” Tiffani said. “There is no exemption. Only defer'"
                  }
                },
                "msg_type": "execute_result",
                "parent_header": {
                  "msg_id": "825a2629-e2fa71a337c653cfbf511609_239_24",
                  "version": "5.3"
                }
              },
              {
                "content": {
                  "execution_state": "idle"
                },
                "msg_type": "status",
                "parent_header": {
                  "msg_id": "825a2629-e2fa71a337c653cfbf511609_239_24",
                  "version": "5.3"

              }
