    "517b436e-dc1c-47b3-90b9-da31f221fde6": {
      "children": [
        "ddcfbdfa-0001-4b14-b977-681fcc708db8"
      ],
      "id": "517b436e-dc1c-47b3-90b9-da31f221fde6",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "user"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "## CODA: GLASS IN THE HAND\r\n\r\nJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them.\r\n\r\nHe’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste stayed anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you couldn’t spit out.\r\n\r\nStone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn’t reach. A tin cup on the side table, out of range. His right hand twitching on its own schedule.\r\n\r\nA guard stood outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive.\r\n\r\nJhace tried to sleep.\r\n\r\nEvery time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn’t tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city’s new baseline living in him.\r\n\r\nHe flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking.\r\n\r\nThen it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place.\r\n\r\nJhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed.\r\n\r\n“I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest.\r\n\r\nOutside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short.\r\n\r\nJhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.\r\n\r\n## CODA: THE SHARD\r\n\r\nJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them.\r\n\r\nStone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn’t lift. A slate he couldn’t reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted heartbeats like it was a job.\r\n\r\nA guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who’d been chewing dust since Kindling.\r\n\r\nThen the breathing stopped.\r\n\r\nNo footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint.\r\n\r\nThe door opened anyway.\r\n\r\nLight entered that didn’t know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape.\r\n\r\nA figure stepped in.\r\n\r\nNo perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn’t track dust. Their shadow didn’t arrive.\r\n\r\nThey didn’t speak.\r\n\r\nThey moved with the economy of someone who’d done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel.\r\n\r\nA hand touched Jhace’s eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact.\r\n\r\nJhace tried to jerk away.\r\n\r\nNothing.\r\n\r\nHe tried to curl his right hand into a fist.\r\n\r\nNothing.\r\n\r\nThe figure took that hand anyway.\r\n\r\nBoth of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage.\r\n\r\nSomething cold settled into the center of his palm.\r\n\r\nA shard.\r\n\r\nCopper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn’t give a damn about skin.\r\n\r\nCold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache.\r\n\r\nThen heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for.\r\n\r\nCopper flooded Jhace’s mouth so fast he gagged and couldn’t move enough to spit.\r\n\r\nLight threaded up from his palm.\r\n\r\nNot a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path.\r\n\r\nIt slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal.\r\n\r\nJhace couldn’t feel his legs. Couldn’t feel the sheets. Couldn’t feel the weight of his own body.\r\n\r\nBut he felt the line.\r\n\r\nHe felt the doubled harmonic he’d been hearing in stone since Kindling arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay.\r\n\r\nHis lungs pulled a breath that wasn’t his decision.\r\n\r\nThe figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact.\r\n\r\nThen they stepped back.\r\n\r\nNo check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood.\r\n\r\nThe door shut without sound.\r\n\r\nThe guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed.\r\n\r\nJhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn’t chosen to close.\r\n\r\nThe tremor was gone.\r\n\r\nIn its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise.\r\n\r\nSomewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.\r\n\r\n*END N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS*"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1768418686.836,
        "end_turn": null,

          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "# N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS\r\n## Chapters 1-4 — LOCKED MANUSCRIPT\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n## CHAPTER 1: THE BELL\r\n\r\nThe bell rang wrong.\r\n\r\nNot the slow warning cadence. Not the measured strike that meant check seals, tighten brackets. This was the frantic peal of something already falling.\r\n\r\nJhace moved before the bell finished its third strike.\r\n\r\nGrabbed the wrong ring of collar keys on the first yank. Swore. Dumped them into his palm and sorted by feel while he ran. One key skittered off the stair edge and disappeared into the dark.\r\n\r\nDidn't go back for it.\r\n\r\nHis kit bounced against his hip. Chalk. Slate. Collar keys. Tools for measuring failure, not stopping it.\r\n\r\nOne. Two. Three. Four.\r\n\r\nThe doubled hum hit him at Junction Twelve.\r\n\r\nSkipped the pipe entirely and punched straight into his sternum. His ribs buzzed like struck iron. His teeth hurt. His mouth flooded metal.\r\n\r\nCopper.\r\n\r\nSharp and immediate, like he'd bitten through wire.\r\n\r\nHe'd been tasting it for two Tides now. Faint at first. A ghost-flavor that came and went with the grid. But this was different. This flooded his mouth so thick he wanted to spit.\r\n\r\nDidn't.\r\n\r\nUnderface rule: waste nothing.\r\n\r\nHis hands started shaking.\r\n\r\nThen the shaking cut out for three heartbeats and he thought, stupidly, his fingers had finally died.\r\n\r\nAnd then the tremor came back twice as fast.\r\n\r\nIt wasn't cold or exertion. It was a fine vibration that made his grip feel unreliable, like his muscles were half-taking orders and half-refusing them.\r\n\r\nHe jammed them into his coat pockets and kept running.\r\n\r\nForced his breath to slow. Counted to ten without looking at the numbers.\r\n\r\nThe tremor kept going.\r\n\r\nPulled one hand out. Squeezed it into a fist until the knuckles burned. Released.\r\n\r\nStill shaking.\r\n\r\nIt wasn't aftershock. It was something settling into him—fine vibration under the skin—the kind that makes you drop a tool and swear at your own hand.\r\n\r\nHe'd seen it on old manifestors in the deep tunnels. Hands that never found stillness. He'd called it age.\r\n\r\nNow he understood, because his fingers kept twitching against his thigh like a bad motor that wouldn't shut off.\r\n\r\nTallow Street came into view through the access tunnel: upper terrace facade folded inward, stone slabs riding down on support beams that should have held twice that load. Dust cloud thick enough to choke on. People screaming from inside the collapse. Someone rang a hand-bell hard enough to crack its cadence.\r\n\r\nEmergency. Emergency. Emergency.\r\n\r\nA foreman waved at him, face streaked with ash. \"We need a hold. We don't have time.\"\r\n\r\nJhace looked at the structure.\r\n\r\nThe failure line wasn't clean. The whole building was trying to become rubble, one breath at a time. Load shifts rippled through it. Stone groaned. Iron squealed.\r\n\r\nNo crew could wedge that fast. The wedge bucket was still half-full. Someone was still tying their apron when the stone started to go.\r\n\r\nThe people inside didn't have Tides.\r\n\r\n\"I'm not—\" Jhace started.\r\n\r\nThe heat arrived.\r\n\r\nNot external. Internal. A sudden furnace-flare behind his sternum that made him gasp.\r\n\r\nCopper-gold dust swirled around his boots.\r\n\r\nHe watched it climb his legs. Static-cling. Looking for him.\r\n\r\nHis chest burned hotter.\r\n\r\nThe doubled hum snapped into alignment inside him—two mis-timed gears finally meshing. The wrongness in the grid became rightness in his blood. His heartbeat and the city's failing pulse locked into the same rhythm.\r\n\r\nHe didn't know what he was doing.\r\n\r\nHe didn't know why his feet carried him under the broken lintel.\r\n\r\nHe moved because the archway dust was already falling in steady sheets and somebody inside was screaming a name he couldn't ignore.\r\n\r\nPut both hands on the main support column.\r\n\r\nLight—thin, copper-gold, filament-fine—threaded from his palms into the stone seam.\r\n\r\nThe building stopped moving.\r\n\r\nIt was only paused—still wrong, still dangerous.\r\n\r\nJhace's breath shook out of him. He could feel every shift in load through his fingertips: the weight of stone, the tension in iron, the panic-motion of bodies inside. The heat in his chest flared with each micro-adjustment.\r\n\r\n\"Move,\" he rasped. \"Get them out.\"\r\n\r\nBoots ran. Voices shouted coordinates. Someone coughed dust and kept crawling.\r\n\r\nA woman slid into his peripheral vision—practical coat, hair tied back, slate already in hand despite the chaos.\r\n\r\nShe didn't look at him with awe.\r\n\r\nShe looked at him with assessment.\r\n\r\n\"How long can you hold?\" she demanded.\r\n\r\nJhace's jaw clenched. The heat in his chest spiked. \"I don't know.\"\r\n\r\n\"Then we find out.\" She stepped into the dust, coughed once, and shouted to the evac crew. \"Clear corridor A first. Count bodies out. No guessing.\"\r\n\r\nJhace tried to turn his head. The moment he shifted his attention, the heat flared sharper—punishment for looking away.\r\n\r\n\"I have to watch the load,\" he said through his teeth.\r\n\r\n\"Then watch it,\" she snapped, already marking a line on her slate. \"I'll watch you.\"\r\n\r\nPeople came out in bursts: three, then five, then a cluster carrying an older man whose leg bent wrong. Each time the load shifted, Jhace's hands tightened and the filament-light brightened in response.\r\n\r\nHis heartbeat counted itself in his ears.\r\n\r\nOne hundred.\r\n\r\nOne hundred twenty.\r\n\r\nHis arms started to tremble.\r\n\r\nNot from fatigue. From something deeper. A fine vibration that spread into his forearms, up to his shoulders, making his grip feel like it belonged to someone else.\r\n\r\n\"Hold steady,\" the woman said, quieter now, close enough he could hear her through the dust.\r\n\r\nHe didn't answer. Answering took breath.\r\n\r\nAt two hundred heartbeats, the tremor spread into his chest. At two-twenty, his vision narrowed at the edges, copper-gold sparks flickering across it.\r\n\r\n\"How many?\" he rasped.\r\n\r\nThe woman didn't look up from the evac line. \"Ten. Eleven. Twelve—\"\r\n\r\nA scream from inside. A child. High and thin and suddenly cut off.\r\n\r\nJhace's hands shook harder.\r\n\r\n\"No,\" he hissed, and the filament-light surged, burning his palms numb.\r\n\r\n\"Go,\" the woman shouted toward the breach. \"Last sweep. Now.\"\r\n\r\nA rescuer crawled under the paused beam line and dragged a small body out by the shoulders. The child coughed dust and cried.\r\n\r\nThe woman's voice cut clean through the chaos. \"Twelve out. Corridor clear.\"\r\n\r\nJhace felt the building try to move again the instant she said it.\r\n\r\n\"Terminate,\" she said. No softness in it. Not cruelty. Protocol.\r\n\r\nJhace let the filament go.\r\n\r\nThe building settled the last inch it had been denied. Stone grinding. Iron squealing. But the void where bodies had been was now empty.\r\n\r\nHe staggered back into open air.\r\n\r\nHis hands wouldn't stop shaking.\r\n\r\nThe tremor was small—millimeters—but it was relentless.\r\n\r\nThe woman stepped beside him, dust streaked across her face. \"Name.\"\r\n\r\n\"Jhace Torrins.\"\r\n\r\nShe wrote it without looking, chalk skating on sweat. \"Duration: two-forty—\" The chalk snapped. She swore under her breath and wrote it anyway.\r\n\r\n\"Don't,\" he snapped. Too hard. Too fast.\r\n\r\nShe met his eyes. Her gaze was flat, measuring, absolutely certain.\r\n\r\n\"If we don't measure it,\" she said, \"we kill you next time.\"\r\n\r\nBetween bells, they made it back to the workshop long enough to rinse dust off skin and reset slates.\r\n\r\nJhace sat at his bench and waited for the chest heat to fade.\r\n\r\nIt didn't. Not fully.\r\n\r\nA banked warmth stayed behind his sternum—steady as a coal bed that refused to go cold. He pressed his palm to his chest. Felt nothing through shirt and ribs.\r\n\r\nInside, the warmth answered anyway. Once. Twice.\r\n\r\nHis hand lingered there a heartbeat too long. Not thinking. Just reaching.\r\n\r\nHe pulled it away. The tremor kept moving, but for one heartbeat—only one—the shake eased.\r\n\r\nThat was the first time the hunger felt real.\r\n\r\nJhace's hands shook between them.\r\n\r\nHe swallowed copper. Tasted grit. Looked at the child being carried past them on a makeshift stretcher—alive, crying, covered in dust but breathing.\r\n\r\nHe nodded once.\r\n\r\n\"Measure,\" he said. \"Then make sure there isn't a next time.\"\r\n\r\nThe woman's slate scratched.\r\n\r\nShe wrote: **Jhace Torrins. Inspector. First manifestation. Tallow Street. Tide 0.**\r\n\r\nBehind them, the bell rang again.\r\n\r\nDifferent street. Same dust in his teeth. Same bell tearing its own throat.\r\n\r\nJhace looked down and realized he was still gripping air.\r\n\r\nCopper sat on his tongue. He went anyway.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n## CHAPTER 2: THE PATTERN\r\n\r\nThe theodolite wouldn't settle.\r\n\r\nTiffani had calibrated it three times in the last hour. Clean lens. Level base. Reference stars locked. Everything aligned the way it had been for twenty-six Turns of lens work.\r\n\r\nThe crosshair still drifted.\r\n\r\nNo shimmer. No warp. Just the star sitting wrong in the frame.\r\n\r\nShe pressed her palm to the primary lens frame.\r\n\r\nVibration.\r\n\r\nFaint. Constant. The doubled hum lived under the normal grid pulse. It made her molars ache and her lens hand tremble.\r\n\r\nShe pulled her hand away and wrote on her slate:\r\n\r\n**Tide 0 — Kindling**  \r\n**Lens frame vibration: doubled hum present.**  \r\n**Star Seven-Three displacement: 0.34 degrees.**  \r\n**Neither atmosphere nor mechanism.**\r\n\r\nHer mouth tasted copper.\r\n\r\nShe'd been ignoring it for the last two Tides. Faint metallic tang that came and went with air currents from the ventilation shafts. But this was different. This arrived sharp and immediate, flooding her tongue.\r\n\r\nSwallowing felt like evidence.\r\n\r\nThe light outside the lens port changed.\r\n\r\nThe light didn't dim or brighten. It bent.\r\n\r\nTiffani looked up.\r\n\r\nFor a second she couldn't move. Her brain offered one sentence, raw and useless: *What the fuck is that?*\r\n\r\nThe sky over Saint Merro wasn't supposed to do anything except hold weather and stars. Today it held something else.\r\n\r\nA line. Straight. Dark. Moving too fast for her eyes to track at first, then slowing enough for her to understand what she was seeing.\r\n\r\nA spindle.\r\n\r\nLong and narrow and wrong.\r\n\r\nHer breath stopped.\r\n\r\nFor a stupid second, she couldn't make her fingers obey. She stood there with her hand half-raised.\r\n\r\nThe spindle crossed her view in a single heartbeat. No friction fire. No sound. Just a displacement of light that made the air look thin.\r\n\r\nThen gravity lurched.\r\n\r\nNot a shift sideways. An up-and-down stagger. Her slate slid across the stone desk. A lens clamp snapped. Glass sang a sharp, stressed note and went silent.\r\n\r\nTiffani caught herself on the observation frame and forced her eyes back to the port.\r\n\r\nThe spindle was gone.\r\n\r\nPunched through. Somewhere below. Into stone or ocean or the Underface—she couldn't tell.\r\n\r\nPressure arrived a breath later. Not a blast. More resistance than heat. Dust fell from the ceiling mortar in a slow, dry drift.\r\n\r\nThen copper-gold particulate began falling outside.\r\n\r\nGlittering. Fine. Catching the lamplight. It coated the stone lip of the observation port and clung to the iron frame in flecks that shimmered when she moved.\r\n\r\nTiffani touched it with her fingertip.\r\n\r\nIt didn't smear. It gritted. Abrasive. Her finger tingled where the dust touched skin.\r\n\r\nShe wrote:\r\n\r\n**Atmospheric intrusion event. Spindle-form object. Estimated velocity: extreme.**  \r\n**Copper-gold particulate fall: immediate post-impact.**  \r\n**Adheres to metal preferentially. Abrasive. Conductive?**\r\n\r\nHer pencil slipped. The last word smeared into a dark streak. She stared at it, angry at her own hand, then rewrote the line harder than necessary.\r\n\r\nA bell rang.\r\n\r\nNot the Glass-House alert. Distant. Down-terrace. The frantic peal of something already falling.\r\n\r\nThen another bell. Different direction. Same cadence.\r\n\r\nEmergency. Emergency. Emergency.\r\n\r\nTiffani's stomach tightened.\r\n\r\nShe grabbed her slate, shoved it into her coat pocket, and ran.\r\n\r\nThe doubled hum followed her down the Glass-House stairs. She could feel it in the stone railing—vibration that hadn't been there this morning. The walls themselves were listening to something new.\r\n\r\nAt the mid-terrace landing, she stopped.\r\n\r\nTallow Street was visible from here: upper facade collapsed inward, dust cloud thick enough to choke on. People running. Voices screaming coordinates she couldn't parse.\r\n\r\nA third bell rang. Sector Nine. Grain district.\r\n\r\nCloser—close enough that the air changed when the facade flexed.\r\n\r\nTiffani made her choice the way she always did: follow the data to its source.\r\n\r\nShe ran toward Sector Nine.\r\n\r\nThe copper taste got stronger with every stride.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n## CHAPTER 3: SECOND HOLD\r\n\r\nThe grain warehouse sat in Sector Nine, where the terraces were newer and the beams were honest enough to still pretend they could be trusted.\r\n\r\nThe collapse wasn't honest.\r\n\r\nSupport columns had sheared at the base—clean breaks that didn't match load failure. The upper floor sagged. Dust poured from the breach in thick, choking waves.\r\n\r\nJhace stood three strides from the entrance, hands shaking so hard he had to grip his belt to keep them still.\r\n\r\nHe'd been here five breath-counts.\r\n\r\nHis boot skated on spilled grain. He caught himself on instinct, palm slapping the wall hard enough to sting.\r\n\r\nToo close. Too human.\r\n\r\nThe tremor from Tallow Street hadn't stopped. It had spread from his hands into his forearms, up to his shoulders. A fine, relentless vibration that made every tool on his belt rattle.\r\n\r\nThe heat in his chest was still there. Banked. Waiting.\r\n\r\nA foreman ran past him toward the breach, saw his Crown inspector marks, and grabbed his shoulder hard enough to bruise.\r\n\r\n\"You held Tallow,\" the foreman said. \"Do it again.\"\r\n\r\nJhace pulled free. \"I don't—\"\r\n\r\n\"There's forty people in there,\" the foreman cut in. \"Maybe more. Wedges won't hold. Straps won't reach. You're what we've got.\"\r\n\r\nThe copper taste flooded Jhace's mouth again.\r\n\r\nHis chest heat flared.\r\n\r\nThe doubled hum he'd felt in the grid arrived inside him—two mis-timed pulses locking into the same rhythm. His heartbeat and the failing building's stress pattern aligned.\r\n\r\nHe didn't want this.\r\n\r\nHe didn't understand this.\r\n\r\nHe moved because the archway dust was already falling in steady sheets and somebody inside was screaming a name he couldn't ignore.\r\n\r\n\"Clear the approach,\" Jhace said, voice rough. \"Get spotters. I need someone counting.\"\r\n\r\nHe stepped under the sagging upper floor.\r\n\r\nThe heat detonated behind his sternum.\r\n\r\nPut both hands on the nearest support column. Filament-light threaded from his palms into the stone—brighter this time, thicker.\r\n\r\nThe warehouse stopped settling.\r\n\r\nThe groan of stressed iron went quiet.\r\n\r\nJhace's breath shook. He could feel the load distribution through his fingertips: uneven, shifting, trying to collapse in three directions at once. His hands tightened. The filament brightened in response.\r\n\r\nThe tremor in his arms worsened.\r\n\r\nNot fine anymore. Visible. His whole body shaking with the effort of holding something that should have already fallen.\r\n\r\n\"Move!\" someone shouted. \"Get them out!\"\r\n\r\nBoots ran. Voices called coordinates. Bodies crawled through gaps in the rubble.\r\n\r\nA woman appeared beside him—practical coat, slate already open, hair tied back with a strip of cloth.\r\n\r\nShe looked at the filament-light threading from his hands. Looked at his face. Looked at the tremor shaking his shoulders.\r\n\r\n\"You're the Tallow Street hold,\" she said.\r\n\r\nNot a question. A confirmation.\r\n\r\n\"Yeah,\" Jhace managed.\r\n\r\n\"How long have you been holding?\"\r\n\r\n\"I don't know. Five breath-counts? Ten?\"\r\n\r\nHer pen scratched. \"Tremor amplitude increased since first manifestation. Cost progression visible.\"\r\n\r\n\"What?\" Jhace's vision narrowed at the edges. Copper-gold sparks flickered across it.\r\n\r\n\"Your body is paying for this,\" she said, still writing. \"If it locks past tolerance, you won't be able to let go.\"\r\n\r\n\"I can't let go,\" Jhace said through clenched teeth. \"Not until they're out.\"\r\n\r\nThe woman looked at the evacuation line. Counted bodies being carried past. \"Twenty-three so far. Estimate forty total inside.\"\r\n\r\n\"Then I hold for forty.\"\r\n\r\n\"Then you might not walk away.\"\r\n\r\nJhace's hands shook harder. The heat in his chest flared so bright it hurt. \"I already know that.\"\r\n\r\nThe woman's jaw tightened. She moved to where he could see her without turning his head and held up her slate.\r\n\r\n\"I'm documenting this,\" she said. \"Duration. Cost markers. Termination threshold. If we don't measure it, the next person who tries this dies without knowing why.\"\r\n\r\n\"What's your name?\" Jhace asked.\r\n\r\n\"Tiffani Merrow. Glass-House observatory.\"\r\n\r\n\"Jhace Torrins.\"\r\n\r\n\"I know,\" Tiffani said. \"I wrote it down at Tallow Street.\"\r\n\r\nShe showed him her slate:\r\n\r\n**Jhace Torrins. Inspector. First manifestation. Duration: 240 heartbeats. Cost: tremor onset, progressive.**\r\n\r\n**Second manifestation. Sector Nine warehouse. Onset: immediate. Tremor amplitude: increased. Copper-gold filament visible, enhanced density.**\r\n\r\nJhace stared at the notes. At his own name written as data.\r\n\r\n\"What caused this?\" he asked.\r\n\r\nTiffani's pen paused. \"Something punched through the sky two hours ago. Spindle. Metallic. Fast enough to ignore friction. Left this—\"\r\n\r\nShe held up her fingertip. Copper-gold grit caught the lamplight.\r\n\r\n\"—everywhere.\"\r\n\r\nJhace looked at the dust swirling around his boots. At the filament threading from his hands. At the way the copper-gold particles moved toward him.\r\n\r\n\"The doubled hum,\" he said.\r\n\r\n\"You felt it too?\" Tiffani's eyes sharpened.\r\n\r\n\"In the grid. Two Tides before this. Wrong frequency. Made my teeth itch.\"\r\n\r\nTiffani wrote: **Doubled hum: confirmed across multiple observers. Grid infrastructure + atmospheric intrusion event = correlation.**\r\n\r\nA rescue crew leader shouted. \"Thirty-eight out. Two still inside. Back corner. Pinned.\"\r\n\r\nJhace's vision blurred. The heat in his chest spiked.\r\n\r\n\"How long have I been holding?\" he rasped.\r\n\r\nTiffani checked a small timepiece on her belt. \"Eighteen breath-counts.\"\r\n\r\n\"Tallow Street was four.\"\r\n\r\n\"Your cost is accelerating,\" Tiffani said. She wiped a nosebleed with the back of her wrist. \"Tremor spread. Vision blur. This isn't sustainable.\"\r\n\r\nJhace tasted blood before she finished the sentence. Copper-warm, running over his lip.\r\n\r\n\"Terminate after extraction,\" Tiffani said. \"Not before. But the instant they're clear, you let go.\"\r\n\r\n\"What if I can't?\"\r\n\r\n\"Then I make you.\"\r\n\r\nThe last two bodies came out on stretchers. Alive. Barely.\r\n\r\n\"Clear!\" the crew leader yelled.\r\n\r\nTiffani's hand closed on Jhace's shoulder—hard enough to hurt, grounding enough to feel real.\r\n\r\n\"Terminate,\" she said. \"Now.\"\r\n\r\nJhace let the filament go.\r\n\r\nThe warehouse settled the last inch it had been denied. Stone grinding. Iron squealing. A beam snapped somewhere in the back, but the void where people had been was empty.\r\n\r\nJhace staggered back.\r\n\r\nHis legs didn't hold. He hit the ground on his knees, hands still shaking, blood dripping from his nose onto the dust-covered stone.\r\n\r\nTiffani crouched beside him, slate open, pen moving.\r\n\r\n**Second manifestation terminated. Duration: 19 breath-counts, 40 heartbeats. Cost: tremor progression (shoulders, torso), vision impairment (peripheral narrowing, copper-gold visual artifacts), epistaxis (nosebleed), motor control compromise (collapse post-termination).**\r\n\r\n**Survival: confirmed. Function: degraded.**\r\n\r\nJhace wiped blood from his mouth with a shaking hand. \"You write everything down.\"\r\n\r\n\"Everything behaves,\" Tiffani said. \"Even this. It has markers. Progression. Termination criteria.\"\r\n\r\n\"It's killing me in increments.\"\r\n\r\n\"Yes,\" Tiffani said, and the word came out harsher than she meant. \"So we track it before it kills you. We don't guess. We don't pray.\"\r\n\r\nJhace looked at her slate. At the notes written in clean, precise handwriting. At his own degradation catalogued as data.\r\n\r\n\"Why are you doing this?\" he asked.\r\n\r\nTiffani met his eyes. Hers were sharp, clear, absolutely certain.\r\n\r\n\"Because the city is breaking faster than crews can fix it,\" she said. \"Because people like you are going to keep holding until they die. Because if we don't measure the cost, the next bell will kill someone without them knowing why.\"\r\n\r\nA bell rang in the distance.\r\n\r\nFourth collapse. Different sector.\r\n\r\nThe copper taste surged in Jhace's mouth.\r\n\r\nThe heat in his chest flared.\r\n\r\nTiffani wrote it down because not writing felt wrong: **Third manifestation probable. Autonomic response. Cost accumulating.**\r\n\r\nShe looked at Jhace. \"Can you stand?\"\r\n\r\n\"I don't know.\"\r\n\r\n\"Try.\"\r\n\r\nJhace pushed himself to his feet. His legs shook. His hands wouldn't stop trembling. But he stood.\r\n\r\n\"Good,\" Tiffani said. \"Because we're going to the next one. And I'm counting every heartbeat.\"\r\n\r\nShe walked toward the sound of the bell.\r\n\r\nJhace followed.\r\n\r\nTiffani wiped chalk off her thumb. It smeared into the sweat.\r\n\r\nThe bell kept ringing.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n## CHAPTER 4: PROTOCOL\r\n\r\nJhace's workshop was a box of iron and heat tucked into the mid-terrace, where you could hear the grid without being crushed by it.\r\n\r\nTiffani sat on an overturned crate with her slate on her knees, left leg extended slightly to ease the knee that had started locking after long stands on stone floors. She didn't rest her back against anything. Always ready to move. Always ready to write.\r\n\r\nJhace stood at his bench, trying to thread a bolt into a collar bracket.\r\n\r\nHis hands shook.\r\n\r\nHe tried again. The bolt dropped. Metal on stone.\r\n\r\nHe stared at it.\r\n\r\nTiffani didn't look up. \"Your tremor amplitude increased during exertion.\"\r\n\r\n\"Because I'm exerting,\" Jhace muttered, bending to pick up the bolt. His fingertips felt numb where the filament-light had burned them. Not pain. Absence.\r\n\r\n\"Because your body is remembering,\" Tiffani corrected. \"It thinks you're still holding.\"\r\n\r\nJhace set the bolt down and flexed his hand hard. The tremor didn't care.\r\n\r\nHe hated that. He hated that his own muscles had become unreliable.\r\n\r\n\"What did you call it out there?\" he asked.\r\n\r\nTiffani flipped her slate so he could see. She'd drawn a crude timeline.\r\n\r\n**Activation onset: chest heat + copper taste**  \r\n**Light manifestation: filament contact-dependent**  \r\n**Tremor: post-termination, permanent marker**\r\n\r\n\"I need a taxonomy,\" she said. \"Stages. Because right now it's just stories.\"\r\n\r\nJhace wiped his palms on a work cloth. \"And you think writing stages keeps people alive.\"\r\n\r\n\"I think it keeps them from lying to themselves.\" She tapped the line where she'd written *permanent*. \"You can't call it temporary. You can't bargain with it.\"\r\n\r\nJhace's jaw worked.\r\n\r\nHe had bargained with Junction Seven. He had told himself the report would matter. That somebody else would fix it. That the city didn't need him to fight every foreman with a schedule board.\r\n\r\nHe looked at his shaking hands. \"Stage One,\" he said, voice low. \"Tremor.\"\r\n\r\nTiffani nodded and wrote: **Stage One: tremor + filament residue. Operational.**\r\n\r\nA knock hit the workshop door.\r\n\r\nBram Kerris leaned in, dust on his boots, sweat at his collar, grin already loaded.\r\n\r\n\"Heard you two grabbed Tallow by the throat,\" Bram said, trying for funny and getting dust instead. He coughed once. \"City tries to crush you, you crush back. Very... romantic. Very dumb.\"\r\n\r\nJhace didn't look up. \"Who's asking?\"\r\n\r\n\"Crown's asking questions,\" Bram said, and somehow still made it sound obscene. \"Not because they care. Because they hate unknown tools walking around without a leash and a receipt.\"\r\n\r\nTiffani's pen paused. \"What questions.\"\r\n\r\n\"Names. Locations. 'Verified capability.' That sort of foreplay,\" Bram said. He stepped fully inside, then stopped. \"Infrastructure office. Some administrator with hands so clean it makes you wonder what he's washing off. Loves forms. Loves categories. Loves pretending paper is stronger than stone.\"\r\n\r\nJhace's jaw tightened. \"To do what.\"\r\n\r\nBram spread his hands. \"To decide who gets to own you. Temple will call it divine. Guild will call it labor. Crown will call it law. Everybody wants a piece. Congratulations—you're popular.\"\r\n\r\n\"We don't belong to any of them,\" Tiffani said.\r\n\r\nBram laughed, quick and wrong. \"Adorable. Tell that to a stamp. Try explaining 'we don't belong' while a ceiling is coming down and someone's screaming for a hold. They'll file you under 'non-compliant' and act indifferent about it.\"\r\n\r\nJhace's tremor ticked in his fingers. \"So you're here to warn us.\"\r\n\r\n\"I'm here to tell you the door is already being measured,\" Bram said. \"I can buy you a Tide or two. Maybe. After that, they'll show up with paperwork and smiles and start deciding which parts of you are 'public infrastructure.'\"\r\n\r\nTiffani's mouth went flat. \"Then we build methodology fast.\"\r\n\r\nBram pointed at her slate. \"Yeah. Before they build it for you. Because if the Crown writes your protocol, it's going to read like a job description. And you won't like the benefits package.\"\r\n\r\nJhace breathed in, tasted copper-gold dust that still clung to his clothes, and felt the city's hum through the floorboards.\r\n\r\n\"Then we build it fast,\" he said. \"Before they decide to build it for us.\"\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n### KILN-QUARTER FEEDER GANTRY (COORDINATED HOLD)\r\n\r\nThe kiln-quarter feeder gantry sat in Sector Fourteen, where the pipes ran hot and the schedules ran hotter.\r\n\r\nThe bell signal came late. That was the first problem.\r\n\r\nWhen Jhace and Tiffani arrived, the gantry wasn't collapsed—yet. The support columns were bowing, groaning under a load they shouldn't have been carrying because somebody had over-pressured the feed line to meet a schedule.\r\n\r\nA foreman screamed, \"Hold it! Hold it now!\"\r\n\r\nJhace moved toward the nearest column out of habit.\r\n\r\nTiffani caught his sleeve. \"Not yet.\"\r\n\r\nJhace's jaw tightened. \"People are inside.\"\r\n\r\n\"People are *leaving*,\" she corrected, pointing.\r\n\r\nShe was right. Workers were already pouring out of the side doors, coughing, eyes wide. The structure wasn't in free fall. It was in warning.\r\n\r\n\"We wedge first,\" Tiffani said. \"Then you hold if wedges fail.\"\r\n\r\nJhace's chest heat sat behind his ribs. His tremor wanted to fight. His stomach wanted to bargain. Neither cared what he preferred.\r\n\r\nHe didn't scratch it.\r\n\r\nHis first wedge slip went in crooked. He had to yank it back out, knuckles scraping stone, and the crowd saw the mistake.\r\n\r\nHe hated that more than the pain.\r\n\r\nHe dropped to one knee and shoved wedge slips under the first bowing brace. Bram's crew followed, hands fast, straps ready. For once, labor did what labor could do.\r\n\r\nThe gantry groaned again. Dust spilled from a seam.\r\n\r\nA young man stepped into the brace line, hands hovering. Corven. Jhace had seen him in the tunnels, quiet, competent.\r\n\r\nCorven swallowed. \"I... I can feel it,\" he said. \"The hum. It's—\"\r\n\r\n\"Louder,\" Jhace finished.\r\n\r\nCorven nodded hard. \"In my teeth.\"\r\n\r\nTiffani stepped close, slate already up. \"If it hits your chest, tell me. If you taste copper, tell me. Do not improvise.\"\r\n\r\nCorven's eyes flicked to the bending column. \"If it goes—\"\r\n\r\n\"Then we act,\" Jhace said. \"But not before.\"\r\n\r\nThe wedges held for two breaths.\r\n\r\nThen the central column shifted.\r\n\r\nNot collapse. A decisive, ugly move—stone settling, iron flexing. The wedges screamed as they compressed.\r\n\r\nThe workers inside were still running.\r\n\r\n\"Now,\" Tiffani said.\r\n\r\nJhace put both hands on the column.\r\n\r\nHeat flared in his chest—familiar now, terrifying in its familiarity—and filament-light threaded into the cinder-dust coated seam.\r\n\r\nThe column stopped shifting.\r\n\r\nCorven stared at Jhace's hands.\r\n\r\n\"Your turn,\" Jhace said through gritted teeth.\r\n\r\nCorven stepped forward and touched the secondary brace.\r\n\r\nHis breath hitched. Copper taste. Chest heat. Then light—thinner than Jhace's, but real—threaded into the brace line.\r\n\r\nCorven's hands started to shake immediately.\r\n\r\n\"Stage One onset during hold,\" Tiffani murmured, writing. \"Early marker.\"\r\n\r\n\"Keep moving! If you stop, you become debris!\" Bram shouted to the evac crew. \"Count them out! Loud. Fast!\"\r\n\r\nJhace's tremor surged in sympathy. His arms burned. His vision tunneled at the edges.\r\n\r\n\"How long?\" Tiffani asked.\r\n\r\n\"Eighty,\" Jhace rasped, counting heartbeats without thinking.\r\n\r\nCorven's jaw clenched. \"I can do—\"\r\n\r\n\"Do *exactly* what the structure needs,\" Jhace snapped. \"Nothing more.\"\r\n\r\nAt one-fifty, the last workers stumbled out. Two hundred-plus bodies, alive because they'd been allowed time.\r\n\r\n\"Terminate,\" Tiffani said.\r\n\r\nJhace released first. The column held under wedges now that the load had shifted down.\r\n\r\nCorven released a heartbeat later and nearly fell.\r\n\r\nJhace caught him by the elbow, and the tremor in his own hands made the grip feel unreliable.\r\n\r\nCorven looked at his shaking fingers with horror. \"It's not stopping.\"\r\n\r\n\"It doesn't,\" Jhace said.\r\n\r\nCorven stared at his shaking fingers.\r\n\r\nHe'd watched Jhace hold. Had seen the tremor. Had understood it in theory.\r\n\r\nTheory didn't matter when your hand wouldn't listen.\r\n\r\n\"How long?\" he asked.\r\n\r\nJhace's jaw tightened. \"Until you stop manifesting.\"\r\n\r\nCorven's mouth twitched. Not a smile. \"So never.\"\r\n\r\n\"Yeah.\"\r\n\r\nCorven set his palm on the edge of the bench. The tremor rattled bone against metal.\r\n\r\nHe pulled back. Flexed. Tried again. Harder.\r\n\r\nIt kept moving—slow, stubborn—running even after you stop pulling.\r\n\r\nTiffani wrote the number anyway: **219 evacuated. Zero casualties. Two operators marked.**\r\n\r\nBram approached, face hard. \"Crown's going to hear about this—and they'll bring forms instead of shovels.\"\r\n\r\nTiffani didn't look up. \"Good.\"\r\n\r\nJhace's chest still burned with the after-heat of holding. The hunger whispered.\r\n\r\nHe swallowed the copper taste and tried to bully the hunger quiet. It answered with a warm twist behind his sternum.\r\n\r\nFor now.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n### ROUTE FAILURE (THREE DEAD)\r\n\r\nThe count was an argument before it was a number.\r\n\r\nThe bell came from Sector Nine, not as a roar—just a single strike repeated until someone answered.\r\n\r\nResidential stairwell. Upper-terrace split. Twelve families still inside, one route.\r\n\r\nJhace was already marked from Sector Nine. Corven was worse. Senne was fresh enough to stand without shaking.\r\n\r\nTiffani looked at the slate wall and made the decision she would later pretend was only math.\r\n\r\nTwo operators at one-eighty each meant coordination, handoffs, spotters, and failure points. One operator at two-forty meant simplicity. A clean line.\r\n\r\n\"Single deployment,\" she said. \"Senne. Duration cap: two-forty. Terminate on my bell.\"\r\n\r\nSenne went pale as the heat took her chest. Copper-gold light laced her forearms. Not pretty. Functional.\r\n\r\nThey reached the stairwell mouth as stone dust sifted from the ceiling. The grid underfoot carried the doubled hum, deep and insistent, a warning that didn't care who listened.\r\n\r\nSenne braced under the central landing. Jhace watched her shoulders set—metalworker's posture in a glassworker's frame.\r\n\r\nTiffani counted. Heartbeats, not seconds. Seconds lied when panic touched them.\r\n\r\nOne hundred.\r\n\r\nOne forty.\r\n\r\nOne eighty.\r\n\r\nAt one-ninety, a secondary beam—one Tiffani hadn't walked because she'd trusted the map—shifted with a sound like teeth grinding.\r\n\r\nThe evacuation lane kinked. The next family stumbled. The stone didn't collapse all at once. It folded, patient and specific, closing the corridor into a narrow pocket.\r\n\r\nThree people were on the wrong side when it did: two adults and a child coughing copper into his sleeve.\r\n\r\nTiffani saw them, and for a moment the slate in her hands felt obscene.\r\n\r\n\"Hold,\" she said, voice flat. \"Keep holding.\"\r\n\r\nSenne's eyes didn't move. She couldn't spare the motion.\r\n\r\nTwo-ten.\r\n\r\nTwo-twenty.\r\n\r\nTwo-thirty.\r\n\r\nThe pocket didn't clear. The child's coughing turned wet.\r\n\r\nAt two-forty, Tiffani rang the termination sequence anyway. Because protocols existed for a reason. Because if she didn't, she would never stop.\r\n\r\nSenne released.\r\n\r\nThe stairwell collapsed into itself with a slow, final sound—stone settling into the shape it had wanted all along.\r\n\r\nThey dug for hours.\r\n\r\nThey found three bodies. The child first, because he was small enough to slip into the wrong angles.\r\n\r\nBack in the workshop, Tiffani logged it with perfect precision.\r\n\r\n**Deployment:** Senne — 240 heartbeats  \r\n**Evacuated:** 47  \r\n**Casualties:** 3  \r\n**Cause:** Inadequate route assessment. Secondary beam shear.\r\n\r\nShe wrote \"cause\" as if precision could make it reversible.\r\n\r\nHer hand moved to write the next revision. The chalk touched slate.\r\n\r\nNothing came out.\r\n\r\nShe stared at the number.\r\n\r\nThree.\r\n\r\nNot a count. Three names she would never know. Three families who would ask why their—\r\n\r\nThe chalk snapped in her grip.\r\n\r\nShe set the broken pieces down carefully. Picked up a new stick. Wrote:\r\n\r\n**Route spotters required. Three per deployment. No exceptions.**\r\n\r\nUnderlined it twice.\r\n\r\nThen she closed the slate and sat in the dark workshop, counting her own breaths because the numbers on the page wouldn't behave. Her fingers kept shaking anyway.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n### WITNESS\r\n\r\nBrother Corran didn't wear Temple colors.\r\n\r\nThe first thing Jhace noticed was the badge—bright, clean, pinned where it couldn't be missed. The second was how the man didn't look tired. Not even a little.\r\n\r\nCorran's eyes went straight to Jhace's hands.\r\n\r\n\"For the record, I'm not here to stop you,\" Corran said, before anyone asked.\r\n\r\nBram hovered by the door.\r\n\r\nTiffani set her slate down deliberately. \"Then why are you here?\"\r\n\r\n\"Because you're real,\" Corran said. \"And because real things attract predators.\"\r\n\r\nJhace's mouth tightened. \"Crown.\"\r\n\r\nCorran's gaze didn't flinch. \"Yes. Also Guild. Also Temple. Anyone who thinks they can turn you into a lever.\"\r\n\r\nBram's voice came dry. \"Ah yes. The holy trinity: prayer, production, paperwork.\"\r\n\r\nTiffani shot him a look. \"Bram.\"\r\n\r\nHe raised his hands. \"What? I'm helping.\"\r\n\r\nBram gestured toward the door. \"Temple says you're divine. Guild says you're labor. Crown says you're property.\"\r\n\r\nHe pulled a folded packet from his coat and set it on Jhace's bench. Paper. Old style. Archive weight.\r\n\r\n\"Stage Three cases,\" Corran said. \"Documented. Not rumors.\"\r\n\r\nTiffani's hands went still. \"How many?\"\r\n\r\n\"Enough.\" Corran's voice was flat, and that flatness carried exhaustion. \"Most die. A few don't. The ones who don't... wish they had.\"\r\n\r\nJhace's tremor ticked up at the word *die*. He forced his hands flat on the bench. \"Why give this to us?\"\r\n\r\nCorran hesitated. That hesitation was the crack in the Crown.\r\n\r\n\"Because someone above me wants a registry,\" he said. \"Names. Locations. Deployment authority. They're calling it order. It's control.\"\r\n\r\nTiffani's eyes narrowed. \"Who?\"\r\n\r\nCorran looked toward the door as if the walls might report him. \"Administrator Corlexi. Infrastructure Compliance.\"\r\n\r\nBram gave a low whistle. \"Clean hands. Clean coat. Probably wipes his ass with forms.\"\r\n\r\nJhace stared at the packet. \"So you're here to warn us.\"\r\n\r\n\"I'm here to give you data,\" Corran corrected. \"If you want to fight control, you need numbers. Not speeches.\"\r\n\r\nTiffani opened the packet and read, eyes moving fast.\r\n\r\nJhace watched her face tighten at certain lines—durations, costs, outcomes. She didn't gasp. She didn't dramatize. She just absorbed.\r\n\r\n\"This case,\" she said, tapping a page. \"Stage Three survival. How?\"\r\n\r\nCorran's mouth went thin. \"He didn't terminate. He couldn't. The hold locked. Autonomic failure started and the manifestation kept running anyway. The structure held until it didn't matter.\"\r\n\r\nJhace felt cold crawl up his spine despite the workshop heat. \"Locked hold.\"\r\n\r\nCorran nodded. \"It's not a tool you put down when you're done. Sometimes it's a hook in your chest that drags you until something breaks.\"\r\n\r\nTiffani's pen scratched. **Stage Three: autonomic failure. Locked hold possible. Termination may fail.**\r\n\r\nBram shifted. \"You said predators.\"\r\n\r\nCorran's eyes met Bram's. \"Tallec is filing complaints. Says you're obstructing industry. Says your protocols slow production.\"\r\n\r\nJhace's jaw clenched so hard it hurt.\r\n\r\nCorran leaned closer, voice low. \"Here's the fracture you wanted. I don't like Corlexi's registry. I don't like Temple's sermons. I don't like Guild's schedules. But I like fewer dead.\"\r\n\r\nHe straightened. \"If you build methodology, build it fast. Because the Crown is already drafting theirs. And theirs won't care what it costs you.\"\r\n\r\nHe turned to leave.\r\n\r\nTiffani called after him. \"Why risk telling us this?\"\r\n\r\nCorran paused at the door. \"Because Junction Seven wasn't the first ignored report,\" he said. \"And I'm tired of pretending ignorance is the same as innocence.\"\r\n\r\nThen he was gone.\r\n\r\nJhace's hands stayed flat on the workbench. \"If this registry happens...\" He swallowed. \"I'm not doing it for paper.\"\r\n\r\nBram exhaled slowly. \"And because guilt is cheaper than funerals. Not by much. But cheaper.\"\r\n\r\nTiffani climbed the workshop stairs slower than she had a Tide ago. Her right knee caught on the third step—not pain, just resistance.\r\n\r\nShe stopped on the landing. Put her palm to the wall until the world steadied.\r\n\r\nThe knee had locked twice this Anneal. Not during a hold. During stairs. During the parts where bodies were supposed to cooperate.\r\n\r\nShe'd logged operator tremor. Operator vein-lines. Operator autonomic failure.\r\n\r\nShe had not logged her own joint learning to refuse weight.\r\n\r\nBecause if she logged it, she'd have to adjust. Delegate. Sit down.\r\n\r\nAnd if she sat down, someone else would coordinate from the deck. Someone else would make the calls.\r\n\r\nShe took the next step anyway.\r\n\r\nShe didn't log it.\r\n\r\nSome costs weren't manifestation. Some were just labor.\r\n\r\nJhace's hands shook on the bench.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n### QUENCH PHASE (STRUCTURAL TRIAGE)\r\n\r\nQuench phase. Mid-terrace workshop.\r\n\r\nJhace sat at the bench with tools he couldn't hold steady and slates he couldn't read without blinking the copper-gold sparks out of his vision.\r\n\r\nTiffani stood at the wall map, chalk in hand, marking collapse sites with symbols Jhace didn't recognize but understood anyway.\r\n\r\nRed: unrecoverable. Black: wedged stable. Copper-gold dust outline: held by manifestor, cost paid.\r\n\r\nThere were more copper-gold marks than black.\r\n\r\n\"Sector Six pressure main is holding,\" Tiffani said, not looking up from her notes. \"Redundant line operational. Sector Nine feeder—\"\r\n\r\n\"Compromised,\" Jhace finished. \"Beam three cracked through. Won't take another shift.\"\r\n\r\nTiffani's chalk paused. \"How do you know that?\"\r\n\r\n\"Because I felt it crack when I was holding the warehouse.\"\r\n\r\n\"You didn't mention that.\"\r\n\r\n\"You were counting bodies. Didn't seem relevant.\"\r\n\r\nTiffani turned, slate in hand, eyes sharp. \"If structural memory persists post-manifestation, that changes—\"\r\n\r\n\"It changes nothing,\" Jhace said. Flat. No emphasis.\r\n\r\nTiffani stared at him for three heartbeats. Then wrote: **Post-manifestation structural retention: investigate.**\r\n\r\nJhace's tremor ticked harder. \"Don't.\"\r\n\r\n\"Too late.\"\r\n\r\nA runner burst through the door, no knock, face pale. \"Administrator. Crown seal. Wants to see the 'manifestor coordination site.'\"\r\n\r\n\"Tell him to wait,\" Tiffani said.\r\n\r\n\"He's already here.\"\r\n\r\nAn administrator appeared in the doorway. Clean coat. Clean hands. Badge polished enough to catch lamplight.\r\n\r\nHe looked at the wall map. At the slates spread across them. At Jhace's shaking hands and Tiffani's dusty coat and the copper-gold residue that clung to every surface.\r\n\r\n\"You're organizing this,\" the administrator said. Not a question.\r\n\r\n\"We're documenting failures,\" Tiffani said. \"And preventing repeats.\"\r\n\r\n\"Same thing.\" The administrator stepped inside like he owned the threshold. \"The Crown is establishing a registry. Verified manifestors. Deployment protocols. Cost tracking.\"\r\n\r\n\"We already have cost tracking,\" Jhace said.\r\n\r\n\"You have notes. We're building a system.\"\r\n\r\n\"We are a system,\" Tiffani said.\r\n\r\nThe administrator smiled. It didn't reach his eyes. \"Then you'll have no trouble integrating with official oversight.\"\r\n\r\n\"Oversight slows response,\" Tiffani said. \"People die while you're filing forms.\"\r\n\r\n\"People die because there's no coordination,\" the administrator countered. \"Your... workshop is functional. Admirable, even. But it's not scalable. The Crown can provide resources. Training. Legal protection for registered manifestors.\"\r\n\r\n\"And what does the Crown want in return?\" Jhace asked.\r\n\r\n\"Compliance. Documentation. Deployment authorization through proper channels.\"\r\n\r\n\"Proper channels are too slow,\" Tiffani said.\r\n\r\n\"Then we make them faster.\" The administrator pulled a folded document from his coat. \"Initial registry enrollment. Voluntary, for now. Names, capabilities, observed cost markers. Standard protocol.\"\r\n\r\nHe set it on the bench between them.\r\n\r\nJhace looked at the document. At the blank lines waiting for his name. At the official seal at the bottom.\r\n\r\n\"No,\" he said.\r\n\r\nThe administrator's smile tightened. \"I understand the impulse toward independence. But unregistered manifestation will eventually be classified as—\"\r\n\r\n\"As what?\" Tiffani cut in. \"Illegal? You going to arrest people for holding buildings so children don't get crushed?\"\r\n\r\n\"We're going to establish order,\" the administrator said. \"Before this becomes chaos.\"\r\n\r\n\"It's already chaos,\" Jhace said. \"We're just keeping people alive through it.\"\r\n\r\nThe administrator picked up the document. Refolded it. Slid it back into his coat.\r\n\r\n\"The offer remains open,\" he said. \"But it won't remain voluntary forever.\"\r\n\r\nHe left.\r\n\r\nTiffani watched him go, then turned back to the wall map. Picked up her chalk. Drew another copper-gold mark.\r\n\r\n\"They're going to force this,\" Jhace said.\r\n\r\n\"Let them try,\" Tiffani said. \"We'll have methodology locked before they finish their first form.\"\r\n\r\nJhace looked at his shaking hands. At the workshop around them. At the slates covered in cost progression and termination thresholds and the names of the dead.\r\n\r\n\"And if methodology isn't enough?\"\r\n\r\nTiffani's chalk stopped. She didn't turn around.\r\n\r\n\"Then we find out what is.\"\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n### FINAL ASSESSMENT\r\n\r\nThe grid stabilized on the third Tide after the Piercing.\r\n\r\nNot fixed. Stabilized. The doubled hum settled into a new baseline—still wrong, still there, but predictable enough to work around.\r\n\r\nCollapses slowed from constant to sporadic.\r\n\r\nJhace sat in the workshop, slate in front of him, trying to write his own cost assessment the way Tiffani had taught him.\r\n\r\n**Duration tolerance: declining. Estimated safe threshold: 180 heartbeats.**  \r\n**Tremor: permanent. Amplitude increasing with each manifestation.**  \r\n**Vision impairment: copper-gold artifacts during and post-hold.**  \r\n**Chest heat: persistent between manifestations. Activation threshold lowering.**\r\n\r\nHe stopped writing.\r\n\r\nThe hunger sat behind his sternum. Warm. Patient. Ready.\r\n\r\nHe could feel structure stress through the workshop floor. Could sense the grid's new rhythm. Could taste copper before the bells rang.\r\n\r\nHis body was learning to want the holds.\r\n\r\nTiffani entered, limping slightly, knee refusing to lock properly. She didn't comment on it. Just sat down with her own slate and started cross-referencing collapse patterns with manifestor deployment.\r\n\r\n\"Corven's at Stage One,\" she said. \"Stable. Functional.\"\r\n\r\n\"Senne?\"\r\n\r\n\"Stage Two warning signs. Hollow chest. Light takes more effort.\"\r\n\r\n\"Kess?\"\r\n\r\n\"Nosebleeds. Vein-lines starting. Stage One transitioning.\"\r\n\r\n\"Daved?\"\r\n\r\nTiffani's pen paused. \"Daved's not manifesting anymore. Doctor's order.\"\r\n\r\nJhace's jaw tightened. \"He agreed to that?\"\r\n\r\n\"He didn't have a choice. His tremor's bad enough he can't hold tools. Can't dress himself some mornings.\"\r\n\r\n\"Stage Two.\"\r\n\r\n\"Stage Three threshold,\" Tiffani corrected. \"One more hold might lock him.\"\r\n\r\nJhace looked at his own shaking hands.\r\n\r\n\"How many manifestors total?\" he asked.\r\n\r\n\"Verified? Seventeen. Suspected but unconfirmed? Maybe forty.\"\r\n\r\n\"And how many at Stage Two or higher?\"\r\n\r\nTiffani met his eyes. \"All of them.\"\r\n\r\nThe workshop went quiet.\r\n\r\nOutside, the city hummed its new doubled rhythm. Bells rang—fewer now, but still there. People learned to live in the spaces between collapses.\r\n\r\n\"The Crown's going to push the registry,\" Jhace said.\r\n\r\n\"Let them,\" Tiffani said. \"We'll have standards locked before they finish arguing jurisdiction.\"\r\n\r\n\"And if they don't like our standards?\"\r\n\r\n\"Then they can write their own. And watch people die under them.\"\r\n\r\nJhace's tremor ticked up. The hunger whispered behind his ribs.\r\n\r\n\"What if I can't stop?\" he asked. Quiet. Almost too quiet to hear.\r\n\r\nTiffani looked up from her slate.\r\n\r\n\"Then I'll stop you,\" she said. \"Same as always.\"\r\n\r\nJhace nodded.\r\n\r\nHe believed her.\r\n\r\nHe wasn't sure it would be enough.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n**END CHAPTER 4**\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n*CHAPTERS 1-4 LOCKED — REVISION COMPLETE*"
          ]
        },

    "570d39f5-e5b5-45d3-b54d-d159fa7ae0ea": {
      "children": [
        "1e2401e6-921e-43c7-a322-0a57ff4e4498"
      ],
      "id": "570d39f5-e5b5-45d3-b54d-d159fa7ae0ea",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "CHAPTER 4: PROTOCOL\nJhace’s workshop was a box of iron and heat tucked into the mid-terrace, where you could hear the grid-hum without being crushed by it.\nTiffani sat on an overturned crate with her slate on her knees, left leg extended slightly to ease the knee that had started locking after long stands on stone floors. She didn’t rest her back against anything. Always ready to move. Always ready to write.\nTiffani shifted her weight off her left leg without comment, like it was just another measurement she refused to log.\nJhace stood at his bench, trying to thread a bolt into a collar bracket.\nHis hands shook.\nThe grid-hum didn't break with a bang. It stuttered—missing beats, catching up—like an old machine slipping teeth.\nHe tried again. The bolt dropped. Metal on stone.\nHe stared at it like it had betrayed him.\nTiffani didn’t look up. His left hand tried to wipe sweat off his mouth and only smeared dust across his lip. He tasted grit. He hated the fact he noticed it. “Your tremor amplitude increased during exertion.”\n“Because I’m exerting,” Jhace muttered, bending to pick up the bolt. His fingertips felt numb where the filament-light had burned them. Not pain. Absence.\n“Because your body is remembering,” Tiffani corrected. “It thinks you’re still holding.”\nJhace set the bolt down and flexed his hand hard. The tremor didn’t care.\nHe hated that. He hated that his own muscles had become unreliable.\n“What did you call it out there?” he asked.\nTiffani flipped her slate so he could see. She’d drawn a crude timeline.\nActivation onset: chest heat + copper taste  \nLight manifestation: filament contact-dependent  \nTremor: post-termination, permanent marker\n“I need a taxonomy,” she said. “Stages. Because right now it’s just stories.”\nJhace wiped his palms on a work cloth. “And you think writing stages keeps people alive.”\n“I think it keeps them from lying to themselves.” She tapped the line where she’d written permanent. “You can’t call it temporary. You can’t bargain with it.”\nJhace’s jaw worked.\nHe had bargained with Junction Seven. He had told himself the report would matter. That somebody else would fix it. That the city didn’t need him to fight every foreman with a schedule board.\nHe looked at his shaking hands. “Stage One,” he said, voice low. “Tremor.”\nTiffani nodded and wrote: Stage One: tremor + filament residue. Functional. Marked.\nShe stared at the word Functional, then drew a hard line through it and rewrote: Operational.\n“Functional makes it sound polite,” she muttered.\n\nA knock hit the workshop door.\nBram Tallow leaned in like he was checking whether the room was still allowed to exist. Dust on his boots, sweat at his collar, grin already loaded.\n“Heard you two grabbed Tallow Street by the throat,” Bram said, trying for funny and getting dust instead. He coughed once. “City tries to crush you, you crush back. Very… romantic. Very dumb.”\nJhace didn’t look up. “Who’s asking?”\n“Crown’s asking questions,” Bram said, and somehow still made it sound like a dirty joke. “Not because they care. Because they hate unknown tools walking around without a leash and a receipt.”\nTiffani’s pen paused. “What questions.”\n“Names. Locations. ‘Verified capability.’ That sort of foreplay,” Bram said. He stepped fully inside, then stopped like the heat-grid might charge him rent. “Infrastructure office. Some administrator with hands so clean it makes you wonder what he’s washing off. Loves forms. Loves categories. Loves pretending paper is stronger than stone.”\nJhace’s jaw tightened. “To do what.”\nBram spread his hands. “To decide who gets to own you. Temple will call it divine. Guild will call it labor. Crown will call it law. Everybody wants a piece. Congratulations—you’re popular.”\n“We don’t belong to any of them,” Tiffani said.\nBram laughed, quick and wrong. “Adorable. Tell that to a stamp. Try explaining ‘we don’t belong’ while a ceiling is coming down and someone’s screaming for a hold. They’ll file you under ‘non-compliant’ and act like that’s a weather condition.”\nJhace’s tremor ticked in his fingers. “So you’re here to warn us.”\n“I’m here to tell you the door is already being measured,” Bram said. “I can buy you a Tide or two. Maybe. After that, they’ll show up with paperwork and smiles and start deciding which parts of you are ‘public infrastructure.’”\nTiffani’s mouth went flat. “Then we build methodology fast.”\nBram pointed at her slate like it offended him personally. “Yeah. Before they build it for you. Because if the Crown writes your protocol, it’s going to read like a job description. And you won’t like the benefits package.”\nJhace breathed in, tasted copper-gold dust that still clung to his clothes, and felt the city’s hum through the floorboards.\n“Then we build it fast,” he said. “Before they decide to build it for us.”\n---\n\n"
        },
        "create_time": 1768370922.0674748,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "570d39f5-e5b5-45d3-b54d-d159fa7ae0ea",
        "metadata": {
          "aggregate_result": {
            "final_expression_output": "CHAPTER 4: PROTOCOL\nJhace’s workshop was a box of iron and heat tucked into the mid-terrace, where you could hear the grid-hum without being crushed by it.\nTiffani sat on an overturned crate with her slate on her knees, left leg extended slightly to ease the knee that had started locking after long stands on stone floors. She didn’t rest her back against anything. Always ready to move. Always ready to write.\nTiffani shifted her weight off her left leg without comment, like it was just another measurement she refused to log.\nJhace stood at his bench, trying to thread a bolt into a collar bracket.\nHis hands shook.\nThe grid-hum didn't break with a bang. It stuttered—missing beats, catching up—like an old machine slipping teeth.\nHe tried again. The bolt dropped. Metal on stone.\nHe stared at it like it had betrayed him.\nTiffani didn’t look up. His left hand tried to wipe sweat off his mouth and only smeared dust across his lip. He tasted grit. He hated the fact he noticed it. “Your tremor amplitude increased during exertion.”\n“Because I’m exerting,” Jhace muttered, bending to pick up the bolt. His fingertips felt numb where the filament-light had burned them. Not pain. Absence.\n“Because your body is remembering,” Tiffani corrected. “It thinks you’re still holding.”\nJhace set the bolt down and flexed his hand hard. The tremor didn’t care.\nHe hated that. He hated that his own muscles had become unreliable.\n“What did you call it out there?” he asked.\nTiffani flipped her slate so he could see. She’d drawn a crude timeline.\nActivation onset: chest heat + copper taste  \nLight manifestation: filament contact-dependent  \nTremor: post-termination, permanent marker\n“I need a taxonomy,” she said. “Stages. Because right now it’s just stories.”\nJhace wiped his palms on a work cloth. “And you think writing stages keeps people alive.”\n“I think it keeps them from lying to themselves.” She tapped the line where she’d written permanent. “You can’t call it temporary. You can’t bargain with it.”\nJhace’s jaw worked.\nHe had bargained with Junction Seven. He had told himself the report would matter. That somebody else would fix it. That the city didn’t need him to fight every foreman with a schedule board.\nHe looked at his shaking hands. “Stage One,” he said, voice low. “Tremor.”\nTiffani nodded and wrote: Stage One: tremor + filament residue. Functional. Marked.\nShe stared at the word Functional, then drew a hard line through it and rewrote: Operational.\n“Functional makes it sound polite,” she muttered.\n\nA knock hit the workshop door.\nBram Tallow leaned in like he was checking whether the room was still allowed to exist. Dust on his boots, sweat at his collar, grin already loaded.\n“Heard you two grabbed Tallow Street by the throat,” Bram said, trying for funny and getting dust instead. He coughed once. “City tries to crush you, you crush back. Very… romantic. Very dumb.”\nJhace didn’t look up. “Who’s asking?”\n“Crown’s asking questions,” Bram said, and somehow still made it sound like a dirty joke. “Not because they care. Because they hate unknown tools walking around without a leash and a receipt.”\nTiffani’s pen paused. “What questions.”\n“Names. Locations. ‘Verified capability.’ That sort of foreplay,” Bram said. He stepped fully inside, then stopped like the heat-grid might charge him rent. “Infrastructure office. Some administrator with hands so clean it makes you wonder what he’s washing off. Loves forms. Loves categories. Loves pretending paper is stronger than stone.”\nJhace’s jaw tightened. “To do what.”\nBram spread his hands. “To decide who gets to own you. Temple will call it divine. Guild will call it labor. Crown will call it law. Everybody wants a piece. Congratulations—you’re popular.”\n“We don’t belong to any of them,” Tiffani said.\nBram laughed, quick and wrong. “Adorable. Tell that to a stamp. Try explaining ‘we don’t belong’ while a ceiling is coming down and someone’s screaming for a hold. They’ll file you under ‘non-compliant’ and act like that’s a weather condition.”\nJhace’s tremor ticked in his fingers. “So you’re here to warn us.”\n“I’m here to tell you the door is already being measured,” Bram said. “I can buy you a Tide or two. Maybe. After that, they’ll show up with paperwork and smiles and start deciding which parts of you are ‘public infrastructure.’”\nTiffani’s mouth went flat. “Then we build methodology fast.”\nBram pointed at her slate like it offended him personally. “Yeah. Before they build it for you. Because if the Crown writes your protocol, it’s going to read like a job description. And you won’t like the benefits package.”\nJhace breathed in, tasted copper-gold dust that still clung to his clothes, and felt the city’s hum through the floorboards.\n“Then we build it fast,” he said. “Before they decide to build it for us.”\n---\n\n",

          "text": "bash -lc ls -l /mnt/data && python - <<'PY'\nimport re\npath='/mnt/data/N01Final.Last.txt'\ntext=open(path,'r',encoding='utf-8').read().splitlines()\n# find chapter 7 header lines\nfor i,line in enumerate(text):\n    if re.search(r'CHAPTER\\s*7', line, re.I):\n        print('found',i+1,line[:120])\nPY"

        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "'sics with the dead in the hallway,” she said. “Extend.”\\nThirty heartbeats passed like a slow knife.\\nMerra shouted from the corridor mouth, “Final group emerging. Ten heartbeats!”\\n“Ten heartbeats is still ten,” Tiffani said. “Move.”\\nDaved’s hands tightened. “Hold.”\\n“Daved!” Jhace lunged a step closer.\\n“Hold.”\\nMerra’s slate slapped stone. “Clear! All civilians safe!”\\n“Terminate all operators,” Tiffani snapped. “Immediate coordinated shutdown.”\\nLights guttered and went out—one by one—except Daved’s.\\n“Can’t,” Daved said.\\n“Daved. Breathe,” Jhace said, too close, too desperate.\\n“Can’t.”\\n“Terminate,” Tiffani said. “That is not a negotiation.”\\n“They’re not out.”\\n“They are out. Merra confirmed. Terminate now.”\\nDaved’s jaw worked like he was chewing copper. “Already crossed.”\\nBram shoved his way in with words because silence was making everyone honest. “Daved—buddy—love the commitment, hate the method acting.”\\n“Bram, shut up,” Jhace hissed.\\n“I can’t,” Bram said. “It’s a disorder.”\\nTiffani stepped in until Daved had to see her. “Look at me. If you extend past this, you cross Stage Three thresho'"
        },

                "msg_type": "execute_input",

                  "msg_id": "825a2629-e2fa71a337c653cfbf511609_239_21",

              {
                "content": {
                  "data": {
                    "text/plain": "'sics with the dead in the hallway,” she said. “Extend.”\\nThirty heartbeats passed like a slow knife.\\nMerra shouted from the corridor mouth, “Final group emerging. Ten heartbeats!”\\n“Ten heartbeats is still ten,” Tiffani said. “Move.”\\nDaved’s hands tightened. “Hold.”\\n“Daved!” Jhace lunged a step closer.\\n“Hold.”\\nMerra’s slate slapped stone. “Clear! All civilians safe!”\\n“Terminate all operators,” Tiffani snapped. “Immediate coordinated shutdown.”\\nLights guttered and went out—one by one—except Daved’s.\\n“Can’t,” Daved said.\\n“Daved. Breathe,” Jhace said, too close, too desperate.\\n“Can’t.”\\n“Terminate,” Tiffani said. “That is not a negotiation.”\\n“They’re not out.”\\n“They are out. Merra confirmed. Terminate now.”\\nDaved’s jaw worked like he was chewing copper. “Already crossed.”\\nBram shoved his way in with words because silence was making everyone honest. “Daved—buddy—love the commitment, hate the method acting.”\\n“Bram, shut up,” Jhace hissed.\\n“I can’t,” Bram said. “It’s a disorder.”\\nTiffani stepped in until Daved had to see her. “Look at me. If you extend past this, you cross Stage Three thresho'"
                  }
                },
