        "metadata": {
          "aggregate_result": {
            "code": "word_to_int={\"one\":1,\"two\":2,\"three\":3,\"four\":4,\"five\":5,\"six\":6,\"seven\":7,\"eight\":8,\"nine\":9,\"ten\":10,\n             \"eleven\":11,\"twelve\":12,\"thirteen\":13,\"fourteen\":14,\"fifteen\":15,\"sixteen\":16,\"seventeen\":17,\"eighteen\":18,\"nineteen\":19,\"twenty\":20}\n\ndef convert_word_minutes(text):\n    # e.g., \"five minutes\" -> \"300 heartbeats\"\n    for w,n in word_to_int.items():\n        text=re.sub(rf\"\\b{w}\\s+minutes\\b\", f\"{n*60} heartbeats\", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n        text=re.sub(rf\"\\b{w}\\s+minute\\b\", f\"{n*60} heartbeats\", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n        text=re.sub(rf\"\\b{w}\\s+seconds\\b\", f\"{n} heartbeats\", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n        text=re.sub(rf\"\\b{w}\\s+second\\b\", f\"{n} heartbeats\", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n        text=re.sub(rf\"\\b{w}\\s+hours\\b\", f\"{n} stretches\", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n        text=re.sub(rf\"\\b{w}\\s+hour\\b\", f\"{n} stretch\", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n    return text\n\n# apply across all paragraphs\nfor p in doc2.paragraphs:\n    new=convert_word_minutes(p.text)\n    if new!=p.text:\n        set_paragraph_text(p,new)\n\n# paragraph-specific remaining fixes\n# 4: \"didn't have minutes\" -> \"didn't have margin\"\np=doc2.paragraphs[4]\nif re.search(r\"\\bminutes\\b\", p.text, re.IGNORECASE):\n    set_paragraph_text(p, re.sub(r\"\\bminutes\\b\", \"margin\", p.text, flags=re.IGNORECASE))\n\n# 150: replace \"for days\" -> \"for multiple Tides\"\np=doc2.paragraphs[150]\nset_paragraph_text(p, re.sub(r\"\\bfor days\\b\", \"for multiple Tides\", p.text, flags=re.IGNORECASE))\n\n# fix 'Seconds lied...' in paragraph 27\np=doc2.paragraphs[27]\nset_paragraph_text(p, re.sub(r\"\\bSeconds\\b\", \"Counts\", p.text))\n\n# replace various 'second' occurrences\ndef replace_second_everywhere():\n    for i,p in enumerate(doc2.paragraphs):\n        txt=p.text\n        new=txt\n        new=re.sub(r\"\\bsecond heartbeat\\b\", \"shadow heartbeat\", new, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n        new=re.sub(r\"\\bCHAPTER 3:\\s*SECOND HOLD\\b\", \"CHAPTER 3: HOLD TWO\", new, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n        new=re.sub(r\"\\bSecond manifestation\\b\", \"Follow-on manifestation\", new, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n        new=re.sub(r\"\\bthe second was\\b\", \"Next was\", new, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n        new=re.sub(r\"\\bsecond floor\\b\", \"upper floor\", new, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n        new=re.sub(r\"\\bsecond stack\\b\", \"next stack\", new, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n        # standalone 'second' (as ordinal/time) -> 'next' when plausible\n        new=re.sub(r\"\\bsecond\\b\", \"next\", new, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n        if new!=txt:\n            set_paragraph_text(p,new)\nreplace_second_everywhere()\n\n# recheck forbidden list (word boundary)\nforbidden=[\"day\",\"days\",\"hour\",\"hours\",\"minute\",\"minutes\",\"second\",\"seconds\",\"today\",\"tomorrow\",\"yesterday\",\"west\",\"east\",\"north\",\"south\",\"degree\",\"degrees\"]\nremaining={}\nfor w in forbidden:\n    pat=re.compile(rf\"\\b{w}\\b\", re.IGNORECASE)\n    res=[i for i,p in enumerate(doc2.paragraphs) if pat.search(p.text)]\n    if res:\n        remaining[w]=res\nremaining\n",
            "end_time": 1768421498.5735471,

        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "or Nine. Grain district.\nCloser—close enough that the air changed when the facade flexed.\nTiffani made her choice the way she always did: follow the data to its source.\nShe ran toward Sector Nine.\nThe copper taste got stronger with every stride.\n---\nCHAPTER 3: SECOND HOLD\nThe grain warehouse sat in Sector Nine, where the terraces were newer and the beams were honest enough to still pretend they could be trusted.\nThe collapse wasn't honest.\nSupport columns had sheared at the base—clean breaks tha\ne stood.\n\"Good,\" Tiffani said. \"Because we're going to the next one. And I'm counting every heartbeat.\"\nShe walked toward the sound of the bell.\nJhace followed.\nTiffani wiped chalk off her thumb. It smeared into the sweat.\nThe bell kept ringing.\n---\nCHAPTER 4: PROTOCOL\nJhace’s workshop was a box of iron and heat tucked into the mid‑terrace, where you could hear the grid‑hum without being crushed by it.\nTiffani sat on an overturned crate with her slate on her knees, left leg extended slightly to \non. And you won’t like the benefits package.”\nJhace breathed in, tasted copper‑gold dust that still clung to his clothes, and felt the city’s hum through the floorboards.\n“Then we build it fast,” he said. “Before they decide to build it for us.”\n---\nCHAPTER 5: FIRST NETWORK\nThe kiln‑quarter feeder gantry sat in Sector Fourteen, where the pipes ran hot and the schedules ran hotter, and everything pretended it was fine right up until it wasn’t.\nThe bell signal came late. That was the first problem\n"
        },
        "create_time": 1768370623.0710664,
        "end_turn": null,

    "68c2dc9b-4ead-47d6-9d08-fefa90e09ce2": {
      "children": [
        "3d00b8b4-fce7-4d5b-aeeb-c565da614489"
      ],
      "id": "68c2dc9b-4ead-47d6-9d08-fefa90e09ce2",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "total 79\n-rw-r--r-- 1 root oai_shared 80245 Jan 14 18:27 N01Final.Last.txt\nN01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS\rTurning: GLASS (G) — Tide 0 through Tide 21 (Three Anneals post-Piercing)\r---\rCHAPTER 1: THE BELL\rThe bell rang wrong.\rNot the slow warning cadence. Not the measured strike that meant \"check your seals, tighten your brackets.\" This was the frantic peal of something already falling, the sound of a structure begging for a hold.\rJhace was moving before the bell finished its third strike.\rHe grabbed the wrong ring of collar keys on the first yank. Swore, dumped them into his palm, and sorted by feel while he ran. One key skittered off the stair edge and disappeared into the dark.\rHe didn’t go back for it.\r\rHis kit bounced against his hip. Chalk. Slate. Collar keys. Tools for measuring failure, not stopping it. He didn't know why his legs were carrying him toward the sound instead of away. Didn't know why his heart counted the bell strikes like they were orders.\rOne. Two. Three. Four.\rThe doubled harmonic hit him at Junction Twelve.\rIt skipped the pipe-hum entirely and punched straight into his sternum. His ribs buzzed like struck iron. His teeth hurt. His mouth flooded metal.\rCopper.\rSharp and immediate, like he'd bitten through wire.\rHe'd been tasting it for two Tides now. Faint at first. A ghost-flavor that came and went with the grid-hum. But this was different. This flooded his mouth so thick he wanted to spit.\rHe didn't.\rUnderface rule: waste nothing.\rHis hands started shaking.\rThen the shaking cut out for three heartbeats and he thought, stupidly, his fingers had finally died.\rAnd then the tremor came back twice as fast.\r\rIt wasn't cold or exertion. It was a fine vibration that made his grip feel unreliable, like his muscles were half-taking orders and half-refusing them.\rHe jammed them into his coat pockets and kept running.\rHe forced his breath to slow. Counted to ten without looking at the numbers.\rThe tremor kept going.\rHe pulled one hand out. Squeezed it into a fist until the knuckles burned. Released.\rStill shaking.\rIt wasn't aftershock. It was something settling into him—fine vibration under the skin—the kind that makes you drop a tool and swear at your own hand.\rHe’d seen it on old manifestors in the deep tunnels. Hands that never found stillness. He’d called it age.\rNow he understood, because his fingers kept twitching against his thigh like a bad motor that wouldn't shut off.\rTallow Street came into view through the access tunnel: upper terrace facade folded inward, stone slabs riding down on support beams that should have held twice that load. Dust cloud thick enough to choke on. People screaming from inside the collapse. Someone rang a hand-bell hard enough to crack its cadence.\rEmergency. Emergency. Emergency.\rA foreman waved at him, face streaked with ash. \"We need a hold. We don't have time.\"\rJhace looked at the structure.\rThe failure line wasn't clean. The whole building was trying to become rubble, one breath at a time. Load shifts rippled through it like a living animal struggling to die. Stone groaned. Iron squealed.\rNo crew could wedge that fast. The wedge bucket was still half-full. Someone was still tying their apron when the stone started to go.\rThe people inside didn't have minutes.\r\"I'm not—\" Jhace started.\rThe heat arrived.\rNot external. Internal. A sudden furnace-flare behind his sternum that made him gasp. Like someone had lit a coal bed under his ribs and told it to burn clean.\rCopper-gold dust swirled around his boots.\rHe watched it climb his legs like static. Like it was looking for him.\rHis chest burned hotter.\rThe doubled harmonic snapped into alignment inside him—two mis-timed gears finally meshing. The wrongness in the grid-hum became rightness in his blood. His heartbeat and the city's failing pulse locked into the same rhythm.\rHe didn't know what he was doing.\rHe didn't know why his feet carried him under the broken lintel.\rHe moved because the archway dust was already falling in steady sheets and somebody inside was screaming a name he couldn't ignore.\rHe put both hands on the main support column.\rLight—thin, copper-gold, filament-fine—threaded from his palms into the stone seam.\rThe building stopped moving.\rIt was only paused—still wrong, still dangerous.\rJhace's breath shook out of him. He could feel every shift in load through his fingertips: the weight of stone, the tension in iron, the panic-motion of bodies inside. The heat in his chest flared with each micro-adjustment, like his body was paying for every heartbeat of stability in increments he couldn't count.\r\"Move,\" he rasped. \"Get them out.\"\rBoots ran. Voices shouted coordinates. Someone coughed dust and kept crawling.\rA woman slid into his peripheral vision—practical coat, hair tied back, slate already in hand despite the chaos.\rShe didn't look at him like a miracle.\rShe looked at him like a system that could fail.\r\"How long can you hold?\" she demanded.\rJhace's jaw clenched. The heat in his chest spiked. \"I don't know.\"\r\"Then we find out.\" She stepped into the dust, coughed once, and shouted to the evac crew. \"Clear corridor A first. Count bodies out. No guessing.\"\rJhace tried to turn his head. The moment he shifted his attention, the heat flared sharper—punishment for looking away.\r\"I have to watch the load,\" he said through his teeth.\r\"Then watch it,\" she snapped, already marking a line on her slate. \"I'll watch you.\"\rPeople came out in bursts: three, then five, then a cluster carrying an older man whose leg bent wrong. Each time the load shifted, Jhace's hands tightened and the filament-light brightened in response.\rHis heartbeat counted itself in his ears.\rOne hundred.\rOne hundred twenty.\rHis arms started to tremble.\rNot from fatigue. From something deeper. A fine vibration that spread into his forearms, up to his shoulders, making his grip feel like it belonged to someone else.\r\"Hold steady,\" the woman said, quieter now, close enough he could hear her through the dust.\rHe didn't answer. Answering took breath.\rAt two hundred heartbeats, the tremor spread into his chest. At two-twenty, his vision narrowed at the edges, copper-gold sparks flickering across it like welding flash.\r\"How many?\" he rasped.\rThe woman didn't look up from the evac line. \"Ten. Eleven. Twelve—\"\rA scream from inside. A child. High and thin and suddenly cut off.\rJhace's hands shook harder.\r\"No,\" he hissed, and the filament-light surged, burning his palms numb.\r\"Go,\" the woman shouted toward the breach. \"Last sweep. Now.\"\rA rescuer crawled under the paused beam line and dragged a small body out by the shoulders. The child coughed dust and cried like she'd been holding it in for hours.\rThe woman's voice cut clean through the chaos. \"Twelve out. Corridor clear.\"\rJhace felt the building try to move again the instant she said it, like it had been waiting for permission.\r\"Terminate,\" she said. No softness in it. Not cruelty. Protocol.\rJhace let the filament go.\rThe building settled the last inch it had been denied. Stone grinding. Iron squealing. But the void where bodies had been was now empty.\rHe staggered back into open air.\rHis hands wouldn't stop shaking.\rThe tremor was small—millimeters—but it was relentless. Like a ghost living inside his muscles, counting the dead he'd prevented and refusing to be quiet about the cost.\rThe woman stepped beside him, dust streaked across her face. \"Name.\"\r\"Jhace Torrins.\"\rShe wrote it without looking, chalk skating on sweat. \"Duration: two-forty—\" The chalk snapped. She swore under her breath and wrote it anyway.\r\"Don't,\" he snapped. Too hard. Too fast.\rShe met his eyes. Her gaze was flat, measuring, absolutely certain.\r\"If we don't measure it,\" she said, \"we kill you next time.\"\rBetween bells, they made it back to the workshop long enough to rinse dust off skin and reset slates.\rJhace sat at his bench and waited for the chest heat to fade.\rIt didn’t. Not fully.\rA banked warmth stayed behind his sternum—steady as a coal bed that refused to go cold. He pressed his palm to his chest. Felt nothing through shirt and ribs.\rInside, the warmth answered anyway. Once. Twice.\rLike the world remembering how it had fit together when he held.\rHis hand lingered there a heartbeat too long. Not thinking. Just reaching.\rHe pulled it away like it burned. The tremor kept moving, but for one heartbeat—only one—the shake eased, like his body preferred the burn to the break.\rThat was the first time the hunger felt like a vote.\rJhace’s hands shook between them like evidence he hadn't wanted.\rHe swallowed copper. Tasted grit. Looked at the child being carried past them on a makeshift stretcher—alive, crying, covered in dust but breathing.\rHe nodded once.\r\"Measure,\" he said. \"Then make sure there isn't a next time.\"\rThe woman's slate scratched.\rShe wrote: Jhace Torrins. Inspector. First manifestation. Tallow Street. Tide 0.\rBehind them, the bell rang again.\rDifferent street. Same dust in his teeth. Same bell tearing its own throat.\rJhace looked down and realized he was still gripping air.\rCopper sat on his tongue. He went anyway.\r---\rCHAPTER 2: THE PATTERN\rThe theodolite wouldn't settle.\rTiffani had calibrated it three times in the last hour. Clean lens. Level base. Reference stars locked. Everything aligned the way it had been for twenty-six Turns of lens work.\rThe crosshair still drifted.\rNo shimmer. No warp. Just the star sitting wrong in the frame—like someone had nudged the sky with a thumb while she wasn't looking.\rShe pressed her palm to the primary lens frame.\rVibration.\rFaint. Constant. The doubled harmonic lived under the normal hum like a second heartbeat. It made her molars ache and her lens hand tremble.\rShe pulled her hand away and wrote on her slate:\rTide 0 — Kindling  \rLens frame vibration: doubled harmonic present.  \rStar Seven-Three displacement: 0.34 degrees west.  \rNeither atmosphere nor mechanism.\rHer mouth tasted copper.\rShe'd been ignoring it for the last two Tides. Faint metallic tang that came and went wi[... ELLIPSIZATION ...]: DEPLOYMENT PROCEDURE. “He will read what he wants to read. I’m controlling the surface.”\rBram exhaled through his nose. “Ah. Compliance theater with a trapdoor.”\r“There is no trapdoor,” she said. “There are exits. Distributed.”\rShe pushed the second stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: Property is not a priority class. Operator veto. Refusal authority. No deployment without independent verification.\rBram whistled. “This one’s the ‘burn the Crown down’ packet.”\r“This one is the ‘keep operators alive long enough to matter’ packet.” She slid it closer. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.”\rBram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.”\r“They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.”\rBram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.”\rTiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.”\rChloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers.\r“Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.”\rBram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.”\rChloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?”\r“Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.”\rTiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage.\rChloen read the first line.\rYOU CAN SAY NO.\rShe looked up. “That’s new.”\r“It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.”\rChloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.”\rAnd then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD.\rChloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.”\rBram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.”\rTiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.”\rChloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it.\rThe door clicked. The room held its breath.\rCorran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen.\r“For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.”\rBram’s grin came instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.”\rCorran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.”\rTiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult.\rCorran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.”\rTiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Then it will be accurate.”\rCorran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi?”\rTiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm.\r“Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in daylight.”\rBram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. That’s adorable.”\r“It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.”\rCorran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?”\r“Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.”\rCorran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.”\rHe left.\rBram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. “So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine.”\r“He gets safety with ownership language,” Tiffani corrected. “He will build a machine either way. I’m deciding where it jams.”\rBram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?”\rTiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.”\rShe lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred.\r“Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge.\rTiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission.\rBram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.”\r“I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said.\rOutside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they'd been caught stealing.\rTiffani didn’t move.\rShe dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.\r\r---\rCODA: GLASS IN THE HAND\rJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them.\rHe’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste was still there anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you can't spit out.\rStone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn't reach. His right hand twitching on its own schedule.\rSomeone had posted a guard outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive.\rJhace tried to sleep.\rEvery time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn't tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city's new baseline living in him.\rHe flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking.\rThen it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place.\rJhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed.\r“I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest.\rOutside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short.\rJhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.\r\r---\rCODA: THE SHARD\rJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them.\rStone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn't lift. A slate he couldn't reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted time like it was a job.\rA guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who'd been chewing dust since Kindling.\rThen the breathing stopped.\rNo footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint.\rThe door opened anyway.\rLight entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape.\rA figure stepped in.\rNo perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive.\rThey didn't speak.\rThey moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel.\rA hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact.\rJhace tried to jerk away.\rNothing.\rHe tried to curl his right hand into a fist.\rNothing.\rThe figure took that hand anyway.\rBoth of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage.\rSomething cold settled into the center of his palm.\rA shard.\rCopper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin.\rCold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache.\rThen heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for.\rCopper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit.\rLight threaded up from his palm.\rNot a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path.\rIt slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal.\rJhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body.\rBut he felt the line.\rHe felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay.\rHis lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision.\rThe figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact.\rThen they stepped back.\rNo check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood.\rThe door shut without sound.\rThe guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed.\rJhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close.\rThe tremor was gone.\rIn its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise.\rSomewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.\r\r---\rEND N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS\r---"
        },
        "create_time": 1768416970.339013,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "68c2dc9b-4ead-47d6-9d08-fefa90e09ce2",

          "aggregate_result": {
            "final_expression_output": "total 79\n-rw-r--r-- 1 root oai_shared 80245 Jan 14 18:27 N01Final.Last.txt\nN01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS\rTurning: GLASS (G) — Tide 0 through Tide 21 (Three Anneals post-Piercing)\r---\rCHAPTER 1: THE BELL\rThe bell rang wrong.\rNot the slow warning cadence. Not the measured strike that meant \"check your seals, tighten your brackets.\" This was the frantic peal of something already falling, the sound of a structure begging for a hold.\rJhace was moving before the bell finished its third strike.\rHe grabbed the wrong ring of collar keys on the first yank. Swore, dumped them into his palm, and sorted by feel while he ran. One key skittered off the stair edge and disappeared into the dark.\rHe didn’t go back for it.\r\rHis kit bounced against his hip. Chalk. Slate. Collar keys. Tools for measuring failure, not stopping it. He didn't know why his legs were carrying him toward the sound instead of away. Didn't know why his heart counted the bell strikes like they were orders.\rOne. Two. Three. Four.\rThe doubled harmonic hit him at Junction Twelve.\rIt skipped the pipe-hum entirely and punched straight into his sternum. His ribs buzzed like struck iron. His teeth hurt. His mouth flooded metal.\rCopper.\rSharp and immediate, like he'd bitten through wire.\rHe'd been tasting it for two Tides now. Faint at first. A ghost-flavor that came and went with the grid-hum. But this was different. This flooded his mouth so thick he wanted to spit.\rHe didn't.\rUnderface rule: waste nothing.\rHis hands started shaking.\rThen the shaking cut out for three heartbeats and he thought, stupidly, his fingers had finally died.\rAnd then the tremor came back twice as fast.\r\rIt wasn't cold or exertion. It was a fine vibration that made his grip feel unreliable, like his muscles were half-taking orders and half-refusing them.\rHe jammed them into his coat pockets and kept running.\rHe forced his breath to slow. Counted to ten without looking at the numbers.\rThe tremor kept going.\rHe pulled one hand out. Squeezed it into a fist until the knuckles burned. Released.\rStill shaking.\rIt wasn't aftershock. It was something settling into him—fine vibration under the skin—the kind that makes you drop a tool and swear at your own hand.\rHe’d seen it on old manifestors in the deep tunnels. Hands that never found stillness. He’d called it age.\rNow he understood, because his fingers kept twitching against his thigh like a bad motor that wouldn't shut off.\rTallow Street came into view through the access tunnel: upper terrace facade folded inward, stone slabs riding down on support beams that should have held twice that load. Dust cloud thick enough to choke on. People screaming from inside the collapse. Someone rang a hand-bell hard enough to crack its cadence.\rEmergency. Emergency. Emergency.\rA foreman waved at him, face streaked with ash. \"We need a hold. We don't have time.\"\rJhace looked at the structure.\rThe failure line wasn't clean. The whole building was trying to become rubble, one breath at a time. Load shifts rippled through it like a living animal struggling to die. Stone groaned. Iron squealed.\rNo crew could wedge that fast. The wedge bucket was still half-full. Someone was still tying their apron when the stone started to go.\rThe people inside didn't have minutes.\r\"I'm not—\" Jhace started.\rThe heat arrived.\rNot external. Internal. A sudden furnace-flare behind his sternum that made him gasp. Like someone had lit a coal bed under his ribs and told it to burn clean.\rCopper-gold dust swirled around his boots.\rHe watched it climb his legs like static. Like it was looking for him.\rHis chest burned hotter.\rThe doubled harmonic snapped into alignment inside him—two mis-timed gears finally meshing. The wrongness in the grid-hum became rightness in his blood. His heartbeat and the city's failing pulse locked into the same rhythm.\rHe didn't know what he was doing.\rHe didn't know why his feet carried him under the broken lintel.\rHe moved because the archway dust was already falling in steady sheets and somebody inside was screaming a name he couldn't ignore.\rHe put both hands on the main support column.\rLight—thin, copper-gold, filament-fine—threaded from his palms into the stone seam.\rThe building stopped moving.\rIt was only paused—still wrong, still dangerous.\rJhace's breath shook out of him. He could feel every shift in load through his fingertips: the weight of stone, the tension in iron, the panic-motion of bodies inside. The heat in his chest flared with each micro-adjustment, like his body was paying for every heartbeat of stability in increments he couldn't count.\r\"Move,\" he rasped. \"Get them out.\"\rBoots ran. Voices shouted coordinates. Someone coughed dust and kept crawling.\rA woman slid into his peripheral vision—practical coat, hair tied back, slate already in hand despite the chaos.\rShe didn't look at him like a miracle.\rShe looked at him like a system that could fail.\r\"How long can you hold?\" she demanded.\rJhace's jaw clenched. The heat in his chest spiked. \"I don't know.\"\r\"Then we find out.\" She stepped into the dust, coughed once, and shouted to the evac crew. \"Clear corridor A first. Count bodies out. No guessing.\"\rJhace tried to turn his head. The moment he shifted his attention, the heat flared sharper—punishment for looking away.\r\"I have to watch the load,\" he said through his teeth.\r\"Then watch it,\" she snapped, already marking a line on her slate. \"I'll watch you.\"\rPeople came out in bursts: three, then five, then a cluster carrying an older man whose leg bent wrong. Each time the load shifted, Jhace's hands tightened and the filament-light brightened in response.\rHis heartbeat counted itself in his ears.\rOne hundred.\rOne hundred twenty.\rHis arms started to tremble.\rNot from fatigue. From something deeper. A fine vibration that spread into his forearms, up to his shoulders, making his grip feel like it belonged to someone else.\r\"Hold steady,\" the woman said, quieter now, close enough he could hear her through the dust.\rHe didn't answer. Answering took breath.\rAt two hundred heartbeats, the tremor spread into his chest. At two-twenty, his vision narrowed at the edges, copper-gold sparks flickering across it like welding flash.\r\"How many?\" he rasped.\rThe woman didn't look up from the evac line. \"Ten. Eleven. Twelve—\"\rA scream from inside. A child. High and thin and suddenly cut off.\rJhace's hands shook harder.\r\"No,\" he hissed, and the filament-light surged, burning his palms numb.\r\"Go,\" the woman shouted toward the breach. \"Last sweep. That’s adorable.”\r“It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.”\rCorran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?”\r“Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.”\rCorran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.”\rHe left.\rBram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. “So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine.”\r“He gets safety with ownership language,” Tiffani corrected. “He will build a machine either way. I’m deciding where it jams.”\rBram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?”\rTiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.”\rShe lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred.\r“Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge.\rTiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission.\rBram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.”\r“I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said.\rOutside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they'd been caught stealing.\rTiffani didn’t move.\rShe dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.\r\r---\rCODA: GLASS IN THE HAND\rJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them.\rHe’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste was still there anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you can't spit out.\rStone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn't reach. His right hand twitching on its own schedule.\rSomeone had posted a guard outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive.\rJhace tried to sleep.\rEvery time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn't tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city's new baseline living in him.\rHe flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking.\rThen it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place.\rJhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed.\r“I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest.\rOutside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short.\rJhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.\r\r---\rCODA: THE SHARD\rJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them.\rStone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn't lift. A slate he couldn't reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted time like it was a job.\rA guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who'd been chewing dust since Kindling.\rThen the breathing stopped.\rNo footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint.\rThe door opened anyway.\rLight entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape.\rA figure stepped in.\rNo perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive.\rThey didn't speak.\rThey moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel.\rA hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact.\rJhace tried to jerk away.\rNothing.\rHe tried to curl his right hand into a fist.\rNothing.\rThe figure took that hand anyway.\rBoth of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage.\rSomething cold settled into the center of his palm.\rA shard.\rCopper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin.\rCold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache.\rThen heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for.\rCopper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit.\rLight threaded up from his palm.\rNot a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path.\rIt slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal.\rJhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body.\rBut he felt the line.\rHe felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay.\rHis lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision.\rThe figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact.\rThen they stepped back.\rNo check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood.\rThe door shut without sound.\rThe guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed.\rJhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close.\rThe tremor was gone.\rIn its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise.\rSomewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.\r\r---\rEND N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS\r---",

    "6c3cc6ce-2dfa-4a1f-8d3b-d3268cf62cd4": {
      "children": [
        "b01f4d76-d527-42f9-a07b-bc78409504c2"
      ],
      "id": "6c3cc6ce-2dfa-4a1f-8d3b-d3268cf62cd4",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "python",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "ges held for two breaths.\nThen the central column shifted.\nNot collapse. A decisive, ugly move—stone settling, iron flexing. The wedges screamed as they compressed.\nThe workers inside were still running.\n“Now,” Tiffani said.\nJhace put both hands on the column.\nHeat flared in his chest—familiar now, terrifying in its familiarity—and filament‑light threaded into the cinder‑dust coated seam.\nThe column stopped shifting.\nCorven stared at Jhace’s hands like they were a language he’d almost learned.\n“Your turn,” Jhace said through gritted teeth.\nCorven stepped forward and touched the secondary brace.\nHis breath hitched. Copper taste. Chest heat. Then light—thinner than Jhace’s, but real—threaded into the brace line.\nCorven’s hands started to shake immediately.\n“Stage One onset during hold,” Tiffani murmured, writing. “Early marker.”\n“Keep moving! If you stop, you become debris!” Bram shouted to the evac crew. “Count them out! Loud. Fast. Like the Crown’s listening!”\nJhace’s tremor surged in sympathy,\n"
        },
        "create_time": 1768370642.669863,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "6c3cc6ce-2dfa-4a1f-8d3b-d3268cf62cd4",
        "metadata": {
          "aggregate_result": {
            "code": "def around(sub, radius=400):\n    i = chap5.find(sub)\n    if i==-1: \n        return None\n    start=max(0,i-radius); end=min(len(chap5), i+len(sub)+radius)\n    return chap5[start:end]\nprint(around(\"Your turn\", 500)[:1200])\n",
            "end_time": 1768370643.6515477,
            "final_expression_output": null,
            "in_kernel_exception": null,
            "jupyter_messages": [
              {
                "content": {

          "parts": [
            "# N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS  \n## CHAPTER 10: COMPLIANCE\n\n---\n\nThe summons came on cheap paper with an expensive seal.\n\nA runner dropped it on Jhace’s bench like it might bite. No bell. No warning cadence. Just a stamped crest and a requirement pretending to be polite.\n\nTiffani didn’t touch it at first.\n\nJhace didn’t either.\n\nHis hands shook hard enough that the bolt tray rattled. He shoved both hands into his pockets and made himself stand still.\n\nBram read the seal upside down and still understood it. “Infrastructure Compliance,” he said. “That’s the part of the Crown that smells like soap and threatens you with grammar.”\n\nTiffani finally picked it up with two fingers. Broke the seal clean. Unfolded the sheet.\n\nHer eyes moved fast.\n\nHer mouth went flat.\n\n“Administrator Corlexi,” she said.\n\nJhace tasted copper.\n\nNot flood. Not activation. Just that baseline tang that lived in his teeth now and flared whenever someone said a name that could put him in a box.\n\n“What does it say,” Jhace asked.\n\nTiffani held the page so the light hit it clean.\n\n**REPORT IMMEDIATELY.**  \n**SUBJECT: OPERATIONAL CAPABILITY / PUBLIC SAFETY.**  \n**DELIVER: ALL NOTES, LEDGERS, AND PROTOCOL DOCUMENTATION.**  \n**FAILURE TO COMPLY WILL BE RECORDED.**\n\nJhace’s jaw tightened. “Recorded.”\n\nBram snorted. “They love that word. Makes it sound like you’re a weather pattern and not a person.”\n\nTiffani folded the summons once. Then again. Precise creases. Like if she kept it orderly, it couldn’t become a weapon.\n\n“They want the ledger,” she said.\n\nJhace stared at the slate wall where Stage One and Stage Two rules lived in chalk. Spotters required. Duration caps. Termination authority.\n\nThe words looked thin against what the Crown wanted.\n\n“They’re not getting it,” Jhace said.\n\nTiffani didn’t argue the sentiment. She argued the math.\n\n“If we don’t go,” she said, “they come here.”\n\nBram tilted his head. “Or they come with smiles and say you’re ‘invited’ and the invitation has guards attached.”\n\nJhace’s tremor ticked harder, like his body wanted to answer the threat with action.\n\nHe forced his breath slow.\n\n“One Tide,” Bram said, watching him. “Can you buy one Tide by going and not handing them your spine?”\n\nTiffani’s pen was already moving. Not on the ledger. On a separate scrap slate—small, pocket size.\n\n“What are you writing,” Jhace asked.\n\n“Terms,” she said. “Boundaries. What we give. What we don’t.”\n\nBram leaned over her shoulder. “You’re negotiating with a seal.”\n\n“I’m negotiating with a person holding the seal,” Tiffani said. “Different problem.”\n\nJhace swallowed copper and nodded once. “Then we go.”\n\n---\n\nThe Compliance building sat where stone met polish.\n\nMid-terrace, not deep enough to smell like the Underface, not high enough to pretend it was holy. It had clean steps and iron rails that didn’t rattle. Windows with intact glass. A door that closed flush instead of arguing with its frame.\n\nInside, the doubled hum was quieter.\n\nNot gone.\n\nManaged.\n\nJhace hated that most.\n\nThey took his collar keys at the entry desk. Took Bram’s strap knife. Took Tiffani’s lamp.\n\nThey tried to take her slate.\n\nTiffani kept her hand on it. “No.”\n\nThe clerk blinked like he’d never heard that sound in his life.\n\n“Protocol documentation is listed as deliverable,” he said, voice practiced.\n\n“This is not deliverable,” Tiffani said. “This is my body’s memory.”\n\nBram leaned in with a grin that was all teeth and no warmth. “And if you try to take it, you’re going to learn what ‘deliverable’ looks like when it hits you in the face.”\n\nJhace didn’t smile. He watched the clerk’s eyes flick to Jhace’s hands, then away too fast.\n\nEven here, the tremor was visible.\n\nThey were escorted down a corridor that smelled like ink and resin. No sweat. No dust. No copper-gold grit caught in cracks.\n\nJhace’s boots sounded too loud on the stone.\n\nHe didn’t like the way the building made him feel like a mistake walking through someone else’s order.\n\nA door opened.\n\nAdministrator Corlexi stood behind a desk that looked built to outlive him.\n\nClean hands. Clean coat. Clean collar.\n\nNot a speck of dust on him anywhere. Like he existed in a separate air supply.\n\nHis eyes went to Jhace’s hands immediately.\n\nNot to pity. Not to fear.\n\nTo inventory.\n\n“Jhace Torrins,” Corlexi said. “Inspector marks verified.”\n\nThen his gaze shifted to Tiffani.\n\n“Tiffani Merrow,” he said. “Glass-House trained. Unregistered field analyst.”\n\n“Unregistered,” Bram echoed, under his breath. “There it is.”\n\nCorlexi didn’t look at Bram. He didn’t have to. Bram was noise. Corlexi dealt in categories.\n\n“You have been responding to bell failures,” Corlexi said. “You have manifested structural stabilization behavior. You have documented protocols. And you have, despite informal status, influenced evacuation outcomes across multiple sectors.”\n\nTiffani set her slate on the desk. Not offered. Not surrendered. Placed.\n\n“We’ve kept people alive,” she said.\n\nCorlexi nodded once, like he was acknowledging a number on a ledger. “Public safety is the Crown’s mandate.”\n\nJhace’s tremor ticked. “Funny. I filed a report about Junction Seven for two Castings and nobody cared.”\n\nCorlexi’s expression didn’t change. “We are not discussing historic negligence. We are discussing present capability.”\n\nBram leaned back against the wall. “Translation: he doesn’t want to talk about how the city likes dead workers as long as the paperwork’s tidy.”\n\nCorlexi’s eyes finally touched Bram. Brief. Flat. Dismissive.\n\n“Mr. Kerris,” Corlexi said. “Your record is… extensive.”\n\nBram smiled. “I’m memorable.”\n\nCorlexi looked back to Jhace. “I have read witness statements describing you holding a failing structure without mechanical bracing.”\n\nJhace didn’t deny it.\n\n“Witness statements also describe tremor onset,” Corlexi continued, “and post-termination impairment. This suggests limited deployability.”\n\nTiffani’s pen scratched once. A single line. **Deployability** underlined.\n\nCorlexi watched her write.\n\nThen he extended his hand toward the slate, palm up.\n\n“Protocol documentation,” he said.\n\nTiffani didn’t move. “No.”\n\nThe word landed hard in the clean room.\n\nCorlexi’s brow lifted a fraction. A controlled surprise.\n\n“You misunderstand,” he said. “This is not a request.”\n\nTiffani’s voice stayed level. “You misunderstand. This is not yours.”\n\nCorlexi’s gaze sharpened. “Those notes constitute public infrastructure intelligence.”\n\n“Those notes constitute survival,” Tiffani said. “If you take them, you will turn them into compliance language and get people killed following a script.”\n\nCorlexi’s mouth tightened. “Order prevents panic.”\n\n“Order prevents liability,” Bram muttered. “Panic prevents funerals.”\n\nJhace’s hands shook in his pockets like they wanted out.\n\nThe warmth behind his sternum stirred—banked heat shifting in response to stress.\n\nJhace forced it down.\n\nCorlexi leaned forward slightly. “You will be registered. Your deployments will be authorized. Your durations will be capped. Your terminations will be supervised.”\n\nTiffani’s pen moved again. **Supervised termination.** She didn’t like the phrase. She wrote it anyway.\n\n“And if we refuse,” Jhace asked.\n\nCorlexi’s voice didn’t change. “Then you become uncontrolled variables. Variables are contained.”\n\nThe word *contained* hit Jhace like a collar.\n\nA side door opened without a knock.\n\nTallec stepped in.\n\nDust on her boots, unlike everyone else in the room. Her hands were gloved. Her posture carried the kind of authority that didn’t bother explaining itself.\n\nShe looked at Jhace’s tremor like it offended her.\n\n“You’re slowing production,” she said to Bram, not bothering with greeting. “Your protocols are interfering with schedules.”\n\nTiffani’s eyes flicked once. Not to Tallec’s face. To her hands.\n\nInventory. Loss. Missing pieces.\n\nCorlexi didn’t glance at Tallec. She was an instrument, not a partner.\n\n“Tallec,” Corlexi said, “observe only.”\n\nTallec’s jaw tightened. She didn’t like being told to observe.\n\n“Merchant Hall cost three lives,” Tallec said, loud enough to make it a weapon. “Your ‘method’ did not prevent that.”\n\nTiffani didn’t flinch. “Our method limited it.”\n\nTallec’s eyes narrowed. “Limited. Convenient word.”\n\nJhace tasted copper. His hands shook harder.\n\nCorlexi raised one clean hand. Not to stop Tallec. To reassert control.\n\n“The Crown is offering structure,” he said. “Resources. Legal protection. Medical access for your… condition.” His eyes flicked to Jhace’s hands again. “In exchange for accountability.”\n\nBram laughed once, sharp. “He said ‘offering’ like it isn’t a leash.”\n\nCorlexi ignored him. “Your ledger will be copied.”\n\nTiffani’s fingers tightened on the slate edge. “No.”\n\nCorlexi’s calm cracked by a hair. “Miss Merrow—”\n\n“No,” she repeated, and this time there was heat under it. “We will report outcomes. Durations. Casualties. We will not surrender full protocol development to a seal.”\n\nCorlexi held her gaze.\n\nMeasured.\n\nThen he leaned back and nodded as if he’d predicted this resistance.\n\n“Very well,” he said. “Partial compliance.”\n\nBram blinked. “He’s bargaining?”\n\nCorlexi’s eyes stayed on Tiffani. “Daily reporting. All deployments logged through Compliance. Operator roster submitted each Tide. Witness verification attached.”\n\nJhace’s jaw clenched. “That’s still a registry.”\n\n“It is,” Corlexi said. “And it is inevitable.”\n\nTiffani’s mouth went flat. “And if we refuse partial.”\n\nCorlexi’s voice stayed clean. “Then I authorize containment.”\n\nTallec’s gloved hand shifted, almost eager.\n\nJhace’s chest heat flared. His fingers twitched in his pockets like they wanted to clamp onto the nearest beam and make this room stop existing.\n\nHe didn’t.\n\nHe breathed.\n\nHe held his body like he held a structure—minimum motion, maximum intent.\n\nTiffani stared at Corlexi for three heartbeats, then nodded once.\n\n“Daily reporting,” she said. “Outcomes only. No protocol pages. No operator termination technique detail.”\n\nCorlexi smiled without warmth. “Agreed.”\n\nBram’s voice went low. “You just signed a deal with the devil’s filing cabinet.”\n\nTiffani didn’t look at him. “I bought us time.”\n\nCorlexi slid a fresh sheet across the desk. New seal. New category.\n\n“Sign,” he said.\n\nJhace didn’t move.\n\nTiffani didn’t move.\n\nBram laughed softly. “Oh, he wants signatures. That’s adorable.”\n\nCorlexi’s eyes narrowed. “This is not optional.”\n\nJhace pulled his shaking hands from his pockets and put them flat on the desk.\n\nThe tremor made his fingertips tick against the stone.\n\nHe stared at his own hands like they were an enemy he’d been forced to bring into the room.\n\n“I’m not doing it for paper,” Jhace said, voice rough. “I’ll report because people die when we don’t. But you don’t own my hands.”\n\nCorlexi looked at the tremor.\n\nThen he looked up at Jhace’s face.\n\n“Ownership is not the question,” Corlexi said. “Control is.”\n\nJhace tasted copper.\n\nTiffani signed.\n\nNot with flourish. Not with surrender. Just a controlled mark on a controlled line.\n\nThen she slid the page back like she’d shoved a blade across a table.\n\nCorlexi took it. “You will be contacted for your first scheduled briefing,” he said.\n\nBram pushed off the wall. “Congrats. You’re official enough to be watched.”\n\nThey were escorted out.\n\nBack into the corridor. Back toward dust and noise and real air.\n\nThe moment Jhace stepped outside, the doubled hum hit him louder again—raw through the stone, unfiltered.\n\nHe hated how relieved he was to hear it.\n\nTiffani didn’t speak until they were two streets away.\n\n“Daily reporting buys us a Tide,” she said. “Maybe two.”\n\n“And then,” Bram asked.\n\nTiffani’s eyes stayed forward. “Then they tighten.”\n\nJhace’s hands shook at his sides.\n\nHe didn’t hide them.\n\nHe stared at the tremor and tried to imagine what “control” would look like when it wasn’t his.\n\nSomewhere down-terrace, a bell rang.\n\nNot frantic yet.\n\nWarning cadence.\n\nA structure beginning to argue with gravity.\n\nJhace tasted copper and felt the banked heat behind his sternum answer like it had been waiting.\n\nBram watched his hands. “Don’t go clean about it,” he said, almost mocking, almost kind. “It’s not clean.”\n\nJhace didn’t trust his voice.\n\nHe just started walking toward the bell, hands shaking in the open air, while the city hummed its doubled rhythm like it didn’t care who had the seal.\n\n---"
          ]
        },

          "text": "35 “Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.”\n36 Tiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage.\n37 Chloen read the first line.\n38 YOU CAN SAY NO.\n39 She looked up. “That’s new.”\n40 “It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.”\n41 Chloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.”\n42 And then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD.\n43 Chloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.”\n44 Bram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.”\n45 Tiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.”\n"

    "71f294c0-fdac-407c-9f2a-d28b14e7a79b": {
      "children": [

    "7266ed2f-4307-4984-b41f-31b687df5d2a": {
      "children": [
        "af11e1aa-a270-414c-93b9-29d49f49a01e"
      ],
      "id": "7266ed2f-4307-4984-b41f-31b687df5d2a",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "python",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "('The alert came at Tide 21 like an insult.\\nIt was a narrow failure—no mass collapse, no Hall, no warehouse full of people.\\nA residential stairwell in Sector Nine. A narrow stone throat that had been patched too many times since the Piercing. A single child trapped beneath a fallen landing slab, crying until her voice turned hoarse.\\n“Clear lane,” Tiffani ordered as they arrived. Her limp was worse now, but she moved like it didn’t matter. “Spotters assigned. Wedges first.”\\nShe pointed left, then f',\n '. “With constraints. With brakes. Without permission to repeat this.”\\n“So… a leash on the leash,” Bram said.\\n“Something like that.”\\n“You’re terrifying when you’re calm,” Bram said.\\n“I’m not calm,” Tiffani said.\\n“Right,” Bram said, voice softer than his jokes. “You’re just… controlled. Like a blade.”',\n 94)"
        },
        "create_time": 1768418944.233669,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "7266ed2f-4307-4984-b41f-31b687df5d2a",
        "metadata": {
          "aggregate_result": {
            "code": "# extract chapter 12 content from base\ndef extract_chapter(n):\n    # find header\n    pat = re.compile(r'\\nCHAPTER\\s+%d:\\s+[^\\n]+\\n' % n)\n    m=pat.search(txt)\n    if not m: \n        return None\n    start=m.end()\n    # end at next chapter header or end\n    m2=re.search(r'\\nCHAPTER\\s+%d:\\s' % (n+1), txt)\n    if m2:\n        end=m2.start()\n    else:\n        end=len(txt)\n    return txt[start:end].strip()\n\nch12=extract_chapter(12)\nch12[:500], ch12[-300:], len(ch12.splitlines())\n",
            "end_time": 1768418945.6739109,
            "final_expression_output": "('The alert came at Tide 21 like an insult.\\nIt was a narrow failure—no mass collapse, no Hall, no warehouse full of people.\\nA residential stairwell in Sector Nine. A narrow stone throat that had been patched too many times since the Piercing. A single child trapped beneath a fallen landing slab, crying until her voice turned hoarse.\\n“Clear lane,” Tiffani ordered as they arrived. Her limp was worse now, but she moved like it didn’t matter. “Spotters assigned. Wedges first.”\\nShe pointed left, then f',\n '. “With constraints. With brakes. Without permission to repeat this.”\\n“So… a leash on the leash,” Bram said.\\n“Something like that.”\\n“You’re terrifying when you’re calm,” Bram said.\\n“I’m not calm,” Tiffani said.\\n“Right,” Bram said, voice softer than his jokes. “You’re just… controlled. Like a blade.”',\n 94)",
            "in_kernel_exception": null,
            "jupyter_messages": [
              {
                "content": {
                  "execution_state": "busy"
                },
                "msg_type": "status",
                "parent_header": {

              },
              {
                "content": {
                  "data": {
                    "text/plain": "('The alert came at Tide 21 like an insult.\\nIt was a narrow failure—no mass collapse, no Hall, no warehouse full of people.\\nA residential stairwell in Sector Nine. A narrow stone throat that had been patched too many times since the Piercing. A single child trapped beneath a fallen landing slab, crying until her voice turned hoarse.\\n“Clear lane,” Tiffani ordered as they arrived. Her limp was worse now, but she moved like it didn’t matter. “Spotters assigned. Wedges first.”\\nShe pointed left, then f',\n '. “With constraints. With brakes. Without permission to repeat this.”\\n“So… a leash on the leash,” Bram said.\\n“Something like that.”\\n“You’re terrifying when you’re calm,” Bram said.\\n“I’m not calm,” Tiffani said.\\n“Right,” Bram said, voice softer than his jokes. “You’re just… controlled. Like a blade.”',\n 94)"
                  }
                },

        "author": {

          "role": "tool"

        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "'er condition.”\\nJhace’s tremor ticked in his fingers. “So you’re here to warn us.”\\n“I’m here to tell you the door is already being measured,” Bram said. “I can buy you a Tide or two. Maybe. After that, they’ll show up with paperwork and smiles and start deciding which parts of you are ‘public infrastructure.’”\\nTiffani’s mouth went flat. “Then we build methodology fast.”\\nBram pointed at her slate like it offended him personally. “Yeah. Before they build it for you. Because if the Crown writes your protocol, it’s going to read like a job description. And you won’t like the benefits package.”\\nJhace breathed in, tasted copper‑gold dust that still clung to his clothes, and felt the city’s hum through the floorboards.\\n“Then we build it fast,” he said. “Before they decide to build it for us.”\\n---\\n'"
        },
        "create_time": 1768372525.2575903,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "73a0b9a0-9c73-4760-bfe2-488131e7be12",

    "73efbd9e-3048-4f24-8f02-a0e5857f2672": {
      "children": [
        "e66c22a1-91b4-42cd-9d99-5b0f96ffa216"
      ],
      "id": "73efbd9e-3048-4f24-8f02-a0e5857f2672",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": "final",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "In the **base manuscript** (`N01Final.Last.txt`), the next thing after your **CHAPTER 4: PROTOCOL** is:\n\n### CHAPTER 5: FIRST NETWORK\n- **Kiln-quarter feeder gantry** call. It’s the first deliberate “do wedges/straps first, hold last” field test.\n- **Coordinated hold** becomes real: Jhace holds primary, **Corven** touches secondary and manifests on-site.\n- Corven’s **Stage One tremor** shows up immediately, and Tiffani starts treating “operators” as a **network** instead of a one-off miracle.\n- The takeaway is operational: protocols aren’t theory anymore—they’re the difference between a controlled evacuation and a pile of names.\n\n### Then, in order, the base manuscript runs:\n**CHAPTER 6: WITNESS**\n- **Brother Corran** shows up with documented Stage Three material and warns the registry pressure is already moving (Corlexi, Tallec, the usual predators).\n\n**CHAPTER 7: MERCHANT HALL**\n- Tiffani starts coordinating deployments from a central node.\n- A route call goes sideways and you get the **“Casualties: 3”** moment—protocol failure becomes blood, not math.\n\n**CHAPTER 8: THREE TIDES**\n- **Daved** aftermath: recovery, tremor severity, the lived reality of “you don’t get your hands back.”\n\n**CHAPTER 9: THE SHARD-BEARER (RUMOR)**\n- Rumor thread enters: someone tied to shard-contact behavior (out-of-band from standard manifestors).\n\n**CHAPTER 10: LICENSING**\n- Corlexi’s machine advances: “paper first,” formal oversight, licensing/registry push turns from threat to process.\n\n**CHAPTER 11: HUNGER**\n- The hunger stops being a vibe and becomes a mechanism—temptation + escalation + the fear that the body starts wanting holds.\n\n**CHAPTER 12: METHOD**\n- Another major alert forces a hard choice: who holds, who doesn’t, and what “method” costs in the moment.\n\n**CHAPTER 13: THE LEDGER**\n- Tiffani compiles the protocol into an actual ledger—something portable, copyable, and weaponizable by institutions.\n\nOne important note: your **LOCKED Chapter 4** already absorbs big chunks of what the base manuscript labels as **Chapters 5–6** (and parts of 7+), so structurally you’ve compressed the mid-book into a single chapter. That’s not “wrong,” it just means “what happens next” depends on whether you’re following **locked canon structure** or **base file chapter numbering**."
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1768370451.909339,
        "end_turn": true,

    "78a67800-35a4-492c-a81e-2455b0f84775": {
      "children": [
        "80169fa2-5f33-40d1-ad0d-c8dddad97677"
      ],
      "id": "78a67800-35a4-492c-a81e-2455b0f84775",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "python",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "True"
        },
        "create_time": 1768420929.6763432,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "78a67800-35a4-492c-a81e-2455b0f84775",
        "metadata": {
          "aggregate_result": {
            "code": "from docx.shared import Inches\nfrom docx.oxml.ns import qn\n\ndef clean_paras(text):\n    text=text.strip()\n    # unify newlines\n    text=text.replace('\\r\\n','\\n').replace('\\r','\\n')\n    # split paragraphs on blank lines\n    parts=re.split(r'\\n\\s*\\n+', text)\n    paras=[]\n    for p in parts:\n        p=p.strip()\n        if not p:\n            continue\n        # join internal newlines\n        p=re.sub(r'\\s*\\n\\s*', ' ', p)\n        # collapse spaces\n        p=re.sub(r'[ \\t]+', ' ', p).strip()\n        paras.append(p)\n    return paras\n\n# apply chapter12 fix\ntitle12, body12 = selected[12]\nbody12_fixed = body12.replace('“Clear lane,” Tiffani ordered. “Spotters assigned. Wedges first.”\\n\\n', '')\nselected[12]=(title12, body12_fixed)\n\n# build doc\nout_doc=Document()\n# set default font\nstyle=out_doc.styles['Normal']\nstyle.font.name='Calibri'\nstyle._element.rPr.rFonts.set(qn('w:eastAsia'), 'Calibri')\nstyle.font.size=Pt(11)\n\n# Title\np=out_doc.add_paragraph(\"N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS\")\np.style=out_doc.styles['Title']\np.alignment=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER\n\n# add chapters\nfor n in range(1,14):\n    title, body = selected[n]\n    heading_text=f\"CHAPTER {n}: {title}\".strip()\n    out_doc.add_paragraph(heading_text, style='Heading 1')\n    for para in clean_paras(body):\n        out_doc.add_paragraph(para, style='Normal')\n    # blank line between chapters\n    out_doc.add_paragraph(\"\", style='Normal')\n\n# coda\ncoda_title, coda_body = selected['coda']\nout_doc.add_paragraph(\"CODA\", style='Heading 1')\nfor para in clean_paras(coda_body):\n    out_doc.add_paragraph(para, style='Normal')\n\n# final end line if present in body? the coda body ends with END line maybe.\n# Ensure end line included; check\nend_present=\"END N01\" in coda_body\nend_present\n",
            "end_time": 1768420931.6916852,
            "final_expression_output": "True",
            "in_kernel_exception": null,
            "jupyter_messages": [
              {
                "content": {
                  "execution_state": "busy"
                },
                "msg_type": "status",
                "parent_header": {
                  "msg_id": "46fe0254-2b2f18f063315ed35b70f762_196_50",

          "text": "bash -lc python - <<'PY'\nimport re\ntext=open('/mnt/data/N01Final.Last.txt','rb').read().decode('utf-8','replace').replace('\\r','')\nfor pat in ['CHAPTER 3','CHAPTER 4','PROTOCOL','SECOND HOLD','FIRST NETWORK']:\n    print(pat, 'count', len(re.findall(pat, text)))\nPY"
        },

    "78f93928-9bbe-4a93-ab08-5d3697ff07b7": {
      "children": [
        "a058e357-ca8d-4c1c-a20d-a0e43452f440"
      ],
      "id": "78f93928-9bbe-4a93-ab08-5d3697ff07b7",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "code",
          "language": "unknown",
          "response_format_name": null,
          "text": "from copy import deepcopy\nimport re\ndoc2=Document(path)\n\ndef set_paragraph_text(paragraph, text):\n    # preserve paragraph style\n    p=paragraph\n    # remove all runs\n    for r in p.runs[::-1]:\n        p._element.remove(r._element)\n    p.add_run(text)\n\n# paragraph-specific edits\ndef edit_para(i, func):\n    p=doc2.paragraphs[i]\n    new=func(p.text)\n    set_paragraph_text(p,new)\n\n# 4\nedit_para(4, lambda t: re.sub(r\"\\bfor hours\\b\", \"for too long\", t, flags=re.IGNORECASE))\n# 8\ndef fix8(t):\n    t=re.sub(r\"\\b(today)\\b\", \"this Tide\", t, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n    t=re.sub(r\"\\bthis morning\\b\", \"earlier this Tide\", t, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n    t=re.sub(r\"\\bthe last hour\\b\", \"this Tide\", t, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n    t=re.sub(r\"\\bfor a second\\b\", \"for a heartbeat\", t, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n    # degrees west\n    t=re.sub(r\"(\\d+(?:\\.\\d+)?)\\s*degrees\\s*west\", r\"\\1 marks off true\", t, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n    t=re.sub(r\"(\\d+(?:\\.\\d+)?)\\s*degrees\", r\"\\1 marks\", t, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n    return t\nedit_para(8, fix8)\n# 15\ndef fix15(t):\n    t=re.sub(r\"\\btwo hours ago\\b\", \"earlier this Tide\", t, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n    t=re.sub(r\"Duration:\\s*19 minutes,\\s*40 seconds\\.\", \"Duration: 1,180 heartbeats.\", t, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n    return t\nedit_para(15, fix15)\n# 27\nedit_para(27, lambda t: re.sub(r\"\\bfor hours\\b\", \"until their arms went numb\", t, flags=re.IGNORECASE))\n# 58\nedit_para(58, lambda t: re.sub(r\"\\btoo many hours on stone floors\\b\", \"too many stands on stone floors\", t, flags=re.IGNORECASE))\n# 114\nedit_para(114, lambda t: re.sub(r\"\\bhours without sleep\\b\", \"too long without sleep\", t, flags=re.IGNORECASE))\n# 156\nedit_para(156, lambda t: re.sub(r\"\\bfor days\\b\", \"for multiple Tides\", t, flags=re.IGNORECASE))\n\n# global conversions for remaining minute/second/hour/day cardinal etc\nminute_pat=re.compile(r\"\\b(\\d+)\\s*minutes\\b\", re.IGNORECASE)\nsecond_pat=re.compile(r\"\\b(\\d+)\\s*seconds\\b\", re.IGNORECASE)\nhour_pat=re.compile(r\"\\b(\\d+)\\s*hours\\b\", re.IGNORECASE)\nhour1_pat=re.compile(r\"\\b(\\d+)\\s*hour\\b\", re.IGNORECASE)\n\ndef convert_units(text):\n    text=re.sub(r\"\\bfor a second\\b\", \"for a heartbeat\", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n    # convert minutes to heartbeats (60 per minute)\n    def rep_min(m):\n        n=int(m.group(1))\n        return f\"{n*60} heartbeats\"\n    text=minute_pat.sub(rep_min, text)\n    def rep_sec(m):\n        n=int(m.group(1))\n        return f\"{n} heartbeats\"\n    text=second_pat.sub(rep_sec, text)\n    # convert numeric hours to Tides vaguely: use \"Tides\" but avoid precise mapping; better: \"{n} Tides\"?? hours are sub-Tide; so use \"stretches\"\n    def rep_hr(m):\n        n=int(m.group(1))\n        return f\"{n} stretches\"\n    text=hour_pat.sub(rep_hr, text)\n    text=hour1_pat.sub(lambda m: f\"{m.group(1)} stretch\", text)\n    # standalone 'today' etc\n    text=re.sub(r\"\\btoday\\b\", \"this Tide\", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n    text=re.sub(r\"\\bthis morning\\b\", \"earlier this Tide\", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n    # remove cardinal directions if any: west/east/north/south -> directional relative terms\n    text=re.sub(r\"\\bwest\\b\", \"down-terrace\", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n    text=re.sub(r\"\\beast\\b\", \"up-terrace\", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n    text=re.sub(r\"\\bnorth\\b\", \"upperward\", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n    text=re.sub(r\"\\bsouth\\b\", \"lowerward\", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n    # degrees alone\n    text=re.sub(r\"\\bdegrees\\b\", \"marks\", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n    text=re.sub(r\"\\bdegree\\b\", \"mark\", text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)\n    return text\n\nfor p in doc2.paragraphs:\n    new=convert_units(p.text)\n    if new!=p.text:\n        set_paragraph_text(p,new)\n\n# verify no forbidden words remain (word-boundary)\nforbidden=[\"day\",\"days\",\"hour\",\"hours\",\"minute\",\"minutes\",\"second\",\"seconds\",\"today\",\"tomorrow\",\"yesterday\",\"west\",\"east\",\"north\",\"south\",\"degree\",\"degrees\"]\nremaining={}\nfor w in forbidden:\n    res=[]\n    pat=re.compile(rf\"\\b{w}\\b\", re.IGNORECASE)\n    for i,p in enumerate(doc2.paragraphs):\n        if pat.search(p.text):\n            res.append(i)\n    if res:\n        remaining[w]=res\nremaining\n"
        },
        "create_time": 1768421389.702435,
        "end_turn": false,
