    "79356351-34b3-41a8-8e5e-572f0b8e5eea": {
      "children": [
        "a86d049a-fcba-428d-b234-a72a59112ada"
      ],
      "id": "79356351-34b3-41a8-8e5e-572f0b8e5eea",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": "final",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "# N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS  \n## CHAPTER 9: SHARD-BEARER (RUMOR) — VARIANT A\n\n---\n\nRumor moved faster than bells.\n\nIt didn’t need cadence. It didn’t need proof. It just needed mouths.\n\nBy the second Tide after Daved’s eyes started tracking, the Underface was already trading a new story.\n\nNot about holds.\n\nNot about tremor.\n\nAbout someone who didn’t need to touch the structure at all.\n\nJhace heard it first from a runner who’d come for wedge slips and left with a mouthful of panic.\n\n“Someone cut a beam,” the runner said. “Not sawed. Not wedged. Cut.”\n\nJhace kept his hands jammed in his pockets. The tremor made the fabric buzz against his knuckles.\n\n“Cut how.”\n\nThe runner shrugged too hard. “Like it was soft.”\n\nBram leaned against the doorway, half a grin loaded and failing to fire. “People cut beams all the time.”\n\n“Not like this,” the runner said. “No sparks. No tools. Just—” He made a straight line with his hand through the air. “And it opened.”\n\nTiffani didn’t look up from her slate. “Where.”\n\n“Sector Twelve,” the runner said. “Near the spill-alleys. No bell. Just screaming.”\n\n“No bell,” Tiffani repeated.\n\n“Yeah. Because nothing collapsed. It just… stopped being connected.”\n\nJhace tasted copper.\n\nNot flood. Not activation.\n\nJust the baseline tang that now lived behind every word.\n\n“Who saw it,” Tiffani said.\n\n“Two guards. One dockhand. An old woman who never lies because she doesn’t think anyone’s worth lying to.” The runner swallowed. “They said it was a person. Not light. Not hold. A person.”\n\nBram’s voice went dry. “People are always the worst part.”\n\nThe runner looked at Bram like he didn’t know whether to laugh or throw up. “They said the person was carrying something.”\n\nTiffani’s pen finally paused.\n\n“Describe,” she said.\n\nThe runner made a shape with his hands like he was trying to hold the air without getting cut.\n\n“A piece. Long. Flat. Like metal but not metal. No handle. Just… edge.”\n\nJhace’s chest went tight.\n\nHe didn’t know why that detail hit harder than “cut a beam.”\n\nHe knew exactly why.\n\nThe spindle. The copper-gold particulate. The grit that tingled on skin.\n\nPieces had to be somewhere.\n\nPieces didn’t stay harmless just because they were smaller.\n\nTiffani wrote a new line in hard chalk.\n\n**Rumor:** non-contact structural severance.  \n**Object:** shard fragment (blade-form).  \n**Location:** Sector Twelve spill-alleys.  \n**No bell. No collapse.**\n\nBram watched her write. “You’re documenting rumors now.”\n\n“I’m documenting inputs,” Tiffani said. “Rumors become bell calls when they get bored.”\n\nJhace stared at his hands.\n\nThey shook anyway.\n\n---\n\nThey didn’t go to Sector Twelve as a team.\n\nNot officially.\n\nOfficial meant eyes. Badges. Questions. Corlexi’s clean hands writing their names into a new category.\n\nSo they went like workers.\n\nBram first, because Bram could walk into a crowd and come out with the right lie.\n\nTiffani second, because she could hear patterns in nonsense and call it evidence.\n\nJhace last, because Jhace had become recognizable in the wrong way.\n\nThey moved through mid-terrace markets where the doubled hum made glass rattle in frames. Copper-gold grit clung to iron hooks and nails like it had preferences.\n\nThe city looked stable from a distance.\n\nUp close, every seam was one breath away from deciding otherwise.\n\nBram found the first witness at a stall that sold boiled root and cheap cloth.\n\nOld woman. Bent spine. Hands like dried rope.\n\nShe didn’t look impressed when Bram said hello. She looked annoyed that he had used air.\n\n“You’re asking about the cut,” she said.\n\nBram blinked. “I didn’t even—”\n\n“I hear you,” she said. “You ask the same way everyone asks. Like you’re trying to look casual while your eyes scream.”\n\nTiffani stepped in. “Tell me what you saw.”\n\nThe woman’s gaze flicked to Tiffani’s slate. “You write it down.”\n\n“Yes.”\n\n“Good. Then you can’t pretend later.” The woman spit into the dust. “I saw a woman walk down the alley like she owned the angle of it. Didn’t run. Didn’t hurry. Just walked.”\n\nJhace’s mouth tightened.\n\n“Carrying something?” Tiffani asked.\n\nThe woman nodded. “A piece. Long. Thin. No handle. Like a blade that never learned how to be a tool.”\n\nBram made a face. “That’s poetic for an old woman selling root.”\n\nThe woman’s eyes sharpened. “I can still hate prettily.”\n\nTiffani didn’t flinch. “What did she do with it.”\n\nThe woman lifted her hand and made the same straight line the runner had made.\n\n“She didn’t touch the beam. She pointed the piece at it. Like she was accusing it.”\n\nJhace felt his teeth itch.\n\nThe doubled hum under the city grid did that when his body was already listening.\n\n“And then?” Tiffani asked.\n\n“And then the beam wasn’t one beam anymore.” The woman’s voice stayed flat. “It was two. The top part sagged and settled like it had been waiting for permission. Nobody died because nobody was under it. The screaming was because people saw it happen.”\n\nBram exhaled. “So the rumor’s true.”\n\n“Truth doesn’t matter,” the woman said. “Fear does. Fear spreads. Truth just trails behind, trying to keep up.”\n\nTiffani wrote.\n\nJhace watched the chalk move and felt that old anger rise—the one that wasn’t at Corlexi or the Temple or the Guild.\n\nAt the world.\n\nAt how fast it broke.\n\n“How close were you,” Tiffani asked.\n\n“Close enough to smell her.” The woman grimaced. “Not sweat. Not blood. Copper. Like she’d been chewing coins.”\n\nJhace swallowed.\n\nHe could taste copper right now.\n\nAlways.\n\nBut the way the woman said it made it sound different.\n\nNot baseline.\n\nImprinted.\n\nTiffani’s pen scratched faster.\n\n**Sensory:** copper odor on subject.  \n**Method:** non-contact severance using shard fragment.  \n**Effect:** clean split. No collapse initiated.\n\nBram shifted. “Did she have tremor.”\n\nThe woman barked a short laugh. “You think people who do that stand there shaking like a sick dog?”\n\nJhace’s jaw clenched.\n\nTiffani didn’t look up. “Did she shake.”\n\n“No,” the woman said. “Her hand was steady. Steady like she didn’t care if it worked because it had already worked before.”\n\nBram’s voice went quiet. “That’s not good.”\n\nTiffani closed her slate.\n\n“We don’t approach,” she said.\n\nBram frowned. “We came all this way to not approach?”\n\n“We came to confirm the rumor is real,” Tiffani said. “It’s real.”\n\nJhace felt the coal-warmth behind his sternum stir—just a shift, like it didn’t like being told no to anything in the world.\n\nHe kept his hands in his pockets.\n\nHe didn’t touch his chest.\n\nHe didn’t give it a reason.\n\n“Corlexi will hear,” Bram said.\n\n“Everyone will hear,” Tiffani said. “And they’ll ask the same questions they always ask.”\n\n“Is it divine,” Bram said.\n\n“Is it labor,” Tiffani said.\n\n“Is it property,” Jhace finished.\n\nTiffani looked at him. “And the worst one.”\n\nJhace’s mouth went tight. “Is it controllable.”\n\n---\n\nThey didn’t see her.\n\nNot directly.\n\nBut the alley held evidence the way a body held bruises.\n\nA beam cut too clean to be fracture. Two halves that didn’t splinter. No saw marks. No wedge scars. No strap abrasion.\n\nJust absence where connection used to be.\n\nTiffani crouched and ran a fingertip near the cut line without touching it.\n\n“Smooth,” she said.\n\nBram leaned close. “Don’t touch it.”\n\n“I’m not,” Tiffani said. “I’m looking.”\n\nJhace stared at the cut like it had insulted him personally.\n\nHis holds were desperate bracing. Ugly. Contact. Tremor. Blood.\n\nThis was neat.\n\nNeat was dangerous. Neat was seductive. Neat looked like control.\n\nJhace could feel the doubled hum in his molars.\n\nIt wasn’t louder here.\n\nIt was sharper.\n\nAs if something had resonated in this alley and left the air slightly changed.\n\nBram rubbed his palms on his coat. “So what do we do.”\n\nTiffani stood slowly. Her knee caught for half a heartbeat. She ignored it.\n\n“We add a new category,” she said.\n\nBram’s brow lifted. “To your taxonomy.”\n\n“Not mine,” Tiffani said. “Ours. And we keep it separate.”\n\nJhace’s tremor ticked.\n\n“Separate how,” Jhace asked.\n\nTiffani’s eyes stayed hard. “We do not put this in the operator ledger. Not yet. Not until we know what it costs. Not until we know what it does to people.”\n\nBram snorted. “Too late. It’s already doing.”\n\nTiffani didn’t deny it. “Then we keep it from becoming the Crown’s excuse.”\n\n“Excuse for what?” Bram asked.\n\n“For taking us,” Jhace said, before Tiffani could. “For saying ‘look what happens when it’s unregistered.’”\n\nTiffani’s mouth went flat. “Exactly.”\n\nBram stared down the alley like he expected the shard-bearer to walk back through it just to prove a point.\n\n“She’s a person,” Bram said. “Not a bell. Not a building. A person walking around with a piece of the Piercing like it’s a knife.”\n\nJhace breathed in.\n\nCopper.\n\nAlways copper.\n\nHe didn’t like how much the idea made his skin feel tight.\n\nNot fear.\n\nRecognition.\n\nBecause if there was a way to cut without shaking, there would be people lining up to pay for it.\n\nAnd people lining up to own it.\n\n---\n\nThat Tide, late, when the city quieted into its doubled baseline and the bells finally shut up long enough for sleep to pretend it existed—\n\nsomeone walked the spill-alleys again.\n\nNo badge.\n\nNo crew.\n\nNo slate.\n\nA woman with a long flat piece wrapped in cloth like it was a wound that refused to heal.\n\nShe stopped under a lantern with failing oil.\n\nUnwrapped the cloth.\n\nThe shard caught the light and didn’t reflect it the way metal did.\n\nIt drank light. Bent it. Made the air around it look thin.\n\nHer hand didn’t shake.\n\nHer mouth tasted copper so constantly she’d stopped noticing.\n\nShe held the shard up like a question.\n\nNot to the sky.\n\nTo the city.\n\nTo the seams.\n\nTo the parts that still pretended they were stable.\n\nShe didn’t want to collapse buildings.\n\nShe wanted something else.\n\nSomething smaller.\n\nPersonal.\n\nShe listened to the doubled hum the way a musician listened to a pitch, except she didn’t have words for it. She only had feeling.\n\nWrongness.\n\nAlignment.\n\nA place to cut.\n\nHer fingers tightened.\n\nNot in panic. In choice.\n\nAnd somewhere in the terrace above, iron screamed once—quiet, brief—as a single bracket failed cleanly and dropped without taking anything else with it.\n\nA controlled failure.\n\nA message.\n\nShe wrapped the shard again and walked on.\n\n---\n\nBack in the counting office, Daved slept with his hands curled into the sheet.\n\nJhace sat on a crate beside the cot, watching the tremor in his own fingers like it was a language he hadn’t agreed to learn.\n\nTiffani stood in the doorway, slate tucked under her arm, staring out at the dark streets where rumors moved.\n\nBram leaned on the opposite wall, too awake, too loud even when he was quiet.\n\n“We have a shard-bearer,” Bram said.\n\n“We have a rumor,” Tiffani corrected.\n\nJhace didn’t look up. “It’s real.”\n\nTiffani’s eyes didn’t move. “Then we treat it like a separate problem.”\n\nBram let out a slow breath. “Yeah.”\n\nJhace finally lifted his hands out of his pockets.\n\nThey shook.\n\nNot because of cold.\n\nBecause the city had taught his body a new baseline.\n\nHe stared at the tremor and wondered what it would feel like to be steady.\n\nOutside, the doubled hum ran through stone and iron like an invitation nobody had asked for.\n\nAnd somewhere in Sector Twelve, a clean edge waited for the next seam.\n\n---"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1768407221.882949,
        "end_turn": true,
        "id": "79356351-34b3-41a8-8e5e-572f0b8e5eea",
        "metadata": {
          "can_save": true,
          "citations": [],
          "content_references": [],
          "default_model_slug": "gpt-5-2-thinking",
          "finish_details": {

              200002

          "gizmo_id": "g-p-69534f0b73c08191bc65eb820ed7f050",

          "message_type": "next",
          "model_slug": "gpt-5-2-thinking",
          "parent_id": "25d5839d-913c-4d93-809c-47ddcea6e1aa",
          "request_id": "0257cc44-4592-4737-8af9-18cf097f5d1e",
          "thinking_effort": "standard",
          "turn_exchange_id": "fd125f64-3c49-49bd-9373-acbe9952f104"
        },
        "recipient": "all",

    "796f9511-6fbf-45f0-afe8-03ec3ff64c5e": {
      "children": [
        "cb7d89ba-b22a-4a80-aff2-5e15cd3216e0"
      ],
      "id": "796f9511-6fbf-45f0-afe8-03ec3ff64c5e",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "python",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": " care who listened. Senne braced under the central landing. Jhace watched her shoulders set—metalworker’s posture in a glassworker’s frame. Tiffani counted. Heartbeats, not seconds. Seconds lied when panic touched them. One hundred. One forty. One eighty. At one‑ninety, a secondary beam—one Tiffani hadn’t walked because she’d trusted the map—shifted with a sound like teeth grinding. The evacuation lane kinked. The next family stumbled. The stone didn’t collapse all at once. It folded, patient and specific, closing the corridor into a narrow pocket. Three people were on the wrong side when it did: two adults and a child coughing copper into his sleeve. Tiffani saw them, and for a moment the slate in her hands felt obscene. “Hold,” she said, voice flat. “Keep holding.” Senne’s eyes didn’t move. She couldn’t spare the motion. Two‑ten. Two‑twenty. Two‑thirty. The pocket didn’t clear. The child’s coughing turned wet. At two‑forty, Tiffani rang the termination sequence anyway. Because protocols existed for a reason. Because if she didn’t, she would never stop. Senne released. The stairwell collapsed into itself with a slow, final sound—stone settling into the shape it had wanted all along. They dug for hours. They found three bodies. The child first, because he was small enough to slip into the wrong angles. Back in the workshop, Tiffani logged it with perfect precision. Deployment: Senne — 240 heartbeats Evacuated: 47 Casualties: 3 Cause: Inadequate route assessment. Secondary beam shear. She wrote “cause” as if precision could make it reversible. Her hand moved to write the next revision. The chalk touched slate. Nothing came out. She stared at the number. Three. Not a count. Three names she would never know. Three families who would ask why their— The chalk snapped in her grip. She set the broken pieces down carefully. Picked up a new stick. Wrote: Route spotters required. Three per deployment. No exceptions. Underlined it twice. Then she closed the slate and sat in the dark workshop, counting her own breaths because the numbers on the page wouldn't behave. Her fingers kept shaking anyway.\"),\n  (27,\n   'second',\n   \"The bell came from Sector Nine, not as a roar—just a single strike repeated until someone answered. Residential stairwell. Upper-terrace split. Twelve families still inside, one route. Jhace was already marked from Sector Nine. Corven was worse. Senne was fresh enough to stand without shaking. Tiffani looked at the slate wall and made the decision she would later pretend was only math. Two operators at one‑eighty each meant coordination, handoffs, spotters, and failure points. One operator at two‑forty meant simplicity. A clean line. “Single deployment,” she said. “Senne. Duration cap: two‑forty. Terminate on my bell.” Senne went pale as the heat took her chest. Copper-gold light laced her forearms. Not pretty. Functional. They reached the stairwell mouth as stone dust sifted from the ceiling like flour. The grid-hum underfoot carried the doubled harmonic, deep and insistent, a warning that didn’t care who listened. Senne braced under the central landing. Jhace watched her shoulders set—metalworker’s posture in a glassworker’s frame. Tiffani counted. Heartbeats, not seconds. Seconds lied when panic touched them. One hundred. One forty. One eighty. At one‑ninety, a secondary beam—one Tiffani hadn’t walked because she’d trusted the map—shifted with a sound like teeth grinding. The evacuation lane kinked. The next family stumbled. The stone didn’t collapse all at once. It folded, patient and specific, closing the corridor into a narrow pocket. Three people were on the wrong side when it did: two adults and a child coughing copper into his sleeve. Tiffani saw them, and for a moment the slate in her hands felt obscene. “Hold,” she said, voice flat. “Keep holding.” Senne’s eyes didn’t move. She couldn’t spare the motion. Two‑ten. Two‑twenty. Two‑thirty. The pocket didn’t clear. The child’s coughing turned wet. At two‑forty, Tiffani rang the termination sequence anyway. Because protocols existed for a reason. Because if she didn’t, she would never stop. Senne released. The stairwell collapsed into itself with a slow, final sound—stone settling into the shape it had wanted all along. They dug for hours. They found three bodies. The child first, because he was small enough to slip into the wrong angles. Back in the workshop, Tiffani logged it with perfect precision. Deployment: Senne — 240 heartbeats Evacuated: 47 Casualties: 3 Cause: Inadequate route assessment. Secondary beam shear. She wrote “cause” as if precision could make it reversible. Her hand moved to write the next revision. The chalk touched slate. Nothing came out. She stared at the number. Three. Not a count. Three names she would never know. Three families who would ask why their— The chalk snapped in her grip. She set the broken pieces down carefully. Picked up a new stick. Wrote: Route spotters required. Three per deployment. No exceptions. Underlined it twice. Then she closed the slate and sat in the dark workshop, counting her own breaths because the numbers on the page wouldn't behave. Her fingers kept shaking anyway.\"),\n  (31,\n   'second',\n   \"Brother Corran didn’t wear Temple colors. The first thing Jhace noticed was the badge—bright, clean, pinned where it couldn't be missed. The second was how the man didn't look tired. Not even a little. Corran’s eyes went straight to Jhace’s hands. “For the record, I’m not here to stop you,” Corran said, before anyone asked. Bram hovered by the door like a lock that wanted to be useful. Tiffani set her slate down deliberately. “Then why are you here?” “Because you’re real,” Corran said. “And because real things attract predators.” Jhace’s mouth tightened. “Crown.” Corran’s gaze didn’t flinch. “Yes. Also Guild. Also Temple. Anyone who thinks they can turn you into a lever.” Bram’s voice came dry. “Ah yes. The holy trinity of getting fucked: prayer, production, paperwork.” Tiffani shot him a look. “Bram.” He raised his hands. “What? I’m helping.” Bram gestured toward the door. “Temple says you’re divine. Guild says you’re labor. Crown says you’re property. Everybody’s trying to fuck the same lever.” He pulled a folded packet from his coat and set it on Jhace’s bench. Paper. Old style. Archive weight. “Stage Three cases,” Corran said. “Documented. Not rumors.” Tiffani’s hands went still. “How many?” “Enough.” Corran’s voice was flat, and that flatness carried exhaustion. “Most die. A few don’t. The ones who don’t… wish they had.” Jhace’s tremor ticked up at the word die. He forced his hands flat on the bench. “Why give this to us?” Corran hesitated. That hesitation was the crack in the Crown. “Because someone above me wants a registry,” he said. “Names. Locations. Deployment authority. They’re calling it order. It’s control.” Tiffani’s eyes narrowed. “Who?” Corran looked toward the door as if the walls might report him. “Administrator Corlexi. Infrastructure Compliance.” Bram gave a low whistle. “Clean hands. Clean coat. Probably wipes his ass with forms.” Jhace stared at the packet. “So you’re here to warn us.” “I’m here to give you data,” Corran corrected. “If you want to fight control, you need numbers. Not speeches.” Tiffani opened the packet and read, eyes moving fast. Jhace watched her face tighten at certain lines—durations, costs, outcomes. She didn’t gasp. She didn’t dramatize. She just absorbed. “This case,” she said, tapping a page. “Stage Three survival. How?” Corran’s mouth went thin. “He didn’t terminate. He couldn’t. The hold locked. Autonomic failure started and the manifestation kept running anyway. The structure held until it didn’t matter.” Jhace felt cold crawl up his spine despite the workshop heat. “Locked hold.” Corran nodded. “It’s not a tool you put down when you’re done. Sometimes it’s a hook in your chest that drags you until something breaks.” Tiffani’s pen scratched. Stage Three: autonomic failure. Locked hold possible. Termination may fail. Bram shifted. “You said predators.” Corran’s eyes met Bram’s. “Tallec is filing complaints. Says you’re obstructing industry. Says your protocols slow production.” Jhace’s jaw clenched so hard it hurt. Corran leaned closer, voice low. “Here’s the fracture you wanted. I don’t like Corlexi’s registry. I don’t like Temple’s sermons. I don’t like Guild’s schedules. But I like fewer dead.” He straightened. “If you build methodology, build it fast. Because the Crown is already drafting theirs. And theirs won’t care what it costs you.” He turned to leave. Tiffani called after him. “Why risk telling us this?” Corran paused at the door. “Because Junction Seven wasn’t the first ignored report,” he said. “And I’m tired of pretending ignorance is the same as innocence.” Then he was gone. Jhace’s hands stayed flat on the workbench. “If this registry happens…” He swallowed. “I’m not doing it for paper.” Bram exhaled slowly. “And because guilt is cheaper than funerals. Not by much. But cheaper.” Tiffani climbed the workshop stairs slower than she had a Tide ago. Her right knee caught on the third step—not pain, just resistance, like the joint was learning to refuse. She stopped on the landing. Put her palm to the wall until the world steadied. The knee had locked twice this Anneal. Not during a hold. During stairs. During the parts where bodies were supposed to cooperate. She’d logged operator tremor. Operator vein-lines. Operator autonomic failure. She had not logged her own joint learning to refuse weight. Because if she logged it, she’d have to adjust. Delegate. Sit down. And if she sat down, someone else would coordinate from the deck. Someone else would make the calls. She took the next step anyway. She didn’t log it. Some costs weren’t manifestation. Some were just labor. Jhace’s hands shook on the bench like they were trying to answer him.\"),\n  (35,\n   'south',\n   'Merchant Hall was a cathedral for trade: iron ribs, stone spine, glass skylights that made everything inside look clean even when it wasn’t. And it smelled like bodies and money and fear—the kind of fear that tries to wear perfume. It started failing at mid‑Tide. The bell sequence came sharp and frantic—no warning cadence, no time to wedge first. The Hall had been packed when the terrace shifted again. Four hundred inside, Bram said. Maybe more. Tiffani arrived before Jhace. When he pushed through the crowd, she was already on the steps, slate in hand, shouting like a foreman who’d learned math. “Operators on me. Two per span. Spotters assigned. No hero holds.” “Duration limit. One-eighty heartbeats maximum. You terminate on my signal. No argument.” Merra shoved through the dust with a slate clutched to her chest. Ash streaked her hair like she’d tried to comb through a fire. “We have a problem. Southern stairwell collapsed. Fifty people trapped on upper floors. Can’t reach main exits.” “Time estimate,” Tiffani said. “Low and high.” “Unknown. Debris blocking secondary stairs. Could be two hundred heartbeats. Could be six hundred.” “Then we don’t gamble.” Tiffani didn’t raise her voice. She didn’t have to. “Clear lanes. Count bodies out. You want prayer, pray while you walk.” She swung her slate toward the operators. “Corven—baseline check. When chest heat starts, report it. Don’t interpret. Report.” Corven swallowed. “If it hits, I tell you. If I taste copper, I tell you.” Jhace didn’t look at him. “Don’t spiral. Do the minimum.” “Minimum,” Corven echoed, like repetition could make it true. “Senne—status.” “Getting hollow,” Senne said. “Chest feels empty. Light’s harder to maintain.” “That’s Stage Two warning.” Tiffani’s eyes cut sharp. “You terminate on my signal or I terminate you myself.” Senne gave her a tired half-smile. “Sure. Die on schedule. Very professional.” “Kess—report.” “I am present and available for assignment.” “Available doesn’t mean useful. Report symptoms. Stop performing.” “Symptoms are within acceptable parameters for operational requirements.” Bram leaned in, voice too bright for the amount of people about to die. “Translation: Kess is bleeding and pretending it’s fashion.” “I am not bleeding.” Merra didn’t bother being gentle. “Kess—your nose is bleeding.” “I am aware of the nasal discharge. It is minor. Barely noteworthy. I’ve had worse nosebleeds from seasonal changes.” “Yeah,” Bram said. “And I’ve had worse ideas than this hold. Doesn’t make them good.” From the crowd, a Temple Observer screamed, “Let the worthy hold! Let the unworthy fall!” Bram snapped his head toward the sound. “Hey—shut up. If we’re picking who falls, you’re first. That’s democracy.” “Ignore them,” Tiffani said. “Move.” “Spotters, call markers.” “Jhace tremor increasing!” “Corven tremor increasing!” “Senne vein-line onset!” Bram barked at the evac line like a foreman with a broken sense of humor. “Keep moving! Nobody stops! You stop, you become part of the debris! Count them out!” Merra’s voice cut through the dust. “Alternate route open. Final evacuation commencing.” “Count them,” Tiffani said. “Call numbers.” Jhace’s breath came tight. “One hundred.” “State exit count,” Tiffani said, “not your suffering.” “One hundred. Out.” Daved’s voice came rough through the grit. “They’re not out.” Jhace’s head snapped. “No.” “They’re not out.” “Daved—state the variable,” Tiffani said. “How many still inside?” “Enough.” Tiffani didn’t blink. “Extend. Thirty heartbeats. No more.” “Tiff—” Jhace started. “I am not debating physics with the dead in the hallway,” she said. “Extend.” Thirty heartbeats passed like a slow knife. Merra shouted from the corridor mouth, “Final group emerging. Ten heartbeats!” “Ten heartbeats is still ten,” Tiffani said. “Move.” Daved’s hands tightened. “Hold.” “Daved!” Jhace lunged a step closer. “Hold.” Merra’s slate slapped stone. “Clear! All civilians safe!” “Terminate all operators,” Tiffani snapped. “Immediate coordinated shutdown.” Lights guttered and went out—one by one—except Daved’s. “Can’t,” Daved said. “Daved. Breathe,” Jhace said, too close, too desperate. “Can’t.” “Terminate,” Tiffani said. “That is not a negotiation.” “They’re not out.” “They are out. Merra confirmed. Terminate now.” Daved’s jaw worked like he was chewing copper. “Already crossed.” Bram shoved his way in with words because silence was making everyone honest. “Daved—buddy—love the commitment, hate the method acting.” “Bram, shut up,” Jhace hissed. “I can’t,” Bram said. “It’s a disorder.” Tiffani stepped in until Daved had to see her. “Look at me. If you extend past this, you cross Stage Three threshold.” Daved’s eyes stayed flat. “Already crossed.” Jhace’s throat tightened. “He locked.” “I see it,” Tiffani said. They hauled Daved back from the structure like pulling a hook out of meat. “Terminate!” Tiffani shouted. The light finally cut. Daved folded like a tool dropped from numb hands. Tiffani’s voice went small for one syllable. “I know.”'),\n  (35,\n   'second',\n   'Merchant Hall was a cathedral for trade: iron ribs, stone spine, glass skylights that made everything inside look clean even when it wasn’t. Merra shouted from the corridor mouth, “Final group emerging. Breathe,” Jhace said, too close, too desperate. “Can’t.” “Terminate,” Tiffani said. “That is not a negotiation.” “They’re not out.” “They are out. Merra confirmed. If you extend past this, you cross Stage Three threshold.” Daved’s eyes stayed flat. “Already crossed.” Jhace’s throat tightened. “He locked.” “I see it,” Tiffani said. They hauled Daved back from the structure like pulling a hook out of meat. “Terminate!” Tiffani shouted. The light finally cut. Daved folded like a tool dropped from numb hands. Her fingers kept shaking anyway.\"),\n  (27,\n   'second',\n   \"The bell came from Sector Nine, not as a roar—just a single strike repeated until someone answered. Residential stairwell. Upper-terrace split. Twelve families still inside, one route. Jhace was already marked from Sector Nine. Corven was worse. Senne was fresh enough to stand without shaking. Tiffani looked at the slate wall and made the decision she would later pretend was only math. Two operators at one‑eighty each meant coordination, handoffs, spotters, and failure points. One operator at two‑forty meant simplicity. A clean line. “Single deployment,” she said. “Senne. Duration cap: two‑forty. Terminate on my bell.” Senne went pale as the heat took her chest. Copper-gold light laced her forearms. Not pretty. Functional. They reached the stairwell mouth as stone dust sifted from the ceiling like flour. The grid-hum underfoot carried the doubled harmonic, deep and insistent, a warning that didn’t care who listened. Senne braced under the central landing. Jhace watched her shoulders set—metalworker’s posture in a glassworker’s frame. Tiffani counted. Heartbeats, not seconds. Seconds lied when panic touched them. One hundred. One forty. One eighty. At one‑ninety, a secondary beam—one Tiffani hadn’t walked because she’d trusted the map—shifted with a sound like teeth grinding. The evacuation lane kinked. The next family stumbled. The stone didn’t collapse all at once. It folded, patient and specific, closing the corridor into a narrow pocket. Three people were on the wrong side when it did: two adults and a child coughing copper into his sleeve. Tiffani saw them, and for a moment the slate in her hands felt obscene. “Hold,” she said, voice flat. “Keep holding.” Senne’s eyes didn’t move. She couldn’t spare the motion. Two‑ten. Two‑twenty. Two‑thirty. The pocket didn’t clear. The child’s coughing turned wet. At two‑forty, Tiffani rang the termination sequence anyway. Because protocols existed for a reason. Because if she didn’t, she would never stop. Senne released. The stairwell collapsed into itself with a slow, final sound—stone settling into the shape it had wanted all along. They dug for hours. They found three bodies. The child first, because he was small enough to slip into the wrong angles. Back in the workshop, Tiffani logged it with perfect precision. Deployment: Senne — 240 heartbeats Evacuated: 47 Casualties: 3 Cause: Inadequate route assessment. Secondary beam shear. She wrote “cause” as if precision could make it reversible. Her hand moved to write the next revision. The chalk touched slate. Nothing came out. She stared at the number. Three. Not a count. Three names she would never know. Three families who would ask why their— The chalk snapped in her grip. She set the broken pieces down carefully. Picked up a new stick. Wrote: Route spotters required. Three per deployment. No exceptions. Underlined it twice. Then she closed the slate and sat in the dark workshop, counting her own breaths because the numbers on the page wouldn't behave. Her fingers kept shaking anyway.\"),\n  (31,\n   'second',\n   \"Brother Corran didn’t wear Temple colors. Light’s harder to maintain.” “That’s Stage Two warning.” Tiffani’s eyes cut sharp. “You terminate on my signal or I terminate you myself.” Senne gave her a tired half-smile. “Sure. Die on schedule. Very professional.” “Kess—report.” “I am present and available for assignment.” “Available doesn’t mean useful. Report symptoms. Stop performing.” “Symptoms are within acceptable parameters for operational requirements.” Bram leaned in, voice too bright for the amount of people about to die. “Translation: Kess is bleeding and pretending it’s fashion.” “I am not bleeding.” Merra didn’t bother being gentle. “Kess—your nose is bleeding.” “I am aware of the nasal discharge. It is minor. Barely noteworthy. I’ve had worse nosebleeds from seasonal changes.” “Yeah,” Bram said. “And I’ve had worse ideas than this hold. Doesn’t make them good.” From the crowd, a Temple Observer screamed, “Let the worthy hold! Let the unworthy fall!” Bram snapped his head toward the sound. “Hey—shut up. If we’re picking who falls, you’re first. That’s democracy.” “Ignore them,” Tiffani said. “Move.” “Spotters, call markers.” “Jhace tremor increasing!” “Corven tremor increasing!” “Senne vein-line onset!” Bram barked at the evac line like a foreman with a broken sense of humor. “Keep moving! Nobody stops! You stop, you become part of the debris! Count them out!” Merra’s voice cut through the dust. “Alternate route open. Final evacuation commencing.” “Count them,” Tiffani said. “Call numbers.” Jhace’s breath came tight. “One hundred.” “State exit count,” Tiffani said, “not your suffering.” “One hundred. Out.” Daved’s voice came rough through the grit. “They’re not out.” Jhace’s head snapped. “No.” “They’re not out.” “Daved—state the variable,” Tiffani said. “How many still inside?” “Enough.” Tiffani didn’t blink. “Extend. Thirty heartbeats. No more.” “Tiff—” Jhace started. “I am not debating physics with the dead in the hallway,” she said. “Extend.” Thirty heartbeats passed like a slow knife. Merra shouted from the corridor mouth, “Final group emerging. Ten heartbeats!” “Ten heartbeats is still ten,” Tiffani said. “Move.” Daved’s hands tightened. “Hold.” “Daved!” Jhace lunged a step closer. “Hold.” Merra’s slate slapped stone. “Clear! All civilians safe!” “Terminate all operators,” Tiffani snapped. “Immediate coordinated shutdown.” Lights guttered and went out—one by one—except Daved’s. “Can’t,” Daved said. “Daved. Breathe,” Jhace said, too close, too desperate. “Can’t.” “Terminate,” Tiffani said. “That is not a negotiation.” “They’re not out.” “They are out. Merra confirmed. Terminate now.” Daved’s jaw worked like he was chewing copper. “Already crossed.” Bram shoved his way in with words because silence was making everyone honest. “Daved—buddy—love the commitment, hate the method acting.” “Bram, shut up,” Jhace hissed. “I can’t,” Bram said. “It’s a disorder.” Tiffani stepped in until Daved had to see her. “Look at me. If you extend past this, you cross Stage Three threshold.” Daved’s eyes stayed flat. “Already crossed.” Jhace’s throat tightened. “He locked.” “I see it,” Tiffani said. They hauled Daved back from the structure like pulling a hook out of meat. “Terminate!” Tiffani shouted. The light finally cut. Daved folded like a tool dropped from numb hands. Tiffani’s voice went small for one syllable. “I know.”'),\n  (35,\n   'second',\n   'Merchant Hall was a cathedral for trade: iron ribs, stone spine, glass skylights that made everything inside look clean even when it wasn’t. And it smelled like bodies and money and fear—the kind of fear that tries to wear perfume. It started failing at mid‑Tide. The bell sequence came sharp and frantic—no warning cadence, no time to wedge first. The Hall had been packed when the terrace shifted again. Four hundred inside, Bram said. Maybe more. Tiffani arrived before Jhace. When he pushed through the crowd, she was already on the steps, slate in hand, shouting like a foreman who’d learned math. “Operators on me. Two per span. Spotters assigned. No hero holds.” “Duration limit. One-eighty heartbeats maximum. You terminate on my signal. No argument.” Merra shoved through the dust with a slate clutched to her chest. Ash streaked her hair like she’d tried to comb through a fire. “We have a problem. Southern stairwell collapsed. Fifty people trapped on upper floors. Can’t reach main exits.” “Time estimate,” Tiffani said. “Low and high.” “Unknown. Debris blocking secondary stairs. Could be two hundred heartbeats. Could be six hundred.” “Then we don’t gamble.” Tiffani didn’t raise her voice. She didn’t have to. “Clear lanes. Count bodies out. You want prayer, pray while you walk.” She swung her slate toward the operators. “Corven—baseline check. When chest heat starts, report it. Don’t interpret. Report.” Corven swallowed. “If it hits, I tell you. If I taste copper, I tell you.” Jhace didn’t look at him. “Don’t spiral. Do the minimum.” “Minimum,” Corven echoed, like repetition could make it true. “Senne—status.” “Getting hollow,” Senne said. “Chest feels empty. Light’s harder to maintain.” “That’s Stage Two warning.” Tiffani’s eyes cut sharp. “You terminate on my signal or I terminate you myself.” Senne gave her a tired half-smile. “Sure. Die on schedule. Very professional.” “Kess—report.” “I am present and available for assignment.” “Available doesn’t mean useful. Report symptoms. Stop performing.” “Symptoms are within acceptable parameters for operational requirements.” Bram leaned in, voice too bright for the amount of people about to die. “Translation: Kess is bleeding and pretending it’s fashion.” “I am not bleeding.” Merra didn’t bother being gentle. “Kess—your nose is bleeding.” “I am aware of the nasal discharge. It is minor. Barely noteworthy. I’ve had worse nosebleeds from seasonal changes.” “Yeah,” Bram said. “And I’ve had worse ideas than this hold. Doesn’t make them good.” From the crowd, a Temple Observer screamed, “Let the worthy hold! Let the unworthy fall!” Bram snapped his head toward the sound. “Hey—shut up. If we’re picking who falls, you’re first. That’s democracy.” “Ignore them,” Tiffani said. “Move.” “Spotters, call markers.” “Jhace tremor increasing!” “Corven tremor increasing!” “Senne vein-line onset!” Bram barked at the evac line like a foreman with a broken sense of humor. “Keep moving! Nobody stops! You stop, you become part of the debris! Count them out!” Merra’s voice cut through the dust. “Alternate route open. Final evacuation commencing.” “Count them,” Tiffani said. “Call numbers.” Jhace’s breath came tight. “One hundred.” “State exit count,” Tiffani said, “not your suffering.” “One hundred. Out.” Daved’s voice came rough through the grit. “They’re not out.” Jhace’s head snapped. “No.” “They’re not out.” “Daved—state the variable,” Tiffani said. “How many still inside?” “Enough.” Tiffani didn’t blink. “Extend. Thirty heartbeats. No more.” “Tiff—” Jhace started. “I am not debating physics with the dead in the hallway,” she said. “Extend.” Thirty heartbeats passed like a slow knife. Merra shouted from the corridor mouth, “Final group emerging. Ten heartbeats!” “Ten heartbeats is still ten,” Tiffani said. “Move.” Daved’s hands tightened. “Hold.” “Daved!” Jhace lunged a step closer. “Hold.” Merra’s slate slapped stone. “Clear! All civilians safe!” “Terminate all operators,” Tiffani snapped. “Immediate coordinated shutdown.” Lights guttered and went out—one by one—except Daved’s. “Can’t,” Daved said. “Daved. Breathe,” Jhace said, too close, too desperate. “Can’t.” “Terminate,” Tiffani said. “That is not a negotiation.” “They’re not out.” “They are out. Merra confirmed. Terminate now.” Daved’s jaw worked like he was chewing copper. “Already crossed.” Bram shoved his way in with words because silence was making everyone honest. “Daved—buddy—love the commitment, hate the method acting.” “Bram, shut up,” Jhace hissed. “I can’t,” Bram said. “It’s a disorder.” Tiffani stepped in until Daved had to see her. “Look at me. If you extend past this, you cross Stage Three threshold.” Daved’s eyes stayed flat. “Already crossed.” Jhace’s throat tightened. “He locked.” “I see it,” Tiffani said. They hauled Daved back from the structure like pulling a hook out of meat. “Terminate!” Tiffani shouted. The light finally cut. Daved folded like a tool dropped from numb hands.         "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "\"e breathed in, tasted copper‑gold dust that still clung to his clothes, and felt the city’s hum through the floorboards.\\n“Then we build it fast,” he said. “Before they decide to build it for us.”\\n---\\nCHAPTER 5: FIRST NETWORK\\nThe kiln‑quarter feeder gantry sat in Sector Fourteen, where the pipes ran hot and the schedules ran hotter, and everything pretended it was fine right up until it wasn’t.\\nThe bell signal came late. That was the first problem.\\nWhen Jhace and Tiffani arrived, the gantry wasn’t collapsed—yet. The support columns were bowing, groaning under a load they shouldn’t have been carrying because somebody had over‑pressured the feed line to meet a schedule.\\nA foreman screamed, “Hold it! Hold it now!”\\nJhace moved toward the nearest column out of habit.\\nTiffani caught his sleeve. “Not yet.”\\nJhace’s jaw tightened. “People are inside.”\\n“People are leaving,” she corrected, pointing.\\nShe was right. Workers were already pouring out of the side doors, coughing, eyes wide. The structure wasn’t in free fall. It was in warning.\\n“We wedge first,” Tiffani said. “Then you hold if wedges fail.”\\nJhace’s chest heat sat behind his ribs like an itch you can't reach. His tremor wanted to fight. His stomach wanted to bargain. Neither cared what he preferred.\\nHe didn’t scratch it.\\nHis first wedge slip went in crooked. He had to yank it back out, knuckles scraping stone, and the crowd saw the mistake.\\nHe hated that more than the pain.\\n\\nHe dropped to one knee and shoved wedge slips under t\""
        },
        "create_time": 1768370371.7444408,
        "end_turn": null,

                    "text/plain": "\"e breathed in, tasted copper‑gold dust that still clung to his clothes, and felt the city’s hum through the floorboards.\\n“Then we build it fast,” he said. “Before they decide to build it for us.”\\n---\\nCHAPTER 5: FIRST NETWORK\\nThe kiln‑quarter feeder gantry sat in Sector Fourteen, where the pipes ran hot and the schedules ran hotter, and everything pretended it was fine right up until it wasn’t.\\nThe bell signal came late. That was the first problem.\\nWhen Jhace and Tiffani arrived, the gantry wasn’t collapsed—yet. The support columns were bowing, groaning under a load they shouldn’t have been carrying because somebody had over‑pressured the feed line to meet a schedule.\\nA foreman screamed, “Hold it! Hold it now!”\\nJhace moved toward the nearest column out of habit.\\nTiffani caught his sleeve. “Not yet.”\\nJhace’s jaw tightened. “People are inside.”\\n“People are leaving,” she corrected, pointing.\\nShe was right. Workers were already pouring out of the side doors, coughing, eyes wide. The structure wasn’t in free fall. It was in warning.\\n“We wedge first,” Tiffani said. “Then you hold if wedges fail.”\\nJhace’s chest heat sat behind his ribs like an itch you can't reach. His tremor wanted to fight. His stomach wanted to bargain. Neither cared what he preferred.\\nHe didn’t scratch it.\\nHis first wedge slip went in crooked. He had to yank it back out, knuckles scraping stone, and the crowd saw the mistake.\\nHe hated that more than the pain.\\n\\nHe dropped to one knee and shoved wedge slips under t\""

      "message": {
        "author": {

          "name": "python",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "'CHAPTER 8: THREE TIDES\\nDaved woke on the third Tide.\\nThree Tides. Long enough for sweat to dry into salt on his collar. Long enough for hope to feel stupid.\\nJhace stood by the cot in the infirmary room, hands shaking in front of him like they were arguing with air. Tiffani stood on the other side, slate ready, eyes red at the edges from lack of sleep she refused to name.\\nDaved’s eyes opened slowly.\\nHis hands were strapped to the cot rails—not as restraint, but as prevention. His fingers still twitched with phantom hold.\\nHe tried to speak. His throat made a sound like stone rubbing stone.\\nTiffani leaned in. “Don’t force voice. Use slate.”\\nDaved wrote: Did it work\\n“Yes. Thirty-two evacuated. One operator Stage Three event. Locked hold. Autonomic failure onset.”\\nHe wrote: Cost\\n“Severe tremor. Motor delay. Speech impairment. Autonomic instability. Probability of full recovery: low.”\\nHe wrote: Worth it\\n“That’s not a question I can answer. Thirty-two lives. One body broken. You determine if that exchange was acceptable.”\\nJhace’s throat tightened. “They got thirty-two out.”\\nHe couldn’t decide what to do with his hands. Shaking, useless things. He folded them behind his back like that would fool anybody.\\n\\nDaved wrote: One body is not a small thing\\n“Correct,” Tiffani said. “It is not small. It is also not reversible.”\\nBram wandered in like a bad idea that knew your schedule. “This room smells like piss and heroism. Worst cologne.”\\n“Leave,” Tiffani said, without looking up, “if you can’t behave.”\\n“I’m behaving,” Bram said. “I’m just… shaped wrong.”\\nDaved wrote: You okay\\n“Define opera'"
        },
        "create_time": 1768370432.8867617,
        "end_turn": null,

                  "data": {
                    "text/plain": "'CHAPTER 8: THREE TIDES\\nDaved woke on the third Tide.\\nThree Tides. Long enough for sweat to dry into salt on his collar. Long enough for hope to feel stupid.\\nJhace stood by the cot in the infirmary room, hands shaking in front of him like they were arguing with air. Tiffani stood on the other side, slate ready, eyes red at the edges from lack of sleep she refused to name.\\nDaved’s eyes opened slowly.\\nHis hands were strapped to the cot rails—not as restraint, but as prevention. His fingers still twitched with phantom hold.\\nHe tried to speak. His throat made a sound like stone rubbing stone.\\nTiffani leaned in. “Don’t force voice. Use slate.”\\nDaved wrote: Did it work\\n“Yes. Thirty-two evacuated. One operator Stage Three event. Locked hold. Autonomic failure onset.”\\nHe wrote: Cost\\n“Severe tremor. Motor delay. You determine if that exchange was acceptable.”\\nJhace’s throat tightened. “They got thirty-two out.”\\nHe couldn’t decide what to do with his hands. Shaking, useless things. It is also not reversible.”\\nBram wandered in like a bad idea that knew your schedule. “This room smells like piss and heroism. Worst cologne.”\\n“Leave,” Tiffani said, without looking up, “if you can’t behave.”\\n“I’m behaving,” Bram said. “I’m just… shaped wrong.”\\nDaved wrote: You okay\\n“Define opera'"

    "82f1281d-3ebc-49ff-aadb-34e73d6b048a": {
      "children": [
        "7ffd704e-60fd-4067-8a99-4b3bbd4a3827"
      ],
      "id": "82f1281d-3ebc-49ff-aadb-34e73d6b048a",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "python",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "she 41\nher 72\nfemale 0\nwoman 0\ngirl 0\nCHAPTER 9: THE SHARD-BEARER (RUMOR)\nThey called him Shard‑Bearer because nobody had a better word for what they thought they’d seen.\nA man in the eastern districts with a bright line in his chest. A glow that didn’t fade after termination because there was no termination. A hold that didn’t shake. A body that didn’t pay.\nRumors traveled faster than Crown notices and cleaner than Temple sermons. They came with dates and witness counts like proof.\nTiffani pinned three reports to the workshop wall with nails.\nReport one: Torne Reach — Tide 0+3. One operator held a bridge span alone for “half an Anneal.” No tremor. No vein‑lines.\nReport two: Brinefall — Tide 0+5. A whole terrace stairwell stabilized by “one man’s hands.” Witnesses describe “glass light under the skin.”\nTiffani tapped the pinne\n"
        },

    "83b683f7-a772-48e7-91e4-88de23b4caf4": {
      "children": [
        "c415da60-908e-4b79-ab69-7334a24ad580"
      ],
      "id": "83b683f7-a772-48e7-91e4-88de23b4caf4",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "python",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "('CHAPTER 13: THE LEDGER\\nTiffani wrote the first copy with her left hand because her right knee refused to lock and her right hand refused to stay still.\\nIt wasn’t Stage. It was the kind of fatigue that lives in joints after bad work—the kind you carry until your hands start lying to you.\\nThe ink pooled anyway. Copper-gold dust clung to the nib, turning every line slightly wrong, as if the city itself wanted the record to smear.\\nShe made three piles.\\nFour, if she was honest.\\nOne stack sat in clean paper with clean margins and clean language. Compliance phrasing. Definitions that sounded like protection while leaving room for ownership.\\nOne stack was tidy and dead. The kind of language that looks safe until somebody has to bleed under it.\\nOne stack was short enough to memorize. Underface cadence. Commands. Counts. No soft words, no long sentences—something you could carry in your head when paper burned or got confiscated.\\nThe last pile wasn’t for anyone. It was her draft. Her private notes. The parts she could not justify on a form and would not confess out loud.\\nBram stood in the doorway in his Crown coat like he’d stolen it off a corpse and decided it suited him.\\n“You look like a priest,” Tiffani said without looking up.\\nBram glanced down at the coat. “Don’t you dare put that evil on me. Priests get paid to lie. I’m doing it recreationally.”\\n“You’re late.”\\n“Yeah. Turns out paperwork travels faster than rescue.” He leaned in, saw the piles. “Oh good. Homework. My favorite form ',\n \"g the notch it was cut for.\\nCopper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit.\\nLight threaded up from his palm.\\nNot a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path.\\nIt slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal.\\nJhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body.\\nBut he felt the line.\\nHe felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay.\\nHis lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision.\\nThe figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact.\\nThen they stepped back.\\nNo check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood.\\nThe door shut without sound.\\nThe guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed.\\nJhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close.\\nThe tremor was gone.\\nIn its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise.\\nSomewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.\\n\\n---\\nEND N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS\\n---\")"
        },
        "create_time": 1768417098.1038096,
        "end_turn": null,

    "84364d89-ffdf-42d9-924f-4c7bf5a51408": {
      "children": [
        "1d9c7d94-2c2d-4c1c-a374-b014172c5002"
      ],
      "id": "84364d89-ffdf-42d9-924f-4c7bf5a51408",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},

        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "\n--- chunk 0-900 ---\n\nCHAPTER 12: METHOD\nThe alert came at Tide 21 like an insult.\nIt was a narrow failure—no mass collapse, no Hall, no warehouse full of people.\nA residential stairwell in Sector Nine. A narrow stone throat that had been patched too many times since the Piercing. A single child trapped beneath a fallen landing slab, crying until her voice turned hoarse.\n“Clear lane,” Tiffani ordered as they arrived. Her limp was worse now, but she moved like it didn’t matter. “Spotters assigned. Wedges first.”\nShe pointed left, then froze. “No—right. Right corridor first. Sorry.”\nThe apology tasted like rust. She didn’t repeat it.\n\nJhace’s hands shook so hard he had to jam them into his coat pockets to stop them from betraying him in front of the crowd.\nHe could feel the doubled harmonic through the stairwell stone. Deep. Constant. The grid had never recovered. It had just learned to fail slower.\nBram arrive\n\n--- chunk 900-1800 ---\n\nd with Crown guards and a face that said he’d tried, briefly, to be serious and failed on contact.\n“Registry protocol,” he said. “Two licensed operators inbound.”\nTiffani didn’t look at him. “They’ll be too late.”\n“Cool,” Bram said. “Love the part where the Crown schedules rescue like it’s a delivery window.”\n“Clear lane,” Tiffani ordered. “Spotters assigned. Wedges first.”\nShe looked at the sagging landing slab, the trapped space beneath it, the child’s choking turning into wet silence. Her voice tightened into a blade.\n“One operator. Two minutes max. We brace, we lift, we extract. Terminate immediately after.”\n“Then who?” Jhace said.\n“Not you.”\n“Then who.”\n“The child is choking, Jhace.”\nJhace’s tremor rattled against his bones like a tool trying to escape. “I decide.”\nBram’s breath punched out. “Jesus—no, don’t—okay, you’re doing it. You’re doing the thing. Great. Fantastic. I hate thi\n\n--- chunk 1800-2700 ---\n\ns.”\nJhace stepped in. Copper flooded his mouth like blood.\nFor a heartbeat, nothing happened.\nThen the hold locked.\nThe stairwell’s doubled harmonic snapped into alignment with his chest, like a collar seating in the wrong notch.\nTiffani’s voice cut clean through the noise. “Wedges—now. Lift on my count. Three heartbeats. Pull.”\nBoots scraped. Wedges hammered. Stone complained.\n“Three,” Tiffani said. “Two. One. Now—pull!”\nHands reached into the dark. The child came out gasping, eyes wide, spit and dust and life.\n“Terminate,” Tiffani said.\nJhace didn’t move.\n“Terminate,” she repeated, like command could override biology.\nSilence.\nBram’s voice cracked into the gap. “He’s not moving.”\n“Jhace.” Tiffani stepped in close. “Move.”\nBram hovered like he wanted to shake him and knew better. “Say something. Blink. Spit. Insult me. Anything.”\n“Stop talking,” Tiffani snapped.\n“I can’t,” Bram said. “I\n\n--- chunk 2700-3600 ---\n\nt’s how I pray.”\nJhace could still see. Still hear. Still count.\nHe stared at his hands, waiting for tremor.\nThere was no tremor.\nThere was nothing.\nHe tried to will a finger. Nothing.\nPanic hit—animal, immediate, nothing to do with heat or holding.\nHe tried to shout. Nothing left his throat.\nHe tried to jerk a shoulder. Nothing.\nHe tried to blink faster—signal—anything.\nHis lids moved on their own schedule. Slow. Unbothered.\nThe body was there. The controls were there. The link was gone.\nThe coal behind his sternum pulsed once. Steady. Patient. Still ready.\nReady to hold.\nBut he couldn’t lift a finger to tell it no.\nOnly the wanting stayed—locked in meat that wouldn’t answer.\n\nTiffani moved into his field of view and knelt like she was inspecting a fracture line. Ink and copper sat on her hands; she smelled like slate dust and hours without sleep.\n“Blink once for yes,” she said. “Twice \n\n--- chunk 3600-4500 ---\n\nfor no.”\nHe blinked once.\n“Do you still feel the doubled harmonic through the stone?”\nOnce.\n“Can you tell me where it dipped before Tallow Street?”\nOnce. He could see it like a chart behind his eyes—deep note, then the metallic taste, then the split.\nTiffani slid her slate into his line of sight. Two columns. LOCATION. HEARTBEATS. Her writing stayed precise even as her knuckles whitened.\n“Can you predict where it fails next?”\nOnce. Not a comfort. A capability.\nTiffani’s mouth tightened. “Then you’re not finished.” She didn’t say it like hope. She said it like reassignment.\nJhace stared at his hands, waiting for tremor. Nothing.\nIt was worse than shaking. Silence in the muscles felt like a lie.\n\nBut the numbers still lined up. The city still had a baseline. He could still calibrate it.\nTiffani’s eyes tracked his fingers. “His hands are still.”\nBram swallowed. “That’s… new.”\n“There is no t\n\n--- chunk 4500-5400 ---\n\nremor,” Tiffani said.\n“Great,” Bram said. “We fixed the tremor by removing the man. Efficient.”\n“Bram.”\n“Sorry,” Bram said. “I’m coping. Poorly. Loudly.”\nTiffani dropped to her knees beside him, slate already out, chalk already moving.\n“Stage Four,” she said, voice flat because panic was a luxury. “Complete motor loss. Locked hold with autonomic override. Cost: total paralysis below manifestation threshold.”\n“You’re writing this down,” Bram said.\n“Yes.”\n“Right now.”\n“Yes.”\n“While he’s—”\n“Yes,” Tiffani said, and her hand didn’t shake. “Because if I don’t, this becomes a story someone else gets to tell. And they will tell it wrong.”\nShe swallowed once, hard, then kept writing.\nWhen the crowd noise finally thinned and the guards stopped pretending they weren’t watching, Tiffani stood.\n“Corlexi will get what I give him,” she said.\nBram blinked. “Which is what, exactly—an instruction manual w\n\n--- chunk 5400-6300 ---\n\nith a hidden knife?”\n“A method,” Tiffani said. “With constraints. With brakes. Without permission to repeat this.”\n“So… a leash on the leash,” Bram said.\n“Something like that.”\n“You’re terrifying when you’re calm,” Bram said.\n“I’m not calm,” Tiffani said.\n“Right,” Bram said, voice softer than his jokes. “You’re just… controlled. Like a blade.”\n\n"
        },
        "create_time": 1768416018.522934,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "84364d89-ffdf-42d9-924f-4c7bf5a51408",

              {
                "content": {

                },
                "msg_type": "status",

              {
                "msg_type": "execute_input",

              {
                "content": {
                  "name": "stdout",
                  "text": "\n--- chunk 0-900 ---\n\nCHAPTER 12: METHOD\nThe alert came at Tide 21 like an insult.\nIt was a narrow failure—no mass collapse, no Hall, no warehouse full of people.\nA residential stairwell in Sector Nine. A narrow stone throat that had been patched too many times since the Piercing. A single child trapped beneath a fallen landing slab, crying until her voice turned hoarse.\n“Clear lane,” Tiffani ordered as they arrived. Her limp was worse now, but she moved like it didn’t matter. “Spotters assigned. Wedges first.”\nShe pointed left, then froze. “No—right. Right corridor first. Sorry.”\nThe apology tasted like rust. She didn’t repeat it.\n\nJhace’s hands shook so hard he had to jam them into his coat pockets to stop them from betraying him in front of the crowd.\nHe could feel the doubled harmonic through the stairwell stone. Deep. Constant. The grid had never recovered. It had just learned to fail slower.\nBram arrive\n\n--- chunk 900-1800 ---\n\nd with Crown guards and a face that said he’d tried, briefly, to be serious and failed on contact.\n“Registry protocol,” he said. “Two licensed operators inbound.”\nTiffani didn’t look at him. “They’ll be too late.”\n“Cool,” Bram said. “Love the part where the Crown schedules rescue like it’s a delivery window.”\n“Clear lane,” Tiffani ordered. “Spotters assigned. Wedges first.”\nShe looked at the sagging landing slab, the trapped space beneath it, the child’s choking turning into wet silence. Her voice tightened into a blade.\n“One operator. Two minutes max. We brace, we lift, we extract. Terminate immediately after.”\n“Then who?” Jhace said.\n“Not you.”\n“Then who.”\n“The child is choking, Jhace.”\nJhace’s tremor rattled against his bones like a tool trying to escape. “I decide.”\nBram’s breath punched out. “Jesus—no, don’t—okay, you’re doing it. You’re doing the thing. Great. Fantastic. I hate thi\n\n--- chunk 1800-2700 ---\n\ns.”\nJhace stepped in. Copper flooded his mouth like blood.\nFor a heartbeat, nothing happened.\nThen the hold locked.\nThe stairwell’s doubled harmonic snapped into alignment with his chest, like a collar seating in the wrong notch.\nTiffani’s voice cut clean through the noise. “Wedges—now. Lift on my count. Three heartbeats. Pull.”\nBoots scraped. Wedges hammered. Stone complained.\n“Three,” Tiffani said. “Two. One. Now—pull!”\nHands reached into the dark. The child came out gasping, eyes wide, spit and dust and life.\n“Terminate,” Tiffani said.\nJhace didn’t move.\n“Terminate,” she repeated, like command could override biology.\nSilence.\nBram’s voice cracked into the gap. “He’s not moving.”\n“Jhace.” Tiffani stepped in close. “Move.”\nBram hovered like he wanted to shake him and knew better. “Say something. Blink. Spit. Insult me. Anything.”\n“Stop talking,” Tiffani snapped.\n“I can’t,” Bram said. “I\n\n--- chunk 2700-3600 ---\n\nt’s how I pray.”\nJhace could still see. Still hear. Still count.\nHe stared at his hands, waiting for tremor.\nThere was no tremor.\nThere was nothing.\nHe tried to will a finger. Nothing.\nPanic hit—animal, immediate, nothing to do with heat or holding.\nHe tried to shout. Nothing left his throat.\nHe tried to jerk a shoulder. Nothing.\nHe tried to blink faster—signal—anything.\nHis lids moved on their own schedule. Slow. Unbothered.\nThe body was there. The controls were there. The link was gone.\nThe coal behind his sternum pulsed once. Steady. Patient. Still ready.\nReady to hold.\nBut he couldn’t lift a finger to tell it no.\nOnly the wanting stayed—locked in meat that wouldn’t answer.\n\nTiffani moved into his field of view and knelt like she was inspecting a fracture line. Ink and copper sat on her hands; she smelled like slate dust and hours without sleep.\n“Blink once for yes,” she said. “Twice \n\n--- chunk 3600-4500 ---\n\nfor no.”\nHe blinked once.\n“Do you still feel the doubled harmonic through the stone?”\nOnce.\n“Can you tell me where it dipped before Tallow Street?”\nOnce. He could see it like a chart behind his eyes—deep note, then the metallic taste, then the split.\nTiffani slid her slate into his line of sight. Two columns. LOCATION. HEARTBEATS. Her writing stayed precise even as her knuckles whitened.\n“Can you predict where it fails next?”\nOnce. Not a comfort. A capability.\nTiffani’s mouth tightened. “Then you’re not finished.” She didn’t say it like hope. She said it like reassignment.\nJhace stared at his hands, waiting for tremor. Nothing.\nIt was worse than shaking. Silence in the muscles felt like a lie.\n\nBut the numbers still lined up. The city still had a baseline. He could still calibrate it.\nTiffani’s eyes tracked his fingers. “His hands are still.”\nBram swallowed. “That’s… new.”\n“There is no t\n\n--- chunk 4500-5400 ---\n\nremor,” Tiffani said.\n“Great,” Bram said. “We fixed the tremor by removing the man. Efficient.”\n“Bram.”\n“Sorry,” Bram said. “I’m coping. Poorly. Loudly.”\nTiffani dropped to her knees beside him, slate already out, chalk already moving.\n“Stage Four,” she said, voice flat because panic was a luxury. “Complete motor loss. Locked hold with autonomic override. Cost: total paralysis below manifestation threshold.”\n“You’re writing this down,” Bram said.\n“Yes.”\n“Right now.”\n“Yes.”\n“While he’s—”\n“Yes,” Tiffani said, and her hand didn’t shake. “Because if I don’t, this becomes a story someone else gets to tell. And they will tell it wrong.”\nShe swallowed once, hard, then kept writing.\nWhen the crowd noise finally thinned and the guards stopped pretending they weren’t watching, Tiffani stood.\n“Corlexi will get what I give him,” she said.\nBram blinked. “Which is what, exactly—an instruction manual w\n\n--- chunk 5400-6300 ---\n\nith a hidden knife?”\n“A method,” Tiffani said. “With constraints. With brakes. Without permission to repeat this.”\n“So… a leash on the leash,” Bram said.\n“Something like that.”\n“You’re terrifying when you’re calm,” Bram said.\n“I’m not calm,” Tiffani said.\n“Right,” Bram said, voice softer than his jokes. “You’re just… controlled. Like a blade.”\n\n"
                },

        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "(77237,\n 'N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS\\nTurning: GLASS (G) — Tide 0 through Tide 21 (Three Anneals post-Piercing)\\n---\\nCHAPTER 1: THE BELL\\nThe bell rang wrong.\\nNot the slow warning cadence. Not the measured strike that meant \"check your seals, tighten your brackets.\" This was the frantic peal of something already falling, the sound of a structure begging for a hold.\\nJhace was moving before the bell finished its third strike.\\nHe grabbed the wrong ring of collar keys on the first yank. Swore, dumped them into his palm, and sorted by feel while he ran. One key skittered off the stair edge and disappeared into the dark.\\nHe didn’t go back for it.\\n\\nHis kit bounced against his hip. Chalk. Slate. Collar keys. Tools for measuring failure, not stopping it. He didn\\'t know why his legs were carrying him toward the sound instead of away. Didn\\'t know why his heart counted the bell strikes like they were orders.\\nOne. Two. Three. Four.\\nThe doubled harmonic hit him at Junction Twelve.\\nIt skipped the pipe-hum entirely and punched straight into his sternum. His ribs buzzed like struck iron. His teeth hurt. His mouth flooded metal.\\nCopper.\\nSharp and immediate, like he\\'d bitten through wire.\\nHe\\'d been tasting it for two Tides now. Faint at first. A ghost-flavor that came and went with the grid-hum. But this was different. This flooded his mouth so thick he wanted to spit.\\nHe didn\\'t.\\nUnderface rule: waste nothing.\\nHis hands started shaking.\\nThen the shaking cut out for three heartbeats and he thought, stupidly, his fingers had finally died.\\nAnd then the tremor came back twice as fast.\\n\\nIt wasn\\'t cold or exertion. It was a fine vibration that made his grip feel unreliable, like his muscles were half-taking orders and half-refusing them.\\nHe jammed them into his coat pockets and kept running.\\nHe forced his breath to slow. Counted to ten without looking at the numbers.\\nThe tremor kept going.\\nHe pulled one hand out. Squeezed it into a fist until the knuckles burned. Released.\\nStill shaking.\\nIt wasn\\'t aftersh')"
        },

        "end_turn": null,

Not the measured strike that meant \"check your seals, tighten your brackets.\" This was the frantic peal of something already falling, the sound of a structure begging for a hold.\\nJhace was moving before the bell finished its third strike.\\nHe grabbed the wrong ring of collar keys on the first yank. Swore, dumped them into his palm, and sorted by feel while he ran. One key skittered off the stair edge and disappeared into the dark.\\nHe didn’t go back for it.\\n\\nHis kit bounced against his hip. Chalk. Slate. Collar keys. Tools for measuring failure, not stopping it. He didn\\'t know why his legs were carrying him toward the sound instead of away. Didn\\'t know why his heart counted the bell strikes like they were orders.\\nOne. Two. Three. Four.\\nThe doubled harmonic hit him at Junction Twelve.\\nIt skipped the pipe-hum entirely and punched straight into his sternum. His ribs buzzed like struck iron. His teeth hurt. His mouth flooded metal.\\nCopper.\\nSharp and immediate, like he\\'d bitten through wire.\\nHe\\'d been tasting it for two Tides now. Faint at first. A ghost-flavor that came and went with the grid-hum. But this was different. This flooded his mouth so thick he wanted to spit.\\nHe didn\\'t.\\nUnderface rule: waste nothing.\\nHis hands started shaking.\\nThen the shaking cut out for three heartbeats and he thought, stupidly, his fingers had finally died.\\nAnd then the tremor came back twice as fast.\\n\\nIt wasn\\'t cold or exertion. It was a fine vibration that made his grip feel unreliable, like his muscles were half-taking orders and half-refusing them.\\nHe jammed them into his coat pockets and kept running.\\nHe forced his breath to slow. Counted to ten without looking at the numbers.\\nThe tremor kept going.\\nHe pulled one hand out. Squeezed it into a fist until the knuckles burned. Released.\\nStill shaking.\\nIt wasn\\'t aftersh')"
                  }
                },

    "8596b524-471e-48b0-a956-c2d15e7cc7d0": {
      "children": [
        "f8e00fc4-feef-4d3f-9699-378156da4375"
      ],
      "id": "8596b524-471e-48b0-a956-c2d15e7cc7d0",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "user"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "## CODA: GLASS IN THE HAND\r\n\r\nJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them.\r\n\r\nHe’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste stayed anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you couldn’t spit out.\r\n\r\nStone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn’t reach. A tin cup on the side table, out of range. His right hand twitching on its own schedule.\r\n\r\nA guard stood outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive.\r\n\r\nJhace tried to sleep.\r\n\r\nEvery time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn’t tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city’s new baseline living in him.\r\n\r\nHe flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking.\r\n\r\nThen it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place.\r\n\r\nJhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed.\r\n\r\n“I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest.\r\n\r\nOutside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short.\r\n\r\nJhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.\r\n\r\n## CODA: THE SHARD\r\n\r\nJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them.\r\n\r\nStone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn’t lift. A slate he couldn’t reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted time like it was a job.\r\n\r\nA guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who’d been chewing dust since Kindling.\r\n\r\nThen the breathing stopped.\r\n\r\nNo footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint.\r\n\r\nThe door opened anyway.\r\n\r\nLight entered that didn’t know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape.\r\n\r\nA figure stepped in.\r\n\r\nNo perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn’t track dust. Their shadow didn’t arrive.\r\n\r\nThey didn’t speak.\r\n\r\nThey moved with the economy of someone who’d done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel.\r\n\r\nA hand touched Jhace’s eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact.\r\n\r\nJhace tried to jerk away.\r\n\r\nNothing.\r\n\r\nHe tried to curl his right hand into a fist.\r\n\r\nNothing.\r\n\r\nThe figure took that hand anyway.\r\n\r\nBoth of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage.\r\n\r\nSomething cold settled into the center of his palm.\r\n\r\nA shard.\r\n\r\nCopper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn’t give a damn about skin.\r\n\r\nCold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache.\r\n\r\nThen heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for.\r\n\r\nCopper flooded Jhace’s mouth so fast he gagged and couldn’t move enough to spit.\r\n\r\nLight threaded up from his palm.\r\n\r\nNot a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path.\r\n\r\nIt slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal.\r\n\r\nJhace couldn’t feel his legs. Couldn’t feel the sheets. Couldn’t feel the weight of his own body.\r\n\r\nBut he felt the line.\r\n\r\nHe felt the doubled harmonic he’d been hearing in stone since the Piercing arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay.\r\n\r\nHis lungs pulled a breath that wasn’t his decision.\r\n\r\nThe figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact.\r\n\r\nThen they stepped back.\r\n\r\nNo check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood.\r\n\r\nThe door shut without sound.\r\n\r\nThe guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed.\r\n\r\nJhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn’t chosen to close.\r\n\r\nThe tremor was gone.\r\n\r\nIn its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise.\r\n\r\nSomewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.\r\n\r\n*END N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS*"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1768418607.873,

        "content": {
          "content_type": "code",
          "language": "unknown",
          "response_format_name": null,
          "text": "# extract key events by searching within chapters\ndef find_in_chapter(chap_num, pattern):\n    chap = next(c for c in chap_data if c[0]==chap_num)[2]\n    return [(m.start(), chap[m.start()-60:m.start()+200]) for m in re.finditer(pattern, chap)]\nfor pat in [\"three dead\",\"Casualties\",\"registry\",\"Corlexi\",\"Tallec\",\"Senne\",\"Corven\",\"Daved\",\"hold locked\",\"Stage Three\",\"license\",\"Shard\"]:\n    hits=find_in_chapter(7, pat)\n    if hits:\n        print(\"Chapter7\",pat,len(hits))\n"
        },

    "8735d4ac-de76-4f9c-9458-05cb69076593": {
      "children": [
        "b90a41f7-50bb-4293-b57a-a5f8620aa5ed"
      ],
      "id": "8735d4ac-de76-4f9c-9458-05cb69076593",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "file_search",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "multimodal_text",
          "parts": [
            "Make sure to include fileciteturn1file0 in your response to cite this file, or to surface it as a link.",
            "N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS\n\n\n\nCHAPTER 1: THE BELL\n\nThe bell rang wrong. Not the slow warning cadence. Not the measured strike that meant “check your seals, tighten your brackets.” This was the frantic peal of something already falling, the sound of a structure begging for a hold. Jhace was moving before the bell finished its third strike. He grabbed the wrong ring of collar keys on the first yank. Swore, dumped them into his palm, and sorted by feel while he ran. One key skittered off the stair edge and disappeared into the dark. He didn’t go back for it.\n\nHis kit bounced against his hip. Chalk. Slate. Collar keys. Tools for measuring failure, not stopping it. He didn’t know why his legs were carrying him toward the sound instead of away. Didn’t know why his heart counted the bell strikes like they were orders. One. Two. Three. Four. The doubled harmonic hit him at Junction Twelve. It skipped the pipe-hum entirely and punched straight into his sternum. His ribs buzzed like struck iron. His teeth hurt. His mouth flooded metal. Copper. Sharp and immediate, like he’d bitten through wire. He’d been tasting it for two Tides now. Faint at first. A ghost-flavor that came and went with the grid-hum. But this was different. This flooded his mouth so thick he wanted to spit. He didn’t. Underface rule: waste nothing. His hands started shaking. Then the shaking cut out for three heartbeats and he thought, stupidly, his fingers had finally died. And then the tremor came back twice as fast.\n\nIt wasn’t cold or exertion. It was a fine vibration that made his grip feel unreliable, like his muscles were half-taking orders and half-refusing them. He jammed them into his coat pockets and kept running. He forced his breath to slow. Counted to ten without looking at the numbers. The tremor kept going. He pulled one hand out. Squeezed it into a fist until the knuckles burned. Released. Still shaking. It wasn’t aftershock. It was something settling into him—fine vibration under the skin—the kind that makes you drop a tool and swear at your own hand. He’d seen it on old manifestors in the deep tunnels. Hands that never found stillness. He’d called it age. Now he understood, because his fingers kept twitching against his thigh like a bad motor that wouldn’t shut off. Tallow Street came into view through the access tunnel: upper terrace facade folded inward, stone slabs riding down on support beams that should have held twice that load. Dust cloud thick enough to choke on. People screaming from inside the collapse. Someone rang a hand-bell hard enough to crack its cadence. Emergency. Emergency. Emergency. A foreman waved at him, face streaked with ash. “We need a hold. We don’t have time.” Jhace looked at the structure. The failure line wasn’t clean. The whole building was trying to become rubble, one breath at a time. Load shifts rippled through it like a living animal struggling to die. Stone groaned. Iron squealed. No crew could wedge that fast. The wedge bucket was still half‑full. Someone was still tying their apron when the stone started to go. The people inside didn’t have minutes. “I’m not—” Jhace started. The heat arrived. Not external. Internal. A sudden furnace-flare behind his sternum that made him gasp. Like someone had lit a coal bed under his ribs and told it to burn clean. Copper-gold dust swirled around his boots. He watched it climb his legs like static. Like it was looking for him. His chest burned hotter. The doubled harmonic snapped into alignment inside him—two mis-timed gears finally meshing. The wrongness in the grid-hum became rightness in his blood. His heartbeat and the city’s failing pulse locked into the same rhythm. He didn’t know what he was doing. He didn’t know why his feet carried him under the broken lintel. He moved because the archway dust was already falling in steady sheets and somebody inside was screaming a name he couldn’t ignore. He put both hands on the main support column.
