    "a9e87938-77af-4e52-b9f4-88c869e8ff1a": {
      "children": [
        "9b9fb36f-fc97-4b18-95f0-a86418d015a1"
      ],
      "id": "a9e87938-77af-4e52-b9f4-88c869e8ff1a",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "user"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "# N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS\r\n## CHAPTER 5: FIRST NETWORK — LOCKED\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\nThe kiln-quarter feeder gantry sat in Sector Fourteen, where the pipes ran hot and the schedules ran hotter.\r\n\r\nThe bell came late.\r\n\r\nThat was the first problem.\r\n\r\nWhen Jhace and Tiffani arrived, the gantry wasn't collapsed—yet. The support columns were bowing, groaning under a load they shouldn't have been carrying because somebody had over-pressured the feed line to meet quota and called it \"necessary.\"\r\n\r\nA foreman screamed, \"Hold it! Hold it now!\"\r\n\r\nJhace moved by habit.\r\n\r\nChest heat answered. Immediate.\r\n\r\nTiffani caught his sleeve.\r\n\r\n\"Not yet.\"\r\n\r\nJhace turned his head a fraction. The moment he shifted focus, the warmth behind his sternum flared—punishment for looking away from the load. The tremor in his hands ticked harder, like it wanted permission.\r\n\r\n\"People are inside,\" he rasped.\r\n\r\n\"People are leaving,\" she said, pointing.\r\n\r\nShe was right. Workers were already pouring out of the side doors, coughing, eyes wide. The structure wasn't in free fall. It was in warning.\r\n\r\n\"We wedge first,\" Tiffani said. \"Then you hold if wedges fail.\"\r\n\r\nJhace's jaw tightened.\r\n\r\nHe hated needing permission to do the only thing that had worked.\r\n\r\nHe dropped to one knee anyway.\r\n\r\nWedge slips. Strap loops. Collar brackets. The old tools. The honest ones.\r\n\r\nHis first wedge went in crooked.\r\n\r\nHe felt it before he saw it. The angle wrong. The bite uneven.\r\n\r\nHe yanked it back out. Knuckles scraped stone. Grainy dust cut under his nail.\r\n\r\nThe foreman saw.\r\n\r\nThe workers saw.\r\n\r\nThat was the second problem.\r\n\r\nHe wasn't a clean solution anymore. He was a man with shaking hands making visible mistakes.\r\n\r\nHe shoved the wedge in again. Straight this time. Hammered it home with the heel of his palm because the mallet handle wouldn't stay still.\r\n\r\nBram's crew swarmed the brace line behind him, hands fast, straps ready, mouths full of curses and grit.\r\n\r\nFor once, labor did what labor could do.\r\n\r\nThe gantry groaned again. Dust spilled from a seam.\r\n\r\nA young man stepped into the brace line, hands hovering like he couldn't decide whether touching the structure made him brave or stupid.\r\n\r\nCorven.\r\n\r\nJhace had seen him in the tunnels. Quiet. Competent. Always watching other men's hands.\r\n\r\nCorven swallowed. \"I… I can feel it,\" he said. \"The hum. It's—\"\r\n\r\n\"Louder,\" Jhace finished.\r\n\r\nCorven nodded hard. \"In my teeth.\"\r\n\r\nTiffani stepped close, slate already up. \"If it hits your chest, tell me. If you taste copper, tell me. Do not improvise.\"\r\n\r\nCorven's eyes flicked to the bending column. \"If it goes—\"\r\n\r\n\"Then we act,\" Jhace said. \"But not before.\"\r\n\r\nThe wedges held for two breath-counts.\r\n\r\nThen the central column shifted.\r\n\r\nNot collapse. A decisive, ugly move—stone settling, iron flexing. The wedges screamed as they compressed. The brace line bowed like it was thinking about surrender.\r\n\r\nWorkers were still inside.\r\n\r\n\"Tiff,\" Jhace said, voice rough.\r\n\r\nShe didn't hesitate.\r\n\r\n\"Now.\"\r\n\r\nJhace put both hands on the column.\r\n\r\nHeat flared behind his sternum—familiar now, terrifying in its familiarity—and copper-gold filament threaded into the seam like the stone had been waiting for him.\r\n\r\nThe column stopped shifting.\r\n\r\nThe groan of stressed iron went quiet.\r\n\r\nJhace's breath shook out of him. He could feel the load distribution through his fingertips: uneven, trying to walk itself sideways. His hands tightened. The filament brightened in response.\r\n\r\n\"Keep moving!\" Bram shouted, voice cutting through the noise like a saw. \"If you stop, you become debris! Count them out! Loud. Fast!\"\r\n\r\nBodies poured out. Coughing. Limping. Dragging each other by collars.\r\n\r\nTiffani didn't count in her head. She counted with her mouth. Audible. Aggressive. Like numbers could bully stone into patience.\r\n\r\n\"One. Two. Three—\"\r\n\r\nCorven stared at Jhace's hands.\r\n\r\nJhace kept his eyes forward. \"Your turn.\"\r\n\r\nCorven flinched.\r\n\r\n\"Secondary brace,\" Jhace said, through clenched teeth. \"Touch. Don't squeeze. Don't try to be a hero.\"\r\n\r\nCorven stepped in and laid both palms against the brace.\r\n\r\nHis breath hitched.\r\n\r\nJhace saw it in his face before he heard it: copper taste arriving like a slap.\r\n\r\nCorven swallowed hard. \"Copper.\"\r\n\r\nTiffani's pen scratched.\r\n\r\nCorven's eyes widened. \"Chest—\"\r\n\r\nHeat took him.\r\n\r\nNot metaphor. Not fear. Actual furnace bloom behind the ribs.\r\n\r\nCorven made a sound like he'd been punched.\r\n\r\nThen light—thinner than Jhace's, but real—threaded from Corven's hands into the brace line.\r\n\r\nThe brace stopped moving.\r\n\r\nCorven's hands started to shake immediately.\r\n\r\nNot after. Not later.\r\n\r\nNow.\r\n\r\n\"Stage One onset during hold,\" Tiffani murmured, writing. \"Early marker.\"\r\n\r\nCorven's jaw clenched. \"I can do—\"\r\n\r\n\"Do exactly what the structure needs,\" Jhace snapped. \"Nothing more.\"\r\n\r\nThe tremor in Jhace's arms surged in sympathy. His shoulders burned. Vision tunneled at the edges, copper-gold sparks skating across the periphery.\r\n\r\n\"How long?\" Tiffani asked.\r\n\r\nJhace didn't answer in words. He answered in counting. Heartbeats ticking behind his teeth.\r\n\r\nEighty.\r\n\r\nOne-twenty.\r\n\r\nOne-fifty.\r\n\r\nThe last workers stumbled out—two hundred-plus bodies alive because they'd been allowed time.\r\n\r\n\"Clear!\" someone shouted.\r\n\r\nTiffani's voice cut clean. \"Terminate.\"\r\n\r\nJhace released first.\r\n\r\nThe column held under wedges now that the load had shifted down and the pressure line had been throttled. Not safe. Not fixed. But no longer falling while people were inside.\r\n\r\nCorven released a heartbeat later and nearly fell.\r\n\r\nJhace caught him by the elbow. The tremor in his own hands made the grip feel unreliable, like even saving someone standing in front of him required negotiation with his nerves.\r\n\r\nCorven looked at his shaking fingers with horror. \"It's not stopping.\"\r\n\r\n\"It doesn't,\" Jhace said.\r\n\r\nCorven stared like he expected the sentence to end with a condition.\r\n\r\nIt didn't.\r\n\r\nTiffani wrote the numbers anyway.\r\n\r\n**Evacuated: 219.**\r\n**Casualties: 0.**\r\n**Operators marked: 2.**\r\n\r\nBram approached, face hard under the dust. \"Crown's going to hear about this. And they'll bring forms instead of shovels.\"\r\n\r\nTiffani didn't look up. \"Good.\"\r\n\r\nCorven swallowed again. Still tasting copper.\r\n\r\n\"How long does it last?\" he asked.\r\n\r\nJhace's jaw tightened. \"Until you stop manifesting.\"\r\n\r\nCorven's mouth twitched. Not a smile. Something worse. \"So never.\"\r\n\r\n\"Yeah.\"\r\n\r\nCorven set his palm on a railing. The tremor rattled bone against metal.\r\n\r\nHe pulled back. Flexed. Tried again. Harder.\r\n\r\nIt kept moving—slow, stubborn—running even after you stop pulling.\r\n\r\nTiffani's pen didn't pause. Because pausing didn't change what was true.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n### ROUTE FAILURE (THREE DEAD)\r\n\r\nThe count was an argument before it was a number.\r\n\r\nThe bell came from Sector Nine, not as a roar—just a single strike repeated until someone answered.\r\n\r\nResidential stairwell. Upper-terrace split. Twelve families still inside, one route.\r\n\r\nJhace was already marked from the gantry. Corven was worse. Senne was fresh enough to stand without shaking.\r\n\r\nTiffani stared at the slate wall and made the decision she would later pretend was only math.\r\n\r\nTwo operators at one-eighty each meant coordination, handoffs, spotters, failure points.\r\n\r\nOne operator at two-forty meant simplicity.\r\n\r\nA clean line.\r\n\r\n\"Single deployment,\" she said. \"Senne. Duration cap: two-forty. Terminate on my bell.\"\r\n\r\nSenne went pale as the heat took her chest. Copper-gold light laced her forearms. Not pretty.\r\n\r\nFunctional.\r\n\r\nThey reached the stairwell mouth as stone dust sifted from the ceiling. The grid underfoot carried the doubled hum, deep and insistent, a warning that didn't care who listened.\r\n\r\nSenne braced under the central landing. Jhace watched her shoulders set—metalworker posture in a glassworker frame.\r\n\r\nTiffani counted. Heartbeats, not comfort. Numbers didn't soften just because people begged.\r\n\r\nOne hundred.\r\n\r\nOne-forty.\r\n\r\nOne-eighty.\r\n\r\nAt one-ninety, a secondary beam—one Tiffani hadn't walked because she'd trusted the map—shifted with a sound like teeth grinding.\r\n\r\nThe evacuation lane kinked.\r\n\r\nThe next family stumbled.\r\n\r\nThe stone didn't collapse all at once. It folded, patient and specific, closing the corridor into a narrow pocket.\r\n\r\nThree people were on the wrong side when it did.\r\n\r\nTwo adults and a child coughing copper into his sleeve.\r\n\r\nTiffani saw them and for a heartbeat the slate in her hands felt obscene.\r\n\r\n\"Hold,\" she said, voice flat. \"Keep holding.\"\r\n\r\nSenne's eyes didn't move. She couldn't spare the motion.\r\n\r\nTwo-ten.\r\n\r\nTwo-twenty.\r\n\r\nTwo-thirty.\r\n\r\nThe pocket didn't clear.\r\n\r\nThe child's coughing turned wet.\r\n\r\nAt two-forty, Tiffani rang the termination sequence anyway.\r\n\r\nBecause protocols existed for a reason.\r\n\r\nBecause if she didn't, she would never stop.\r\n\r\nSenne released.\r\n\r\nThe stairwell collapsed into itself with a slow, final sound—stone settling into the shape it had wanted all along.\r\n\r\nThey dug for hours.\r\n\r\nThey found three bodies. The child first, because he was small enough to slip into the wrong angles.\r\n\r\nBack in the workshop, Tiffani logged it with perfect precision.\r\n\r\n**Deployment:** Senne — 240 heartbeats\r\n**Evacuated:** 47\r\n**Casualties:** 3\r\n**Cause:** Inadequate route assessment. Secondary beam shear.\r\n\r\nShe wrote *cause* as if precision could make it reversible.\r\n\r\nHer hand moved to write the next revision. The chalk touched slate.\r\n\r\nNothing came out.\r\n\r\nShe stared at the number.\r\n\r\nThree.\r\n\r\nNot a count.\r\n\r\nThree names she would never know.\r\n\r\nThe chalk snapped in her grip.\r\n\r\nShe set the broken pieces down carefully. Picked up a new stick. Wrote:\r\n\r\n**Route spotters required. Three per deployment. No exceptions.**\r\n\r\nUnderlined it twice.\r\n\r\nThen she closed the slate and sat in the dark workshop, counting her own breaths because the numbers on the page wouldn't behave.\r\n\r\nHer fingers kept shaking anyway.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n**END CHAPTER 5**\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n*LOCKED — REVISION COMPLETE*"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1768394303.293,
        "end_turn": null,

    "abf6fc56-980a-4bfe-9a8d-2f9972eaa24b": {
      "children": [
        "6eab49da-8cad-4b0c-b737-2d918ba3a737"
      ],
      "id": "abf6fc56-980a-4bfe-9a8d-2f9972eaa24b",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "python",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "d. His vision tunneled at the edges.\n“How long?” Tiffani asked.\n“Eighty,” Jhace rasped, counting heartbeats without thinking.\nCorven’s jaw clenched. “I can do—”\n“Do exactly what the structure needs,” Jhace snapped. “Nothing more.”\nAt one‑fifty, the last workers stumbled out. Two hundred‑plus bodies, alive because they’d been allowed time.\n“Terminate,” Tiffani said.\nJhace released first. The column held under wedges now that the load had shifted down.\nCorven released a heartbeat later and nearly fell.\nJhace caught him by the elbow, and the tremor in his own hands made the grip feel unreliable.\nCorven looked at his shaking fingers with a kind of horror. “It’s not stopping.”\n“It doesn’t,” Jhace said.\nCorven stared at his shaking fingers like they’d betrayed him mid-shift.\nHe’d watched Jhace hold. Had seen the tremor. Had understood it in theory.\nTheory didn’t matter when your hand wouldn’t listen.\n“How long?” he asked.\nJhace’s jaw tightened. “Until you stop manifesting.”\nCorven’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. “So never.”\n“Yeah.”\nCorven set his palm on the edge of the bench. The tremor rattled bone against metal.\nHe pulled back like it burned. Flexed. Tried again. Harder.\nIt kept moving\n"
        },
        "create_time": 1768370645.090213,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "abf6fc56-980a-4bfe-9a8d-2f9972eaa24b",

          "aggregate_result": {
            "code": "print(around(\"Corven looked at his shaking fingers\", 600)[:1200])\n",
            "end_time": 1768370646.1835914,
            "final_expression_output": null,

        "content": {
          "content_type": "code",
          "language": "unknown",
          "response_format_name": null,
          "text": "def snippets_for(word):\n    pat=re.compile(rf\"\\b{word}\\b\", re.IGNORECASE)\n    for i,p in enumerate(doc2.paragraphs):\n        m=pat.search(p.text)\n        if m:\n            txt=p.text\n            print(i, txt[max(0,m.start()-60):m.end()+60])\nprint(\"days\"); snippets_for(\"days\")\nprint(\"minutes\"); snippets_for(\"minutes\")\nprint(\"second\"); snippets_for(\"second\")\nprint(\"seconds\"); snippets_for(\"seconds\")\n"
        },
        "create_time": 1768421454.552006,

          "text": "idxs=sorted(par_hits.keys())\nfor pi in idxs:\n    print(\"\\n--- paragraph\",pi,\"terms\",par_hits[pi],\"---\")\n    print(doc.paragraphs[pi].text)\n"
        },
        "create_time": 1768421248.867368,
        "end_turn": false,
        "id": "b0f12ed1-247e-46a9-97e0-ead868086de3",

    "b1989984-4192-4142-bc1b-694e5f93f6ec": {
      "children": [
        "78a67800-35a4-492c-a81e-2455b0f84775"
      ],
      "id": "b1989984-4192-4142-bc1b-694e5f93f6ec",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "code",
          "language": "unknown",
          "response_format_name": null,
          "text": "from docx.shared import Inches\nfrom docx.oxml.ns import qn\n\ndef clean_paras(text):\n    text=text.strip()\n    # unify newlines\n    text=text.replace('\\r\\n','\\n').replace('\\r','\\n')\n    # split paragraphs on blank lines\n    parts=re.split(r'\\n\\s*\\n+', text)\n    paras=[]\n    for p in parts:\n        p=p.strip()\n        if not p:\n            continue\n        # join internal newlines\n        p=re.sub(r'\\s*\\n\\s*', ' ', p)\n        # collapse spaces\n        p=re.sub(r'[ \\t]+', ' ', p).strip()\n        paras.append(p)\n    return paras\n\n# apply chapter12 fix\ntitle12, body12 = selected[12]\nbody12_fixed = body12.replace('“Clear lane,” Tiffani ordered. “Spotters assigned. Wedges first.”\\n\\n', '')\nselected[12]=(title12, body12_fixed)\n\n# build doc\nout_doc=Document()\n# set default font\nstyle=out_doc.styles['Normal']\nstyle.font.name='Calibri'\nstyle._element.rPr.rFonts.set(qn('w:eastAsia'), 'Calibri')\nstyle.font.size=Pt(11)\n\n# Title\np=out_doc.add_paragraph(\"N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS\")\np.style=out_doc.styles['Title']\np.alignment=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER\n\n# add chapters\nfor n in range(1,14):\n    title, body = selected[n]\n    heading_text=f\"CHAPTER {n}: {title}\".strip()\n    out_doc.add_paragraph(heading_text, style='Heading 1')\n    for para in clean_paras(body):\n        out_doc.add_paragraph(para, style='Normal')\n    # blank line between chapters\n    out_doc.add_paragraph(\"\", style='Normal')\n\n# coda\ncoda_title, coda_body = selected['coda']\nout_doc.add_paragraph(\"CODA\", style='Heading 1')\nfor para in clean_paras(coda_body):\n    out_doc.add_paragraph(para, style='Normal')\n\n# final end line if present in body? the coda body ends with END line maybe.\n# Ensure end line included; check\nend_present=\"END N01\" in coda_body\nend_present\n"
        },
        "create_time": 1768420902.437755,
        "end_turn": false,
        "id": "b1989984-4192-4142-bc1b-694e5f93f6ec",

    "b2182eb7-2ca4-4244-8c20-c9ae8d7d2add": {
      "children": [
        "8418132c-6212-402f-bee0-6c2d01d66239"
      ],
      "id": "b2182eb7-2ca4-4244-8c20-c9ae8d7d2add",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "python",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "150 --- CODA: THE SHARD Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them. Stone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn't lift. A slate he couldn't reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted time like it was a job. A guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who'd been chewing dust since Kindling. Then the breathing stopped. No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint. The door opened anyway. Light entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape. A figure stepped in. No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive. They didn't speak. They moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel. A hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact. Jhace tried to jerk away. Nothing. He tried to curl his right hand into a fist. Nothing. The figure took that hand anyway. Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage. Something cold settled into the center of his palm. A shard. Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin. Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache. Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for. Copper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit. Light threaded up from his palm. Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path. It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal. Jhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body. But he felt the line. He felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay. His lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision. The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact. Then they stepped back. No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood. The door shut without sound. The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed. Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close. The tremor was gone. In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise. Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.\n156 The guard’s breathing stopped. No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint. The door opened anyway. Light entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape. A figure stepped in. No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive. They didn't speak. They moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel. A hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact. Jhace tried to jerk away. Nothing. He tried to curl his right hand into a fist. Nothing. The figure took that hand anyway. Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage. Something cold settled into the center of his palm. A shard. Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin. Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache. Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for. Copper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit. Light threaded up from his palm. Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path. It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal. Jhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body. But he felt the line. He felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay. His lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision. The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact. Then they stepped back. No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood. The door shut without sound. The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed. Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close. The tremor was gone. In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise. Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.\n"
        },

            "final_expression_output": null,
            "in_kernel_exception": null,
            "jupyter_messages": [

                "content": {

                  "msg_id": "46fe0254-2b2f18f063315ed35b70f762_196_65",

              {
                "content": {
                  "name": "stdout",
                  "text": "150 --- CODA: THE SHARD Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them. Stone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn't lift. A slate he couldn't reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted time like it was a job. A guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who'd been chewing dust since Kindling. Then the breathing stopped. No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint. The door opened anyway. Light entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape. A figure stepped in. No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive. They didn't speak. They moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel. A hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact. Jhace tried to jerk away. Nothing. He tried to curl his right hand into a fist. Nothing. The figure took that hand anyway. Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage. Something cold settled into the center of his palm. A shard. Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin. Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache. Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for. Copper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit. Light threaded up from his palm. Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path. It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal. Jhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body. But he felt the line. He felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay. His lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision. The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact. Then they stepped back. No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood. The door shut without sound. The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed. Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close. The tremor was gone. In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise. Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.\n156 The guard’s breathing stopped. No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint. The door opened anyway. Light entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape. A figure stepped in. No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive. They didn't speak. They moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel. A hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact. Jhace tried to jerk away. Nothing. He tried to curl his right hand into a fist. Nothing. The figure took that hand anyway. Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage. Something cold settled into the center of his palm. A shard. Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin. Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache. Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for. Copper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit. Light threaded up from his palm. Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path. It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal. Jhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body. But he felt the line. He felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay. His lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision. The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact. Then they stepped back. No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood. The door shut without sound. The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed. Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close. The tremor was gone. In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise. Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.\n"
                },
                "msg_type": "stream",

                  "msg_id": "46fe0254-2b2f18f063315ed35b70f762_196_65",

          "text": "import re, pathlib, textwrap\ntext = pathlib.Path(\"/mnt/data/N01Final.Last.txt\").read_text(errors=\"ignore\")\n# normalize newlines\nt = text.replace('\\r\\n','\\n').replace('\\r','\\n')\n# find chapter headers\nheaders = [(m.group(0), m.start()) for m in re.finditer(r\"CHAPTER\\s+\\d+:\\s+[A-Z0-9 \\-\\(\\)']+\", t)]\nheaders[:15], len(headers)\n"
        },

          "text": "139 CHAPTER 13: THE LEDGER\n140 Tiffani wrote the first copy with her left hand because her right knee refused to lock and her right hand refused to stay still. It wasn’t Stage. It was the kind of fatigue that lives in joints after \n141 \n142 CODA: GLASS IN THE HAND Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them. He’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste was still there anyway—wedged behind his teet\n143 \n144 CODA: THE SHARD Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them. Stone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn’t lift. A slate he c\n145 \n146 END N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS\n147 \n148 CHAPTER 10: LICENSING\n149 They arrived with paper first. The first page had a coffee ring on it. The seal ink was smudged where somebody’s thumb had dragged across it like the Crown was just another sweaty foreman.\n150 A Crown clerk with clean boots unrolled forms on Jhace’s bench like a priest laying out offerings. Behind the clerk stood two guards, a Temple observer, and Tallec—smiling like he’d finally found a wa\n151 Jhace signed the registry with a hand that wouldn’t hold still. When it was done, the clerk rolled the papers and left as if the room wasn’t full of broken bodies and hard choices. Tallec lingered in \n152 \n153 CHAPTER 11: HUNGER\n154 The hunger wasn’t metaphor. It lived behind Jhace’s sternum as a warmth that never fully left after the first hold. A phantom heat that flared whenever the grid‑hum dipped, whenever dust fell, wheneve\n155 Outside, a bell rang—different cadence. Not collapse. A drill signal. Crown‑mandated training. Jhace’s chest heat flared anyway, eager and stupid. He hated himself for that flare. He stood, hands shak\n156 \n"

    "b4abd247-e241-4382-ba4b-ae661f873081": {
      "children": [
        "8596b524-471e-48b0-a956-c2d15e7cc7d0"
      ],
      "id": "b4abd247-e241-4382-ba4b-ae661f873081",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": "final",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "Grok’s codas are stronger than the ones I spit out. Cleaner sensory, better “infirmary object inventory,” and the Shard scene has that cold procedural cruelty you want.\n\nBut there’s one **hard violation** in Grok’s **CODA: THE SHARD**:\n\n- **“for days”** → forbidden. Real-world time unit. Must be **Tides**.\n\nAlso a couple micro-repeats you can trim without losing punch (double “cot” opener; double “slate he couldn’t reach” across both codas is fine, but in CODA: THE SHARD it’s stacked a bit heavy).\n\nBelow is a **tight, compliant patch** of Grok’s codas. Same voice. Same moves. No “days.” No extra invention.\n\n---\n\n## CODA: GLASS IN THE HAND (PATCHED)\n\nJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them.\n\nHe’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste stayed anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you couldn’t spit out.\n\nStone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn’t reach. His right hand twitching on its own schedule.\n\nA guard stood outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive.\n\nJhace tried to sleep.\n\nEvery time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn’t tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city’s new baseline living in him.\n\nHe flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking.\n\nThen it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place.\n\nJhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed.\n\n“I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest.\n\nOutside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short.\n\nJhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.\n\n---\n\n## CODA: THE SHARD (PATCHED)\n\nJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them.\n\nStone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn’t lift. A slate he couldn’t reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted like it was a job.\n\nA guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who’d been chewing dust since Kindling.\n\nThen the breathing stopped.\n\nNo footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint.\n\nThe door opened anyway.\n\nLight entered that didn’t know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape.\n\nA figure stepped in.\n\nNo perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn’t track dust. Their shadow didn’t arrive.\n\nThey didn’t speak.\n\nThey moved with the economy of someone who’d done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel.\n\nA hand touched Jhace’s eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact.\n\nJhace tried to jerk away.\n\nNothing.\n\nHe tried to curl his right hand into a fist.\n\nNothing.\n\nThe figure took that hand anyway.\n\nBoth of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage.\n\nSomething cold settled into the center of his palm.\n\nA shard.\n\nCopper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn’t give a damn about skin.\n\nCold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache.\n\nThen heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for.\n\nCopper flooded Jhace’s mouth so fast he gagged and couldn’t move enough to spit.\n\nLight threaded up from his palm.\n\nNot a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path.\n\nIt slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal.\n\nJhace couldn’t feel his legs. Couldn’t feel the sheets. Couldn’t feel the weight of his own body.\n\nBut he felt the line.\n\nHe felt the doubled harmonic he’d been hearing in stone for **Tides** arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay.\n\nHis lungs pulled a breath that wasn’t his decision.\n\nThe figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact.\n\nThen they stepped back.\n\nNo check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood.\n\nThe door shut without sound.\n\nThe guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed.\n\nJhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn’t chosen to close.\n\nThe tremor was gone.\n\nIn its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise.\n\nSomewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.\n\n*END N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS*\n\n---\n\nIf you want, I can also do a *meaner* trim pass (another ~3–5% shorter) without losing any information—just shaving duplicate inventory beats."
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1768418519.167877,
        "end_turn": true,
        "id": "b4abd247-e241-4382-ba4b-ae661f873081",

      "parent": "d0793e2e-36ae-4cca-9ec3-61b0f4f623db"
    },
    "b5161ade-ef07-47d9-a9f1-6a688543cd84": {
      "children": [
        "cadd91b8-034a-4e40-a81c-9cbad00ead0d"
      ],
      "id": "b5161ade-ef07-47d9-a9f1-6a688543cd84",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "code",
          "language": "unknown",
          "response_format_name": null,
          "text": "def parse_head(head):\n    ch=re.match(r'##\\s+CHAPTER\\s+(\\d+):\\s*(.*)', head)\n    if ch:\n        return (\"chapter\", int(ch.group(1)), ch.group(2).strip())\n    if head.startswith(\"## CODA\"):\n        return (\"coda\", None, head.replace(\"##\",\"\").strip())\n    return (\"other\", None, head)\nselected={}\nordered=[]\nfor head,body in sections:\n    typ,num,title=parse_head(head)\n    if typ==\"chapter\":\n        if num not in selected:\n            selected[num]=(title, body)\n            ordered.append(num)\n    elif typ==\"coda\":\n        selected['coda']=(title, body)\n# ensure all 1-13 present\nmissing=[n for n in range(1,14) if n not in selected]\nmissing, ordered[:5], ordered[-3:]\n"
        },
        "create_time": 1768420887.944116,
        "end_turn": false,
        "id": "b5161ade-ef07-47d9-a9f1-6a688543cd84",
        "metadata": {
          "can_save": false,
          "citations": [],
          "classifier_response": "default",
          "content_references": [],
          "default_model_slug": "gpt-5-2-thinking",
          "finish_details": {
            "stop_tokens": [
              200012
            ],
            "type": "stop"
          },
          "gizmo_id": "g-p-69534f0b73c08191bc65eb820ed7f050",
          "is_complete": true,
          "message_type": "next",
          "model_slug": "gpt-5-2-thinking",
          "parent_id": "d612e316-93be-4950-b71e-99d53aa6179b",
          "reasoning_status": "is_reasoning",
          "reasoning_title": "Selecting unique chapters and parsing numbers",
          "request_id": "ba40f93d-5914-4ac2-921e-0e253d2f234b",
          "thinking_effort": "standard",
          "turn_exchange_id": "2eb67aff-46cd-4a26-b4f8-65371be9fd70"
        },

    "b587f7ee-2028-4f45-8141-458937d08dd3": {
      "children": [
        "d7f4d2fe-9033-4c95-b55f-48c6ef86a765"
      ],
      "id": "b587f7ee-2028-4f45-8141-458937d08dd3",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "user"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "## CHAPTER 12: METHOD\r\n\r\nThe alert came at Tide 21 like an insult.\r\n\r\nIt was a narrow failure—no mass collapse, no Hall, no warehouse full of people.\r\n\r\nA residential stairwell in Sector Nine. A narrow stone throat that had been patched too many times since the Piercing. A single child trapped beneath a fallen landing slab, crying until her voice turned hoarse.\r\n\r\nTiffani arrived first. Her limp was worse now, but she moved like it didn’t matter. She scanned the structure in three heartbeats. Load paths. Weak seams. The slab’s weight distribution.\r\n\r\n“Clear lane,” she ordered as the others reached the site. “Spotters assigned. Wedges first.”\r\n\r\nShe pointed left, then froze. “No—right. Right corridor first. Sorry.”\r\n\r\nThe apology tasted like rust. She didn’t repeat it.\r\n\r\nJhace’s hands shook so hard he had to jam them into his coat pockets to stop them from betraying him in front of the crowd.\r\n\r\nHe could feel the doubled harmonic through the stairwell stone. Deep. Constant. The grid had never recovered. It had just learned to fail slower.\r\n\r\nBram arrived with Crown guards and a face that said he’d tried, briefly, to be serious and failed on contact.\r\n\r\n“Registry protocol,” he said. “Two licensed operators inbound.”\r\n\r\nTiffani didn’t look at him. “They’ll be too late.”\r\n\r\n“Cool,” Bram said. “Love the part where the Crown schedules rescue like it’s a delivery window.”\r\n\r\n“Clear lane,” Tiffani ordered. “Spotters assigned. Wedges first.”\r\n\r\nShe looked at the sagging landing slab, the trapped space beneath it, the child’s choking turning into wet silence. Her voice tightened into a blade.\r\n\r\n“One operator. Two minutes max. We brace, we lift, we extract. Terminate immediately after.”\r\n\r\n“Then who?” Jhace said.\r\n\r\n“Not you.”\r\n\r\n“Then who.”\r\n\r\n“The child is choking, Jhace.”\r\n\r\nJhace’s tremor rattled against his bones like a tool trying to escape. “I decide.”\r\n\r\nBram’s breath punched out. “Jesus—no, don’t—okay, you’re doing it. You’re doing the thing. Great. Fantastic. I hate this.”\r\n\r\nJhace stepped in. Copper flooded his mouth like blood.\r\n\r\nFor a heartbeat, nothing happened.\r\n\r\nThen the hold locked.\r\n\r\nThe stairwell’s doubled harmonic snapped into alignment with his chest, like a collar seating in the wrong notch.\r\n\r\nTiffani’s voice cut clean through the noise. “Wedges—now. Lift on my count. Three heartbeats. Pull.”\r\n\r\nBoots scraped. Wedges hammered. Stone complained.\r\n\r\n“Three,” Tiffani said. “Two. One. Now—pull!”\r\n\r\nHands reached into the dark. The child came out gasping, eyes wide, spit and dust and life.\r\n\r\n“Terminate,” Tiffani said.\r\n\r\nJhace didn’t move.\r\n\r\n“Terminate,” she repeated, like command could override biology.\r\n\r\nSilence.\r\n\r\nBram’s voice cracked into the gap. “He’s not moving.”\r\n\r\n“Jhace.” Tiffani stepped in close. “Move.”\r\n\r\nBram hovered like he wanted to shake him and knew better. “Say something. Blink. Spit. Insult me. Anything.”\r\n\r\n“Stop talking,” Tiffani snapped.\r\n\r\n“I can’t,” Bram said. “It’s how I pray.”\r\n\r\nJhace could still see. Still hear. Still count.\r\n\r\nHe stared at his hands, waiting for tremor.\r\n\r\nThere was no tremor.\r\n\r\nThere was nothing.\r\n\r\nHe tried to will a finger. Nothing.\r\n\r\nPanic hit—animal, immediate, nothing to do with heat or holding.\r\n\r\nHe tried to shout. Nothing left his throat.\r\n\r\nHe tried to jerk a shoulder. Nothing.\r\n\r\nHe tried to blink faster—signal—anything.\r\n\r\nHis lids moved on their own schedule. Slow. Unbothered.\r\n\r\nThe body was there. The controls were there. The link was gone.\r\n\r\nThe coal behind his sternum pulsed once. Steady. Patient. Still ready.\r\n\r\nReady to hold.\r\n\r\nBut he couldn’t lift a finger to tell it no.\r\n\r\nOnly the wanting stayed—locked in meat that wouldn’t answer.\r\n\r\nTiffani moved into his field of view and knelt like she was inspecting a fracture line. Ink and copper sat on her hands; she smelled like slate dust and hours without sleep.\r\n\r\n“Blink once for yes,” she said. “Twice for no.”\r\n\r\nHe blinked once.\r\n\r\n“Do you still feel the doubled harmonic through the stone?”\r\n\r\nOnce.\r\n\r\n“Can you tell me where it dipped before Tallow Street?”\r\n\r\nOnce. He could see it like a chart behind his eyes—deep note, then the metallic taste, then the split.\r\n\r\nTiffani slid her slate into his line of sight. Two columns. LOCATION. HEARTBEATS. Her writing stayed precise even as her knuckles whitened.\r\n\r\n“Can you predict where it fails next?”\r\n\r\nOnce. Not a comfort. A capability.\r\n\r\nTiffani’s mouth tightened. “Then you’re not finished.” She didn’t say it like hope. She said it like reassignment.\r\n\r\nJhace stared at his hands, waiting for tremor. Nothing.\r\n\r\nIt was worse than shaking. Silence in the muscles felt like a lie.\r\n\r\nTiffani dropped to her knees beside him, slate already out, chalk already moving.\r\n\r\n“Stage Four,” she said, voice flat because panic was a luxury. “Complete motor loss. Locked hold with autonomic override. Cost: total paralysis below manifestation threshold.”\r\n\r\nBram swallowed. “You’re writing this down.”\r\n\r\n“Yes.”\r\n\r\n“Right now.”\r\n\r\n“Yes.”\r\n\r\n“While he’s—”\r\n\r\n“Yes,” Tiffani said, and her hand didn’t shake. “Because if I don’t, this becomes a story someone else gets to tell. And they will tell it wrong.”\r\n\r\nShe swallowed once, hard, then kept writing.\r\n\r\nWhen the crowd noise finally thinned and the guards stopped pretending they weren’t watching, Tiffani stood.\r\n\r\n“Corlexi will get what I give him,” she said.\r\n\r\nBram blinked. “Which is what, exactly—an instruction manual with a hidden knife?”\r\n\r\n“A method,” Tiffani said. “With constraints. With brakes. Without permission to repeat this.”\r\n\r\n“So… a leash on the leash,” Bram said.\r\n\r\n“Something like that.”\r\n\r\n“You’re terrifying when you’re calm,” Bram said.\r\n\r\n“I’m not calm,” Tiffani said.\r\n\r\n“Right,” Bram said, voice softer than his jokes. “You’re just… controlled. Like a blade.”"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1768419939.162,
        "end_turn": null,

          "parts": [
            "From **this chat thread**, I had *verbatim locked text* to pull for:\n\n- **Chapter 1** (you pasted it in full)\n- **Chapter 2** (you pasted it in full)\n- **Chapter 3** (you pasted it in full)\n- **Chapter 4** (you pasted the *locked* rewrite)\n- **Chapter 5** (you pasted it in full)\n- **Chapter 6** (you pasted the Grok-locked version)\n- **Chapter 7** (you pasted it in full)\n- **Chapter 8** (you pasted it in full)\n- **Chapter 9** (you pasted the corrected hybrid text in full)\n- **Chapter 10** (you pasted it in full and locked it)\n- **Chapter 11** (you pasted the Grok version and then locked the “no training yard shown” scope)\n- **Chapter 13** (you pasted it; then you told me to remove the repeat block(s) and lock)\n- **Coda** (you pasted the single seamless version you want)\n\nFrom the **base manuscript file** (`N01Final.Last.txt`), I had to pull:\n\n- **Chapter 12** (because you did *not* paste Chapter 12 text anywhere in this thread)\n\nThat’s the clean split: **12 came from base**; everything else listed above came **from what you posted/locked here** (with Chapter 13 being “your text + repeat removal”)."
          ]
        },

            "code": "for i,p in enumerate(doc2.paragraphs):\n    if re.search(r\"\\bseconds\\b\", p.text, re.IGNORECASE):\n        set_paragraph_text(p, re.sub(r\"\\bseconds\\b\", \"heartbeats\", p.text, flags=re.IGNORECASE))\n# final check\nremaining={}\nfor w in forbidden:\n    pat=re.compile(rf\"\\b{w}\\b\", re.IGNORECASE)\n    res=[i for i,p in enumerate(doc2.paragraphs) if pat.search(p.text)]\n    if res:\n        remaining[w]=res\nremaining\n",

    "b93ed126-9962-4597-9b31-549669528e13": {
      "children": [
        "d3a224b3-e542-4394-bc63-94a3ed85e0a0"
      ],
      "id": "b93ed126-9962-4597-9b31-549669528e13",
      "message": {

          "metadata": {},

        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "eryone else forgetting how to breathe. Just light—copper‑gold, dense, not filament‑thin. It didn’t thread into the structure like Jhace’s holds did. It sat inside the beam like a replacement spine, telling wood and stone what shape to keep. Tiffani stopped beside Jhace. Her pen was already moving. Shard‑Bearer. Visual confirmation. The man’s chest glowed through his shirt. Not a flicker. A steady line, as if someone had pressed a shard under skin and the body had agreed. “No tremor,” Tiffani murmured. “Not paying,” Jhace said. The words tasted wrong the moment he spoke them. “Incorrect,” she said. “Look at his eyes.” Jhace looked. The man’s eyes were copper‑gold and flat, putting off their own light like heated coins. Jhace couldn’t find a tremor in it. That scared him more than any shake. The beam settled another fraction. The store stopped complaining. The man released the timber and walked away without checking who escaped. No count. No confirmation. No termination signal. No glance back. Tiffani underlined her note twice. Cost unidentified. Method unreplicable. Do not engage. Jhace’s tremor ticked up. Not from fear. From wanting to know what it would feel like to hold without shaking.\n\n### 53\nA Crown clerk with clean boots unrolled forms on Jhace’s bench like a priest laying out offerings. Behind the clerk stood two guards, a Temple observer, and Tallec—smiling like he’d finally found a way to win without touching a pipe. And at the center, Bram Tallow in a different coat than usual: administrator’s cut, badge pinned. Still Bram, but now carrying Crown weight. “Don’t start,” Jhace said, seeing the coat. Bram’s eyes flicked down to Jhace’s shaking hands and then away. “I didn’t ask for this.” Jhace looked at the coat. The badge. The way Bram wore it like it didn’t fit his bones. “You took it anyway.” Bram’s grin tried to show up and died halfway. “Yeah. Because if I don’t, they send someone who actually likes forms. Someone who’ll file you under ‘infrastructure asset’ and sleep like a baby.” He touched the badge once. Like it burned. “I’m terrified,” he said, quiet enough to almost count as a confession. “Terrified they turn you into numbers. Terrified I help them do it. Terrified I get good at it.” The grin came back, wrong‑shaped. “So I’m coping by making dick jokes. Extremely healthy. Very professional.” Tiffani stepped forward. “What is it?” Bram nodded at the clerk. “Manifestor Registry. Compliance documentation. Deployment authority.” “By order of Infrastructure Compliance Office. Administrator Corlexi presiding.” “Oh good,” Bram said. “A sentence that already feels like a noose.” The clerk didn’t look up. “Name. District. Verified activation capability. Cost markers. Compliance agreement. You will not manifest outside authorized events.” “Define ‘deployment authority,’” Tiffani said. “Infrastructure Compliance Office.” “That is not a definition,” Tiffani said. “That is a label.” “It is the issuing body.” “Then who determines what qualifies as an ‘authorized event’?” “Infrastructure Compliance Office.” Jhace’s jaw worked. “What happens if we refuse?” “Then you are unlicensed. Unlicensed manifestation will be prosecuted as endangerment.” Tallec leaned in with a smile that didn’t reach his eyes. “Finally. Structure. No more renegade holds.” Bram stared at him like he’d stepped in something. “Tallec, you look like a man who’d schedule his own mother’s funeral for throughput.” “At least someone respects schedules,” Tallec said. “Don’t start,” Jhace warned. “I didn’t start,” Bram said. “He started. I’m just adding sex noises.” Corran stepped in like he didn’t believe doors applied to him. “Schedules don’t respect physics.” The clerk’s eyes flicked up, irritated. “Brother Corran. You are not assigned here.” “For the record,” Corran said, “I’m observing.” He looked at Jhace. “And if the Crown orders you to hold a vault instead of a residential stairwell?” Tallec’s voice went oily. “Assets protect assets.” Jhace’s hands went flat on the bench, because he didn’t trust them to do something irreversible. “Next time I hold, it’ll be because something is falling. Not because you want it to stay standing.” “Romantic,” Bram said. “I only break my body for strangers, not for paperwork.” Tiffani’s gaze stayed on the form. “If we don’t sign, we lose access to incidents. They will send licensed manifestors—or force untrained ones—to hold.” She set her pen down with a little click, the way you set down a blade when you're done being polite. “We sign,” she said. “But our standard rides with it.” The clerk’s mouth opened. “That is not—” “Duration limits,” Tiffani said. “Spotters. Termination criteria. Cost tracking. You will not turn people into disposable beams.” Bram leaned toward the clerk. “She means ‘stop treating people like disposable beams,’ but she’s saying it in math so you can understand.” “The registry does not negotiate terms with operators.” “Then the registry gets incomplete operators,” Tiffani said. “Your choice.” Tallec’s smile widened. “You become assets. Next time you hold, you’ll do it where the Crown tells you to hold. Not where you feel like playing hero.” “‘Playing hero,’” Bram echoed. “That’s rich coming from a man who thinks schedules are moral philosophy.” “Production keeps people alive,” Tallec said. “So does not crushing them,” Jhace said. “This conversation is over,” Tallec said, like he had the authority to end reality. “Was it a conversation?” Bram asked. “Felt more like a threat with paperwork attached.”\n\n### 58\nOutside, a bell rang—different cadence. Not collapse. A drill signal. Crown‑mandated training. Jhace’s chest heat flared anyway, eager and stupid. He hated himself for that flare. He stood, hands shaking, and went to the training yard because refusal would make them criminals. Tiffani followed, slate in hand, limping slightly from too many hours on stone floors. Daved watched them go and muttered, almost too quiet to hear: “Hungry hands hold until they break.” Jhace pretended he didn’t hear. He did. ---\n\n### 114\nTiffani moved into his field of view and knelt like she was inspecting a fracture line. Ink and copper sat on her hands; she smelled like slate dust and hours without sleep.\n\n### 148\nTiffani wrote the first copy with her left hand because her right knee refused to lock and her right hand refused to stay still. It wasn’t Stage. It was the kind of fatigue that lives in joints after bad work—the kind you carry until your hands start lying to you. The ink pooled anyway. Copper-gold dust clung to the nib, turning every line slightly wrong, as if the city itself wanted the record to smear. She made three piles. Four, if she was honest. One stack sat in clean paper with clean margins and clean language. Compliance phrasing. Definitions that sounded like protection while leaving room for ownership. One stack was tidy and dead. The kind of language that looks safe until somebody has to bleed under it. One stack was short enough to memorize. Underface cadence. Commands. Counts. No soft words, no long sentences—something you could carry in your head when paper burned or got confiscated. The last pile wasn’t for anyone. It was her draft. Her private notes. The parts she could not justify on a form and would not confess out loud. Bram stood in the doorway in his Crown coat like he’d stolen it off a corpse and decided it suited him. “You look like a priest,” Tiffani said without looking up. Bram glanced down at the coat. “Don’t you dare put that evil on me. Priests get paid to lie. I’m doing it recreationally.” “You’re late.” “Yeah. Turns out paperwork travels faster than rescue.” He leaned in, saw the piles. “Oh good. Homework. My favorite form of punishment.” Tiffani slid the clean stack toward him first. “This goes to Corlexi.” Bram’s fingers hovered. “This the ‘please don’t leash us’ letter?” “This is the ‘you get what I give you’ packet,” she said, tapping the pages hard enough to leave a smudge. “Limits. Spotters. A way out when the body says no.” Bram squinted. “And the part where we can tell them to go fuck themselves?” Tiffani didn’t blink. “Not included.” Bram’s smile went sharp. “That’s either genius or suicidal.” “It’s survivable.” She turned the next page over and wrote a title line in the margin: DEPLOYMENT AUTHORITY. Then she crossed it out and rewrote: DEPLOYMENT PROCEDURE. “He will read what he wants to read. I’m controlling the surface.” Bram exhaled through his nose. “Ah. Compliance theater with a trapdoor.” “There is no trapdoor,” she said. “There are exits. Distributed.” She pushed the second stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: Property is not a priority class. Operator veto. Refusal authority. No deployment without independent verification. Bram whistled. “This one’s the ‘burn the Crown down’ packet.” “This one is the ‘keep operators alive long enough to matter’ packet.” She slid it closer. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.” Bram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.” “They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.” Bram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.” Tiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.” Chloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers. “Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.” Bram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.” Chloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?” “Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.” Tiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage. Chloen read the first line. YOU CAN SAY NO. She looked up. “That’s new.” “It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.” Chloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.” And then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD. Chloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.” Bram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.” Tiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.” Chloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it. The door clicked. The room held its breath. Corran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen. “For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.” Bram’s grin came instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.” Corran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.” Tiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult. Corran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.” Tiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Then it will be accurate.” Corran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi?” Tiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm. “Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in daylight.” Bram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. That’s adorable.” “It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.” Corran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?” “Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.” Corran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.” He left. Bram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. “So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine.” “He gets safety with ownership language,” Tiffani corrected. “He will build a machine either way. I’m deciding where it jams.” Bram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?” Tiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.” She lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred. “Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge. Tiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission. Bram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.” “I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said. Outside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they'd been caught stealing. Tiffani didn’t move. She dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.\n\n### 150\n--- CODA: THE SHARD Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them. Stone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn't lift. A slate he couldn't reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted time like it was a job. A guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who'd been chewing dust since Kindling. Then the breathing stopped. No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint. The door opened anyway. Light entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape. A figure stepped in. No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive. They didn't speak. They moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel. A hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact. Jhace tried to jerk away. Nothing. He tried to curl his right hand into a fist. Nothing. The figure took that hand anyway. Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage. Something cold settled into the center of his palm. A shard. Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin. Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache. Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for. Copper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit. Light threaded up from his palm. Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path. It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal. Jhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body. But he felt the line. He felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay. His lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision. The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact. Then they stepped back. No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood. The door shut without sound. The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed. Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close. The tremor was gone. In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise. Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.\n\n### 156\nThe guard’s breathing stopped. No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint. The door opened anyway. Light entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape. A figure stepped in. No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive. They didn't speak. They moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel. A hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact. Jhace tried to jerk away. Nothing. He tried to curl his right hand into a fist. Nothing. The figure took that hand anyway. Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage. Something cold settled into the center of his palm. A shard. Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin. Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache. Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for. Copper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit. Light threaded up from his palm. Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path. It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal. Jhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body. But he felt the line. He felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay. His lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision. The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact. Then they stepped back. No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood. The door shut without sound. The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed. Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close. The tremor was gone. In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise. Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.\n"
        },

Bram’s eyes flicked down to Jhace’s shaking hands and then away. “I didn’t ask for this.” Jhace looked at the coat. The badge.
