    "e8326c23-928c-4e5b-8676-33b2408e9d03": {
      "children": [
        "168d0c49-8d25-4de4-9276-a41e9506d56b"
      ],
      "id": "e8326c23-928c-4e5b-8676-33b2408e9d03",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "user"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "yeah you did too much....\n## CHAPTER 10: LICENSING\r\n\r\nThey arrived with paper first.\r\n\r\nThe first page had a coffee ring on it. The seal ink was smudged where somebody’s thumb had dragged across it like the Crown was just another sweaty foreman.\r\n\r\nA Crown clerk with clean boots unrolled forms on Jhace’s bench like a priest laying out offerings.\r\n\r\nBehind the clerk stood two guards, a Temple observer, and Tallec—smiling like he’d finally found a way to win without touching a pipe.\r\n\r\nAnd at the center, Bram Tallow in a different coat than usual: administrator’s cut, badge pinned. Still Bram, but now carrying Crown weight.\r\n\r\n“Don’t start,” Jhace said, seeing the coat.\r\n\r\nBram’s eyes flicked down to Jhace’s shaking hands and then away. “I didn’t ask for this.”\r\n\r\nJhace looked at the coat. The badge. The way Bram wore it like it didn’t fit his bones. “You took it anyway.”\r\n\r\nBram’s grin tried to show up and died halfway. “Yeah. Because if I don’t, they send someone who actually likes forms. Someone who’ll file you under ‘infrastructure asset’ and sleep like a baby.”\r\n\r\nHe touched the badge once. Like it burned.\r\n\r\n“I’m terrified,” he said, quiet enough to almost count as a confession. “Terrified they turn you into numbers. Terrified I help them do it. Terrified I get good at it.”\r\n\r\nThe grin came back, wrong-shaped. “So I’m coping by making dick jokes. Extremely healthy. Very professional.”\r\n\r\nTiffani stepped forward. “What is it?”\r\n\r\nBram nodded at the clerk. “Manifestor Registry. Compliance documentation. Deployment authority.”\r\n\r\n“By order of Infrastructure Compliance Office. Administrator Corlexi presiding.”\r\n\r\n“Oh good,” Bram said. “A sentence that already feels like a noose.”\r\n\r\nThe clerk didn’t look up. “Name. District. Verified activation capability. Cost markers. Compliance agreement. You will not manifest outside authorized events.”\r\n\r\n“Define ‘deployment authority,’” Tiffani said.\r\n\r\n“Infrastructure Compliance Office.”\r\n\r\n“That is not a definition,” Tiffani said. “That is a label.”\r\n\r\n“It is the issuing body.”\r\n\r\n“Then who determines what qualifies as an ‘authorized event’?”\r\n\r\n“Infrastructure Compliance Office.”\r\n\r\nJhace’s jaw worked. “What happens if we refuse?”\r\n\r\n“Then you are unlicensed. Unlicensed manifestation will be prosecuted as endangerment.”\r\n\r\nTallec leaned in with a smile that didn’t reach his eyes. “Finally. Structure. No more renegade holds.”\r\n\r\nBram stared at him like he’d stepped in something. “Tallec, you look like a man who’d schedule his own mother’s funeral for throughput.”\r\n\r\n“At least someone respects schedules,” Tallec said.\r\n\r\n“Don’t start,” Jhace warned.\r\n\r\n“I didn’t start,” Bram said. “He started. I’m just adding sex noises.”\r\n\r\nCorran stepped in like he didn’t believe doors applied to him. “Schedules don’t respect physics.”\r\n\r\nThe clerk’s eyes flicked up, irritated. “Brother Corran. You are not assigned here.”\r\n\r\n“For the record,” Corran said, “I’m observing.”\r\n\r\nHe looked at Jhace. “And if the Crown orders you to hold a vault instead of a residential stairwell?”\r\n\r\nTallec’s voice went oily. “Assets protect assets.”\r\n\r\nJhace’s hands went flat on the bench, because he didn’t trust them to do something irreversible. “Next time I hold, it’ll be because something is falling. Not because you want it to stay standing.”\r\n\r\n“Romantic,” Bram said. “I only break my body for strangers, not for paperwork.”\r\n\r\nTiffani’s gaze stayed on the form. “If we don’t sign, we lose access to incidents. They will send licensed manifestors—or force untrained ones—to hold.”\r\n\r\nShe set her pen down with a little click, the way you set down a blade when you're done being polite. “We sign,” she said. “But our standard rides with it.”\r\n\r\nThe clerk’s mouth opened. “That is not—”\r\n\r\n“Duration limits,” Tiffani said. “Spotters. Termination criteria. Cost tracking. You will not turn people into disposable beams.”\r\n\r\nBram leaned toward the clerk. “She means ‘stop treating people like disposable beams,’ but she’s saying it in math so you can understand.”\r\n\r\n“The registry does not negotiate terms with operators.”\r\n\r\n“Then the registry gets incomplete operators,” Tiffani said. “Your choice.”\r\n\r\nTallec’s smile widened. “You become assets. Next time you hold, you’ll do it where the Crown tells you to hold. Not where you feel like playing hero.”\r\n\r\n“‘Playing hero,’” Bram echoed. “That’s rich coming from a man who thinks schedules are moral philosophy.”\r\n\r\n“Production keeps people alive,” Tallec said.\r\n\r\n“So does not crushing them,” Jhace said.\r\n\r\n“This conversation is over,” Tallec said, like he had the authority to end reality.\r\n\r\n“Was it a conversation?” Bram asked. “Felt more like a threat with paperwork attached.”\r\n\r\nJhace signed the registry with a hand that wouldn’t hold still.\r\n\r\nWhen it was done, the clerk rolled the papers and left as if the room wasn’t full of broken bodies and hard choices.\r\n\r\nTallec lingered in the doorway. “Next time you hold,” he said to Jhace, “you’ll do it where the Crown tells you.”\r\n\r\nJhace met his eyes. “Next time I hold, it’ll be because something is falling. Not because you want it to stay standing.”\r\n\r\nTallec laughed and walked away.\r\n\r\nThe grid-hum under the floor kept its doubled harmonic.\r\n\r\nThe doubled harmonic kept humming under the floor like a threat that didn’t need words."
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1768415009.412,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "e8326c23-928c-4e5b-8676-33b2408e9d03",

    "e8ba661b-78a7-4030-995d-1e2a16221a0f": {
      "children": [
        "5b5b67fa-9a7d-4c03-a332-69e619a01998"
      ],
      "id": "e8ba661b-78a7-4030-995d-1e2a16221a0f",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "user"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "this was groks version\n## CODA: GLASS IN THE HAND\r\n\r\nJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them.\r\n\r\nHe’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste stayed anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you couldn’t spit out.\r\n\r\nStone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn’t reach. His right hand twitching on its own schedule.\r\n\r\nA guard stood outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive.\r\n\r\nJhace tried to sleep.\r\n\r\nEvery time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn’t tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city’s new baseline living in him.\r\n\r\nHe flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking.\r\n\r\nThen it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place.\r\n\r\nJhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed.\r\n\r\n“I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest.\r\n\r\nOutside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short.\r\n\r\nJhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.\r\n\r\n## CODA: THE SHARD\r\n\r\nJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them.\r\n\r\nStone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn’t lift. A slate he couldn’t reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted time like it was a job.\r\n\r\nA guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who’d been chewing dust since Kindling.\r\n\r\nThen the breathing stopped.\r\n\r\nNo footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint.\r\n\r\nThe door opened anyway.\r\n\r\nLight entered that didn’t know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape.\r\n\r\nA figure stepped in.\r\n\r\nNo perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn’t track dust. Their shadow didn’t arrive.\r\n\r\nThey didn’t speak.\r\n\r\nThey moved with the economy of someone who’d done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel.\r\n\r\nA hand touched Jhace’s eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact.\r\n\r\nJhace tried to jerk away.\r\n\r\nNothing.\r\n\r\nHe tried to curl his right hand into a fist.\r\n\r\nNothing.\r\n\r\nThe figure took that hand anyway.\r\n\r\nBoth of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage.\r\n\r\nSomething cold settled into the center of his palm.\r\n\r\nA shard.\r\n\r\nCopper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn’t give a damn about skin.\r\n\r\nCold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache.\r\n\r\nThen heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for.\r\n\r\nCopper flooded Jhace’s mouth so fast he gagged and couldn’t move enough to spit.\r\n\r\nLight threaded up from his palm.\r\n\r\nNot a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path.\r\n\r\nIt slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal.\r\n\r\nJhace couldn’t feel his legs. Couldn’t feel the sheets. Couldn’t feel the weight of his own body.\r\n\r\nBut he felt the line.\r\n\r\nHe felt the doubled harmonic he’d been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay.\r\n\r\nHis lungs pulled a breath that wasn’t his decision.\r\n\r\nThe figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact.\r\n\r\nThen they stepped back.\r\n\r\nNo check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood.\r\n\r\nThe door shut without sound.\r\n\r\nThe guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed.\r\n\r\nJhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn’t chosen to close.\r\n\r\nThe tremor was gone.\r\n\r\nIn its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise.\r\n\r\nSomewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.\r\n\r\n*END N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS*"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1768418514.144,
        "end_turn": null,

          "text": "# slice chapters\nchap_data=[]\nfor i,m in enumerate(matches):\n    start=m.start()\n    end=matches[i+1].start() if i+1<len(matches) else len(text)\n    chap_text=text[start:end].strip()\n    chap_data.append((int(m.group(1)), m.group(2).strip(), chap_text))\n[(c[0], c[1], len(c[2])) for c in chap_data[:5]]\n"

    "eb76905d-e426-45f5-bcab-f3a139300710": {

        "ed88c0cd-245b-4ea0-a5a6-c760bb04c7ac"
      ],
      "id": "eb76905d-e426-45f5-bcab-f3a139300710",
      "message": {
        "author": {

        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "code",
          "language": "unknown",
          "response_format_name": null,
          "text": "import re\ndef find_word(word):\n    res=[]\n    pat=re.compile(rf\"\\b{re.escape(word)}\\b\", re.IGNORECASE)\n    for i,p in enumerate(doc.paragraphs):\n        if pat.search(p.text):\n            res.append((i,p.text))\n    return res\nfor w in [\"day\",\"days\",\"hour\",\"hours\",\"minute\",\"minutes\",\"second\",\"seconds\",\"today\",\"tomorrow\",\"yesterday\",\"west\",\"east\",\"north\",\"south\",\"degree\",\"degrees\"]:\n    res=find_word(w)\n    if res:\n        print(w, len(res), [i for i,_ in res])\n"
        },
        "create_time": 1768421361.26522,
        "end_turn": false,
        "id": "eb76905d-e426-45f5-bcab-f3a139300710",

    "eb8d1e6b-ed50-4528-9187-6f00c9f097de": {
      "children": [
        "3f5bafad-b473-4a54-a444-5880f859a561"
      ],
      "id": "eb8d1e6b-ed50-4528-9187-6f00c9f097de",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": "final",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "# N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS  \n## CHAPTER 8: THREE TIDES — VARIANT A\n\n---\n\nDaved didn’t wake like people woke.\n\nHe came back in pieces.\n\nA blink that didn’t track. A breath that hitched and then forgot what it was doing. Fingers that twitched once, like the body was testing whether it still owned them.\n\nJhace stood at the foot of the cot and watched all of it happen.\n\nThe room wasn’t a ward. It was a repurposed counting office behind Merchant Hall. Stone walls. Iron brackets. Two rows of cots shoved too close together because “space” was a privilege the city had run out of.\n\nIt smelled like sweat and antiseptic resin and copper you couldn’t wash off.\n\nTiffani sat on an overturned stool with her slate on her knees.\n\nNot writing.\n\nJust holding it there like if she put it down the numbers would crawl off the page and start screaming.\n\nBram paced near the doorway, hands in his hair, then hands at his sides, then hands back in his hair. Like he couldn’t decide what posture made him less useless.\n\nA medic leaned over Daved with a lamp and clean cloth. Not Temple. Not Crown. Just a worker with practiced hands and tired eyes.\n\n“His jaw’s locked,” the medic said.\n\n“He was locked,” Bram muttered, like saying it softer would change what it meant.\n\nThe medic checked Daved’s pulse with two fingers. Counted under their breath.\n\n“Heart’s irregular,” they said. “Breath is shallow. He’s conscious enough to be miserable. Not conscious enough to answer.”\n\nJhace’s hands shook at his sides.\n\nFine vibration. Constant. The kind you could pretend was cold until you tried to hold a tool and it proved you wrong.\n\nHe tasted copper.\n\nNot flood. Not activation. Just the low tang that had taken up residence since the Piercing and refused to pay rent.\n\n“How long,” Jhace said, and hated how small his voice came out, “does this last.”\n\nThe medic’s eyes flicked to Jhace’s tremor, then away like it was impolite to stare at the obvious.\n\n“Three Tides,” they said. “If he’s going to stabilize without secondary failure. If he’s going to breathe on his own without assistance. If his hands are going to remember how to be hands.”\n\n“Three Tides,” Bram echoed. “That’s… manageable. That’s a number.”\n\nThe medic didn’t smile. “It’s a checkpoint. Not a promise.”\n\nTiffani finally looked up. Her face was dust-streaked and blank in the way blankness becomes when it’s holding something back.\n\n“Stage Three,” she said.\n\nThe medic nodded once. “Locked hold is a body event. Not just a light event. People talk like it’s magic. It’s not. It’s stress. It’s collapse inside the ribs.”\n\nJhace stared at Daved’s hands.\n\nThey weren’t shaking.\n\nThey were still.\n\nStillness can be worse than tremor. Tremor means you’re still trying.\n\nDaved’s fingers lay curled into the sheet like he’d been told to hold and his body had obeyed too well.\n\n“He saved them,” Bram said, too fast. Too bright. “He held the last span. He did what we asked.”\n\n“No,” Tiffani said.\n\nHer voice cut the room clean. Not cruelty. Correction.\n\n“We didn’t ask,” she said. “We ordered. And he didn’t stop.”\n\nBram’s mouth tightened. “You want to assign blame, do it later.”\n\n“I’m assigning mechanism,” Tiffani said. “If we can’t say what happened without comforting ourselves, we can’t prevent it.”\n\nJhace’s tremor ticked harder.\n\nHe wanted to tell her to shut up. Wanted to tell her the ledger could wait. Wanted to tell her Daved was still warm and that meant they were allowed to hope.\n\nInstead he said, “What do we do.”\n\nThe medic straightened. “We keep him warm. We keep him breathing. We keep him from trying to hold again.”\n\nBram barked a laugh that died halfway. “Good luck. The bells don’t care about doctor orders.”\n\nThe medic looked at Bram like Bram was a child who kept licking hot iron.\n\n“Then you make it care,” the medic said. “You pull him out of rotation. You put someone else in. You live with that.”\n\nJhace felt something cold settle in his gut.\n\nSomeone else.\n\nAnother name.\n\nAnother set of hands that would shake forever.\n\nTiffani’s pen started moving again.\n\nJhace didn’t look at the slate. He could feel the words forming anyway.\n\n**Daved. Locked. Removed from rotation.**\n\nIt didn’t matter if she wrote it. The city would write it in him.\n\n---\n\nOn the second Tide, Daved could speak.\n\nBarely.\n\nThe medic propped him up against rolled cloth so he wouldn’t choke on his own spit. Daved’s eyes were open now. Flat. Not calm.\n\nJust empty of surprise.\n\nJhace stood close enough to hear without leaning in, because leaning in felt like begging.\n\nDaved’s throat worked. “Did—”\n\nJhace answered before the question finished. “They got out.”\n\nDaved blinked once. Slow.\n\n“Good,” he rasped.\n\nHis voice sounded like dust dragged over stone.\n\nTiffani stood at the foot of the cot, slate in hand, face controlled.\n\n“You locked,” she said.\n\nDaved’s eyes moved to her. “I know.”\n\n“Termination failed,” she said.\n\nDaved’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. Not even bitterness. Just recognition.\n\n“Termination didn’t matter,” he said. “My hands didn’t listen.”\n\nJhace’s tremor surged up his forearm like it wanted to crawl into his shoulder again.\n\nDaved’s gaze flicked to Jhace’s shaking fingers.\n\n“You’re next,” Daved said, voice flat.\n\nJhace swallowed copper. “Don’t.”\n\n“It’s not a threat,” Daved whispered. “It’s math.”\n\nTiffani’s pen scratched.\n\nJhace watched her write and hated it and needed it.\n\nDaved’s eyes closed for two heartbeats. Opened again.\n\n“How many dead,” he asked.\n\nTiffani didn’t answer.\n\nBram did, because Bram couldn’t stand silence when silence meant guilt.\n\n“Three,” Bram said. “Stairwell. Route failure. Not you.”\n\nDaved’s eyes stayed on the ceiling. “Still three.”\n\nJhace breathed out through his nose and tasted copper again, sharper this time.\n\nNot activation.\n\nJust the world reminding him it had teeth.\n\n---\n\nOn the third Tide, Daved tried to sit up without help.\n\nThe attempt looked pathetic and heroic at the same time.\n\nHis abdominal muscles engaged like they were moving through mud. His shoulders shook. His breath went thin.\n\nHe got halfway up before his arms gave out.\n\nJhace stepped forward on instinct to catch him.\n\nDaved’s hand shot out and grabbed Jhace’s sleeve.\n\nNot steady. Not clean.\n\nBut a grip.\n\nAnd it shook.\n\nStage One. Or whatever came after Stage Three when the body crawled back from the edge and found it had been changed.\n\nDaved stared at his own hand like it was an animal he didn’t trust.\n\n“It’s back,” he said.\n\nJhace nodded once. “Yeah.”\n\nDaved’s jaw tightened. “I can’t feel my fingers right.”\n\nThe medic stepped in, checked the grip, checked the skin color, checked the tremor amplitude with eyes trained to read small failures.\n\n“You can feel them enough,” the medic said. “To know they aren’t yours the way they used to be.”\n\nDaved’s breath came out in a short, ugly sound. “I can’t—”\n\n“You can,” the medic said. “Just not what you want.”\n\nJhace watched Daved’s hand tremble against his sleeve.\n\nHe pictured Daved trying to hold a spoon.\n\nTrying to tie a collar knot.\n\nTrying to fasten his own coat.\n\nHe pictured Daved waking every Tide and negotiating with his hands like they were a hostile crew.\n\nThe medic touched Daved’s wrist. “No holds,” they said. “Doctor’s order.”\n\nDaved laughed once. It turned into a cough.\n\n“You write that on a bell?” he rasped.\n\nBram’s voice came rough. “We’ll write it on your face if we have to.”\n\nTiffani didn’t smile.\n\nShe wrote: **Removed from rotation. Non-negotiable.**\n\nJhace looked at her. “You think writing it makes it true.”\n\n“I think writing it makes it enforceable,” Tiffani said.\n\n“By who.”\n\nTiffani’s eyes didn’t move. “By us.”\n\nBram swallowed. “Until someone with a seal decides ‘us’ isn’t official.”\n\nTiffani’s mouth went flat. “Then we’ll be unofficial and alive.”\n\nJhace tasted copper and felt the tremor in his hands pulse like a heartbeat.\n\nDaved watched him.\n\n“Don’t try to be clean about it,” Daved said, quiet. “It’s not clean.”\n\nJhace nodded once.\n\nHe didn’t trust his voice.\n\nHe stared at his hands until the shaking felt like a language he would have to learn.\n\nThree Tides.\n\nCheckpoint. Not promise.\n\nOutside the counting office, the city hummed its doubled rhythm and pretended that was stability.\n\nJhace’s hands shook anyway.\n\n---"
          ]
        },

            "Chapter 8 is now **LOCKED** exactly as pasted.\n\nLocked set is now: **Chapters 1–8**.\n\nCanon additions from your locked Ch8 that are now binding going forward:\n- **Three-Tide recovery frame** is explicit and structured (Tide one eyes, Tide two voice, Tide three motor attempt).\n- Daved’s post-lock return includes **tremor onset** on Tide three + **removed from rotation, non-negotiable**.\n- Tiffani admits the **copper taste persistence** (in teeth; food/water wrong) and explicitly refuses to log it because it’s “not a Stage marker.”\n- Bram’s “piss and heroism” / “shaped wrong” banter is canon tone for aftermath rooms.\n\nNext in base order is **Chapter 9** (the rumor-thread pivot: shard-contact behavior entering the ecosystem).\n\nIf you want **Variant A for Chapter 9**, I’ll draft it to match the locked 1–8 cadence and keep it strictly inside that chapter’s scope (no later-chapter hunger mechanics unless the base chapter contains them)."
          ]
        },

          "text": "# split into sections\nsections=[]\nfor idx,m in enumerate(matches):\n    start=m.start()\n    head=m.group(1).strip()\n    body_start=m.end()\n    end = matches[idx+1].start() if idx+1<len(matches) else len(txt)\n    body=txt[body_start:end].strip(\"\\n\")\n    sections.append((head, body))\nsections[0][1][:300]\n"

          "text": "days 2 [150, 156]\nhour 1 [8]\nhours 5 [4, 15, 27, 58, 114]\nminutes 3 [4, 14, 15]\nsecond 6 [8, 13, 15, 31, 49, 148]\nseconds 2 [15, 27]\ntoday 1 [8]\nwest 1 [8]\ndegrees 1 [8]\n"

        "metadata": {
          "aggregate_result": {
            "code": "import re\ndef find_word(word):\n    res=[]\n    pat=re.compile(rf\"\\b{re.escape(word)}\\b\", re.IGNORECASE)\n    for i,p in enumerate(doc.paragraphs):\n        if pat.search(p.text):\n            res.append((i,p.text))\n    return res\nfor w in [\"day\",\"days\",\"hour\",\"hours\",\"minute\",\"minutes\",\"second\",\"seconds\",\"today\",\"tomorrow\",\"yesterday\",\"west\",\"east\",\"north\",\"south\",\"degree\",\"degrees\"]:\n    res=find_word(w)\n    if res:\n        print(w, len(res), [i for i,_ in res])\n",
            "end_time": 1768421368.9593787,
            "final_expression_output": null,
            "in_kernel_exception": null,

    "ee768109-c3ce-49bb-9330-2b053dfc5c30": {
      "children": [
        "5118b744-3ee4-452e-8e0e-0ec20a69879a"
      ],
      "id": "ee768109-c3ce-49bb-9330-2b053dfc5c30",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "python",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "104 Jhace tried to jerk away.\n105 Nothing.\n106 He tried to curl his right hand into a fist.\n107 Nothing.\n108 The figure took that hand anyway.\n109 Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage.\n110 Something cold settled into the center of his palm.\n111 A shard.\n112 Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin.\n113 Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache.\n114 Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for.\n115 Copper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit.\n116 Light threaded up from his palm.\n117 Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path.\n118 It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal.\n119 Jhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body.\n120 But he felt the line.\n121 He felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay.\n122 His lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision.\n"
        },
        "create_time": 1768417241.4434967,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "ee768109-c3ce-49bb-9330-2b053dfc5c30",
        "metadata": {
          "aggregate_result": {
            "code": "for i in range(104,123):\n    print(i, paras[i])\n",

            "final_expression_output": null,
            "in_kernel_exception": null,

    "eea30fd4-d53e-49c8-aa0a-b141f8803a56": {
      "children": [
        "f1b8adf5-909d-45de-be00-a4b96803445e"
      ],
      "id": "eea30fd4-d53e-49c8-aa0a-b141f8803a56",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "python",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "('Jhace could still see. Still hear. Still count.\\nHe stared at his hands, waiting for tremor.\\nThere was no tremor.\\nThere was nothing.\\nHe tried to will a finger. Nothing.\\nPanic hit—animal, immediate, nothing to do with heat or holding.\\nHe tried to shout. Nothing left his throat.\\nHe tried to jerk a shoulder. Nothing.\\nHe tried to blink faster—signal—anything.\\nHis lids moved on their own schedule. Slow. Unbothered.\\nThe body was there. The controls were there. The link was gone.\\nThe coal behind his sternum pulsed once. Steady. Patient. Still ready.\\nReady to hold.\\nBut he couldn’t lift a finger to tell it no.\\nOnly the wanting stayed—locked in meat that wouldn’t answer.\\n\\nTiffani moved into his field of view and knelt like she was inspecting a fracture line. Ink and copper sat on her hands; she smelled like slate dust and hours without sleep.\\n“Blink once for yes,” she said. “Twice for no.”\\nHe blinked once.\\n“Do you still feel the doubled harmonic through the stone?”\\nOnce.\\n“Can you tell me where it',\n \" sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body.\\nBut he felt the line.\\nHe felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for days arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay.\\nHis lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision.\\nThe figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact.\\nThen they stepped back.\\nNo check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood.\\nThe door shut without sound.\",\n (841, 1025, 185))"
        },
        "create_time": 1768401488.8055823,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "eea30fd4-d53e-49c8-aa0a-b141f8803a56",
        "metadata": {
          "aggregate_result": {
            "code": "start=841; end=1025\nexcerpt = \"\\n\".join(lines[start-1:end])\nexcerpt[:1000], excerpt[-500:], (start, end, len(excerpt.splitlines()))",
            "end_time": 1768401491.1634064,

    "eebbeafd-1ec4-497e-abc3-ba837b080670": {
      "children": [
        "43038b4f-6f0e-4dee-8e5e-c9eca3581737"
      ],
      "id": "eebbeafd-1ec4-497e-abc3-ba837b080670",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "python",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "CHAPTER 11: HUNGER\nThe hunger wasn’t metaphor.\nIt lived behind Jhace’s sternum as a warmth that never fully left after the first hold. A phantom heat that flared whenever the grid‑hum dipped, whenever dust fell, whenever someone screamed the word hold like it was prayer.\nTiffani called it activation pressure.\nJhace called it wanting.\nHe sat beside Daved’s cot in the infirmary again, watching the older man’s hands twitch in sleep.\nDaved’s eyes opened. He didn’t need a slate this time. His voice was still rough, but it existed.\n“Feels… good,” Daved rasped.\nJhace frowned. “What does?”\n“The start,” Daved said. “Chest heat. When it locks in. For a heartbeat you feel… useful. Strong. Like the city can’t touch you.”\nJhace’s tremor worsened, like his body agreed.\n“And then,” Daved continued, eyes flat, “it takes you apart.”\nTiffani wrote without looking up. “Addictive response mixed with autonomic threat. Noted.”\nJhace frowned. “What does.”\n“The reinforcement loop,” Tiffani said. “Chest heat, perceived control, reduced panic during manifestation. It becomes desire.”\nDaved’s eyes narrowed. “Liar.”\nJhace’s head snapped. “What.”\n“You want it,” Daved said. “The hold. You want to feel it again.”\n“I don’t want to chase the rumor.”\n“You want it anyway.”\nJhace didn’t answer. His tremor did.\n“That want will kill you faster than the hold,” Daved said.\n“He is correct,” Tiffani said. “The want is not evidence. It is impulse.”\nJhace’s voice went raw. “I want to hold without paying. I want to save every time the bell rings and not come apart.”\n“Hungry hands hold until they break,” Daved said.\n“Then we do not feed it,” Tiffani said. “We constrain it with protocol.”\n“Protocol doesn’t stop bells,” Jhace said.\n“No,” Tiffani said. “It stops stupidity.”\nDaved’s mouth twitched. “Sometimes. Not always.”\n“Then we decide what we sacrifice,” Tiffani said. “The Crown can wait. The Temple can wait. The Guild can wait.”\nJhace’s gaze stayed forward. “We decide.”\n“And when you decide wrong?” Daved asked.\n“We document it,” Tiffani said. “We learn. We don’t repeat.”\n“Some mistakes don’t give you time to learn,” Daved said.\n\nOutside, a bell rang—different cadence. Not collapse. A drill signal. Crown‑mandated training.\nJhace’s chest heat flared anyway, eager and stupid.\nHe hated himself for that flare.\nHe stood, hands shaking, and went to the training yard because refusal would make them criminals.\nTiffani followed, slate in hand, limping slightly from too many hours on stone floors.\nDaved watched them go and muttered, almost too quiet to hear:\n“Hungry hands hold until they break.”\nJhace pretended he didn’t hear.\nHe did.\n---\n"
        },
        "create_time": 1768415279.1030884,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "eebbeafd-1ec4-497e-abc3-ba837b080670",

    "ef6528b6-b126-4115-91b7-09a219bfafcb": {
      "children": [
        "15a89baa-0404-4d37-8dba-26ede55440fc"
      ],
      "id": "ef6528b6-b126-4115-91b7-09a219bfafcb",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "user"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "your version or this one?\n## CODA\r\n\r\nJhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them.\r\n\r\nHe’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste stayed anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you couldn’t spit out.\r\n\r\nStone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn’t reach. A tin cup on the side table, out of range. His right hand twitching on its own schedule.\r\n\r\nA guard stood outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive.\r\n\r\nJhace tried to sleep.\r\n\r\nEvery time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn’t tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city’s new baseline living in him.\r\n\r\nHe flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking.\r\n\r\nThen it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place.\r\n\r\nJhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed.\r\n\r\n“I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest.\r\n\r\nOutside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short.\r\n\r\nJhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.\r\n\r\nThe breathing stopped.\r\n\r\nNo footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint.\r\n\r\nThe door opened anyway.\r\n\r\nLight entered that didn’t know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape.\r\n\r\nA figure stepped in.\r\n\r\nNo perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn’t track dust. Their shadow didn’t arrive.\r\n\r\nThey didn’t speak.\r\n\r\nThey moved with the economy of someone who’d done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel.\r\n\r\nA hand touched Jhace’s eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact.\r\n\r\nJhace tried to jerk away.\r\n\r\nNothing.\r\n\r\nHe tried to curl his right hand into a fist.\r\n\r\nNothing.\r\n\r\nThe figure took that hand anyway.\r\n\r\nBoth of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage.\r\n\r\nSomething cold settled into the center of his palm.\r\n\r\nA shard.\r\n\r\nCopper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn’t give a damn about skin.\r\n\r\nCold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache.\r\n\r\nThen heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for.\r\n\r\nCopper flooded Jhace’s mouth so fast he gagged and couldn’t move enough to spit.\r\n\r\nLight threaded up from his palm.\r\n\r\nNot a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path.\r\n\r\nIt slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal.\r\n\r\nJhace couldn’t feel his legs. Couldn’t feel the sheets. Couldn’t feel the weight of his own body.\r\n\r\nBut he felt the line.\r\n\r\nHe felt the doubled harmonic he’d been hearing in stone since Kindling arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay.\r\n\r\nHis lungs pulled a breath that wasn’t his decision.\r\n\r\nThe figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact.\r\n\r\nThen they stepped back.\r\n\r\nNo check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood.\r\n\r\nThe door shut without sound.\r\n\r\nThe guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed.\r\n\r\nJhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn’t chosen to close.\r\n\r\nThe tremor was gone.\r\n\r\nIn its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise.\r\n\r\nSomewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.\r\n\r\n*END N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS*"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1768418812.082,
        "end_turn": null,

    "f0256b6b-924c-4329-bb5a-52c83300effa": {
      "children": [
        "2a33d39a-4149-4b0a-8f25-dbd8dc7fc9a0"
      ],
      "id": "f0256b6b-924c-4329-bb5a-52c83300effa",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "python",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "“Go,” the woman shouted toward the breach. “Last sweep. Now.” A rescuer crawled under the paused beam line and dragged a small body out by the shoulders. The child coughed dust and cried like she’d been holding it in for hours. The woman’s voice cut clean through the chaos. “Twelve out. Corridor clear.” Jhace felt the building try to move again the instant she said it, like it had been waiting for permission. “Terminate,” she said. No softness in it. Not cruelty. Protocol. Jhace let the filament go. The building settled the last inch it had been denied. Stone grinding. Iron squealing. But the void where bodies had been was now empty. He staggered back into open air. His hands wouldn’t stop shaking. The tremor was small—millimeters—but it was relentless. Like a ghost living inside his muscles, counting the dead he’d prevented and refusing to be quiet about the cost. The woman stepped beside him, dust streaked across her face. “Name.” “Jhace Torrins.” She wrote it without looking, chalk skating on sweat. “Duration: two‑forty—” The chalk snapped. She swore under her breath and wrote it anyway. “Don’t,” he snapped. Too hard. Too fast. She met his eyes. Her gaze was flat, measuring, absolutely certain. “If we don’t measure it,” she said, “we kill you next time.” Between bells, they made it back to the workshop long enough to rinse dust off skin and reset slates. Jhace sat at his bench and waited for the chest heat to fade. It didn’t. Not fully. A banked warmth stayed behind his sternum—steady as a coal bed that refused to go cold. He pressed his palm to his chest. Felt nothing through shirt and ribs. Inside, the warmth answered anyway. Once. Twice. Like the world remembering how it had fit together when he held. His hand lingered there a heartbeat too long. Not thinking. Just reaching. He pulled it away like it burned. The tremor kept moving, but for one heartbeat—only one—the shake eased, like his body preferred the burn to the break. That was the first time the hunger felt like a vote. Jhace’s hands shook between them like evidence he hadn’t wanted. He swallowed copper. Tasted grit. Looked at the child being carried past them on a makeshift stretcher—alive, crying, covered in dust but breathing. He nodded once. “Measure,” he said. “Then make sure there isn’t a next time.” The woman’s slate scratched. She wrote: Jhace Torrins. Inspector. First manifestation. Tallow Street. Tide 0. Behind them, the bell rang again. Different street. Same dust in his teeth. Same bell tearing its own throat. Jhace looked down and realized he was still gripping air. Copper sat on his tongue. He went anyway.\n6 \n7 CHAPTER 2: THE PATTERN\n8 The theodolite wouldn’t settle. Tiffani had calibrated it three times in the last hour. Clean lens. Level base. Reference stars locked. Everything aligned the way it had been for twenty-six Turns of lens work. The crosshair still drifted. No shimmer. No warp. Just the star sitting wrong in the frame—like someone had nudged the sky with a thumb while she wasn’t looking. She pressed her palm to the primary lens frame. Vibration. Faint. Constant. The doubled harmonic lived under the normal hum like a second heartbeat. It made her molars ache and her lens hand tremble. She pulled her hand away and wrote on her slate: Tide 0 — Kindling\nLens frame vibration: doubled harmonic present.\nStar Seven-Three displacement: 0.34 degrees west.\nNeither atmosphere nor mechanism. Her mouth tasted copper. She’d been ignoring it for the last two Tides. Faint metallic tang that came and went with air currents from the ventilation shafts. But this was different. This arrived sharp and immediate, flooding her tongue like she’d been chewing wire. The light outside the lens port changed. The light didn’t dim or brighten. It bent. Tiffani looked up. For a second she couldn’t move. Her brain offered one sentence, raw and useless: What the fuck is that? The sky over Saint Merro wasn’t supposed to do anything except hold weather and stars. Today it held something else. A line. Straight. Dark. Moving too fast for her eyes to track at first, then slowing enough for her to understand what she was seeing. A spindle. Long and narrow and wrong, like someone had driven a forged bar through the world and walked away. Her breath stopped. For a stupid second, she couldn’t make her fingers obey. She stood there with her hand half-raised, as if she could physically stop the sky from doing whatever it was doing.\n9 The spindle crossed her view in a single heartbeat. No friction fire. No sound. Just a displacement of light that made the air look thin. Then gravity lurched. Not a shift sideways. An up-and-down stagger, like the planet had been grabbed and shaken once. Her slate slid across the stone desk. A lens clamp snapped. Glass sang a sharp, stressed note and went silent. Tiffani caught herself on the observation frame and forced her eyes back to the port. The spindle was gone. Punched through. Somewhere below. Into stone or ocean or the Underface—she couldn’t tell. Pressure arrived a breath later. Not a blast. More like the city inhaling and forgetting how to exhale. Dust fell from the ceiling mortar in a slow, dry drift. Then copper-gold particulate began falling outside. Glittering. Fine. Catching the lamplight like ground glass in a furnace beam. It coated the stone lip of the observation port and clung to the iron frame in flecks that shimmered when she moved. Tiffani touched it with her fingertip. It didn’t smear like ash. It gritted. Abrasive. Her finger tingled where the dust touched skin. She wrote: Atmospheric intrusion event. Spindle-form object. Estimated velocity: extreme.\nCopper-gold particulate fall: immediate post-impact.\nAdheres to metal preferentially. Abrasive. Conductive? Her pencil slipped. The last word smeared into a dark streak. She stared at it, angry at her own hand, then rewrote the line harder than necessary.\n10 A bell rang. Not the Glass-House alert. Distant. Down-terrace. The frantic peal of something already falling. Then another bell. Different direction. Same cadence. Emergency. Emergency. Emergency. Tiffani’s stomach tightened. She grabbed her slate, shoved it into her coat pocket, and ran. The doubled harmonic followed her down the Glass-House stairs. She could feel it in the stone railing—vibration that hadn’t been there this morning. The walls themselves were listening to something new. At the mid-terrace landing, she stopped. Tallow Street was visible from here: upper facade collapsed inward, dust cloud thick enough to choke on. People running. Voices screaming coordinates she couldn’t parse. A third bell rang. Sector Nine. Grain district. Closer—close enough that the air changed when the facade flexed. Tiffani made her choice the way she always did: follow the data to its source. She ran toward Sector Nine. The copper taste got stronger with every stride.\n11 \n12 CHAPTER 3: SECOND HOLD\n13 The grain warehouse sat in Sector Nine, where the terraces were newer and the beams were honest enough to still pretend they could be trusted. The collapse wasn’t honest. Support columns had sheared at the base—clean breaks that didn’t match load failure. The upper floor sagged like a shelf someone had stepped on. Dust poured from the breach in thick, choking waves. Jhace stood three strides from the entrance, hands shaking so hard he had to grip his belt to keep them still. He’d been here five minutes. His boot skated on spilled grain. He caught himself on instinct, palm slapping the wall hard enough to sting. Too close. Too human.\n14 The tremor from Tallow Street hadn’t stopped. It had spread from his hands into his forearms, up to his shoulders. A fine, relentless vibration that made every tool on his belt rattle. The heat in his chest was still there. Banked. Waiting. A foreman ran past him toward the breach, saw his Crown inspector marks, and grabbed his shoulder hard enough to bruise. “You held Tallow,” the foreman said. “Do it again.” Jhace pulled free. “I don’t—” “There’s forty people in there,” the foreman cut in. “Maybe more. Wedges won’t hold. Straps won’t reach. You’re what we’ve got.” The copper taste flooded Jhace’s mouth again. His chest heat flared. The doubled harmonic he’d felt in the grid-hum arrived inside him—two mis-timed pulses locking into the same rhythm. His heartbeat and the failing building’s stress pattern aligned like they were the same system. He didn’t want this. He didn’t understand this. He moved because the archway dust was already falling in steady sheets and somebody inside was screaming a name he couldn’t ignore. “Clear the approach,” Jhace said, voice rough. “Get spotters. I need someone counting.” He stepped under the sagging upper floor. The heat detonated behind his sternum. He put both hands on the nearest support column. Filament-light threaded from his palms into the stone—brighter this time, thicker, like the copper-gold dust in the air was feeding it. The warehouse stopped settling. The groan of stressed iron went quiet. Jhace’s breath shook. He could feel the load distribution through his fingertips: uneven, shifting, trying to collapse in three directions at once. His hands tightened. The filament brightened in response. The tremor in his arms worsened. Not fine anymore. Visible. His whole body shaking with the effort of holding something that should have already fallen. “Move!” someone shouted. “Get them out!” Boots ran. Voices called coordinates. Bodies crawled through gaps in the rubble. A woman appeared beside him—practical coat, slate already open, hair tied back with a strip of cloth. She looked at the filament-light threading from his hands. Looked at his face. Looked at the tremor shaking his shoulders. “You’re the Tallow Street hold,” she said. Not a question. A confirmation. “Yeah,” Jhace managed. “How long have you been holding?” “I don’t know. Five minutes? Ten?” Her pen scratched. “Tremor amplitude increased since first manifestation. Cost progression visible.” “What?” Jhace’s vision narrowed at the edges. Copper-gold sparks flickered across it. “Your body is paying for this,” she said, still writing even as her thumb cramped. “If it locks past tolerance, you won’t be able to let go.” “I can’t let go,” Jhace said through clenched teeth. “Not until they’re out.” The woman looked at the evacuation line. Counted bodies being carried past. “Twenty-three so far. Estimate forty total inside.” “Then I hold for forty.” “Then you might not walk away.” Jhace’s hands shook harder. The heat in his chest flared so bright it hurt. “I already know that.” The woman’s jaw tightened. She moved to where he could see her without turning his head and held up her slate like a mirror. “I’m documenting this,” she said. “Duration. Cost markers. Termination threshold. If we don’t measure it, the next person who tries this dies without knowing why.” “What’s your name?” Jhace asked. “Tiffani Merrow. Glass-House observatory.” “Jhace Torrins.” “I know,” Tiffani said. “I wrote it down at Tallow Street.” She showed him her slate: Jhace Torrins. Inspector. First manifestation. Duration: 240 heartbeats. Cost: tremor onset, progressive. Second manifestation. Sector Nine warehouse. Onset: immediate. Tremor amplitude: increased. Copper-gold filament visible, enhanced density. Jhace stared at the notes. At his own name written like data. “What caused this?” he asked. Tiffani’s pen paused. “Something punched through the sky two hours ago. Spindle. Metallic. Fast enough to ignore friction. Left this—” She held up her fingertip. Copper-gold grit caught the lamplight. “—everywhere.” Jhace looked at the dust swirling around his boots. At the filament threading from his hands. At the way the copper-gold particles moved toward him like they recognized him. “The doubled harmonic,” he said. “You felt it too?” Tiffani’s eyes sharpened. “In the grid-hum. Two Tides before this. Wrong frequency. Made my teeth itch.” Tiffani wrote: Doubled harmonic: confirmed across multiple observers. Grid infrastructure + atmospheric intrusion event = correlation. A rescue crew leader shouted. “Thirty-eight out. Two still inside. Back corner. Pinned.” Jhace’s vision blurred. The heat in his chest spiked. “How long have I been holding?” he rasped. Tiffani checked a small timepiece on her belt. “Eighteen minutes.” “Tallow Street was four.” “Your cost is accelerating,” Tiffani said. She wiped a nosebleed with the back of her wrist like it was nothing. “Tremor spread. Vision blur. This isn’t sustainable.” Jhace tasted blood before she finished the sentence. Copper-warm, running over his lip. “Terminate after extraction,” Tiffani said. “Not before. But the instant they’re clear, you let go.” “What if I can’t?” “Then I make you.” The last two bodies came out on stretchers. Alive. Barely. “Clear!” the crew leader yelled. Tiffani’s hand closed on Jhace’s shoulder—hard enough to hurt, grounding enough to feel real. “Terminate,” she said. “Now.” Jhace let the filament go. The warehouse settled the last inch it had been denied. Stone grinding. Iron squealing. A beam snapped somewhere in the back, but the void where people had been was empty. Jhace staggered back. His legs didn’t hold. He hit the ground on his knees, hands still shaking, blood dripping from his nose onto the dust-covered stone. Tiffani crouched beside him, slate open, pen moving. Second manifestation terminated. Duration: 19 minutes, 40 seconds. Cost: tremor progression (shoulders, torso), vision impairment (peripheral narrowing, copper-gold visual artifacts), epistaxis (nosebleed), motor control compromise (collapse post-termination). Survival: confirmed. Function: degraded. Jhace wiped blood from his mouth with a shaking hand. “You write everything like it’s going to behave.” “Everything does behave,” Tiffani said. “Even this. It has markers. Progression. Termination criteria.” “It’s killing me in increments.” “Yes,” Tiffani said, and the word came out harsher than she meant. “So we track it before it kills you. We don’t guess. We don’t pray.” Jhace looked at her slate. At the notes written in clean, precise handwriting. At his own degradation catalogued like data. “Why are you doing this?” he asked. Tiffani met his eyes. Hers were sharp, clear, absolutely certain. “Because the city is breaking faster than crews can fix it,” she said. “Because people like you are going to keep holding until they die. Because if we don’t measure the cost, the next bell will kill someone without them knowing why.” A bell rang in the distance. Fourth collapse. Different sector. The copper taste surged in Jhace’s mouth. The heat in his chest flared. Tiffani wrote it down because not writing felt like lying: Third manifestation probable. Autonomic response. Cost accumulating. She looked at Jhace. “Can you stand?” “I don’t know.” “Try.” Jhace pushed himself to his feet. His legs shook. His hands wouldn’t stop trembling. But he stood. “Good,” Tiffani said. “Because we’re going to the next one. And I’m counting every heartbeat.” She walked toward the sound of the bell. Jhace followed. Tiffani wiped chalk off her thumb. It smeared into the sweat. The bell kept ringing.\n15 \n16 CHAPTER 4: PROTOCOL\n17 Jhace’s workshop was a box of iron and heat tucked into the mid‑terrace, where you could hear the grid‑hum without being crushed by it. Tiffani sat on an overturned crate with her slate on her knees, left leg extended slightly to ease the knee that had started locking after long stands on stone floors. She didn’t rest her back against anything. Always ready to move. Always ready to write. Tiffani shifted her weight off her left leg without comment, like it was just another measurement she refused to log. Jhace stood at his bench, trying to thread a bolt into a collar bracket. His hands shook. The grid‑hum didn’t break with a bang. It stuttered—missing beats, catching up—like an old machine slipping teeth. He tried again. The bolt dropped. Metal on stone. He stared at it like it had betrayed him. Tiffani didn’t look up. His left hand tried to wipe sweat off his mouth and only smeared dust across his lip. He tasted grit. He hated the fact he noticed it. “Your tremor amplitude increased during exertion.” “Because I’m exerting,” Jhace muttered, bending to pick up the bolt. His fingertips felt numb where the filament‑light had burned them. Not pain. Absence. “Because your body is remembering,” Tiffani corrected. “It thinks you’re still holding.” Jhace set the bolt down and flexed his hand hard. The tremor didn’t care. He hated that. He hated that his own muscles had become unreliable. “What did you call it out there?” he asked. Tiffani flipped her slate so he could see. She’d drawn a crude timeline. Activation onset: chest heat + copper taste\nLight manifestation: filament contact‑dependent\nTremor: post‑termination, permanent marker “I need a taxonomy,” she said. “Stages. Because right now it’s just stories.” Jhace wiped his palms on a work cloth. “And you think writing stages keeps people alive.” “I think it keeps them from lying to themselves.” She tapped the line where she’d written permanent. “You can’t call it temporary. You can’t bargain with it.” Jhace’s jaw worked. He had bargained with Junction Seven. He had told himself the report would matter. That somebody else would fix it. That the city didn’t need him to fight every foreman with a schedule board. He looked at his shaking hands. “Stage One,” he said, voice low. “Tremor.” Tiffani nodded and wrote: Stage One: tremor + filament residue. Functional. Marked. She stared at the word Functional, then drew a hard line through it and rewrote: Operational. “Functional makes it sound polite,” she muttered.\n18 A knock hit the workshop door. Bram Tallow leaned in like he was checking whether the room was still allowed to exist. Dust on his boots, sweat at his collar, grin already loaded. “Heard you two grabbed Tallow Street by the throat,” Bram said, trying for funny and getting dust instead. He coughed once. “City tries to crush you, you crush back. Very… romantic. Very dumb.” Jhace didn’t look up. “Who’s asking?” “Crown’s asking questions,” Bram said, and somehow still made it sound like a dirty joke. “Not because they care. Because they hate unknown tools walking around without a leash and a receipt.” Tiffani’s pen paused. “What questions.” “Names. Locations. ‘Verified capability.’ That sort of foreplay,” Bram said. He stepped fully inside, then stopped like the heat-grid might charge him rent. “Infrastructure office. Some administrator with hands so clean it makes you wonder what he’s washing off. Loves forms. Loves categories. Loves pretending paper is stronger than stone.” Jhace’s jaw tightened. “To do what.” Bram spread his hands. “To decide who gets to own you. Temple will call it divine. Guild will call it labor. Crown will call it law. Everybody wants a piece. Congratulations—you’re popular.” “We don’t belong to any of them,” Tiffani said. Bram laughed, quick and wrong. “Adorable. Tell that to a stamp. Try explaining ‘we don’t belong’ while a ceiling is coming down and someone’s screaming for a hold. They’ll file you under ‘non-compliant’ and act like that’s a weather condition.” Jhace’s tremor ticked in his fingers. “So you’re here to warn us.” “I’m here to tell you the door is already being measured,” Bram said. “I can buy you a Tide or two. Maybe. After that, they’ll show up with paperwork and smiles and start deciding which parts of you are ‘public infrastructure.’” Tiffani’s mouth went flat. “Then we build methodology fast.” Bram pointed at her slate like it offended him personally. “Yeah. Before they build it for you. Because if the Crown writes your protocol, it’s going to read like a job description. And you won’t like the benefits package.” Jhace breathed in, tasted copper‑gold dust that still clung to his clothes, and felt the city’s hum through the floorboards. “Then we build it fast,” he said. “Before they decide to build it for us.”\n19 \n"
        },

                "text": "“Go,” the woman shouted toward the breach. “Last sweep. Now.” A rescuer crawled under the paused beam line and dragged a small body out by the shoulders. The child coughed dust and cried like she’d been holding it in for hours. The woman’s voice cut clean through the chaos. “Twelve out. Corridor clear.” Jhace felt the building try to move again the instant she said it, like it had been waiting for permission. “Terminate,” she said. No softness in it. Not cruelty. Protocol. Jhace let the filament go. The building settled the last inch it had been denied. Stone grinding. Iron squealing. But the void where bodies had been was now empty. He staggered back into open air. His hands wouldn’t stop shaking. The tremor was small—millimeters—but it was relentless. Like a ghost living inside his muscles, counting the dead he’d prevented and refusing to be quiet about the cost. The woman stepped beside him, dust streaked across her face. “Name.” “Jhace Torrins.” She wrote it without looking, chalk skating on sweat. “Duration: two‑forty—” The chalk snapped. She swore under her breath and wrote it anyway. “Don’t,” he snapped. Too hard. Too fast. She met his eyes. Her gaze was flat, measuring, absolutely certain. “If we don’t measure it,” she said, “we kill you next time.” Between bells, they made it back to the workshop long enough to rinse dust off skin and reset slates. Jhace sat at his bench and waited for the chest heat to fade. It didn’t. Not fully. A banked warmth stayed behind his sternum—steady as a coal bed that refused to go cold. He pressed his palm to his chest. Felt nothing through shirt and ribs. Inside, the warmth answered anyway. Once. Twice. Like the world remembering how it had fit together when he held. His hand lingered there a heartbeat too long. Not thinking. Just reaching. He pulled it away like it burned. The tremor kept moving, but for one heartbeat—only one—the shake eased, like his body preferred the burn to the break. That was the first time the hunger felt like a vote. Jhace’s hands shook between them like evidence he hadn’t wanted. He swallowed copper. Tasted grit. Looked at the child being carried past them on a makeshift stretcher—alive, crying, covered in dust but breathing. He nodded once. “Measure,” he said. “Then make sure there isn’t a next time.” The woman’s slate scratched. She wrote: Jhace Torrins. Inspector. First manifestation. Tallow Street. Tide 0. Behind them, the bell rang again. Different street. Same dust in his teeth. Same bell tearing its own throat. Jhace looked down and realized he was still gripping air. Copper sat on his tongue. Wrong frequency. Made my teeth itch.” Tiffani wrote: Doubled harmonic: confirmed across multiple observers. Grid infrastructure + atmospheric intrusion event = correlation. A rescue crew leader shouted. “Thirty-eight out. Two still inside. Back corner. Pinned.” Jhace’s vision blurred. The heat in his chest spiked. “How long have I been holding?” he rasped. Tiffani checked a small timepiece on her belt. “Eighteen minutes.” “Tallow Street was four.” “Your cost is accelerating,” Tiffani said. She wiped a nosebleed with the back of her wrist like it was nothing. “Tremor spread. Vision blur. This isn’t sustainable.” Jhace tasted blood before she finished the sentence. Copper-warm, running over his lip. “Terminate after extraction,” Tiffani said. “Not before. But the instant they’re clear, you let go.” “What if I can’t?” “Then I make you.” The last two bodies came out on stretchers. Alive. Barely. “Clear!” the crew leader yelled. Tiffani’s hand closed on Jhace’s shoulder—hard enough to hurt, grounding enough to feel real. “Terminate,” she said. “Now.” Jhace let the filament go. The warehouse settled the last inch it had been denied. Stone grinding. Iron squealing. A beam snapped somewhere in the back, but the void where people had been was empty. Jhace staggered back. His legs didn’t hold. He hit the ground on his knees, hands still shaking, blood dripping from his nose onto the dust-covered stone. Tiffani crouched beside him, slate open, pen moving. Second manifestation terminated. Duration: 19 minutes, 40 seconds. Cost: tremor progression (shoulders, torso), vision impairment (peripheral narrowing, copper-gold visual artifacts), epistaxis (nosebleed), motor control compromise (collapse post-termination). Survival: confirmed. Function: degraded. Jhace wiped blood from his mouth with a shaking hand. “You write everything like it’s going to behave.” “Everything does behave,” Tiffani said. “Even this. It has markers. Progression. Termination criteria.” “It’s killing me in increments.” “Yes,” Tiffani said, and the word came out harsher than she meant. “So we track it before it kills you. We don’t guess. We don’t pray.” Jhace looked at her slate. At the notes written in clean, precise handwriting. At his own degradation catalogued like data. “Why are you doing this?” he asked. Tiffani met his eyes. Hers were sharp, clear, absolutely certain. “Because the city is breaking faster than crews can fix it,” she said. “Because people like you are going to keep holding until they die. Because if we don’t measure the cost, the next bell will kill someone without them knowing why.” A bell rang in the distance. Fourth collapse. Different sector. The copper taste surged in Jhace’s mouth. The heat in his chest flared. Tiffani wrote it down because not writing felt like lying: Third manifestation probable. Autonomic response. Cost accumulating. She looked at Jhace. “Can you stand?” “I don’t know.” “Try.” Jhace pushed himself to his feet. His legs shook. His hands wouldn’t stop trembling. But he stood. “Good,” Tiffani said. “Because we’re going to the next one. And I’m counting every heartbeat.” She walked toward the sound of the bell. Jhace followed. Tiffani wiped chalk off her thumb. It smeared into the sweat. The bell kept ringing.\n15 \n16 CHAPTER 4: PROTOCOL\n17 Jhace’s workshop was a box of iron and heat tucked into the mid‑terrace, where you could hear the grid‑hum without being crushed by it. Tiffani sat on an overturned crate with her slate on her knees, left leg extended slightly to ease the knee that had started locking after long stands on stone floors. She didn’t rest her back against anything. Always ready to move. Always ready to write. Tiffani shifted her weight off her left leg without comment, like it was just another measurement she refused to log. Jhace stood at his bench, trying to thread a bolt into a collar bracket. His hands shook. The grid‑hum didn’t break with a bang. It stuttered—missing beats, catching up—like an old machine slipping teeth. He tried again. The bolt dropped. Metal on stone. He stared at it like it had betrayed him. Tiffani didn’t look up. His left hand tried to wipe sweat off his mouth and only smeared dust across his lip. He tasted grit. He hated the fact he noticed it. “Your tremor amplitude increased during exertion.” “Because I’m exerting,” Jhace muttered, bending to pick up the bolt. His fingertips felt numb where the filament‑light had burned them. Not pain. Absence. “Because your body is remembering,” Tiffani corrected. “It thinks you’re still holding.” Jhace set the bolt down and flexed his hand hard. The tremor didn’t care. He hated that. He hated that his own muscles had become unreliable. “What did you call it out there?” he asked. Tiffani flipped her slate so he could see. She’d drawn a crude timeline. Activation onset: chest heat + copper taste\nLight manifestation: filament contact‑dependent\nTremor: post‑termination, permanent marker “I need a taxonomy,” she said. “Stages. Because right now it’s just stories.” Jhace wiped his palms on a work cloth. “And you think writing stages keeps people alive.” “I think it keeps them from lying to themselves.” She tapped the line where she’d written permanent. “You can’t call it temporary. You can’t bargain with it.” Jhace’s jaw worked. He had bargained with Junction Seven. He had told himself the report would matter. That somebody else would fix it. That the city didn’t need him to fight every foreman with a schedule board. He looked at his shaking hands. “Stage One,” he said, voice low. “Tremor.” Tiffani nodded and wrote: Stage One: tremor + filament residue. Functional. Marked. She stared at the word Functional, then drew a hard line through it and rewrote: Operational. “Functional makes it sound polite,” she muttered.\n18 A knock hit the workshop door. Bram Tallow leaned in like he was checking whether the room was still allowed to exist. Dust on his boots, sweat at his collar, grin already loaded. “Heard you two grabbed Tallow Street by the throat,” Bram said, trying for funny and getting dust instead. He coughed once. “City tries to crush you, you crush back. Very… romantic. Very dumb.” Jhace didn’t look up. “Who’s asking?” “Crown’s asking questions,” Bram said, and somehow still made it sound like a dirty joke. “Not because they care. Because they hate unknown tools walking around without a leash and a receipt.” Tiffani’s pen paused. “What questions.” “Names. Locations. ‘Verified capability.’ That sort of foreplay,” Bram said. He stepped fully inside, then stopped like the heat-grid might charge him rent. “Infrastructure office. Some administrator with hands so clean it makes you wonder what he’s washing off. Loves forms. Loves categories. Loves pretending paper is stronger than stone.” Jhace’s jaw tightened. “To do what.” Bram spread his hands. “To decide who gets to own you. Temple will call it divine. Guild will call it labor. Crown will call it law. Everybody wants a piece. Congratulations—you’re popular.” “We don’t belong to any of them,” Tiffani said. Bram laughed, quick and wrong. “Adorable. Tell that to a stamp. Try explaining ‘we don’t belong’ while a ceiling is coming down and someone’s screaming for a hold. They’ll file you under ‘non-compliant’ and act like that’s a weather condition.” Jhace’s tremor ticked in his fingers. “So you’re here to warn us.” “I’m here to tell you the door is already being measured,” Bram said. “I can buy you a Tide or two. Maybe. After that, they’ll show up with paperwork and smiles and start deciding which parts of you are ‘public infrastructure.’” Tiffani’s mouth went flat. “Then we build methodology fast.” Bram pointed at her slate like it offended him personally. “Yeah. Before they build it for you. Because if the Crown writes your protocol, it’s going to read like a job description. And you won’t like the benefits package.” Jhace breathed in, tasted copper‑gold dust that still clung to his clothes, and felt the city’s hum through the floorboards. “Then we build it fast,” he said. “Before they decide to build it for us.”\n19 \n",
                "time": 1768420858.4294448
              }

    "f05d684e-3493-42c0-88fb-bde4b891deed": {
      "children": [
        "a9e87938-77af-4e52-b9f4-88c869e8ff1a"
      ],
      "id": "f05d684e-3493-42c0-88fb-bde4b891deed",
      "message": {
        "author": {

        "channel": "final",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "Chapter 4 re-locked as pasted. Continuity now matches base manuscript placement: **Protocol ends before the first “field-test” chapter.**\n\n## CHAPTER 5: FIRST NETWORK\n\nThe kiln-quarter feeder gantry sat in Sector Fourteen, where the pipes ran hot and the schedules ran hotter.\n\nThe bell came late.\n\nThat was the first problem.\n\nWhen Jhace and Tiffani arrived, the gantry wasn’t collapsed—yet. The support columns were bowing, groaning under a load they shouldn’t have been carrying because somebody had over-pressured the feed line to meet quota and called it “necessary.”\n\nA foreman screamed, “Hold it! Hold it now!”\n\nJhace moved by habit.\n\nChest heat answered like a dog hearing its name.\n\nTiffani caught his sleeve.\n\n“Not yet.”\n\nJhace turned his head a fraction. The moment he shifted focus, the warmth behind his sternum flared—punishment for looking away from the load. The tremor in his hands ticked harder, like it wanted permission.\n\n“People are inside,” he rasped.\n\n“People are leaving,” she said, pointing.\n\nShe was right. Workers were already pouring out of the side doors, coughing, eyes wide. The structure wasn’t in free fall. It was in warning.\n\n“We wedge first,” Tiffani said. “Then you hold if wedges fail.”\n\nJhace’s jaw tightened.\n\nHe hated needing permission to do the only thing that had worked.\n\nHe dropped to one knee anyway.\n\nWedge slips. Strap loops. Collar brackets. The old tools. The honest ones.\n\nHis first wedge went in crooked.\n\nHe felt it before he saw it. The angle wrong. The bite uneven.\n\nHe yanked it back out. Knuckles scraped stone. Grainy dust cut under his nail.\n\nThe foreman saw.\n\nThe workers saw.\n\nThat was the second problem.\n\nHe wasn’t a clean solution anymore. He was a man with shaking hands making visible mistakes.\n\nHe shoved the wedge in again. Straight this time. Hammered it home with the heel of his palm because the mallet handle wouldn’t stay still.\n\nBram’s crew swarmed the brace line behind him, hands fast, straps ready, mouths full of curses and grit.\n\nFor once, labor did what labor could do.\n\nThe gantry groaned again. Dust spilled from a seam.\n\nA young man stepped into the brace line, hands hovering like he couldn’t decide whether touching the structure made him brave or stupid.\n\nCorven.\n\nJhace had seen him in the tunnels. Quiet. Competent. Always watching other men’s hands.\n\nCorven swallowed. “I… I can feel it,” he said. “The hum. It’s—”\n\n“Louder,” Jhace finished.\n\nCorven nodded hard. “In my teeth.”\n\nTiffani stepped close, slate already up. “If it hits your chest, tell me. If you taste copper, tell me. Do not improvise.”\n\nCorven’s eyes flicked to the bending column. “If it goes—”\n\n“Then we act,” Jhace said. “But not before.”\n\nThe wedges held for two breath-counts.\n\nThen the central column shifted.\n\nNot collapse. A decisive, ugly move—stone settling, iron flexing. The wedges screamed as they compressed. The brace line bowed like it was thinking about surrender.\n\nWorkers were still inside.\n\n“Tiff,” Jhace said, voice rough.\n\nShe didn’t hesitate.\n\n“Now.”\n\nJhace put both hands on the column.\n\nHeat flared behind his sternum—familiar now, terrifying in its familiarity—and copper-gold filament threaded into the seam like the stone had been waiting for him.\n\nThe column stopped shifting.\n\nThe groan of stressed iron went quiet.\n\nJhace’s breath shook out of him. He could feel the load distribution through his fingertips: uneven, trying to walk itself sideways. His hands tightened. The filament brightened in response.\n\n“Keep moving!” Bram shouted, voice cutting through the noise like a saw. “If you stop, you become debris! Count them out! Loud. Fast!”\n\nBodies poured out. Coughing. Limping. Dragging each other by collars.\n\nTiffani didn’t count in her head. She counted with her mouth. Audible. Aggressive. Like numbers could bully stone into patience.\n\n“One. Two. Three—”\n\nCorven stared at Jhace’s hands.\n\nJhace kept his eyes forward. “Your turn.”\n\nCorven flinched.\n\n“Secondary brace,” Jhace said, through clenched teeth. “Touch. Don’t squeeze. Don’t try to be a hero.”\n\nCorven stepped in and laid both palms against the brace.\n\nHis breath hitched.\n\nJhace saw it in his face before he heard it: copper taste arriving like a slap.\n\nCorven swallowed hard. “Copper.”\n\nTiffani’s pen scratched.\n\nCorven’s eyes widened. “Chest—”\n\nHeat took him.\n\nNot metaphor. Not fear. Actual furnace bloom behind the ribs.\n\nCorven made a sound like he’d been punched.\n\nThen light—thinner than Jhace’s, but real—threaded from Corven’s hands into the brace line.\n\nThe brace stopped moving.\n\nCorven’s hands started to shake immediately.\n\nNot after. Not later.\n\nNow.\n\n“Stage One onset during hold,” Tiffani murmured, writing. “Early marker.”\n\nCorven’s jaw clenched. “I can do—”\n\n“Do exactly what the structure needs,” Jhace snapped. “Nothing more.”\n\nThe tremor in Jhace’s arms surged in sympathy. His shoulders burned. Vision tunneled at the edges, copper-gold sparks skating across the periphery.\n\n“How long?” Tiffani asked.\n\nJhace didn’t answer in words. He answered in counting. Heartbeats ticking behind his teeth.\n\nEighty.\n\nOne-twenty.\n\nOne-fifty.\n\nThe last workers stumbled out—two hundred-plus bodies alive because they’d been allowed time.\n\n“Clear!” someone shouted.\n\nTiffani’s voice cut clean. “Terminate.”\n\nJhace released first.\n\nThe column held under wedges now that the load had shifted down and the pressure line had been throttled. Not safe. Not fixed. But no longer falling while people were inside.\n\nCorven released a heartbeat later and nearly fell.\n\nJhace caught him by the elbow. The tremor in his own hands made the grip feel unreliable, like even saving someone standing in front of him required negotiation with his nerves.\n\nCorven looked at his shaking fingers with horror. “It’s not stopping.”\n\n“It doesn’t,” Jhace said.\n\nCorven stared like he expected the sentence to end with a condition.\n\nIt didn’t.\n\nTiffani wrote the numbers anyway.\n\n**Evacuated: 219.**  \n**Casualties: 0.**  \n**Operators marked: 2.**\n\nBram approached, face hard under the dust. “Crown’s going to hear about this. And they’ll bring forms instead of shovels.”\n\nTiffani didn’t look up. “Good.”\n\nCorven swallowed again, still tasting copper like a curse. “How long does it last?”\n\nJhace’s jaw tightened. “Until you stop manifesting.”\n\nCorven’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. Something worse. “So never.”\n\n“Yeah.”\n\nCorven set his palm on a railing. The tremor rattled bone against metal.\n\nHe pulled back. Flexed. Tried again. Harder.\n\nIt kept moving—slow, stubborn—running even after you stop pulling.\n\nTiffani’s pen didn’t pause. Because pausing didn’t change what was true.\n\n---\n\n### ROUTE FAILURE (THREE DEAD)\n\nThe count was an argument before it was a number.\n\nThe bell came from Sector Nine, not as a roar—just a single strike repeated until someone answered.\n\nResidential stairwell. Upper-terrace split. Twelve families still inside, one route.\n\nJhace was already marked from the gantry. Corven was worse. Senne was fresh enough to stand without shaking.\n\nTiffani stared at the slate wall and made the decision she would later pretend was only math.\n\nTwo operators at one-eighty each meant coordination, handoffs, spotters, failure points.\n\nOne operator at two-forty meant simplicity.\n\nA clean line.\n\n“Single deployment,” she said. “Senne. Duration cap: two-forty. Terminate on my bell.”\n\nSenne went pale as the heat took her chest. Copper-gold light laced her forearms. Not pretty.\n\nFunctional.\n\nThey reached the stairwell mouth as stone dust sifted from the ceiling. The grid underfoot carried the doubled hum, deep and insistent, like a warning that didn’t care who listened.\n\nSenne braced under the central landing. Jhace watched her shoulders set—metalworker posture in a glassworker frame.\n\nTiffani counted. Heartbeats, not comfort. Numbers didn’t soften just because people begged.\n\nOne hundred.\n\nOne-forty.\n\nOne-eighty.\n\nAt one-ninety, a secondary beam—one Tiffani hadn’t walked because she’d trusted the map—shifted with a sound like teeth grinding.\n\nThe evacuation lane kinked.\n\nThe next family stumbled.\n\nThe stone didn’t collapse all at once. It folded, patient and specific, closing the corridor into a narrow pocket like a mouth deciding to bite down.\n\nThree people were on the wrong side when it did.\n\nTwo adults and a child coughing copper into his sleeve.\n\nTiffani saw them and for a heartbeat the slate in her hands felt obscene.\n\n“Hold,” she said, voice flat. “Keep holding.”\n\nSenne’s eyes didn’t move. She couldn’t spare the motion.\n\nTwo-ten.\n\nTwo-twenty.\n\nTwo-thirty.\n\nThe pocket didn’t clear.\n\nThe child’s coughing turned wet.\n\nAt two-forty, Tiffani rang the termination sequence anyway.\n\nBecause protocols existed for a reason.\n\nBecause if she didn’t, she would never stop.\n\nSenne released.\n\nThe stairwell collapsed into itself with a slow, final sound—stone settling into the shape it had wanted all along.\n\nThey dug for hours.\n\nThey found three bodies. The child first, because he was small enough to slip into the wrong angles.\n\nBack in the workshop, Tiffani logged it with perfect precision.\n\n**Deployment:** Senne — 240 heartbeats  \n**Evacuated:** 47  \n**Casualties:** 3  \n**Cause:** Inadequate route assessment. Secondary beam shear.\n\nShe wrote *cause* as if precision could make it reversible.\n\nHer hand moved to write the next revision. The chalk touched slate.\n\nNothing came out.\n\nShe stared at the number.\n\nThree.\n\nNot a count.\n\nThree names she would never know.\n\nThe chalk snapped in her grip.\n\nShe set the broken pieces down carefully. Picked up a new stick. Wrote:\n\n**Route spotters required. Three per deployment. No exceptions.**\n\nUnderlined it twice.\n\nThen she closed the slate and sat in the dark workshop, counting her own breaths because the numbers on the page wouldn’t behave.\n\nHer fingers kept shaking anyway.\n\n---"
          ]
        },
